> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Evolution of Microprocessors and Microcontrollers

### 1.1.1 Microprocessor – Formal Definition
A **microprocessor (µP)** is a single-chip, general-purpose, clock-driven, register-based central processing unit (CPU) fabricated on a very-large-scale integration (VLSI) semiconductor die. It contains only the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Control Unit (CU)**, and **register array**. Memory, I/O, and peripheral devices must be connected externally through a system bus (Address, Data, Control).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A microprocessor is *not* a computer by itself. It becomes a computer only when interfaced with memory (RAM/ROM) and I/O devices.

### 1.1.2 Microcontroller – Formal Definition
A **microcontroller (µC)** is a single-chip microcomputer fabricated using VLSI technology that contains a CPU, internal memory (ROM for program + RAM for data), parallel and serial I/O ports, timers/counters, an interrupt controller, and often analog-to-digital converters — **all on the same silicon die**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):** "A microcontroller is a complete computer-on-a-chip optimized for embedded control, integrating the processor, program memory (ROM/Flash), data memory (RAM), I/O, and peripherals on a single IC."

### 1.1.3 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a city:
- The **microprocessor** is like the *city government brain* (CPU only). To function, it needs roads (buses), offices (memory), and public services (I/O) all built around it externally.
- The **microcontroller** is like a *self-contained smart apartment building* — the brain, the mailroom (RAM), the library (ROM), the elevator (I/O), the intercom (UART), and the fire alarm (timers) are **all pre-built inside**. It only needs power and a task description.

### 1.1.4 Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | Microprocessor (µP) | Microcontroller (µC) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Von Neumann (typical) | Harvard (typical, e.g., 8051) |
| Memory | External (RAM/ROM) | Internal + optional external |
| I/O Ports | External | On-chip |
| Cost | Higher system cost | Lower system cost |
| Power | Higher consumption | Optimized for low power |
| Target Use | General-purpose computing | Embedded control |
| Example | Intel 8085, 8086, Core i7 | Intel 8051, PIC16, MSP430, ARM Cortex-M |
| BIT width | 4 / 8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bit | 4 / 8 / 16 / 32 bit |

### 1.1.5 Timeline of Evolution

| Year | Milestone Device | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1971 | Intel 4004 | World's first 4-bit µP (2300 transistors) |
| 1972 | Intel 8008 | First 8-bit µP |
| 1974 | Intel 8080 | 8-bit, 5000 transistors, used in Altair 8800 |
| 1976 | Intel 8085 | 8-bit, 246 instructions |
| 1978 | Intel 8086 | 16-bit foundation of x86 family |
| 1980 | Intel 8051 | First commercial 8-bit µC (MCS-51 family) |
| 1985 | Intel 80386 | 32-bit, fully 32-bit registers |
| 1990s | PIC, AVR | Rise of RISC-based 8-bit µC |
| 2000s+ | ARM Cortex-M | Dominant 32-bit embedded µC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Fact:** The Intel 8051 (released in 1980) was originally designed by John H. Wharton. It became the most successful 8-bit µC architecture, with hundreds of variants from manufacturers like Atmel (now Microchip), Philips/NXP, Silicon Labs, Dallas/Maxim, and Infineon.

---

## 1.2 Overview of Intel 8051 Family of Microcontrollers

The **MCS-51 family** (Intel 8051 family) refers to a series of 8-bit microcontrollers originally manufactured by Intel beginning in 1980. The family is structured around the original 8051 core, with various members adding or removing features (memory size, I/O, peripherals).

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition
The **8051 family** is a set of 8-bit, Harvard-architecture, CISC microcontrollers sharing a common instruction set and core architecture, differentiated by on-chip memory size, number of timers, I/O lines, and peripheral mix.

### 1.2.2 Key Salient Features of the Original 8051
- **8-bit CPU** with registers A, B, and 16-bit DPTR.
- **Harvard architecture** — separate program and data memory buses.
- **4 KB on-chip ROM** (program memory).
- **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (data memory).
- **Four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3).
- **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0, T1).
- **Full-duplex UART** (serial port).
- **5 interrupt sources** with 2 priority levels.
- **64 KB external program memory** address space.
- **64 KB external data memory** address space.
- **111 instructions** in the instruction set.
- **12 MHz** typical clock (one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 8051 uses **CISC architecture** with variable-length instructions (1, 2, or 3 bytes). One machine cycle = 12 oscillator cycles.

### 1.2.3 Important Family Members

| Member | ROM | RAM | Timers | UART | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8031 | 0 KB | 128 B | 2 | 1 | ROM-less, needs external ROM |
| 8051 | 4 KB | 128 B | 2 | 1 | Original member |
| 8052 | 8 KB | 256 B | 3 | 1 | Enhanced version of 8051 |
| 8751 | 4 KB (EPROM) | 128 B | 2 | 1 | UV-erasable EPROM |
| AT89C51 (Atmel) | 4 KB (Flash) | 128 B | 2 | 1 | Flash-based, modern variant |
| AT89S52 | 8 KB (Flash) | 256 B | 3 | 1 | Modern workhorse |
| P89V51RD2 (NXP) | 64 KB (Flash) | 1 KB | 3 | 1 | High-end variant |

### 1.2.4 Intuitive Analogy
Think of the 8051 family as the **Maruti 800 car family**:
- The **base model** (8051) has a basic engine and minimal features.
- The **8031** is a "skeleton" — frame only, no engine (no ROM), customer adds engine.
- The **8052** is the "deluxe" model with more boot space (RAM) and an extra AC vent (timer).
- The **AT89C51/52** are modernized versions with fuel injection (Flash memory) instead of carburetor (ROM/EPROM).

---

## 1.3 Architecture of Intel 8051 – The ALU (Block-Level Introduction)

The **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** of the 8051 is an 8-bit arithmetic and logical computation engine responsible for performing all data manipulation operations under direction of the Control Unit.

### 1.3.1 Formal Definition
The **8051 ALU** is an 8-bit combinational and sequential arithmetic-logic unit that accepts two 8-bit operands (one always from the Accumulator), performs operations specified by the opcode, sets status flags in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register, and returns the 8-bit result in the Accumulator.

### 1.3.2 Key Capabilities
- 8-bit **addition** with carry, **subtraction** with borrow.
- 8-bit **multiplication** (8×8 → 16-bit result).
- 8-bit **division** (8÷8 → 8-bit quotient + 8-bit remainder).
- Logical operations: **AND, OR, XOR, NOT**.
- **Bit-level** operations on individual bits of internal RAM (bit-addressable Boolean processor).
- **Rotate** and **swap** operations.
- **Increment** and **decrement**.
- **Decimal adjust** (DA A) for BCD arithmetic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unique 8051 Feature — Boolean Processor:** The 8051's ALU contains an additional *bit-manipulation* engine that can perform SETB, CLR, ANL (bit), ORL (bit), CPL, and MOV (bit) on 128 individually addressable bits in the SFR and bit-addressable RAM region. This made the 8051 exceptionally powerful for control applications (relays, switches, solenoid valves).

### 1.3.3 Intuitive Analogy
The ALU is the **kitchen of a restaurant**:
- The **Accumulator (A)** is the chef's main mixing bowl.
- The **second operand** (from RAM or a register) is the ingredient added to the bowl.
- The **opcode** (instruction) is the recipe (stir-fry, boil, chop).
- The **PSW flags** (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity) are the taste-test results: spicy? sweet? overflowing? well-balanced?
- The **result** comes back in the mixing bowl (Accumulator) ready to serve (store) elsewhere.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Architecture — Deep Block-Level Analysis

The 8051 employs a **Harvard architecture**, meaning separate address and data buses exist for **program memory (code ROM)** and **data memory (RAM)**. This allows simultaneous fetch of an opcode and operand, accelerating execution.

### 2.1.1 The 8051 Internal Block Diagram (Logical View)

The 8051 consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — contains the ALU, Accumulator (A or ACC), B register, PSW, and registers used for temporary data storage.
2. **Program Memory (ROM, 4 KB on-chip)** — non-volatile storage for the program instructions.
3. **Data Memory (RAM, 128 bytes on-chip)** — volatile read/write storage; subdivided into the *register banks*, *bit-addressable area*, and *general-purpose area*.
4. **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — a separate 128-byte address space (80H–FFH) for I/O ports, timers, serial port, interrupt control, etc.
5. **Four I/O Ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) — 32 bidirectional I/O lines.
6. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1).
7. **Serial Port (UART)** — full-duplex asynchronous communication.
8. **Interrupt Controller** — handles 5 sources with 2 priority levels.
9. **Clock Circuitry** — on-chip oscillator amplifier requiring an external crystal (typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**).
10. **Bus Control** — generates external memory access signals (PSEN, ALE, EA).

### 2.1.2 Key Pin-Out Summary (40-pin DIP)

| Pin(s) | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1–8 | P1.0–P1.7 | Port 1 (8-bit bidirectional I/O) |
| 9 | RST | Reset (active High, 2 machine cycles) |
| 10–17 | P3.0–P3.7 | Port 3 (alternate functions: RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |
| 18 | XTAL2 | Crystal output |
| 19 | XTAL1 | Crystal input |
| 20 | GND | Ground |
| 21–28 | P2.0–P2.7 | Port 2 (high address byte for external memory) |
| 29 | PSEN | Program Store Enable (external ROM read strobe) |
| 30 | ALE | Address Latch Enable (latches low byte of address) |
| 31 | EA | External Access (Low = execute external ROM) |
| 32–39 | P0.0–P0.7 | Port 0 (multiplexed low address + data bus) |
| 40 | VCC | +5V supply |

---

## 2.2 The 8051 Memory Organization — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Harvard Memory Map

**Program Memory (up to 64 KB):**
- **0000H–0002H** → Reset vector (jump to initialization code).
- **0003H–000AH** → External Interrupt 0 (INT0) ISR.
- **000BH–0012H** → Timer 0 Overflow ISR.
- **0013H–001AH** → External Interrupt 1 (INT1) ISR.
- **001BH–0022H** → Timer 1 Overflow ISR.
- **0023H** → Serial Port ISR.
- **002BH** → Timer 2 Overflow ISR (8052 only).
- **0030H–0FFFH** → User code area (when 4 KB on-chip ROM is used).
- **1000H–FFFFH** → External program memory.

**Data Memory (64 KB external + 256 bytes internal):**

Internal RAM layout (00H–7FH, 128 bytes):

| Address Range | Region | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00H–1FH | Register Banks R0–R7 | 4 banks × 8 bytes | Selected via PSW bits RS1, RS0 |
| 20H–2FH | Bit-Addressable Area | 16 bytes (128 bits) | Boolean processor target |
| 30H–7FH | General-Purpose RAM | 80 bytes | Scratchpad |

Internal RAM layout (80H–FFH, Special Function Registers, 128 bytes):

| Address | SFR | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 80H | P0 | Port 0 |
| 81H | SP | Stack Pointer |
| 82H | DPL | Data Pointer (low) |
| 83H | DPH | Data Pointer (high) |
| 87H | PCON | Power Control |
| 88H | TCON | Timer Control |
| 89H | TMOD | Timer Mode |
| 8AH | TL0 | Timer 0 low |
| 8BH | TL1 | Timer 1 low |
| 8CH | TH0 | Timer 0 high |
| 8DH | TH1 | Timer 1 high |
| 90H | P1 | Port 1 |
| 98H | SCON | Serial Control |
| 99H | SBUF | Serial Data Buffer |
| A0H | P2 | Port 2 |
| A8H | IE | Interrupt Enable |
| B0H | P3 | Port 3 |
| B8H | IP | Interrupt Priority |
| D0H | PSW | Program Status Word |
| E0H | A (ACC) | Accumulator |
| F0H | B | B Register |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** Not all 128 bytes of the SFR area (80H–FFH) are defined. Only those listed above are accessible. Reading undefined SFRs returns undefined data.

### 2.2.2 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit by Bit

| Bit | Symbol | Address (bit) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | CY | D7H | Carry Flag (arithmetic carry/borrow, Boolean accumulator) |
| PSW.6 | AC | D6H | Auxiliary Carry (BCD operation flag) |
| PSW.5 | F0 | D5H | User-defined flag 0 |
| PSW.4 | RS1 | D4H | Register Bank Select 1 |
| PSW.3 | RS0 | D3H | Register Bank Select 0 |
| PSW.2 | OV | D2H | Overflow Flag (signed arithmetic) |
| PSW.1 | – | D1H | Reserved (user-definable on some derivatives) |
| PSW.0 | P | D0H | Parity Flag (set if A has odd number of 1s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Register Bank Selection via RS1, RS0:**
> - **00** → Bank 0 (R0–R7 at 00H–07H)
> - **01** → Bank 1 (R0–R7 at 08H–0FH)
> - **10** → Bank 2 (R0–R7 at 10H–17H)
> - **11** → Bank 3 (R0–R7 at 18H–1FH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parity Flag Behavior:** The P flag is **automatically set/cleared by hardware after every instruction that writes to the Accumulator**. It is set to 1 if the resulting A has an *even* number of 1s (even parity convention).

---

## 2.3 The 8051 Instruction Set — Comprehensive Analysis

The 8051 has **111 distinct instructions** that combine into many more mnemonics. Instructions are classified by:

### 2.3.1 Instruction Categories

| Category | Mnemonics | Byte Size | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Transfer | MOV, MOVX, MOVC, XCH, XCHD, PUSH, POP, SWAP | 1, 2, 3 | 1 or 2 |
| Arithmetic | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, INC, DEC, MUL, DIV, DA A | 1 or 2 | 1 or 4 |
| Logical | ANL, ORL, XRL, CLR, CPL, RL, RLC, RR, RRC | 1 or 2 | 1 or 2 |
| Boolean (Bit) | ANL (bit), ORL (bit), MOV (bit), CLR (bit), SETB (bit), CPL (bit), JC, JNC, JB, JNB, JBC | 2 | 2 |
| Program Branch | ACALL, LCALL, RET, RETI, AJMP, LJMP, SJMP, JMP @A+DPTR, JZ, JNZ, CJNE, DJNZ, NOP | 1, 2, 3 | 1, 2, 4 |
| Pseudo-ops (Assembler) | ORG, END, DB, DW, EQU, BIT, DS, $ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The 8051 has **24 addressing modes** (not 11 as in 8085). The most powerful is *indexed addressing* (MOVC A,@A+DPTR), used for table lookups (e.g., ASCII conversion, 7-segment display patterns).

### 2.3.2 Instruction Timing — Crystal Frequency Relation

The fundamental timing relationship in the 8051 is:

$$T_{cycle} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

where $T_{osc}$ is one oscillator period and $T_{cycle}$ is one machine cycle. Each machine cycle is the basic unit for instruction timing. Given a typical crystal of $11.0592\,\text{MHz}$:

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$$

This specific frequency is chosen because it is an integer multiple of standard baud rates: $11.0592\,\text{MHz} / 12 = 921.6\,\text{kHz}$ machine cycles per second, which divides evenly into 9600 baud (9600 = 921600 / 96), making UART timing precise.

---

## 2.4 The 8051 Addressing Modes — Comprehensive Analysis

The 8051 supports **five (or eight) primary addressing modes** for data transfer and arithmetic/logical operations.

### 2.4.1 Immediate Addressing
The operand is a constant embedded in the instruction.

$$\text{MOV A, \#25H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow 25H$$

### 2.4.2 Register Addressing
The operand is one of the eight working registers R0–R7 of the currently selected bank.

$$\text{MOV A, R3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow R3$$

### 2.4.3 Direct Addressing
The operand is the byte at a specified 8-bit data memory address.

$$\text{MOV A, 30H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow (30H)$$

Used to access the lower 128 bytes of RAM and the SFRs.

### 2.4.4 Register Indirect Addressing
The operand's address is held in a pointer register (R0, R1, SP, or DPTR). The `@` symbol denotes indirection.

$$\text{MOV A, @R0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow ((R0))$$

Used for accessing external data memory with `MOVX`, program memory with `MOVC`, and the stack with `PUSH/POP`.

### 2.4.5 Indexed Addressing
Used only with `MOVC` and `JMP`. Combines DPTR (or PC) with the Accumulator as an offset.

$$\text{MOVC A, @A+DPTR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow (A + DPTR)$$

### 2.4.6 Extended / Bit Addressing (Boolean)
Operates on a single bit within the bit-addressable region (20H–2FH) or in the SFR bit-addressable area.

$$\text{SETB P1.0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P1.0 \leftarrow 1$$

$$\text{ANL C, 20H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C \leftarrow C \text{ AND } (20H)$$

### 2.4.7 Relative Addressing (Branch instructions)
Used by `SJMP` and all conditional jumps. The operand is a signed 8-bit offset (−128 to +127) added to the PC.

$$\text{SJMP LABEL} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 2 + \text{offset}$$

### 2.4.8 Absolute Addressing (ACALL/AJMP)
The 11-bit address is embedded directly: 5 most-significant bits come from the current PC, 8 LSBs from the instruction, allowing a 2-KB page-relative jump.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 – High-Yield Formula & Constant Table**

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| One machine cycle | $T_{cy} = 12 \cdot T_{osc}$ | seconds | $T_{osc} = 1/f_{osc}$ |
| Execution time | $T_{exec} = N_{cy} \cdot T_{cy}$ | seconds | $N_{cy}$ = machine cycles for the instruction |
| Baud rate (Timer 1, Mode 2) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - X)}$ | bps | $X$ = TH1 reload value |
| TH1 reload for 9600 baud | $X = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot 32 \cdot \text{Baud} \cdot 2^{\text{SMOD}}}$ | — | For 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0: $X = 253 = 0xFD$ |
| Timer count for delay | $N = \text{delay} / T_{cy}$ | counts | Total increments before overflow |
| TH/TL reload value | $X = 2^{16} - N$ (16-bit) | — | For Mode 1 timers |
| Program memory max | $2^{16} = 65536 = 64\,\text{KB}$ | bytes | 16-bit PC |
| Stack pointer initial value | SP = 07H (default) | bytes | Grows upward; first PUSH stores at 08H |
| Addressable bits (Boolean) | $128$ | bits | 16 bytes × 8 = 128 |
| I/O ports | $4 \times 8 = 32$ | lines | P0, P1, P2, P3 |
| Register banks | $4$ | banks | Selected by PSW[4:3] |
| Number of interrupts | $5$ (8051) / $6$ (8052) | — | INT0, T0, INT1, T1, UART (+T2) |
| Power supply | $V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$ | volts | ±10% tolerance |
| Max clock frequency | $12\,\text{MHz}$ (original) | Hz | Modern variants support up to 40+ MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Table-Error Rule:** For absolute-value expressions, we use $\vert x \vert$ (with `\vert`) to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of 8051 Timer Delay — Step by Step

**Problem:** Generate a delay of exactly **50 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** (16-bit), with $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$.

### Step 1: Compute the machine cycle period.

The relationship between oscillator period and machine cycle period is:

$$T_{cy} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Substituting $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$:

$$T_{cy} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{cy} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

The machine cycle is the time increment by which the timer register increments by 1.

### Step 2: Compute the total number of timer counts needed.

The required number of timer counts $N$ is the total delay divided by the period of one increment:

$$N = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{cy}}$$

$$N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N = \frac{50\,000}{1.085}$$

$$N \approx 46\,082 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 3: Check feasibility against 16-bit register range.

The maximum value a 16-bit timer (Mode 1) can hold is:

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65\,536 \text{ counts}$$

Since $N \approx 46\,082 < 65\,536$, the delay **fits in a single timer overflow**. No software loop is required.

### Step 4: Compute the reload value for TH0 and TL0.

In Mode 1, the timer counts up from the initial value to $2^{16}$, then overflows and sets the TF0 flag. The reload value $X$ is:

$$X = 2^{16} - N$$

$$X = 65\,536 - 46\,082$$

$$X = 19\,454$$

Converting $19\,454$ to hexadecimal:

$$19\,454 \div 4096 = 4 \text{ remainder } 3\,046 \quad (\text{high nibble: 4})$$

$$3\,046 \div 256 = 11 \text{ remainder } 230 \quad (\text{low byte high nibble: B, low: E6})$$

More precisely:

$$19\,454 = 4 \times 4096 + 3\,046$$
$$3\,046 = 11 \times 256 + 230 = 0xB \times 256 + 0xE6$$
$$\Rightarrow 19\,454 = 0x4BE6$$

### Step 5: Reload value breakdown.

$$\text{TH0} = 0x4B \quad \text{(high byte of initial value)}$$
$$\text{TL0} = 0xE6 \quad \text{(low byte of initial value)}$$

The full derivation chain is consolidated below:

$$T_{cy} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$N = \frac{50\,000}{1.085} \approx 46\,082$$

$$X = 65\,536 - 46\,082 = 19\,454 = 0x4BE6$$

$$\text{TH0} = 0x4B, \quad \text{TL0} = 0xE6$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Timer 1 Reload for 9600 Baud UART

**Problem:** Compute the TH1 reload value for the 8051 UART operating at **9600 baud, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$ and SMOD = 0.

### Step 1: State the baud-rate equation.

The standard 8051 UART baud rate using Timer 1 in Mode 2 with the Serial Port in Mode 1 or 3 is:

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

where $X$ = TH1 reload value, SMOD = PCON.7 (0 or 1).

### Step 2: Substitute known values.

Given $\text{Baud} = 9600$, $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$, SMOD = 0:

$$9\,600 = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \cdot \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

$$9\,600 = \frac{1}{32} \cdot \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to isolate the $(256 - X)$ term.

$$9\,600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

$$9\,600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

$$(256 - X) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \cdot 9\,600}$$

$$(256 - X) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$(256 - X) = 3$$

### Step 4: Solve for $X$.

$$X = 256 - 3 = 253$$

$$X = 253 = 0xFD$$

### Step 5: Verify the result.

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \cdot \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \cdot 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot 3}$$

$$= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1\,152} = 9\,600\,\text{bps} \;\checkmark$$

Therefore, **TH1 = 0xFD = 253** is the correct reload value for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz with SMOD = 0.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Evolution Comparison

| Generation | Example Device | Data Bus | Address Bus | Memory Addressing | Transistors | Major Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen (1971) | Intel 4004 | 4-bit | 12-bit (4 KB) | 4 KB max | 2,300 | Calculators, controllers |
| 1st Gen 8-bit | Intel 8008 | 8-bit | 14-bit (16 KB) | 16 KB | 3,500 | Terminals, calculators |
| 2nd Gen (1974) | Intel 8080 | 8-bit | 16-bit (64 KB) | 64 KB | 4,500 | Altair 8800 PC, industrial |
| 2nd Gen (1976) | Intel 8085 | 8-bit | 16-bit | 64 KB | 6,500 | Widely used in training, control |
| 3rd Gen (1978) | Intel 8086 | 16-bit | 20-bit (1 MB) | 1 MB | 29,000 | IBM PC precursor |
| 3rd Gen (1979) | Intel 8088 | 8-bit ext, 16-bit int | 20-bit | 1 MB | 29,000 | Original IBM PC |
| 3rd Gen (1980) | **Intel 8051 (µC)** | 8-bit | 16-bit (64 KB) | 64 KB + 64 KB | 60,000 | **Embedded control** |
| 4th Gen (1985) | Intel 80386 | 32-bit | 32-bit (4 GB) | 4 GB | 275,000 | Desktop OS, multitasking |
| 5th Gen (1993+) | Pentium | 64-bit ext, 32-bit int | 32-bit | 4 GB | 3.1 M | Multimedia, mainstream PC |
| Modern (2000+) | Cortex-M4 | 32-bit | 32-bit | 4 GB | — | IoT, motor control, drones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 8051 endures:** The 8051's combination of simple architecture, rich Boolean processing, deterministic timing, and vast ecosystem (still in production by NXP, Microchip, Infineon, Silicon Labs) keeps it relevant for automotive, industrial, and IoT edge devices even in 2024.

---

## 3.4 Stack Operation — Analytical Walkthrough

The 8051 stack is a **software-controlled, upward-growing, internal-RAM-resident LIFO structure**. The Stack Pointer (SP) is an 8-bit SFR (81H) that points to the *last used* location. The stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses).

**Reset initializes SP = 07H.** This means the first `PUSH` stores data at `08H`, then SP becomes 08H.

**Example sequence:**

1. After reset: `SP = 0x07`
2. `PUSH ACC` → stores A at `08H`, then `SP = 0x08`
3. `PUSH B` → stores B at `09H`, then `SP = 0x09`
4. `POP B` → loads B from `09H`, then `SP = 0x08`
5. `POP ACC` → loads A from `08H`, then `SP = 0x07`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Because the default SP=07H, the first stack operations *overwrite register bank 1* (R0–R7 of bank 1 at 08H–0FH). To prevent this, **always initialize SP explicitly** (e.g., `MOV SP, #0x30`) at the start of any program that uses the stack.

---

## 3.5 Register Bank Switching — Context Save Without Stack

**Scenario:** An ISR must use R0–R7, but the main program also uses them. Instead of pushing all 8 registers, switch register banks using PSW.3 and PSW.4 (RS0, RS1).

**Algorithm:**

$$\text{Switch to bank 2: } \text{SETB RS0; CLR RS1}$$

Now R0–R7 of the ISR refer to physical RAM 10H–17H, preserving the main program's R0–R7 at 00H–07H (bank 0).

$$\text{Restore: } \text{CLR RS0; CLR RS1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the 8051's elegant efficiency features — a 2-bit PSW write switches an entire bank of registers, saving up to 16 bytes of stack operations and 16 machine cycles per context switch.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Block-Level Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **internal block architecture** of the Intel 8051, showing the major functional units and their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUcore[CPU CORE - ALU, A, B, PSW] --> RegBanks[REGISTER BANKS R0 R7]
    CPUcore --> SP[STACK POINTER]
    CPUcore --> DPTR[DPTR 16 BIT]
    ROM[4 KB PROGRAM ROM 0000H 0FFFH] --> BusCtrl[BUS CONTROL LOGIC]
    RAM[128 BYTE DATA RAM 00H 7FH] --> BusCtrl
    SFR[SFR SPACE 80H FFH] --> BusCtrl
    BusCtrl --> CPUcore
    P0[PORT 0 P0 0 TO P0 7] --> BusCtrl
    P1[PORT 1 P1 0 TO P1 7] --> BusCtrl
    P2[PORT 2 P2 0 TO P2 7] --> BusCtrl
    P3[PORT 3 P3 0 TO P3 7] --> BusCtrl
    T0[TIMER 0 16 BIT] --> BusCtrl
    T1[TIMER 1 16 BIT] --> BusCtrl
    UART[UART SERIAL PORT] --> BusCtrl
    INT[INTERRUPT CONTROLLER 5 SOURCES] --> CPUcore
    OSC[OSCILLATOR AND CLOCK] --> CPUcore
    OSC --> T0
    OSC --> T1
    OSC --> UART
```

---

## 4.2 Harvard Architecture — Code vs. Data Memory Separation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HarvardBus
        PC[PROGRAM COUNTER PC 16 BIT]
        CodeBus[CODE ADDRESS BUS 16 BIT]
        DataBus[DATA ADDRESS BUS 16 BIT]
    end
    PC --> CodeBus
    CodeBus --> ROMMem[PROGRAM MEMORY 64 KB MAX - INTERNAL 4 KB ROM PLUS EXTERNAL]
    DataBus --> RAMMem[DATA MEMORY - INTERNAL 128 BYTES RAM PLUS EXTERNAL 64 KB]
    CPU[8051 CPU] --> PC
    CPU --> DataBus
    ROMMem -.FETCH.-> CPU
    RAMMem -.READ WRITE.-> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harvard matters:** Because program and data memory use *separate buses*, the 8051 can fetch the next instruction from ROM in the same machine cycle that it reads/writes a data byte in RAM. This is the source of the 8051's "1-cycle per byte" data-move efficiency.

---

## 4.3 8051 CPU and ALU Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Accumulator[8 BIT ACCUMULATOR A OR ACC] --> ALU[8 BIT ALU]
    BReg[8 BIT B REGISTER] --> ALU
    TempReg1[8 BIT TEMPORARY REG 1] --> ALU
    TempReg2[8 BIT TEMPORARY REG 2] --> ALU
    ALU --> Accumulator
    ALU --> FlagLogic[FLAG LOGIC CIRCUIT]
    FlagLogic --> PSWReg[PSW REGISTER - CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P]
    PSWReg --> FlagLogic
    ControlUnit[INSTRUCTION DECODER AND CONTROL UNIT] --> ALU
    ControlUnit --> Accumulator
    ControlUnit --> FlagLogic
    Opcode[INSTRUCTION OPCODE] --> ControlUnit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALU Operands Rule:** In nearly all ALU operations, **one operand is always the Accumulator**. The other operand is provided from a register (R0–R7), a direct address, an indirect address, or an immediate value embedded in the instruction. After the operation, the result is **always** returned to the Accumulator.

---

## 4.4 8051 Memory Map — Internal RAM Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InternalRAM[INTERNAL DATA RAM 128 BYTES 00H TO 7FH]
        RB0[00H TO 07H - REGISTER BANK 0 - R0 R7]
        RB1[08H TO 0FH - REGISTER BANK 1 - R0 R7]
        RB2[10H TO 17H - REGISTER BANK 2 - R0 R7]
        RB3[18H TO 1FH - REGISTER BANK 3 - R0 R7]
        BitArea[20H TO 2FH - BIT ADDRESSABLE AREA 128 BITS]
        GPR[30H TO 7FH - GENERAL PURPOSE RAM 80 BYTES]
    end
    subgraph SFRArea[SFR SPACE 80H TO FFH]
        P0SFR[80H - P0]
        SPSFR[81H - SP]
        DPSFR[82H 83H - DPTR]
        P1SFR[90H - P1]
        SCONSFR[98H - SCON]
        P2SFR[A0H - P2]
        P3SFR[B0H - P3]
        PSWSFR[D0H - PSW]
        ASFR[E0H - ACC]
        BSFR[F0H - B]
    end
    InternalRAM --> SFRArea
```

---

## 4.5 Addressing Mode Flow — How the CPU Locates the Operand

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Instruction[FETCH INSTRUCTION FROM PROGRAM MEMORY] --> Decode[DECODE OPCODE]
    Decode --> Mode1{ADDRESSING MODE}
    Mode1 -->|IMMEDIATE| OperandImm[OPERAND IS CONSTANT IN INSTRUCTION]
    Mode1 -->|REGISTER| OperandReg[OPERAND IS R0 R7 OF CURRENT BANK]
    Mode1 -->|DIRECT| OperandDir[OPERAND IS BYTE AT 8 BIT GIVEN ADDRESS]
    Mode1 -->|REGISTER INDIRECT| OperandInd[ADDRESS HELD IN R0 R1 SP OR DPTR]
    Mode1 -->|INDEXED| OperandIdx[ADDRESS = A PLUS DPTR OR A PLUS PC]
    Mode1 -->|BIT ADDRESS| OperandBit[OPERAND IS A SINGLE BIT IN 20H 2FH OR SFR]
    OperandImm --> Execute[EXECUTE INSTRUCTION]
    OperandReg --> Execute
    OperandDir --> Execute
    OperandInd --> Execute
    OperandIdx --> Execute
    OperandBit --> Execute
    Execute --> Writeback[WRITE RESULT TO A OR DESTINATION]
    Writeback --> UpdateFlags[UPDATE PSW FLAGS]
    UpdateFlags --> Next[FETCH NEXT INSTRUCTION]
```

---

## 4.6 Evolution Flow — Microprocessor to Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MP1[1971 INTEL 4004 - FIRST 4 BIT µP] --> MP2[1974 INTEL 8080 - 8 BIT µP]
    MP2 --> MP3[1976 INTEL 8085 - ENHANCED 8 BIT µP]
    MP3 --> MP4[1978 INTEL 8086 - 16 BIT µP]
    MP4 --> MP5[1985 INTEL 80386 - 32 BIT µP]
    MP1 --> MC1[NEED FOR SINGLE CHIP SOLUTION]
    MC1 --> MC2[1980 INTEL 8051 - FIRST COMMERCIAL 8 BIT µC]
    MC2 --> MC3[8052 ENHANCED 8 KB ROM 256 RAM]
    MC3 --> MC4[8751 EPROM VERSION]
    MC4 --> MC5[AT89C51 AT89S52 FLASH VERSIONS]
    MC5 --> MC6[MODERN 8051 DERIVATIVES NXP P89 INFINEON XC800]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows two parallel evolutionary tracks — the **microprocessor track** (top) and the **microcontroller track** (bottom). The "fork" in 1971 shows that microcontrollers emerged as a *response* to the need to integrate memory and I/O with the processor, eliminating the external glue logic of microprocessor systems.

---

## 4.7 8051 Instruction Cycle and Machine Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Crystal[CRYSTAL OSCILLATOR f_osc] --> OscPeriod[OSCILLATOR PERIOD T_osc = 1 / f_osc]
    OscPeriod --> Divide[12 OSCILLATOR PERIODS GROUPED]
    Divide --> MachineCycle[ONE MACHINE CYCLE T_cy = 12 / f_osc]
    MachineCycle --> InstExec[SINGLE BYTE SINGLE CYCLE INSTRUCTION EXECUTES]
    MachineCycle --> Multicycle[2 OR 4 MACHINE CYCLE INSTRUCTIONS]
    Multicycle --> Branch[MUL DIV AND LONG JUMPS USE 4 CYCLES]
    InstExec --> FetchExec[SIMULTANEOUS FETCH AND EXECUTE DUE TO HARVARD]
    FetchExec --> NextInst[NEXT INSTRUCTION FETCH]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. List any two examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Single-chip CPU only; needs external memory and I/O | Single-chip microcomputer with CPU, memory, I/O integrated |
| Memory | External (RAM/ROM) | Internal + optional external |
| Cost of full system | Higher (many external ICs) | Lower (single chip solution) |
| Power consumption | Higher | Optimized for low power |
| Application | General-purpose computers | Embedded control systems |
| Example | Intel 8085, Intel 8086 | Intel 8051, PIC16F877 |

**Q2. [3 Marks] List the salient features of Intel 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**
1. 8-bit CPU with **4 KB on-chip ROM** and **128 bytes on-chip RAM**.
2. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 digital I/O lines.
3. **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0, T1) with four operating modes each.
4. **Full-duplex UART** (serial communication port).
5. **5 interrupt sources** (INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial) with 2 priority levels.
6. **Harvard architecture** with separate 64 KB program and 64 KB data memory address spaces.
7. **111 instructions** including powerful bit-manipulation (Boolean) instructions.
8. **On-chip oscillator** with external crystal (typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**); one machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Memory Organization

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Draw and explain the internal block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller. List all major functional units.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 internal block diagram is presented below (textual rendition for the written exam):

```
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       8051 MICROCONTROLLER                      |
|  +--------+   +-----------+   +-----------+   +------------+    |
|  |   CPU  |   | 4 KB ROM  |   | 128 B RAM |   |  SFR AREA  |   |
|  | (ALU+A |   | (Program  |   | (Internal |   | (Special   |   |
|  |  +B+   |<->|  Memory)  |   |   Data)   |   |  Function  |   |
|  |  PSW)  |   | 0000-0FFF |   |  00-7F    |   |  Registers)|   |
|  +--------+   +-----------+   +-----------+   +------------+    |
|       |             |               |                |          |
|       |     +-------+-------+-------+--------+-------+          |
|       |     |       BUS CONTROL AND DECODING LOGIC    |        |
|       |     +---+-------+-------+--------+--------+---+        |
|       |         |       |       |        |        |             |
|       |      +-----+ +-----+ +-----+  +-------+ +-------+        |
|       |      | P0  | | P1  | | P2  |  | T0/T1 | | UART  |       |
|       |      +-----+ +-----+ +-----+  +-------+ +-------+       |
|       |                                                       |
|       +---> [INTERRUPT CONTROLLER] <---[OSCILLATOR CIRCUIT]    |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Major Functional Units:**

1. **CPU** — Contains the 8-bit ALU, Accumulator (A), B register, and Program Status Word (PSW). Performs arithmetic, logical, and data-transfer operations.
2. **Program Memory (4 KB ROM)** — Stores program instructions; address space 0000H–0FFFH on-chip, expandable to 64 KB externally.
3. **Data Memory (128 B RAM)** — Stores variables, stack, and working data; address space 00H–7FH.
4. **SFR Space (128 B at 80H–FFH)** — Holds control registers for ports, timers, serial, interrupts, and stack pointer.
5. **Four I/O Ports (P0–P3)** — 32 digital I/O lines; P3 has alternate functions (RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, RD, WR).
6. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — T0 and T1 with four programmable modes.
7. **UART (Serial Port)** — Full-duplex asynchronous serial communication.
8. **Interrupt Controller** — Manages 5 interrupt sources with 2 priority levels.
9. **Oscillator & Clock Circuitry** — Drives CPU and peripherals from external crystal.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the 8051 internal RAM organization in detail with a neat diagram. List the SFRs and their addresses.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 internal RAM is divided into three primary regions within the 128 bytes at addresses **00H–7FH**, plus a separate 128-byte **SFR area at 80H–FFH**.

**Region 1 — Register Banks (00H–1FH, 32 bytes):**
The lower 32 bytes are divided into 4 banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). Only one bank is active at a time, selected by PSW.3 (RS0) and PSW.4 (RS1).

- Bank 0: 00H–07H (default after reset)
- Bank 1: 08H–0FH
- Bank 2: 10H–17H
- Bank 3: 18H–1FH

**Region 2 — Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH, 16 bytes = 128 bits):**
This region allows individual bits to be addressed using instructions like `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C, bit`, etc. Bits are addressed 00H–7FH within this region (where bit 00H = 20H.0, bit 7FH = 2FH.7).

**Region 3 — General-Purpose RAM (30H–7FH, 80 bytes):**
Used for scratchpad variables, arrays, buffers, and stack.

**SFR Space (80H–FFH, 128 bytes):**
Although the full 128 bytes are addressable, only selected locations are physically implemented. Key SFRs are listed:

| Address | SFR | Address | SFR |
|---|---|---|---|
| 80H | P0 | A8H | IE |
| 81H | SP | B0H | P3 |
| 82H | DPL | B8H | IP |
| 83H | DPH | C8H | T2CON (8052) |
| 87H | PCON | D0H | PSW |
| 88H | TCON | E0H | A (ACC) |
| 89H | TMOD | F0H | B |
| 8A–8D | TL0, TL1, TH0, TH1 | | |
| 90H | P1 | | |
| 98H | SCON | | |
| 99H | SBUF | | |
| A0H | P2 | | |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2.A:** Many students *forget to mention the SFR bit-addressable region* (80H–FFH, 16 of the SFR bytes are also bit-addressable: P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B). For full marks in part (b), always state that **218 bits are bit-addressable** in total (128 in 20H–2FH + 90 in 11 selected SFRs) and give at least 2 examples (e.g., `SETB P1.0`, `CLR TR0`).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Addressing Modes & Instruction Set

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style - Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain all the addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller with suitable examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 supports **8 distinct addressing modes**, which fall into 5 primary categories for operand access plus 3 specialized modes for branches and bit operations.

**1. Immediate Addressing** — The operand is a constant embedded in the instruction itself, prefixed by `#`.

$$\text{MOV A, \#0x3F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow 0x3F = 0011\,1111_2$$

**2. Register Addressing** — The operand is one of R0–R7 in the currently active register bank.

$$\text{MOV A, R4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow R4$$
$$\text{ADD A, R6} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow A + R6$$

**3. Direct Addressing** — The 8-bit address of the operand is given directly in the instruction. Used for the lower 128 bytes of RAM and all SFRs.

$$\text{MOV 30H, A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (30H) \leftarrow A$$
$$\text{MOV P1, A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Port 1 latch} \leftarrow A$$

**4. Register Indirect Addressing** — The address of the operand is held in a pointer register (R0, R1 for 8-bit; SP for stack; DPTR for 16-bit external access). The `@` symbol denotes indirection.

$$\text{MOV A, @R0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow ((R0))$$

For external data memory:
$$\text{MOVX A, @DPTR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow (\text{external RAM at address DPTR})$$

**5. Indexed Addressing** — Used only by `MOVC` and `JMP`. The address is computed as `A + DPTR` (or `A + PC` for `MOVC`).

$$\text{MOVC A, @A+DPTR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow \text{code ROM}[A + DPTR]$$

This is the basis of table lookups (e.g., ASCII conversion, hex-to-7-segment).

**6. Bit Addressing (Boolean)** — Operates on a single bit (00H–7FH bit addresses; or SFR-bit names like `P1.0`).

$$\text{SETB P1.7} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P1.7 \leftarrow 1$$
$$\text{ANL C, 20H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C \leftarrow C \text{ AND } (20H.0)$$

**7. Relative Addressing** — Used by `SJMP` and conditional jumps; the 8-bit signed offset is added to PC.

$$\text{SJMP BACK} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 2 + \text{offset (signed, } -128 \text{ to } +127)$$

**8. Absolute Addressing** — Used by `ACALL` and `AJMP`; the 11-bit destination address is formed from the upper 5 bits of the current PC and the 8-bit immediate page-relative address, allowing a 2-KB range.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the 8051 instruction set. List its categories and give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 instruction set comprises **111 instructions** divided into **5 functional groups**:

**Category 1 — Data Transfer Instructions** (Move data between registers, RAM, SFRs, and external memory.)

- `MOV A, #25H` — Load Accumulator with immediate value 25H.
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — Read from external data memory addressed by DPTR.
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — Read code (program) memory at offset A+DPTR (table lookup).
- `PUSH ACC` — Push Accumulator onto stack; SP increments, then A is stored.
- `XCH A, R3` — Exchange contents of A and R3.

**Category 2 — Arithmetic Instructions** (Add, subtract, multiply, divide, increment, decrement, BCD adjust.)

- `ADD A, R2` — Add R2 to A; result in A; CY, AC, OV flags updated.
- `SUBB A, 30H` — Subtract byte at 30H and borrow flag from A.
- `MUL AB` — Multiply A by B; 16-bit result in B (high) and A (low); OV set if result > 255.
- `DIV AB` — Divide A by B; quotient in A, remainder in B; OV set if B=0.
- `INC DPTR` — Increment 16-bit Data Pointer.
- `DA A` — Decimal-adjust A for BCD arithmetic.

**Category 3 — Logical Instructions** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, clear, complement, swap.)

- `ANL A, #0F0H` — Bitwise AND A with mask 0F0H.
- `ORL P1, A` — Bitwise OR contents of A with Port 1.
- `XRL A, @R0` — Bitwise XOR A with byte pointed to by R0.
- `CPL A` — Bitwise complement (NOT) of A.
- `RL A` — Rotate A left by 1 bit (8-bit rotate without carry).
- `RLC A` — Rotate A left through the Carry flag (9-bit rotate).
- `SWAP A` — Swap the upper and lower nibbles of A.

**Category 4 — Boolean (Bit) Instructions** (Single-bit operations on the 218 bit-addressable locations.)

- `SETB P1.0` — Set P1.0 to logic 1.
- `CLR 20H` — Clear bit at 20H.0 (bit-address 00H) to 0.
- `ANL C, /30H` — AND Carry with the *complement* of bit 30H.0.
- `MOV C, P3.3` — Copy INT1 pin state to Carry flag.
- `JC LABEL` — Jump to LABEL if Carry = 1.

**Category 5 — Program Branch Instructions** (Unconditional and conditional jumps, calls, returns.)

- `LJMP 2000H` — Long jump to absolute address 2000H (3-byte instruction).
- `SJMP HERE` — Short jump, ±127 bytes relative.
- `ACALL SUB` — Absolute call within 2-KB page.
- `LCALL 3000H` — Long call to absolute address (3-byte).
- `RET` — Return from subroutine; pops PC from stack.
- `RETI` — Return from interrupt; pops PC and re-enables interrupt logic.
- `CJNE A, #50H, LOOP` — Compare A with 50H; if not equal, jump to LOOP.
- `DJNZ R7, LOOP` — Decrement R7; if not zero, jump to LOOP (loop counter).
- `JZ NEXT` — Jump if A = 0.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5.2.B:** 
> 1. **For Part (a):** Students often miss **Bit Addressing** as a separate addressing mode. The 8051 has it as a distinct mode (different from direct addressing) because it produces a 1-bit operand.
> 2. **For Part (b):** When listing examples, **do not mix up `MOVX` (external data) and `MOVC` (code memory)** — they are very different instructions targeting different memory spaces. The 8051's Harvard architecture is what makes this distinction necessary.
> 3. **Common mark-loss:** Forgetting that `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` are the only instructions that use the **B register** as a primary operand, and that they take 4 machine cycles (not 1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **Microprocessor** = CPU only (ALU + Registers + CU); needs external memory & I/O.
- ✅ **Microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on a single chip.
- ✅ **First µP:** Intel 4004 (1971, 4-bit). **First commercial 8-bit µC:** Intel 8051 (1980).
- ✅ **8051 Family** = MCS-51; common members 8031, 8051, 8052, 8751, AT89C51, AT89S52.
- ✅ **8051 features:** 8-bit CPU, 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4 I/O ports, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts, 111 instructions.
- ✅ **Harvard architecture** — separate code and data buses (key to 8051's efficiency).
- ✅ **Reset vector:** 0000H. **SP after reset:** 07H (always reinitialize for safety).
- ✅ **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** With 11.0592 MHz crystal, $T_{cy} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$.
- ✅ **4 register banks** (R0–R7 each), selected by **PSW.3 (RS0)** and **PSW.4 (RS1)**.
- ✅ **Bit-addressable area:** 16 bytes (20H–2FH) = 128 bits; 11 selected SFRs add 90 more bits → **218 total bit-addressable bits**.
- ✅ **PSW bits:** CY (PSW.7), AC (PSW.6), F0 (PSW.5), RS1 (PSW.4), RS0 (PSW.3), OV (PSW.2), P (PSW.0).
- ✅ **Parity flag (P)** is automatically set if Accumulator has an **even** number of 1s.
- ✅ **5 (or 6) interrupts:** INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial (+T2 on 8052); 2 priority levels.
- ✅ **Addressing modes:** Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed, Bit, Relative, Absolute.
- ✅ **Instruction categories:** Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean (Bit), Program Branch.
- ✅ **`MOVX`** = external data RAM access; **`MOVC`** = code (program) memory access.
- ✅ **`MUL AB`** → 16-bit result in B:A (high:low); **`DIV AB`** → quotient in A, remainder in B.
- ✅ **`PUSH` increments SP first**, then stores; **`POP` reads first**, then decrements SP.
- ✅ **Crystal frequency choice:** 11.0592 MHz is standard because it divides cleanly into all common UART baud rates (9600, 19200, 115200, etc.).
- ✅ **For 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0:** TH1 = 0xFD = 253.
- ✅ **Accumulator (A)** is the primary operand in nearly all 8051 ALU operations; the result is **always** returned to A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal RAM and ROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal RAM and ROM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8051 Microcontroller** is an 8-bit Harvard-architecture processor that integrates two distinct on-chip (internal) memory spaces: a **128-byte Internal RAM** (also called *Lower 128* or *Data Memory*) and a **4 KB Internal ROM** (also called *Program Memory* or *Code Memory*). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Internal RAM is the volatile working memory used by the CPU to store temporary variables, the stack, and the register file during program execution. The Internal ROM is the non-volatile mask-programmed memory that permanently stores the executable machine code of the user program, reset vectors, and interrupt service routine (ISR) vectors.

| Memory | Type | Volatility | Size | Address Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Internal RAM | Static RAM (SRAM) | **Volatile** | **128 Bytes** | `00H – 7FH` |
| Internal ROM | Mask ROM | **Non-Volatile** | **4 KB (4096 Bytes)** | `0000H – 0FFFH` |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **8051 microcontroller as a carpenter's workbench**:
- The **Internal ROM** is the *instruction manual* bound and glued to the wall above the bench. The carpenter cannot tear pages out or scribble notes on it; the rules are permanently fixed. This is where the *fetched program* lives.
- The **Internal RAM** is the *tabletop surface* itself. The carpenter actively places tools (variables), pinpoints the current job (Program Counter), marks temporary measurements (bit flags), and piles up unfinished tasks (the stack). The moment the power is switched off, the table is wiped clean.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Harvard architecture means the program (ROM) and data (RAM) memories have **separate address buses, separate data buses, and separate control signals** (`PSEN` for ROM, `RD/WR` for RAM). They can therefore be accessed *simultaneously* in a single machine cycle — this is why the 8051 is fast for an 8-bit device.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

- **Program Counter (PC)** default reset value: `0000H` (points to the very first byte of Internal ROM).
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** default reset value: `07H` (points to the top of Register Bank 1).
- **Operating Frequency Range:** **1 MHz to 12 MHz** crystal oscillator is typical (standard metric for the original MCS-51 family).
- **Instruction Cycle:** **12 oscillator periods** = 1 machine cycle (at 12 MHz, 1 µs per machine cycle).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal RAM Organization (128 Bytes → `00H`–`7FH`)

The 128-byte RAM is **NOT** a flat linear buffer. It is divided into three distinct functional zones. Understanding this partition is a **guaranteed 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

#### Zone A: Register Banks (Addresses `00H`–`1FH`, 32 Bytes)
- Contains **4 register banks**, each of **8 bytes** (registers `R0`–`R7`).
- Only **one bank is "active"** at any instant, selected by the **RS0** and **RS1** bits of the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.

$$
\text{Active Bank} = \begin{cases} \text{Bank 0} & RS1RS0 = 00 \\ \text{Bank 1} & RS1RS0 = 01 \\ \text{Bank 2} & RS1RS0 = 10 \\ \text{Bank 3} & RS1RS0 = 11 \end{cases}
$$

- **Bank 0** starts at `00H` → `R0` at `00H`, `R1` at `01H`, ..., `R7` at `07H`.
- **Bank 1** starts at `08H` → `R0` at `08H`, ..., `R7` at `0FH`.
- **Bank 2** starts at `10H` → `R0` at `10H`, ..., `R7` at `17H`.
- **Bank 3** starts at `18H` → `R0` at `18H`, ..., `R7` at `1FH`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After a hardware reset, **Bank 0 is active by default** and the Stack Pointer is initialized to `07H` (which is the byte **immediately AFTER** Bank 0's last register). This means the very first `PUSH` instruction will write to `08H` (which is `R0` of Bank 1). This is a classic KTU exam trap.

#### Zone B: Bit-Addressable Area (Addresses `20H`–`2FH`, 16 Bytes)
- This is the most unique feature of the 8051's internal RAM.
- **16 bytes** × **8 bits/byte** = **128 individually addressable bits**, addressed from `00H` to `7FH` of the *bit address space*.
- Bit address `00H` corresponds to bit 0 of byte `20H`; bit `07H` corresponds to bit 7 of byte `20H`, and so on.
- Instructions like `SETB 20H.0` or `CLR 2FH.7` directly manipulate these bits — a feature used heavily in **single-bit control applications** like relay driving and switch debouncing.

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining 16 bytes of the bit-addressable area (i.e., registers with byte addresses `28H`, `29H`, `2AH`, ..., `2FH`) are part of the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** mapping zone conceptually, but in the *internal 128-byte RAM*, they remain bit-addressable general-purpose bytes.

#### Zone C: General-Purpose RAM (Addresses `30H`–`7FH`, 80 Bytes)
- Plain byte-addressable scratchpad memory.
- Used for: variables, arrays, the user stack (if it grows beyond the default location), and lookup tables.

### 2.2 Internal ROM Organization (4 KB → `0000H`–`0FFFH`)

The 4 KB on-chip ROM holds the executable code. The address space is partitioned as follows:

| Address Range | Size | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `0000H` – `0002H` | 3 bytes | **Reset Vector** (execution begins here) |
| `0003H` – `000AH` | 8 bytes | **External Interrupt 0** (INT0) ISR vector |
| `000BH` – `0012H` | 8 bytes | **Timer 0 Overflow** ISR vector |
| `0013H` – `001AH` | 8 bytes | **External Interrupt 1** (INT1) ISR vector |
| `001BH` – `0022H` | 8 bytes | **Timer 1 Overflow** ISR vector |
| `0023H` – `002AH` | 8 bytes | **Serial Port** ISR vector |
| `002BH` – `0FFFH` | 4053 bytes | **User Program Code Space** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each interrupt vector is allocated exactly **8 bytes** of code space. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, a **LJMP** (3-byte long jump) instruction is typically placed at the vector address to redirect execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in the program memory. This is a frequently asked KTU question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Internal RAM size | $N_{RAM} = 2^7 = 128$ | Bytes | $n=7$ address lines |
| Total Internal ROM size | $N_{ROM} = 4 \times 2^{10} = 4096$ | Bytes | 4 KB |
| Total Bits (Bit-Addr Zone) | $N_{bits} = 16 \times 8 = 128$ | Bits | Addresses `00H`–`7FH` (bit space) |
| Number of Register Banks | $N_{banks} = 4$ | Banks | Selected via PSW.3 and PSW.4 |
| Registers per Bank | $N_{regs} = 8$ | Registers | `R0`–`R7` |
| Default Stack Pointer (Reset) | $SP_{reset} = 07H$ | — | Points to next free byte |
| Machine Cycle Time | $T_{cy} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | $T_{cy} = 1\ \mu s$ at 12 MHz |
| Effective Addressable RAM (Bank 0) | $\text{Range} = 00H + 8i$ | where $i \in \vert 0,1,2,3 \vert$ | Maps to `R0`–`R7` |
| Bit-Address Byte Range | $20H + j$ | where $j \in \vert 0,1,\dots,15 \vert$ | Covers `20H`–`2FH` |
| Vector Space per Interrupt | $S_{vector} = 8$ | Bytes | Use `LJMP` if ISR is larger |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 4 KB ROM is sufficient for medium-complexity embedded applications like washing machine controllers, microwave oven timers, and simple sensor-based data loggers. For larger code, external ROM is interfaced using the **Port 0 / Port 2 address-data multiplexing** technique.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit Address → Byte Address Mapping

The bit-addressable zone allows any of the 128 bits to be uniquely addressed. Given a bit address `b` (in the range `00H`–`7FH`), we need to find:
1. The **byte address** that contains this bit.
2. The **bit position** (0 to 7) within that byte.

**Given:** Bit Address $b = 25H$ (i.e., decimal 37).

**Step 1 — Decompose the bit address into "byte offset" and "bit within byte":**
Since each byte holds 8 bits, we perform integer division by 8.

$$
q = \left\lfloor \dfrac{b}{8} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
b_{pos} = b \bmod 8
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formulas:**

$$
q = \left\lfloor \dfrac{37}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 4.625 \right\rfloor = 4
$$

$$
b_{pos} = 37 \bmod 8 = 37 - (4 \times 8) = 37 - 32 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Convert byte offset to physical byte address:**
The bit-addressable zone starts at byte address `20H`. So:

$$
A_{byte} = 20H + q = 20H + 04H = 24H
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the bit position:**

$$
\text{Address} = 24H.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Byte } 24H, \text{ bit } 5
$$

**Final Result:** Bit address `25H` maps to the 5th bit of internal RAM byte `24H`.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Address to Bit-Addressable Byte Mapping Table

| Bit Addr | Byte Addr | Bit | Bit Addr | Byte Addr | Bit | Bit Addr | Byte Addr | Bit | Bit Addr | Byte Addr | Bit |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `00H` | `20H` | 0 | `20H` | `22H` | 0 | `40H` | `24H` | 0 | `60H` | `26H` | 0 |
| `07H` | `20H` | 7 | `27H` | `22H` | 7 | `47H` | `24H` | 7 | `67H` | `26H` | 7 |
| `08H` | `21H` | 0 | `28H` | `23H` | 0 | `48H` | `25H` | 0 | `68H` | `27H` | 0 |
| `0FH` | `21H` | 7 | `2FH` | `23H` | 7 | `4FH` | `25H` | 7 | `6FH` | `27H` | 7 |
| `10H` | (not in bit zone) | | | | | `50H` | `28H` | 0 | `70H` | `2AH` | 0 |
| `17H` | | | | | | `57H` | `28H` | 7 | `77H` | `2AH` | 7 |
| `18H` | | | | | | `58H` | `29H` | 0 | `78H` | `2BH` | 0 |
| `1FH` | | | | | | `5FH` | `29H` | 7 | `7FH` | `2BH` | 7 |

> [!NOTE]
> Bytes `28H`–`2FH` are technically part of the SFR space (mapped at `A8H`–`B7H` in SFR addressing). However, when accessed via internal RAM addressing (using direct address `28H`–`2FH`), they appear as bit-addressable general-purpose RAM. This dual-mapping is a subtle 8051 design feature.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Memory Zone Summary

| Zone | Address Range | Size (Bytes) | Addressing Modes | Special Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Register Bank 0 | `00H`–`07H` | 8 | Register, Direct | Default active bank |
| Register Bank 1 | `08H`–`0FH` | 8 | Register, Direct | Overlaps with stack! |
| Register Bank 2 | `10H`–`17H` | 8 | Register, Direct | — |
| Register Bank 3 | `18H`–`1FH` | 8 | Register, Direct | — |
| Bit-Addressable | `20H`–`2FH` | 16 | Direct, Bit | 128 bit addresses |
| General Purpose | `30H`–`7FH` | 80 | Direct, Indirect | Typical user stack space |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: How PSW Bits Select the Active Register Bank

**Problem:** Given `PSW = 0001 1100B` (i.e., `1CH`), identify the currently active register bank and the byte address of register `R5`.

**Step 1 — Identify RS0 and RS1 in PSW:**
PSW bit layout (relevant bits only):

$$
\text{PSW} = \underbrace{CY}_{PSW.7}\ \underbrace{AC}_{PSW.6}\ \underbrace{F0}_{PSW.5}\ \underbrace{RS1}_{PSW.4}\ \underbrace{RS0}_{PSW.3}\ \underbrace{OV}_{PSW.2}\ \underbrace{-}_{PSW.1}\ \underbrace{P}_{PSW.0}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract RS1 and RS0:**
From `PSW = 0001 1100B`:
- Bit 4 (RS1) = `1`
- Bit 3 (RS0) = `1`

**Step 3 — Determine active bank:**
RS1 RS0 = `11` → **Register Bank 3 is active.**

**Step 4 — Compute the address of R5 in Bank 3:**

$$
A_{R5} = \text{Bank}_{base} + 5
$$

$$
A_{R5} = 18H + 05H = 1DH
$$

**Final Result:** When `PSW = 1CH`, register `R5` is physically located at internal RAM byte `1DH`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal RAM Memory Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Internal RAM 128 Bytes - Address 00H to 7FH]) --> ZoneA
    Start --> ZoneB
    Start --> ZoneC

    ZoneA["Zone A: Register Banks\n00H to 1FH - 32 Bytes"]
    ZoneB["Zone B: Bit Addressable\n20H to 2FH - 16 Bytes - 128 Bits"]
    ZoneC["Zone C: General Purpose\n30H to 7FH - 80 Bytes"]

    ZoneA --> Bank0["Bank 0\n00H to 07H\nR0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"]
    ZoneA --> Bank1["Bank 1\n08H to 0FH\nR0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"]
    ZoneA --> Bank2["Bank 2\n10H to 17H\nR0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"]
    ZoneA --> Bank3["Bank 3\n18H to 1FH\nR0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"]

    ZoneB --> BitLow["Bit Addr 00H to 3FH\nCovers byte 20H to 27H"]
    ZoneB --> BitHigh["Bit Addr 40H to 7FH\nCovers byte 28H to 2FH"]

    ZoneC --> UserSpace["User Variables\nStack Growth\nArrays\nBuffers"]

    Bank0 -.Active after reset.-> Active0((Selected by PSW))
    Bank1 -.Selectable.-> Active0
    Bank2 -.Selectable.-> Active0
    Bank3 -.Selectable.-> Active0
```

### 4.2 Internal ROM Memory Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Reset([Power ON - PC jumps to 0000H]) --> Vec0000["0000H\nReset Vector\n3 Bytes"]
    Vec0000 --> Vec0003["0003H\nINT0 ISR\n8 Bytes"]
    Vec0003 --> Vec000B["000BH\nTimer 0 ISR\n8 Bytes"]
    Vec000B --> Vec0013["0013H\nINT1 ISR\n8 Bytes"]
    Vec0013 --> Vec001B["001BH\nTimer 1 ISR\n8 Bytes"]
    Vec001B --> Vec0023["0023H\nSerial Port ISR\n8 Bytes"]
    Vec0023 --> UserCode["002BH to 0FFFH\nUser Program Space\n4053 Bytes"]

    UserCode --> Main["Main Program"]
    UserCode --> SubA["Subroutines"]
    UserCode --> SubB["Lookup Tables"]
    UserCode --> SubC["Interrupt Service Routines via LJMP"]

    style Reset fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style Vec0000 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style Vec0003 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style Vec000B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style Vec0013 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style Vec001B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style Vec0023 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
    style UserCode fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#008844
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: How CPU Accesses Internal RAM and ROM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU - Arithmetic Logic Unit\nand Control Unit"]
    PC["Program Counter PC\n16-bit"]
    SP["Stack Pointer SP\n8-bit"]
    PSW["PSW - RS1 RS0 bits\nBank Selection"]

    CPU --> PC
    CPU --> SP
    CPU --> PSW

    PC -->|Address 0000H to 0FFFH| ROM["Internal ROM\n4KB Code Memory\nPSEN signal"]
    SP -->|Address 00H to 7FH| RAM["Internal RAM\n128 Bytes Data Memory\nRD and WR signals"]
    PSW -->|Bank Selection| Banks["R0 to R7 of Selected Bank\n4 Banks in 00H to 1FH"]

    CPU -->|Direct, Indirect\nRegister, Immediate| RAM
    CPU -->|Bit level ops| Bits["Bit Addressable 20H to 2FH\n128 bits"]

    style CPU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style ROM fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc5500
    style RAM fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#008844
    style Banks fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#663399
    style Bits fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The PSEN (Program Store Enable) signal is used **only** for ROM fetches, while the RD (read) and WR (write) signals are used **only** for RAM. This physical separation is the essence of Harvard architecture and the reason the 8051 can fetch an instruction from ROM in the same cycle it reads/writes data from RAM.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Internal RAM and Internal ROM of the 8051 microcontroller in terms of volatility, size, address range, and the control signals used to access them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Internal RAM | Internal ROM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Volatility | **Volatile** (data lost on power off) | **Non-Volatile** (data retained permanently) |
| Size | 128 Bytes | 4 KB (4096 Bytes) |
| Address Range | `00H` – `7FH` | `0000H` – `0FFFH` |
| Control Signal | `RD` (read) and `WR` (write) | `PSEN` (Program Store Enable) |
| Function | Temporary data, variables, stack | Permanent program code storage |
| Type | Static RAM (SRAM) | Mask ROM |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List the three functional zones of the 8051 internal RAM with their address ranges. Why is the zone `20H`–`2FH` called "bit-addressable"?**

**Model Answer:**
The three functional zones are:
1. **Register Banks:** `00H` – `1FH` (32 bytes, 4 banks of 8 registers each)
2. **Bit-Addressable Area:** `20H` – `2FH` (16 bytes)
3. **General-Purpose RAM:** `30H` – `7FH` (80 bytes)

The zone `20H`–`2FH` is called bit-addressable because each of the **128 individual bits** in this 16-byte region can be independently accessed and manipulated using bit-level instructions such as `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL`, `ORL`, and conditional jump instructions like `JB` and `JNB`. This allows single-bit operations without disturbing other bits in the same byte.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Question Paper Note:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Module question in Part B carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice (OR option). You may attempt EITHER Choice A OR Choice B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Draw the internal RAM memory map of the 8051 microcontroller. Explain the role of the PSW register bits RS0 and RS1 in selecting the active register bank. If `PSW = 18H`, identify the active bank and the byte address of `R3`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the organization of the 4 KB Internal ROM of the 8051. Explain why `LJMP` instructions are typically placed at the interrupt vector addresses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Memory Map:** Already covered in Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram). Key zones to mention:
- `00H`–`1FH`: Register Banks 0–3
- `20H`–`2FH`: Bit-addressable area
- `30H`–`7FH`: General-purpose RAM

**PSW Role:** PSW bits **RS0 (PSW.3)** and **RS1 (PSW.4)** form a 2-bit selector code that determines which of the four register banks is currently mapped to the CPU's register file `R0`–`R7`.

**Calculation for `PSW = 18H = 0001 1000B`:**
- Bit 4 (RS1) = `1`
- Bit 3 (RS0) = `0`
- RS1 RS0 = `10` → **Register Bank 2 is active**

**Address of R3 in Bank 2:**

$$
A_{R3} = \text{Bank}_2\text{ base} + 3 = 10H + 03H = 13H
$$

**Final Answer:** Active bank is **Bank 2**; `R3` is at byte address `13H`.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**ROM Organization:** The 4 KB Internal ROM spans `0000H` to `0FFFH`. The first 43 bytes (approximately) are reserved for **interrupt vectors**:

| Vector | Address | Source |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Reset | `0000H` | Power-on reset |
| External Int 0 | `0003H` | INT0 pin |
| Timer 0 Overflow | `000BH` | T0 flag |
| External Int 1 | `0013H` | INT1 pin |
| Timer 1 Overflow | `001BH` | T1 flag |
| Serial Port | `0023H` | TI/RI flag |

**Why `LJMP` at Vector Addresses:**
Each vector slot is only **8 bytes** long. The actual interrupt service routine (ISR) is almost always longer than 8 bytes. Therefore, programmers place a **3-byte `LJMP` instruction** at the vector address, which redirects the CPU to execute the actual ISR code stored elsewhere in the program memory (`002BH`–`0FFFH` user area). This technique keeps the vector region clean and avoids overlapping ISRs.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the bit-addressable area of the 8051 internal RAM. A bit address is given as `3AH`. Determine the byte address and the bit position (0 to 7) it corresponds to. Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8051. What is its reset value, and what memory location does the first `PUSH` instruction write to? Why is the initial value of `07H` considered "risky" in some applications? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Bit-Addressable Area Explanation:** (As in Q1 above) — 16 bytes from `20H`–`2FH`, providing 128 individually accessible bits (addresses `00H`–`7FH` in the bit address space).

**Calculation for bit address `3AH` (decimal 58):**

**Step 1 — Convert hex to decimal:**

$$
3AH = (3 \times 16) + 10 = 48 + 10 = 58
$$

**Step 2 — Compute byte offset within bit zone:**

$$
q = \left\lfloor \dfrac{58}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 7.25 \right\rfloor = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Compute bit position:**

$$
b_{pos} = 58 \bmod 8 = 58 - (7 \times 8) = 58 - 56 = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert offset to physical byte address:**

$$
A_{byte} = 20H + 07H = 27H
$$

**Verification:** Byte `27H` is the 8th byte of the bit zone (offsets 0 to 7). Within that byte, bit position 2 is the 3rd bit. The bit addresses within byte `27H` are `38H` to `3FH`. Therefore, bit `3AH` = `38H` + 2 = `3AH` ✓

**Final Answer:** Bit address `3AH` corresponds to **byte `27H`, bit position 2**.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Role of the Stack Pointer (SP):** The SP is an 8-bit special function register (SFR address `81H`) that holds the address of the **top of the stack** in internal RAM. It is automatically incremented before a `PUSH` and automatically decremented after a `POP`. It is used to:
- Save the return address during `LCALL`/`ACALL`.
- Save the PC during interrupt servicing.
- Temporarily hold CPU state (e.g., `PUSH PSW`).
- Pass parameters in nested subroutines.

**Reset Value:** `SP = 07H`

**First PUSH writes to:**
Before the data is stored, SP is **incremented** by 1. So:

$$
SP_{new} = 07H + 01H = 08H
$$

Data is written to address `08H`, which is **R0 of Register Bank 1**.

**Why is `07H` Risky?**
- If the program uses **Register Bank 1** (selected via PSW bits RS1/RS0), the very first `PUSH` will overwrite `R0` of Bank 1, leading to **data corruption** and unpredictable program behavior.
- The safe practice is to **initialize the SP** at the start of every program using a directive such as:

$$
\text{MOV SP, } \;\#30H
$$

or any safe address in the general-purpose RAM zone (`30H`–`7FH`).

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Stack Pointer Trap:** Many students forget that `PUSH` *increments* SP first. Writing that the first `PUSH` "stores data at `07H`" is **wrong** — it stores at `08H`. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Bit Address vs Byte Address Confusion:** Bit address `3AH` is **NOT** the same as byte address `3AH`. Forgetting to add the base offset `20H` results in byte address `07H` instead of `27H`. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Active Bank Calculation:** Reading PSW bits from the wrong side (MSB vs LSB) is a common error. Always remember **RS1 is bit 4** and **RS0 is bit 3** of PSW.
> 4. **Vector Space Size:** Each interrupt gets **8 bytes**, not 16 or 4. If the ISR is larger, a `LJMP` is mandatory. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Harvard Architecture:** Do not state that ROM and RAM share a common bus. They have **separate buses and separate control signals** (`PSEN` for ROM, `RD/WR` for RAM). (-1 mark)

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Internal RAM and ROM of 8051**

- ☐ The 8051 has **128 bytes Internal RAM** (`00H`–`7FH`) and **4 KB Internal ROM** (`0000H`–`0FFFH`).
- ☐ Internal RAM is **volatile** (SRAM); Internal ROM is **non-volatile** (Mask ROM).
- ☐ RAM has **three zones:** Register Banks (`00H`–`1FH`), Bit-Addressable (`20H`–`2FH`), and General Purpose (`30H`–`7FH`).
- ☐ **4 register banks** × **8 registers** (`R0`–`R7`) each = 32 bytes.
- ☐ Active bank is selected by **PSW bits RS1 (bit 4) and RS0 (bit 3)**.
- ☐ Default active bank after reset = **Bank 0**.
- ☐ **Bit-addressable zone** holds 16 bytes = **128 individually addressable bits** (`00H`–`7FH` in bit space).
- ☐ Bit-to-byte mapping: $A_{byte} = 20H + \lfloor b/8 \rfloor$, $b_{pos} = b \bmod 8$.
- ☐ **Stack Pointer (SP)** default value after reset = **`07H`**. First `PUSH` writes to `08H` (Bank 1, R0).
- ☐ Always reinitialize SP to `30H` or higher at the start of your program for safety.
- ☐ Internal ROM starts at address `0000H` — the **Reset Vector**.
- ☐ Each of the **5 interrupt vectors** is allocated **8 bytes** of program memory.
- ☐ ISRs longer than 8 bytes use a **3-byte `LJMP`** to redirect to actual code in the user area.
- ☐ The Harvard architecture uses **separate buses and control signals**: `PSEN` for ROM, `RD`/`WR` for RAM.
- ☐ Machine cycle time: $T_{cy} = 12 / f_{osc}$ → at 12 MHz crystal, $T_{cy} = 1\ \mu s$.
- ☐ Bit addresses `00H`–`7FH` are **bit-space addresses**, **not** the same as the byte-space addresses `00H`–`7FH` of internal RAM — they are two **different address spaces** mapped to the same physical region.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillator and Reset Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oscillator and Reset Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Oscillator Circuit in a Microcontroller?

An **oscillator circuit** in a microcontroller is the on-board (or externally attached) timing sub-system that generates a stable, periodic **clock signal**. This clock waveform acts as the *heartbeat* of the CPU, dictating the rate at which the instruction pointer advances, registers latch data, peripherals toggle, and timers count. In the 8051 architecture, the oscillator block is realized using an external quartz crystal connected across the **XTAL1 (Pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 18)** pins, which form the two terminals of an internal **Pierce-style CMOS inverter amplifier** built into the silicon die.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Terminology):**
> *The oscillator circuit of a microcontroller is a feedback network comprising an inverting amplifier and a frequency-selective resonant element (typically a quartz crystal), which produces a stable square-wave clock of fixed frequency f<sub>OSC</sub> that synchronises all internal and external operations of the CPU.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heartbeat of a Drummer"

Imagine a military parade. The marching soldiers (the CPU instructions) cannot move in a coordinated way without a steady drumbeat. The **drummer is the oscillator** — he sets the tempo. A faster drummer = faster march (higher clock frequency = faster instruction execution). A sloppy or irregular drummer = the parade collapses (an unstable clock = erratic CPU behaviour, glitches, or complete lock-up).

Similarly, the microcontroller's **CPU**, **timers**, **serial port**, and **peripherals** all march strictly to the beat of the clock. If the oscillator drifts, every timed action in the system drifts with it.

### 1.3 What is a Reset Circuit?

A **reset circuit** is the auxiliary hardware that forces the microcontroller into a **deterministic, well-defined initial state** at power-up, during brown-outs, or on user demand. The 8051's reset pin (**RST, Pin 9**) is **active-HIGH** — meaning the CPU remains in the reset state as long as RST = 1 for a minimum of **two machine cycles (24 oscillator periods)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Reset is Non-Negotiable in Embedded Design:**
> - At V<sub>CC</sub> = 0, internal flip-flops are in random states.
> - Without a controlled reset, the program counter (PC) may start executing from an arbitrary address, often leading to **latch-up, bus contention, or code runaway**.
> - A proper reset guarantees: PC ← 0000H, SP ← 07H, all ports ← FFH, all SFRs ← 00H, and execution begins from address 0000H.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The CTRL+ALT+DEL of Microcontrollers"

Every laptop user knows that when the OS hangs, we hold the power button or press a hard-reset combination. The reset circuit is the **hardware equivalent**: it does not "save your work"; it brutally, predictably dumps everything and reloads the world to a known initial state. Without it, your microcontroller is the equivalent of a computer that boots into chaos.

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

The following table highlights the canonical numbers used in 8051 oscillator/reset design:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Crystal Frequency (f<sub>OSC</sub>)** | **11.0592 MHz** | Exact multiple of standard baud rates (9600, 19200) |
| Alternate Crystal | **12 MHz** | Common for instruction-cycle timing exercises |
| Machine Cycle | **12 / f<sub>OSC</sub>** | 8051 takes 12 oscillator pulses per machine cycle |
| RST Pin Minimum HIGH Time | **2 machine cycles = 24 T<sub>OSC</sub>** | Required to guarantee valid reset |
| RST Pin Voltage Threshold | **≈ 0.7 × V<sub>CC</sub>** | Schmitt-trigger input, high noise immunity |
| Quartz Crystal Cut | **AT-cut** | Standard for 1–25 MHz microcontrollers |
| Crystal Load Capacitance (C<sub>L</sub>) | **20 pF – 32 pF** | Determined by crystal vendor datasheet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The 8051's serial port baud-rate generator is divided from the oscillator. The value **11.0592 MHz** is chosen because 11.0592 MHz ÷ 12 = 921.6 kHz machine cycle, and 921.6 kHz ÷ 16 = 57,600 Hz, and 57,600 ÷ (16, 32, 64) yields **exact standard baud rates with 0% error**. KTU problems frequently exploit this.
