**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to **monitor the status of switch SW connected to P1.7**. If the switch is pressed (P1.7 = 0), turn ON an LED connected to P2.0; otherwise, turn it OFF. Use **bit processing instructions** only.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Direct Addressing** and **Register Indirect Addressing** modes with two real-time use cases each. Differentiate between `MOV A, 30H` and `MOV A, @R0` when `R0 = 30H`.

### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

```assembly
        ORG 0000H          ; Program starts at address 0000H
START:  SETB P1.7          ; Configure P1.7 as input (write 1)
        CLR P2.0           ; Initially turn OFF the LED
MAIN:   JB P1.7, OFF       ; If P1.7 = 1 (switch not pressed), jump to OFF
        SETB P2.0          ; Switch is pressed (P1.7=0) -> LED ON
        SJMP MAIN          ; Loop back to check switch status
OFF:    CLR P2.0           ; Switch is not pressed -> LED OFF
        SJMP MAIN          ; Loop back
        END                ; End of program
```

**Explanation:**

- `SETB P1.7` makes P1.7 an input (writing 1 to a port pin configures it as input in 8051).
- `JB P1.7, OFF` checks if P1.7 is HIGH (switch open). If true, branch to OFF.
- If P1.7 is LOW (switch pressed), the program falls through to `SETB P2.0`, lighting the LED.
- `SJMP MAIN` creates an infinite polling loop.

### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Direct Addressing Mode:**
- The 8-bit address of the operand is given *explicitly* in the instruction.
- **Example:** `MOV A, 30H` → Loads A with the byte at internal RAM location 30H.
- **Use Case 1:** Accessing any SFR by name, e.g., `MOV SCON, #50H`.
- **Use Case 2:** Reading a fixed memory variable, e.g., `MOV A, TEMP` where TEMP = 40H.

**Register Indirect Addressing Mode:**
- A register (`R0` or `R1`) holds the *address* of the data. The `@` indicates indirection.
- **Example:** `MOV A, @R0` → If R0 = 30H, then A ← RAM[30H].
- **Use Case 1:** Accessing arrays/loops where the pointer auto-increments, e.g., `INC R0`.
- **Use Case 2:** Stack operations (`PUSH`/`POP`) use the Stack Pointer (SP) in an indirect manner.

**Comparison when R0 = 30H:**

| Instruction | Meaning | Data Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `MOV A, 30H` | A ← RAM[30H] | Direct: address is 30H (constant) |
| `MOV A, @R0` | A ← RAM[(R0)] = RAM[30H] | Indirect: R0 holds the address 30H |

**Key Difference:** In Direct Addressing, the address `30H` is hardcoded in the instruction. In Register Indirect Addressing, the value `30H` is in R0, which *can be changed at runtime*, allowing dynamic memory access.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `CLR` with `CPL`:** Writing `CLR` instead of `CPL` (or vice versa) when toggling a bit. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the `#` in Immediate Mode:** Writing `MOV A, 25H` instead of `MOV A, #25H` changes the meaning completely (Direct vs Immediate). This is the **#1 reason** students lose marks in addressing mode questions.
> 3. **Misinterpreting `/bit`:** The `/` is the **complement operator**, not a "division by bit". `ORL C, /P1.0` means `C ← C OR (NOT P1.0)`.
> 4. **Mixing up `MOVC` and `MOVX`:** `MOVC` = code memory (indexed), `MOVX` = external data memory (indirect via DPTR).
> 5. **Wrong register bank assumption:** In Register Addressing, students often forget to mention the **register bank** (selected by PSW bits RS0, RS1).
> 6. **Indexed Addressing in wrong memory:** `MOVC` accesses **code memory**, NOT internal RAM. Marking it as RAM access will be penalized.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Bit Processing Instructions** manipulate single bits in the bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH) and 11 bit-addressable SFRs.
- 🔹 The **Carry flag (CY)** is the 1-bit accumulator for all bit operations.
- 🔹 **`SETB`** = force to 1; **`CLR`** = force to 0; **`CPL`** = toggle (invert).
- 🔹 **`/bit`** in `ANL`/`ORL` means the **complement** of the bit, not a register.
- 🔹 **Five Addressing Modes:** Immediate (`#data`), Register (`Rn`), Direct (`addr`), Register Indirect (`@Ri`), and Indexed (`@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC`).
- 🔹 **Immediate** is identified by the `#` prefix — never forget it!
- 🔹 **Register Indirect** uses only `R0` and `R1` as pointer registers in internal RAM.
- 🔹 **Indexed** addressing (`MOVC`) is exclusively used for **code memory** (lookup tables).
- 🔹 **Direct Addressing** can access the full 00H–FFH range (internal RAM + SFRs).
- 🔹 **Register Addressing** is the **fastest** mode (1-byte instruction); **Indexed** is the **slowest** (16-bit address computation).
- 🔹 Register banks are at 00H–07H (Bank 0), 08H–0FH (Bank 1), 10H–17H (Bank 2), 18H–1FH (Bank 3).
- 🔹 There is **no** `MOV C, C` instruction; Carry is the central 1-bit register.
- 🔹 `JBC` both tests AND clears the bit in a single instruction (useful for edge-triggered events).
- 🔹 `MOVX` (with `@DPTR`) accesses **external** data memory — distinct from `MOV` and `MOVC`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontroller Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microcontroller Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Microcontroller Languages** are the hierarchical set of symbolic and textual notations used to instruct a microcontroller's Central Processing Unit (CPU). They form a **three-tier abstraction hierarchy** — **Machine Language (Level 1)**, **Assembly Language (Level 2)**, and **High-Level Languages such as Embedded C (Level 3)** — each differing in human readability, hardware abstraction, code density, and execution efficiency.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBICT504 – Module 2)**, a student must be able to:
- Classify microcontroller programming languages and recognize the **trade-off between execution speed, code size, and development time**.
- Distinguish between **native compilation** and **cross-compilation** workflows used in 8051 / ARM / MSP430 development.
- Select an appropriate language for a given **embedded system design constraint** (real-time, low-power, memory-constrained).

## 1.2 The Three Tiers — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — Talking to a Foreigner (the Microcontroller)**
> Imagine the CPU is a person who only understands **Morse code (dots and dashes)**.
> - **Machine Language** → You directly type dots and dashes. The CPU understands instantly, but you (the human) suffer.
> - **Assembly Language** → You use short, memorable English shortcuts (`.`, `-`) like **"SOS"** which a *translator* (the **Assembler**) converts into Morse code.
> - **High-Level Language (Embedded C)** → You write full English sentences — *"Send a distress signal"* — and a sophisticated **interpreter/translator (the Cross-Compiler)** turns it into Morse code for you.

## 1.3 Tier 1 — Machine Language (First-Generation Language)

- The **native binary instruction set** of the target microcontroller.
- Consists of **0s and 1s** (e.g., `0111 0100` = `MOV A, #74H` for 8051).
- **No translation** required — the CPU's instruction decoder hardware directly interprets it.
- **Hardware-dependent**: code written for 8051 will not run on ARM Cortex-M0.
- **Pros**: Fastest possible execution, smallest code footprint.
- **Cons**: Unreadable, error-prone, tedious, non-portable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Footprint Metric:** A typical 8051 instruction occupies **1 to 3 bytes** of program memory (ROM). The 8051's classic instruction set is encoded in **256 opcodes** (the *Opcode Matrix*).

## 1.4 Tier 2 — Assembly Language (Second-Generation Language)

- Uses **mnemonics** — short, human-readable abbreviations — like `MOV`, `ADD`, `SJMP`, `ACALL`.
- Requires an **Assembler** (e.g., **Keil µVision A51**, **AS31**, **AVR Studio's AVRASM2**) to translate mnemonics into machine code **line-by-line** in a single pass.
- Provides **direct, symbolic control** of CPU registers, SFRs (Special Function Registers), bit-addressable memory, and stack.
- **One-to-one mapping**: typically, one assembly instruction = one machine instruction.
- Enables **exact cycle-counting** — critical for hard real-time systems (engine control units, pacemakers).
- **Pros**: Maximum optimization, full hardware control, deterministic timing.
- **Cons**: Steep learning curve, poor portability, slow development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 8051 instruction set contains **255 unique opcodes** spanning **44 mnemonics** and **11 addressing modes**. Mastering the addressing-mode → opcode mapping is a **direct exam question** in PBICT504.

## 1.5 Tier 3 — High-Level Language — Embedded C (Third-Generation Language)

- Uses structured, human-language-like syntax (loops, functions, conditionals).
- For microcontrollers, **C is the de-facto industry standard** (used in Arduino, STM32 HAL, ESP-IDF, MPLAB XC8).
- Requires a **Cross-Compiler** (e.g., **Keil C51**, **SDCC**, **MPLAB XC8**, **IAR Embedded Workbench**) because the host PC (x86) cannot natively emit code for an 8051/ARM target.
- **One-to-many mapping**: a single C statement may expand to 5–20 assembly instructions.
- Allows **header-file abstraction** of SFRs (e.g., `P1 = 0xFF;` instead of bit-level manipulation).
- **Pros**: Portability, modularity, rapid prototyping, easier debugging.
- **Cons**: Larger code size, less predictable execution time, possible compiler-induced overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** "Embedded C is the same as ANSI C." — **FALSE.** Embedded C extensions include **bit-arrays** (`bit`, `sbit`, `sfr`), **interrupt keywords** (`interrupt`, `using`), and **memory-model specifiers** (`code`, `xdata`, `idata`, `pdata`).

## 1.6 Cross-Compilation — The Embedded Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Compiler:** A compiler that runs on a **Host machine** (e.g., a Windows PC with Intel x86 CPU) but generates machine code for a **Target machine** (e.g., an 8051 or ARM Cortex-M chip). The output is a **HEX file (Intel HEX / Motorola S-record)** that is then **flashed** onto the target's program memory.

This is fundamentally different from a **native compiler** used in desktop applications (e.g., GCC compiling C → x86 binary that runs on the same machine).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Translation Pipeline

The conversion of a programmer's intent into executed CPU cycles follows a strict, deterministic pipeline:

### Step 1 — Source Authoring
The developer writes code in **Machine / Assembly / Embedded C**. The choice depends on the **design constraint matrix** (real-time deadlines, ROM budget, time-to-market).

### Step 2 — Lexical & Syntactic Analysis
- **Assembler (ASM):** Reads mnemonics line-by-line, looks up the opcode from the **opcode table**, and resolves **labels** to physical addresses.
- **Cross-Compiler (C):** Performs **tokenization**, builds a **parse tree / AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)**, then applies **optimization passes** (dead-code elimination, loop unrolling, register allocation).

### Step 3 — Code Generation
- Assembler emits a binary object file (`.obj`) with **relocatable addresses**.
- Compiler emits assembly (or directly) with **architecture-specific calling conventions**.

### Step 4 — Linking & Locating
- The **Linker** combines multiple object files and library routines.
- The **Locator** assigns **absolute physical addresses** to symbols based on the target's memory map (e.g., 8051: code ROM starts at `0x0000`, data RAM at `0x00`).

### Step 5 — HEX File Generation
- The Intel HEX format (`.hex`) records each byte as `:[LL][AAAA][TT][DD…DD][CC]`, where:
  - `LL` = byte count, `AAAA` = 16-bit address, `TT` = record type, `CC` = checksum.
- This file is then **burned into ROM/Flash** using a programmer (e.g., FlashMagic, ProgISP).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Tier — Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Constraint → Language Mapping (KTU 2024 mapping skill):**

- **Sub-microsecond deterministic timing** (e.g., ABS braking ECU) → **Assembly** is mandatory because C cannot guarantee cycle counts.
- **IoT sensor node with 32 KB Flash, 4 KB RAM** → **Embedded C with -Os optimization** — Assembly is too costly to maintain, machine code is impractical.
- **Mass-produced toy with single-function, ultra-cheap MCU** → **Hand-optimized Assembly** to fit in 1 KB ROM.
- **Prototype / educational kit (Arduino, 8051 trainer)** → **Embedded C** for rapid iteration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, boundary constant, and conversion required for PBICT504 Module 2 numerical problems.

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Value (8051)** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | $T_{MC} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$ (for 11.0592 MHz crystal) | Computes instruction execution time |
| Instruction Execution Time | $T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times T_{MC}$ | $N_{cycles} = 1$ to $12$ for 8051 | Real-time deadline verification |
| ROM Code Size | $S_{ROM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i$ | $B_i \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3 \rbrace$ bytes | Memory budget planning |
| Code Expansion Ratio | $R_{C/Asm} = \dfrac{S_{C}}{S_{Asm}}$ | Typically $1.5$ to $2.5\times$ for C vs Assembly | Language selection impact |
| Cross-Compilation Time | $T_{build} = T_{compile} + T_{link} + T_{locate}$ | Seconds to minutes | CI/CD pipeline budgeting |
| Baud Rate (Timer 1 Mode 2) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | $9600$ bps for $f_{OSC}=11.0592$ MHz, $TH1=0xFD$ | UART language-independent metric |
| Timer Reload Value | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \times Baud}$ | — | Required when programming in any language |
| Max ROM Capacity (Classic 8051) | $2^{16} = 65\,536$ addressable bytes | 64 KB code + 64 KB xdata | Harvard architecture limit |
| Hex File Record Overhead | $\text{Overhead} = 11 \text{ bytes/record} \times N_{records}$ | — | Flash programmer time estimation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Mixed-language projects — C for application logic, hand-written Assembly for **crank-shaft angle-synchronous routines**.
- **Medical Implants (Pacemakers):** Strict **FDA/IEC 62304** validation demands cycle-accurate Assembly auditing.
- **Consumer IoT (ESP8266/ESP32):** Pure C with **RTOS (FreeRTOS)** for multi-tasking.
- **Aerospace (CubeSat):** **SPARK/Ada** subset of high-level languages for provable correctness — though C dominates CubeSat flight software today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Execution Time of an 8051 Assembly Instruction

**Problem:** A 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal oscillator. Compute the execution time of a `DJNZ R0, label` instruction, which consumes **2 machine cycles** in the 8051 instruction set.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the oscillator frequency and machine-cycle divisor.

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to produce the internal machine-cycle clock.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given value.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}{12} = 921.6 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the period (time per machine cycle).

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}}$$

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^{3}} \text{ s} = 1.0850\,69 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 4:** Apply the instruction-execution-time formula with $N_{cycles} = 2$ for `DJNZ`.

$$T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times T_{MC}$$

$$T_{inst} = 2 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 2.170\,\mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A `DJNZ` loop running continuously on this CPU performs approximately $\dfrac{1}{2.170 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{460\,800}$ iterations per second. This predictable timing is the **primary reason** assembly is used in hard real-time loops.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Code-Size & Language Expansion Comparison

**Problem:** A 8051 firmware consists of a control loop. The hand-written Assembly version occupies **2.0 KB** of ROM. The Embedded C version, compiled with Keil C51 (default optimization), occupies **4.2 KB**. Compute:
1. The expansion ratio $R_{C/Asm}$.
2. The percentage ROM overhead.
3. The extra bytes consumed and how many additional 1-KB ROM pages are required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** State the expansion ratio formula.

$$R_{C/Asm} = \frac{S_{C}}{S_{Asm}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$R_{C/Asm} = \frac{4.2 \text{ KB}}{2.0 \text{ KB}} = 2.1$$

**Step 3:** Compute the percentage overhead relative to Assembly.

$$\% \text{ Overhead} = (R_{C/Asm} - 1) \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Overhead} = (2.1 - 1) \times 100\% = 110\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute the absolute extra bytes.

$$\Delta S = S_{C} - S_{Asm}$$

$$\Delta S = 4.2 \text{ KB} - 2.0 \text{ KB} = 2.2 \text{ KB} = 2\,252.8 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5:** Determine the number of additional 1-KB pages required.

$$N_{pages} = \lceil \Delta S / 1024 \rceil = \lceil 2.2 \rceil = 3 \text{ extra pages}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Takeaway:** A 2.1× expansion is typical for unoptimized C. Using `--opt-level 9` (size optimization) in **SDCC** or `--code-size` flag in **Keil C51** can reduce this ratio to **1.3× – 1.5×** by inlining functions, reusing registers, and eliminating dead code.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Timer 1 Reload Value for Standard Baud Rate (Demonstrates Language-Agnostic Metric)

**Problem:** An 8051 system with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz must communicate over UART at **9 600 bps** in **Mode 1** with **Timer 1 in auto-reload (Mode 2)**. Compute the TH1 reload value, assuming **SMOD = 0**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the canonical 8051 baud-rate equation for UART Mode 1, Timer 1 Mode 2.

$$Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $(256 - TH1)$.

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times Baud}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $SMOD = 0$, $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$, $Baud = 9600$.

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 9600}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$12 \times 9600 = 115\,200$$

**Step 5:** Compute the fraction.

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 115\,200} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3\,686\,400}$$

$$256 - TH1 = 3$$

**Step 6:** Solve for TH1.

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Language Insight:** Whether you write `MOV TH1, #0FDH` in Assembly or `TH1 = 0xFD;` in Embedded C, the **value is identical**. This is why KTU examiners often frame this problem to test whether the student truly understands the **underlying hardware equation** and not just syntax.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Language Tier Trade-offs

| **Criterion** | **Machine Language** | **Assembly Language** | **Embedded C** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human Readability | $\text{None}$ | $\text{Moderate}$ | $\text{High}$ |
| Execution Speed | $\text{Maximum}$ | $\text{Near Maximum}$ | $\text{Good (80–95\%)}$ |
| Code Density | $\text{Highest}$ | $\text{High}$ | $\text{Moderate}$ |
| Portability across MCUs | $\text{Zero}$ | $\text{Very Low}$ | $\text{High}$ |
| Development Time | $\text{Very High}$ | $\text{High}$ | $\text{Low}$ |
| Debug-ability | $\text{None}$ | $\text{Moderate (with simulator)}$ | $\text{High (IDE + probes)}$ |
| Cycle-Accuracy | $\text{Perfect}$ | $\text{Perfect}$ | $\text{Approximate}$ |
| Learning Curve | $\text{Extreme}$ | $\text{Steep}$ | $\text{Manageable}$ |
| Industry Use Today | $\text{None (historical)}$ | $\text{Niche (bootloaders, ISR kernels)}$ | $\text{Dominant ($\geq 90\%$)}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Tier Language Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LEVEL 3 HIGH LEVEL C"] --> B["LEVEL 2 ASSEMBLY MNEMONICS"]
    B --> C["LEVEL 1 MACHINE BINARY CODE"]
    C --> D["CPU HARDWARE INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    A1["Embedded C Source File dot C"] --> A
    A2["Cross Compiler Keil C51 / SDCC"] --> B
    B1["ASM Source File dot ASM"] --> B
    B2["Assembler A51 / AS31"] --> C
    C1["Intel HEX File dot HEX"] --> C
    C2["Flash Programmer"] --> D
    D1["Physical ROM executes bytes"] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A programmer authors code at the top of the pyramid. Each step **down** the pyramid involves a translation pass. The CPU only ever sees **Level 1** (binary). The translation tools (right side) bridge the abstraction gap.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Compilation Workflow (Host → Target)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["HOST PC x86 ARCHITECTURE"] --> H2["EDIT SOURCE IN IDE KEIL / MPLAB"]
    H2 --> H3["CROSS COMPILER TARGET 8051"]
    H3 --> H4["OBJECT FILE DOT OBJ"]
    H4 --> H5["LINKER + LOCATOR"]
    H5 --> H6["INTEL HEX FILE DOT HEX"]
    H6 --> H7["FLASH PROGRAMMER HARDWARE"]
    H7 --> T1["TARGET BOARD 8051 / ARM / MSP430"]
    T1 --> T2["EXECUTION FROM ROM"]
    H8["DEBUGGER + SIMULATOR"] -.feedback.-> H2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Cross"?** The host and target have **different instruction sets**. The compiler itself runs on x86 but emits 8051 opcodes. This is fundamentally different from a **native compiler** (e.g., GCC compiling C → x86 binary that runs on the same machine).

---

## 4.3 HEX File Record Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["RECORD START COLON"] --> F1["LL BYTE COUNT 1 BYTE"]
    F1 --> F2["AAAA LOAD OFFSET 2 BYTES"]
    F2 --> F3["TT RECORD TYPE 1 BYTE"]
    F3 --> F4["DD DATA PAYLOAD N BYTES"]
    F4 --> F5["CC CHECKSUM 1 BYTE"]
    T0["TYPE 00 DATA"] --> F3
    T1["TYPE 01 END OF FILE"] --> F3
    T2["TYPE 04 EXTENDED LINEAR ADDRESS"] --> F3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Record types you must recognize for KTU:**
> - `00` = Data record (carries ROM bytes).
> - `01` = End-of-File marker (the programmer stops after this).
> - `04` = Extended Linear Address — used when the ROM image exceeds **64 KB** (16-bit address space).

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Language to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"REAL TIME DEADLINE LESS THAN 10 US"} -->|YES| ASM["ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE"]
    Q1-->|NO| Q2{"ROM BUDGET LESS THAN 4 KB"}
    Q2-->|YES| OPT["EMBEDDED C WITH SIZE OPTIMIZATION OS"]
    Q2-->|NO| Q3{"DEVELOPMENT DEADLINE LESS THAN 2 WEEKS"}
    Q3-->|YES| C["EMBEDDED C WITH ARDUINO OR HAL"]
    Q3-->|NO| C2["EMBEDDED C WITH FULL OPTIMIZATION"]
    ASM--> NOTE["EXPECT 2 TO 3X EFFORT BUT GUARANTEED CYCLE ACCURACY"]
    OPT--> NOTE2["EXPECT 1.3 TO 1.5X CODE BLOAT VERSUS ASM"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Machine Language, Assembly Language, and High-Level Language used in microcontrollers.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-scored):**

| Aspect | Machine Language | Assembly Language | High-Level Language (Embedded C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Notation | Binary 0s and 1s | Mnemonics like MOV, ADD | C statements with functions |
| Translator | None | Assembler | Cross-Compiler |
| Readability | None | Moderate | High |
| Portability | None | Low | High |
| Execution | Fastest | Near fastest | 80–95% of native |
| Code Size | Smallest | Small | 1.3–2.5× larger |

(1 mark for each clear, correct contrast. 3 marks total.)

---

### **Q2. What is a cross-compiler? Why is it required for microcontroller programming?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

A **cross-compiler** is a compiler that executes on a **host machine** (typically a PC with an x86/ARM CPU running Windows/Linux) but generates machine code for a **different target machine** (e.g., 8051, MSP430, ARM Cortex-M). It is required because:
1. The target microcontroller has **insufficient resources** (RAM, storage, OS) to host a full compiler.
2. The host provides a **richer development environment** — editor, simulator, version control.
3. The output is a **HEX file** that is flashed to the target's non-volatile memory.

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three valid justifications.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a) Explain the three levels of microcontroller programming languages with suitable examples. Discuss the role of an assembler and a cross-compiler in the embedded development workflow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Three levels (5 marks — 1.67 each):**

- **Level 1 — Machine Language:** Binary instructions directly executed by the CPU. Example: `0111 0100` is the opcode for `MOV A, #data` in 8051. The CPU's instruction decoder hardware interprets it without any translation. **Zero abstraction**, **zero portability**.

- **Level 2 — Assembly Language:** Symbolic mnemonics that map **one-to-one** to opcodes. Example: `MOV A, #25H` is the assembly form. An **assembler** (e.g., Keil A51) reads the `.asm` file, looks up each mnemonic in the **opcode table**, and produces a binary object file. The developer retains **full, cycle-accurate control** of registers, SFRs, and bit-addressable memory.

- **Level 3 — High-Level Language (Embedded C):** Structured syntax with functions, loops, and conditionals. Example: `P1 = 0x0F;` writes the value 0x0F to Port 1. The **cross-compiler** (Keil C51, SDCC) translates one C statement into **multiple assembly/machine instructions** after performing parsing, optimization, and code generation.

**2. Role of Assembler and Cross-Compiler (2 marks):**

- **Assembler:** Single-pass (or two-pass) translator — converts mnemonics to opcodes, resolves labels, generates a **relocatable .OBJ file**.
- **Cross-Compiler:** Multi-stage translator — lexical analysis, parsing, semantic checking, optimization (dead code elimination, register allocation), and final code generation producing either assembly (then assembled) or directly a HEX file.

---

**(b) A 8051 system uses a 11.0592 MHz crystal. An Assembly routine consists of 1 200 instructions, where 600 instructions take 1 machine cycle, 400 take 2 machine cycles, and 200 take 3 machine cycles. Compute the total execution time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Machine cycle frequency.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2:** Machine cycle period.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^{3}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 3:** Total machine cycles consumed.

$$N_{total} = (600 \times 1) + (400 \times 2) + (200 \times 3)$$

$$N_{total} = 600 + 800 + 600 = 2\,000 \text{ machine cycles}$$

**Step 4:** Total execution time.

$$T_{exec} = N_{total} \times T_{MC} = 2\,000 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 2\,170\,\mu s = 2.170 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Maximum throughput = 1 200 instructions / 2.170 ms ≈ **553 000 instructions/second** for this mixed-cycle routine. Pure 1-cycle instructions alone would yield ~921 600 instr/s. The result is consistent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare Machine Language, Assembly Language, and Embedded C in terms of execution speed, code size, portability, and development time. State two scenarios where Assembly is preferred over Embedded C. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison Table (5 marks — 1.25 each row):**

| Criterion | Machine Language | Assembly Language | Embedded C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Execution Speed | Fastest (100%) | Near-fastest (95–100%) | 80–95% |
| Code Size | Smallest (1×) | Small (~1×) | 1.3–2.5× larger |
| Portability | None | Very low (per architecture) | High (with stdint.h, CMSIS) |
| Development Time | Extremely high (weeks) | High (days) | Low (hours to days) |

**2. Scenarios where Assembly is preferred (2 marks — 1 each):**

- **Hard real-time control loops** with sub-microsecond deterministic deadlines — e.g., **anti-lock braking system (ABS)**, where a missed deadline can be life-critical. The **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** must be provable.
- **Ultra-low-memory bootloaders** (≤ 1 KB ROM) for cost-sensitive MCUs where every byte of Flash counts and the function is too simple to justify C's overhead.

---

**(b) A 8051 firmware occupies 3.5 KB when written in Assembly. When re-implemented in Embedded C with default optimization, it occupies 7.7 KB. The target MCU has 8 KB of Flash. Determine: (i) the expansion ratio, (ii) the percentage ROM overhead, and (iii) whether the C version fits in the 8 KB Flash. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Expansion Ratio:**

$$R_{C/Asm} = \frac{S_C}{S_{Asm}} = \frac{7.7}{3.5} = 2.2$$

**(ii) Percentage Overhead:**

$$\% \text{ Overhead} = (R - 1) \times 100\% = (2.2 - 1) \times 100\% = 120\%$$

**(iii) Fits in 8 KB Flash?**

$$S_C = 7.7 \text{ KB} \leq 8 \text{ KB} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{YES, it fits.}$$

**Remaining Flash:**

$$\Delta_{free} = 8 \text{ KB} - 7.7 \text{ KB} = 0.3 \text{ KB} = 307.2 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Although the C version fits, only **307 bytes remain** — insufficient for adding features, interrupt vectors, or a bootloader. The designer should either:
> 1. Switch to a higher-density MCU (e.g., 16 KB Flash variant), or
> 2. Recompile with `-Os` size optimization in SDCC/Keil C51, which typically reduces the footprint by **30–40%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on "Microcontroller Languages" questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Compiler" and "Cross-Compiler"** — A 2-mark deduction is typical if you say "we use a compiler" without specifying **cross-compilation** when the host and target architectures differ.
> 2. **Forgetting the divide-by-12 factor** — On the 8051, the machine cycle is **1/12 of the oscillator period**, not 1/1. Misapplying this on a 7-mark numerical costs ~3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring SMOD bit effect on baud rate** — When $SMOD = 1$, the factor $2^{SMOD}$ doubles the baud rate. KTU papers often vary this between sub-parts.
> 4. **Confusing RAM types in Embedded C** — Mixing up `data`, `idata`, `xdata`, `pdata`, `code` memory spaces. Each has a different addressing speed and physical location.
> 5. **Skipping the Intel HEX record-type list** — Examiners expect you to identify `:00…01` as the End-of-File marker and `:04…` as the Extended Linear Address.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Three Tiers:** Machine (binary) → Assembly (mnemonics) → High-Level (Embedded C).
- [x] **1-to-1 vs 1-to-many mapping:** ASM = 1 instruction = 1 opcode; C = 1 statement = N instructions.
- [x] **Cross-Compiler Definition:** Host ≠ Target architecture; outputs HEX file.
- [x] **Native vs Cross:** GCC on x86 → x86 binary (native); Keil C51 on x86 → 8051 binary (cross).
- [x] **Assembler Role:** Mnemonic → opcode table lookup → relocatable object file.
- [x] **8051 Machine Cycle Formula:** $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$.
- [x] **Instruction Time Formula:** $T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times T_{MC}$.
- [x] **Expansion Ratio:** $R_{C/Asm} = S_C / S_{Asm} \approx 1.5$–$2.5$.
- [x] **Baud Rate Equation:** $\;Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 (256 - TH1)}$.
- [x] **HEX File Records:** `:LLAAAATTDDCC` — Type 00 = Data, 01 = EOF, 04 = Ext. Address.
- [x] **8051 Instruction Size:** 1 to 3 bytes; 255 unique opcodes; 44 mnemonics; 11 addressing modes.
- [x] **Embedded C Extensions:** `bit`, `sbit`, `sfr`, `interrupt`, `using`, `code`, `xdata`, `idata`, `pdata`.
- [x] **Language Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - **Sub-µs deadline** → Assembly
  - **< 4 KB ROM** → C with `-Os`
  - **Fast prototyping** → C with HAL/Arduino
  - **Mass-produced ultra-cheap MCU** → Hand-tuned ASM
- [x] **Common Crystal for 8051:** **11.0592 MHz** — chosen because it yields exact integer baud rates at 9 600 / 19 200 / 115 200 bps with SMOD = 0.
- [x] **Industry Trend:** > **90%** of new embedded projects use C/C++; Assembly is reserved for ISRs, bootloaders, and DSP inner loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ports # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, a **Port** is a **bidirectional, 8-bit parallel Input/Output (I/O) interface** that serves as the primary physical conduit for data exchange between the CPU core (Accumulator, SFRs) and the external world (peripherals, sensors, memory, and human-machine interfaces). The standard 8051 silicon die provides **four such ports**, named **P0, P1, P2, and P3**, each comprising **8 physical pins** (bit-addressable lines Px.0 through Px.7). Each port is internally mapped to a **Special Function Register (SFR)** at addresses `80H (P0)`, `90H (P1)`, `A0H (P2)`, and `B0H (P3)` in the **Special Function Register (SFR) memory space**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 ports are **NOT identical** in electrical construction. P1, P2, and P3 have **internal pull-up resistors**, whereas **P0 has open-drain outputs** (no internal pull-up). This structural difference is the single most frequently tested topic in KTU university exams. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine the microcontroller as a **busy corporate office**: - The **CPU** is the manager sitting inside a sealed room. - The **Ports** are the **four windows** (P0, P1, P2, P3) built into the office wall facing the street. - A **customer (external device)** can hand a document to the manager *only* through one of these windows. - Each window has **8 shutters** (the 8 bits/lines of the port). - The manager decides *whether* a window is for **reading** (Input) or **handing out** (Output) by writing a control bit. - **Port 3's** windows are special — they have a second set of shutters behind the first, used for **specific tasks** like ringing a bell (interrupt) or talking on a dedicated phone line (serial communication). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Number of Ports** | **4 (P0, P1, P2, P3)** | Fixed by 8051 silicon design | | **Lines per Port** | **8 bits (parallel)** | Each line bit-addressable | | **SFR Address Range** | `80H – FFH` | P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H | | **Logic High Voltage (V_OH)** | **+5V DC** | TTL-compatible | | **Logic Low Voltage (V_OL)** | **0V DC (GND)** | Sink current capability | | **Internal Pull-up Resistance (P1–P3)** | **≈ 30 kΩ to 40 kΩ** | Weak pull-up (MOSFET-based) | > [!NOTE] > **Definitions for KTU Board Exam:** > - **Bit Addressable:** Each of the 8 bits of a port can be individually accessed using instructions like `SETB P1.0` or `CLR P2.5`. > - **Bidirectional:** A single port line can act as either Input or Output (configured via software by writing '1' to the corresponding bit in P1, P2, P3). > - **Quasi-bidirectional (P1–P3):** A unique 8051 term meaning the port can be input or output, but the output drive strength differs based on direction. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the Four Ports ### 2.1.1 Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3 — "Quasi-Bidirectional Ports" These three ports share an **identical internal structure** consisting of: 1. **A D-Type Latch (1-bit memory cell)** — Holds the value written by the CPU. 2. **An internal Pull-up FET (MOSFET)** — A weak pull-up resistor (≈ 30–40 kΩ equivalent) that pulls the pin HIGH when the latch contains '1'. 3. **A strong Pull-down FET** — Active only when the latch contains '0' to drive the pin to ground. 4. **An Output Buffer (Tri-state driver)** — Connects to the physical pin. 5. **An Input Buffer** — Reads the physical pin state during a read operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to configure a pin as Input (for P1, P2, P3):** > To make a pin behave as an **input**, the programmer must write a **logic '1'** to the corresponding latch bit. This disables the strong pull-down FET and connects the weak internal pull-up. The actual external signal then overrides the weak pull-up (HIGH = '1', LOW = '0'). If '0' is written to the latch and the pin is read, the output driver will fight the external signal — **this is a classic KTU short-answer question**. ### 2.1.2 Port 0 — "True Bidirectional / Open-Drain Port" Port 0 is structurally unique: - **NO internal pull-up resistor.** It uses **open-drain** outputs. - Each pin requires an **external pull-up resistor** (typically **10 kΩ**) when used as a general-purpose I/O line. - During **external memory access (when EA = 0 or address > 4KB for 8052 etc.)**, Port 0 multiplexes the **lower-order address byte (A0–A7)** AND the **data byte (D0–D7)**. A **latch (e.g., 74HC373)** is required externally to demultiplex address from data using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal. - During **Flash programming**, Port 0 also receives the program code bytes. ### 2.1.3 Port 3 — "Alternate Function Port" Port 3 is a **dual-function port**. When the alternate function is NOT required, it behaves exactly like P1. When alternate functions are enabled, the special function output replaces the normal port output. The internal latch must be set to '1' for the alternate function to take control. | P3 Bit | Alternate Function | Pin Name | Description | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | **P3.0** | **RxD** | Receive Data | Serial communication input | | **P3.1** | **TxD** | Transmit Data | Serial communication output | | **P3.2** | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 | Active-low interrupt request | | **P3.3** | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 | Active-low interrupt request | | **P3.4** | **T0** | Timer 0 Input | External clock for Timer 0 | | **P3.5** | **T1** | Timer 1 Input | External clock for Timer 1 | | **P3.6** | **WR** | Write Strobe | External RAM write enable | | **P3.7** | **RD** | Read Strobe | External RAM read enable | ## 2.2 Port 2's Role — Address Bus (Upper Byte) Port 2 serves a second purpose: during external memory access beyond the internal 4 KB ROM (or for 8031 which has no internal ROM), Port 2 emits the **upper 8-bit address bus (A8–A15)**. Unlike P0, P2 does **NOT** need an external latch because the upper address byte remains stable for the entire memory cycle (only P0 toggles between A0–A7 and D0–D7). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics | Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | **Current Sink (any port pin LOW)** | $I_{OL} \approx 1.6 \text{ mA}$ per pin | Driving LED, relay coil, optocoupler | | **Total Sink per Port (8 bits)** | $\sum I_{OL} \leq 10 \text{ mA}$ to $26 \text{ mA}$ | BJT/transistor base drive budget | | **Source Current via Internal Pull-up** | $I_{OH} \approx 60 \text{ \mu A}$ (very weak) | Cannot reliably drive an LED HIGH; needs buffer (e.g., ULN2003, 74LS244) | | **External Pull-up for P0** | $R_{pullup} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (typical) | Pulls line HIGH when no device drives it | | **Pull-up time constant** | $\tau = R_{pullup} \times C_{pin}$ | Determines rise time; affects high-speed bus signaling | | **Port Read vs SFR Read** | `MOV A, P1` reads **pin** | `MOV A, 90H` reads **latch (SFR)** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse reading the **port pin** vs reading the **port latch**. The instruction `MOV A, P1` reads the **physical pin state** (external signal). The instruction `MOV A, 90H` (direct SFR address) reads the **internal latch** (last value written). This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question. ## 2.4 Why This Architecture? (The 'Why') - **Open-drain P0** allows **wired-OR bus sharing** among multiple devices and **voltage level shifting** by changing V_CC of the pull-up resistor. - **Weak internal pull-ups** in P1–P3 save silicon area while still allowing input reading without external components. - **Multiplexed address/data bus (P0 + P2)** was a design compromise to keep the 40-pin DIP package compact while still supporting 16-bit external addressing (64 KB address space). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Current Sourcing Limitation of P1–P3 Pull-ups The internal pull-up of P1–P3 is implemented as a **p-channel MOSFET** in saturation/linear region. We derive the **maximum source current** available when driving an external load (e.g., an LED or TTL gate). ### Step 1 — Identify the pull-up resistance The equivalent pull-up resistance is given as: $$R_{pull\text{-}up} \approx 30 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 40 \text{ k}\Omega$$ For worst-case engineering calculation, we use the minimum value (lowest resistance = highest source current capability): $$R_{min} = 30 \text{ k}\Omega$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the source current into a load Assume a load pulls the pin LOW with a voltage drop. Using **Ohm's Law**: $$I_{source} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{load}}{R_{pull\text{-}up}}$$ With $V_{CC} = 5\text{V}$ and $V_{load} \approx 0.4\text{V}$ (TTL LOW threshold): $$I_{source} = \frac{5\text{V} - 0.4\text{V}}{30 \text{ k}\Omega}$$ $$I_{source} = \frac{4.6 \text{ V}}{30{,}000 \text{ }\Omega}$$ $$I_{source} \approx 153 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$$ ### Step 3 — Compare to LED drive requirement A standard red LED requires approximately $10 \text{ mA}$ to glow visibly. Therefore: $$\frac{I_{required}}{I_{available}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ }\mu\text{A}}{153 \text{ }\mu\text{A}} \approx 65.4$$ The internal pull-up can supply only **~1.5%** of the current needed. **Conclusion:** P1–P3 CANNOT directly drive an LED HIGH. The LED must be wired in **active-LOW** configuration (sinking current when port pin outputs '0'), where the strong pull-down FET can sink up to $1.6 \text{ mA}$ per pin — still insufficient for bright LED, so a driver transistor is needed. ### Step 4 — Engineering takeaway (real-world utility) This is why industrial designs use: - **ULN2003** (Darlington sink driver) for relays. - **74LS244** (octal buffer) for sourcing high current. - **MOSFET** switches for motor/load control. ## 3.2 Derivation: External Pull-up Resistor Value for Port 0 Port 0 lacks an internal pull-up. For **timing-sensitive applications**, the resistor value is chosen to balance rise time and power consumption. ### Step 1 — Define rise time constraint The RC charging equation for the pin (with pin capacitance $C_{pin} \approx 10 \text{ pF}$): $$V(t) = V_{final} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ For the voltage to reach **63.2% of final value** (one time constant): $$\tau = R_{pull\text{-}up} \times C_{pin}$$ ### Step 2 — Set rise time target Assume target rise time $t_{r} \approx 100 \text{ ns}$ to $1 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$: $$R_{pull\text{-}up} = \frac{t_{r}}{C_{pin}}$$ For $t_r = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$: $$R_{pull\text{-}up} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}}$$ $$R_{pull\text{-}up} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the standard compromise A value between **4.7 kΩ and 10 kΩ** is standard in KTU lab manuals. Using $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$: $$\tau = 10{,}000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} = 100 \text{ ns}$$ This is comfortably below most microcontroller instruction cycle times (≈ 1 µs at 12 MHz crystal). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Port Configuration Truth Table | SFR Latch Bit Value | Pin Direction (P1, P2, P3) | Pull-up FET | Pull-down FET | External Pin Behavior | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | **0** | **Output (LOW)** | OFF | ON | Pin driven **strongly LOW** (sinks current) | | **1** | **Input mode** | ON (weak) | OFF | Pin **floating**; external source drives value | | **1** | **Output (HIGH)** | ON (weak) | OFF | Pin pulled HIGH weakly (sources only ~60 µA) | | **X** (Port 0) | Output | Open-drain (both OFF when latched '0' for input) | Active when latched '1' | **Requires external pull-up** | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** This matrix is the basis of the **read-modify-write** problem in 8051. When you read the port, you read the **pin**, but writing modifies the **latch**. The `Read-Modify-Write` instructions (`INC P1`, `ANL P1, #data`) first read the pins, modify the value, then write back to the latch — which can cause unintended bit changes if pin values differ from latch values. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an 8051 Port Pin (P1–P3) ```mermaid flowchart TB CPUBus["CPU Internal Data Bus
8-bit bidirectional"] LatchUnit["D-Type Latch
1-bit memory cell
Stores SFR bit value"] PullUpFET["P-Channel Pull-Up FET
Weak Pull-Up ~30 kΩ to 40 kΩ"] PullDownFET["N-Channel Pull-Driver FET
Strong Low-Side Driver
Sink ~1.6 mA"] OutBuf["Output Buffer Stage
Tri-State Driver"] InBuf["Input Buffer Stage
Schmitt Trigger"] PinNode["Physical Port Pin
External Pin on 40-pin DIP"] CPUBus -->|"Write '1'"| LatchUnit CPUBus -->|"Write '0'"| LatchUnit LatchUnit -->|"Q=1 disables"| PullDownFET LatchUnit -->|"Q=1 enables"| PullUpFET LatchUnit -->|"Q=0 enables"| PullDownFET PullUpFET --> OutBuf PullDownFET --> OutBuf OutBuf --> PinNode PinNode --> InBuf InBuf -->|"Read Pin"| CPUBus LatchUnit -.->|"Read SFR (direct addr)"| CPUBus ``` ## 4.2 Port 0 Functional Architecture (Open-Drain + Address/Data Mux) ```mermaid flowchart TB CPUData["CPU Data Bus D0-D7"] AddrMux["Internal Address/Data Mux
Controlled by ALE Signal"] ExtLatch["External Latch 74HC373
Demultiplexes Address from Data"] AddrLow["External Address Bus A0-A7
Latched Output to Memory"] DataBus["External Data Bus D0-D7
Bidirectional to Memory"] PullUp["External Pull-Up Resistor
R = 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ
Required for I/O Operation"] P0Pin["Port 0 Physical Pins
32 to 39 on 40-pin DIP"] ALESig["ALE Signal from 8051
Active HIGH latch strobe"] CPUData --> AddrMux AddrMux -->|"During Address Phase"| ExtLatch AddrMux -->|"During Data Phase"| P0Pin ExtLatch --> AddrLow P0Pin <-->|"Bidirectional Data"| DataBus ALESig -->|"Strobes at falling edge"| ExtLatch PullUp -->|"To Vcc 5V"| P0Pin P0Pin -.->|"Read back through internal buffer"| CPUData ``` ## 4.3 Port 3 Alternate Function Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR P3Port["Port 3 SFR at B0H"] P3Bit0["P3.0"] P3Bit1["P3.1"] P3Bit2["P3.2"] P3Bit3["P3.3"] P3Bit4["P3.4"] P3Bit5["P3.5"] P3Bit6["P3.6"] P3Bit7["P3.7"] P3Port --- P3Bit0 P3Port --- P3Bit1 P3Port --- P3Bit2 P3Port --- P3Bit3 P3Port --- P3Bit4 P3Port --- P3Bit5 P3Port --- P3Bit6 P3Port --- P3Bit7 P3Bit0 --> RxD["RxD
Serial Input UART"] P3Bit1 --> TxD["TxD
Serial Output UART"] P3Bit2 --> INT0["INT0
External Interrupt 0"] P3Bit3 --> INT1["INT1
External Interrupt 1"] P3Bit4 --> T0["T0
Timer 0 External Input"] P3Bit5 --> T1["T1
Timer 1 External Input"] P3Bit6 --> WR["WR
External Write Strobe"] P3Bit7 --> RD["RD
External Read Strobe"] ``` ## 4.4 Port Role During External Memory Access (Summary Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB M["8051 Microcontroller Core"] P0R["Port 0 Role
Multiplexed Address A0-A7 plus Data D0-D7"] P2R["Port 2 Role
Upper Address A8-A15"] P3R["Port 3 Role
WR and RD Strobe plus ALE enable"] ExtMem["External Memory
ROM at 0000H-FFFFH
RAM at 0000H-FFFFH (Data)"] ExtLatch74["74HC373 Address Latch
Holds Lower Address"] M --> P0R M --> P2R M --> P3R P0R --> ExtLatch74 ExtLatch74 --> ExtMem P2R --> ExtMem P3R --> ExtMem P0R <-->|"Data Bus"| ExtMem ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Explain the internal structure of a port pin of 8051 with a neat block diagram.** **Model Answer:** A port pin of 8051 (for P1, P2, P3) consists of the following components: 1. **D-Type Latch:** A 1-bit memory cell that stores the value written by the CPU via the SFR. 2. **Internal Pull-up FET:** A p-channel MOSFET acting as a weak pull-up resistor (≈ 30–40 kΩ) to hold the pin HIGH when latch = 1. 3. **Output Driver (Pull-down FET):** A strong n-channel MOSFET that pulls the pin LOW when latch = 0. 4. **Output Buffer:** Tri-state buffer that connects the internal signal to the external pin. 5. **Input Buffer:** Reads the external pin state back into the CPU during input operations. **Key Point:** Port 0 lacks the internal pull-up FET and requires an external pull-up resistor of ~10 kΩ. *(3 marks — KTU standard: 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for function, 1 mark for Port 0 distinction)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Differentiate between reading a port pin and reading a port latch.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Reading Port Pin | Reading Port Latch | |---|---|---| | **Instruction Example** | `MOV A, P1` | `MOV A, 90H` | | **Source** | Physical external pin voltage | Internal SFR latch | | **Use Case** | Reading external sensor data | Read-modify-write operations | | **Behavior** | Returns whatever is currently on the pin | Returns last value written by CPU | **Important:** `Read-Modify-Write` instructions like `INC P1`, `ANL P1, #0FH`, `CPL P1.0` actually read the **pin state**, modify it, and write back — which can cause **"Read-Modify-Write hazards"** if pin voltage differs from latch value due to external loading. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a) Describe the four I/O ports of the 8051 microcontroller with their alternate functions and internal structure. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The 8051 microcontroller has four 8-bit I/O ports — P0, P1, P2, and P3 — all mapped to SFR addresses `80H, 90H, A0H, B0H` respectively. **Port 0 (P0 — Address `80H`):** - It is a **true bidirectional, open-drain port** with **no internal pull-up resistor**. - It serves two roles: (i) General-purpose I/O (when external memory is not used), requiring an external pull-up resistor (typically 10 kΩ). (ii) During external memory access, it acts as the **multiplexed lower-order address bus (A0–A7)** and **data bus (D0–D7)**. A latch (74HC373) demultiplexes the address from data using the **ALE** signal. **Port 1 (P1 — Address `90H`):** - A **quasi-bidirectional port** with internal pull-up resistors (~30 kΩ). - It is used **purely for general-purpose I/O** — no alternate function. - Each pin can sink ~1.6 mA and source only ~60 µA through internal pull-up. **Port 2 (P2 — Address `A0H`):** - Structurally similar to P1 (quasi-bidirectional, internal pull-up). - Alternate function: during external memory access beyond 4 KB, it provides the **upper address byte A8–A15**. Unlike P0, no external latch is needed because the upper address remains stable throughout the cycle. **Port 3 (P3 — Address `B0H`):** - Quasi-bidirectional port with **dual functions**: - P3.0 → RxD (Serial Input) - P3.1 → TxD (Serial Output) - P3.2 → INT0 (External Interrupt 0) - P3.3 → INT1 (External Interrupt 1) - P3.4 → T0 (Timer 0 External Input) - P3.5 → T1 (Timer 1 External Input) - P3.6 → WR (External Write Strobe) - P3.7 → RD (External Read Strobe) For the alternate function to take effect, the corresponding SFR latch bit must be set to **'1'**. *(7 marks — 2 marks per port, 1 mark for alternate function table)* --- **(b) Explain the dual role of Port 0 during external memory interfacing. With a block diagram, describe how P0, P2, and P3 are used to interface external ROM and RAM. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Dual Role of Port 0:** Port 0 functions in two distinct modes: 1. **Mode 1 — General Purpose I/O:** When the microcontroller executes code from internal ROM (EA = 1) and does not access external memory, P0 acts as a normal I/O port. However, since it is **open-drain**, external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ) are mandatory. 2. **Mode 2 — Address/Data Bus:** When external memory is accessed, P0 is configured by hardware to act as a **multiplexed address and data bus**. During the first half of the memory cycle, it outputs **A0–A7**. During the second half, it carries **D0–D7** (read or write data). **External Memory Interface Block Diagram:** ``` 8051 74HC373 External Memory +-------+ +-------+ +-----------+ P0 -|A0-A7/D0-D7|-->+-------->|D0-D7 |---A0-A7--->| ROM/RAM | | | | | | | | ALE-|>------- Strobe |LE Q0| | | | | | | | | | P2 -|A8-A15|-------------->|A8-A15 |------------>|Addr Bus | | | +-------+ | | P3.6-|WR----|-----------------WR strobe---> | | P3.7-|RD----|-----------------RD strobe---> | | +-------+ +-----------+ ``` **Step-by-step operation during external memory READ cycle:** 1. CPU places the **16-bit address** on P0 (lower byte) and P2 (upper byte). 2. **ALE** signal goes HIGH → external latch (74HC373) captures A0–A7. 3. **ALE** goes LOW → latch holds the address; P0 is now freed for data. 4. CPU activates **RD (P3.7)** — external memory places the data byte on P0. 5. CPU reads the data from P0 and latches into the accumulator. **Step-by-step WRITE cycle:** 1. Address placed on P0 + P2; latched by ALE. 2. CPU places data on P0. 3. CPU activates **WR (P3.6)** strobe; external memory latches the data. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to mention that the **external pull-up resistor on P0 is ONLY needed during I/O mode**, not during bus mode (because in bus mode, internal pull-ups are disabled automatically and external memory chips drive the lines). > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the role of **EA pin** with ALE. EA = Enable Access (selects internal vs external ROM); ALE = Address Latch Enable. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Drawing the 74HC373 incorrectly — its **LE (Latch Enable)** is **active-HIGH**, and the **ALE pulse is active-HIGH** during the address phase. The latch captures on the **falling edge** of ALE. *(7 marks — 2 marks P0 dual role, 3 marks interface diagram, 2 marks READ/WRITE cycles)* --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2025, Expected Pattern]** **(a) Explain with a neat diagram the structure of Port 1 of 8051. How does it differ from Port 0? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Structure of Port 1:** Port 1 is a **quasi-bidirectional 8-bit I/O port** with each pin containing: 1. A **bit-addressable D-latch** that stores the CPU-written value. 2. An **internal pull-up FET** (p-channel MOSFET with equivalent resistance of ~30 kΩ). 3. A **strong pull-down driver FET** (n-channel, capable of sinking ~1.6 mA). 4. An **output buffer** to drive the external pin. 5. An **input buffer** (Schmitt trigger) for reading pin state. **Operation as Output:** Writing '1' to latch → pull-up FET ON, pull-down FET OFF → pin is weakly HIGH. Writing '0' → pull-down FET ON → pin strongly LOW. **Operation as Input:** The CPU must first write '1' to the latch (this is called "pre-conditioning the port"). This disables the output driver and connects the weak pull-up; the external signal can then drive the pin. **Differences Between Port 0 and Port 1:** | Parameter | Port 0 | Port 1 | |---|---|---| | **Pull-up Resistor** | None (open-drain) | Internal (~30 kΩ) | | **External Pull-up Needed** | Yes (~10 kΩ) | No | | **Alternate Function** | Address/Data bus (A0–A7 / D0–D7) | None (pure I/O) | | **Output Drive HIGH** | Cannot source current | Weak source (~60 µA) | | **Output Drive LOW** | Strong sink (~3.2 mA total) | Strong sink (~1.6 mA/pin) | | **Bidirectionality** | True bidirectional | Quasi-bidirectional | | **Pin Count When Used** | All 8 pins need external resistor | None | *(7 marks — 3 marks structure diagram, 2 marks operation, 2 marks difference table)* --- **(b) Write an 8051 C program to toggle all 8 bits of Port 1 continuously with a delay. Explain the importance of writing '1' to the port before reading it as input. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **C Program (Keil C51 syntax):** ```c #include void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) { unsigned int i, j; for(i = 0; i < ms; i++) for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++); // ~1 ms delay @ 11.0592 MHz crystal } void main(void) { P1 = 0x00; // Initialize P1 as output (all bits LOW) while(1) { P1 = 0xFF; // All bits HIGH delay_ms(500); P1 = 0x00; // All bits LOW delay_ms(500); } } ``` **Explanation of `P1 = 0xFF` before reading P1 as input:** When we want to use a port pin of P1 as an **input**, we must write '1' to that pin's latch bit first. The reasons are: 1. **Disable the strong pull-down driver:** Writing '1' turns OFF the n-channel pull-down FET, which would otherwise fight any external HIGH signal by shorting it to ground. 2. **Enable the weak internal pull-up:** This ensures the pin is held in a known HIGH state when no external device is driving it. 3. **Configure the output buffer to high-impedance:** This isolates the internal output driver from the external pin, allowing the external circuit to drive the pin voltage. 4. **Prevent accidental short-circuit:** If '0' were written to the latch and the external device tried to drive HIGH, the strong pull-down FET would sink current, potentially damaging the microcontroller or the external device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Example:** `while(P1.0 == 0);` waits until external switch (connected to P1.0 with pull-up) is pressed (active-LOW). Before this loop, the code must execute `P1 = 0xFF;` to make P1.0 an input. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Pitfall 1:** Writing to P1 with `P1 = 0xFF` only **conditions the port for input**; it does NOT force the pin HIGH as an output. The output is weakly HIGH (~60 µA), insufficient to drive loads. > - **Pitfall 2:** Students often forget that `P1` and `0x90` refer to the **same SFR**; the difference is that `P1` is the symbolic name recognized by the Keil compiler, while `0x90` is its direct hex address. > - **Pitfall 3:** When using `Read-Modify-Write` instructions like `P1 |= 0x01`, the compiler reads the **pin state**, not the latch. If P1.0 is pulled LOW externally while the latch holds 1, the bit will be incorrectly cleared after the OR operation. *(7 marks — 3 marks program, 2 marks logic, 2 marks input configuration explanation)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - [ ] 8051 has **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines**. - [ ] SFR addresses: **P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H** — all **bit-addressable**. - [ ] **Port 0** = open-drain, **NO** internal pull-up, requires **external 10 kΩ pull-up** for I/O mode; multiplexes **A0–A7 / D0–D7** during external memory access. - [ ] **Port 1** = quasi-bidirectional, internal pull-up ~30 kΩ, **no alternate function**, pure I/O. - [ ] **Port 2** = quasi-bidirectional, internal pull-up ~30 kΩ, alternate function = **A8–A15** during external memory access. - [ ] **Port 3** = quasi-bidirectional with **8 alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD). Latch must be set to '1' for alternate function. - [ ] **To read a port pin as input:** write '1' to the corresponding latch bit first (pre-condition the port). - [ ] **Source current** through internal pull-up ≈ **60 µA** (weak); **Sink current** ≈ **1.6 mA/pin** (strong). - [ ] LEDs are best wired in **active-LOW** configuration (sinking current). - [ ] **External latch 74HC373** is required for P0 address/data demultiplexing; ALE signal controls the latch. - [ ] **EA pin** selects internal vs external ROM; **ALE pin** strobes the address latch. - [ ] **Read-Modify-Write hazard** exists with instructions like `ANL P1, A`, `INC P1`, `CPL P1.x` — they read the **pin**, not the latch. - [ ] **8052** variants add P4 and additional SFRs, but standard 8051 has only P0–P3. - [ ] Maximum total port source current per port ≈ **10 mA** (some datasheets specify 26 mA sink) — exceeding this **damages the chip**. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question says *"with neat diagram"* — draw the latch + pull-up FET + pull-down FET structure for at least ONE port pin. Even a rough sketch gets 2 extra marks. Always label the SFR address in the diagram. Mentioning `EA`, `ALE`, and `PSEN` in any external-memory question earns bonus partial credit.

topics.title: Timers; Comparison of Machine Language, Assembly Language, and High-Level Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timers; Comparison of Machine Language, Assembly Language, and High-Level Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Timers in 8051 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, a **Timer** is a dedicated hardware peripheral consisting of internal flip-flop-based registers that increment automatically on every machine cycle (or on an external event), used to generate precise time delays, measure intervals, or count external events without placing a software burden on the CPU. The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counter units**: **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**, each of which can be independently configured via the **TMOD** and **TCON** Special Function Registers (SFRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBICT504 – Module 2):** *Timers/Counters of 8051 – TMOD, TCON registers, Timer modes, and delay generation using assembly language programming.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stopwatch" Concept

Imagine you are conducting an experiment in a chemistry lab and you need to measure exactly **1.085 microseconds** of a reaction. You cannot possibly click a stopwatch that fast. Instead, you have an **electronic stopwatch (Timer)** that ticks once every 1.085 µs because of an internal crystal. You preset the counter to a value, start it, and when it rolls over (overflows), it sets a **flag** (an alarm bell) that tells the CPU "Time is up!"

- **Crystal (Oscillator)** → Tick source (heartbeat)
- **÷12 Prescaler** → Slows the tick to a usable rate
- **THx / TLx Registers** → The actual counter (16-bit by default)
- **TFx Flag** → The "bell" that rings on overflow
- **TRx Bit** → Start/Stop switch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** For a **11.0592 MHz** crystal (the standard KTU lab frequency), **one timer tick = 1.085 µs**. For a **12 MHz** crystal, **one timer tick = 1 µs**. This number is the foundation of every delay calculation you will ever do.

---

### 1.3 Comparison of Programming Languages — Formal Definitions

| Language Level | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Machine Language** | The lowest-level programming language consisting entirely of binary digits (**0s and 1s**) that the CPU can directly decode and execute. Each instruction is a unique bit pattern. |
| **Assembly Language** | A low-level symbolic language that uses **mnemonics** (human-readable abbreviations like `MOV`, `ADD`, `INC`) and labels to represent machine instructions, requiring an **assembler** to translate into machine code. |
| **High-Level Language (HLL)** | A programming language (e.g., **C, C++, Python**) abstracted from hardware, using English-like syntax, data structures, and control statements, requiring a **compiler** or interpreter for translation. |

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Talking to a Foreign Worker"

- **Machine Language** = Pointing at objects and grunting (perfectly understood, but impossible to remember for complex tasks).
- **Assembly Language** = Speaking in a structured dialect of the worker's tongue (e.g., "MOV A, B" = "give me the box A, take from B"). Still tedious but manageable.
- **High-Level Language** = Speaking in your own native English and having a **professional translator** (compiler) convert your requests into the worker's dialect.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The 8051 was historically programmed in **Assembly** for tight, time-critical code, but modern KTU labs and industry use **Embedded C** (a high-level language) for portability and readability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TMOD Register — The Timer "Mode Selector"

The **TMOD** (Timer Mode) register is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** SFR at address **89H**. It configures the operational mode and behavior of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The lower nibble controls Timer 0 and the upper nibble controls Timer 1.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Name** | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | M1.1 | M0.1 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | M1.0 | M0.0 |
| **Timer** | \multicolumn{4}{\vert c \vert}{Timer 1} | \multicolumn{4}{\vert c \vert}{Timer 0} |

- **GATE**: When 1, timer is controlled by software (TRx) **AND** external INTx pin (hardware gating). When 0, timer is controlled by TRx only.
- **C/T̄**: 1 = **Counter mode** (counts external pulses on T0/T1 pin); 0 = **Timer mode** (counts internal machine cycles).
- **M1, M0**: Mode selection bits (4 modes: Mode 0, 1, 2, 3).

### 2.2 TCON Register — The Timer "Control & Status"

The **TCON** register at address **88H** is **bit-addressable** and contains the timer run control bits and overflow flags.

| Bit | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Addr** | 8FH | 8EH | 8DH | 8CH | 8BH | 8AH | 89H | 88H |

- **TRx (Timer Run)**: 1 = Start the timer; 0 = Stop the timer (set by software).
- **TFx (Timer Flag)**: 1 = Timer has overflowed (set by hardware, cleared by software).

### 2.3 The Four Timer Modes — Operational Breakdown

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | Bit Width | Max Count | Reload? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility) | 13 | **8192** | No |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | **16-bit Timer** (most common) | 16 | **65536** | No |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | **8-bit Auto-Reload** (used for baud rate) | 8 | **256** | Yes (auto) |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | **Split Timer** (Timer 0 only) | 8 + 8 | 256 each | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Frequency Topic:** Mode 1 is **most commonly tested** for delay generation. Mode 2 is **most commonly tested** for serial port baud rate generation (e.g., 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | **Machine Cycle Period** (time taken by 1 timer tick) | seconds |
| 2 | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | **Timer Tick Frequency** | Hz |
| 3 | $\text{Delay (Mode 1)} = (65536 - N) \times T_{MC}$ | 16-bit delay formula | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{Delay (Mode 2)} = (256 - N) \times T_{MC}$ | 8-bit auto-reload delay | seconds |
| 5 | $N = 65536 - \dfrac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Initial value for THx:TLx | decimal |
| 6 | $\text{THx} = \dfrac{N}{256}$ | High-byte of 16-bit initial value | hex |
| 7 | $\text{TLx} = N \mod 256$ | Low-byte of 16-bit initial value | hex |
| 8 | $\text{Baud Rate (Mode 2)} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{32 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Serial port baud rate formula | bps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Lab Crystal Values:**
> - **$f_{osc}$ = 11.0592 MHz** → $T_{MC}$ = 1.085 µs (used because it yields **integer baud rates** like 9600, 4800, 1200)
> - **$f_{osc}$ = 12 MHz** → $T_{MC}$ = 1 µs (simpler math, but produces fractional baud rates)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Timers** (washing machines, microwave ovens) use microcontroller timers to switch relays at exact intervals.
- **PWM Generation** for motor speed control in robotics uses timers to mark duty-cycle edges.
- **Baud Rate Generation** for UART communication relies on Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (auto-reload).
- **Real-Time Clocks (RTC)** in embedded systems use timer overflows to maintain a 1-second tick.
- **Event Counting** (e.g., counting bottles on a conveyor belt) uses counter mode by feeding pulses into T0/T1 pins.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally to generate the basic timing signal. Therefore, the time for one machine cycle is derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{\text{Number of Oscillator Periods per MC}}{\text{Oscillator Frequency}} \\[4pt]
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- The crystal oscillates at $f_{osc}$ cycles per second.
- 8051 hardware uses a ÷12 prescaler, meaning **12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle**.
- Therefore, 1 timer tick takes $T_{MC}$ seconds.

For the standard KTU lab frequency of **11.0592 MHz**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \\[4pt]
T_{MC} &= 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\[4pt]
T_{MC} &= 1.085 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: 1 ms Delay in Mode 1

**Problem:** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP) to generate a delay of **1 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**.

**Step 1: Compute Machine Cycle Period**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Number of Timer Ticks Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Ticks} &= \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{MC}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1000 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} \\[4pt]
&= 921.66 \text{ ticks} \\[4pt]
&\approx 922 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Initial Timer Value (N)**

In Mode 1, the timer counts from N to 65536 (2^16) and overflows. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 65536 - 922 \\[4pt]
N &= 64614
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert N to Hexadecimal and Split into TH0 and TL0**

$$
\begin{aligned}
64614 \div 256 &= 252 \text{ remainder } 102 \\[4pt]
252_{10} &= \text{FC}_{16} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{TH0} = \text{FCH} \\[4pt]
102_{10} &= 66_{16} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{TL0} = 66\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $(\text{FCH} \times 256 + 66\text{H}) = 64614$ → After 65536 - 64614 = 922 ticks × 1.085 µs = **1000.07 µs ≈ 1 ms** ✓

**Step 5: Write the Assembly Language Program**

```assembly
ORG 0000H
      MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode
BACK: MOV TH0, #0FCH    ; Load high byte of initial value
      MOV TL0, #066H    ; Load low byte of initial value
      SETB TR0          ; Start Timer 0
AGAIN:JNB TF0, AGAIN    ; Wait until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
      CLR TR0           ; Stop Timer 0
      CLR TF0           ; Clear overflow flag
      SJMP BACK         ; Repeat (generates continuous ~1 ms pulses)
END
```

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Mode 2 Auto-Relay Delay (250 µs)

**Problem:** Calculate the value to be loaded in TH1 to generate **250 µs** delay in Mode 2, $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz.

**Step 1: Machine Cycle Period**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Ticks Required**

$$
\text{Ticks} = \frac{250 \text{ µs}}{1 \text{ µs}} = 250
$$

**Step 3: Initial Value (Mode 2 is 8-bit, so max is 256)**

$$
N = 256 - 250 = 6 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{TH1} = 06\text{H}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Language Comparison

| Parameter | Machine Language | Assembly Language | High-Level Language (C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Notation** | Binary (0/1) | Mnemonics (`MOV A, #25H`) | English-like (`a = b + c;`) |
| **Translator** | **None** (direct execution) | **Assembler** | **Compiler** |
| **Execution Speed** | **Fastest** (no translation overhead) | **Very Fast** | **Slower** (compiler overhead) |
| **Code Size** | Smallest | Small | Largest (sometimes 2-3× larger) |
| **Readability** | **Very Poor** (e.g., `01110100`) | Moderate (need hardware knowledge) | **Excellent** (hardware-independent) |
| **Error Probability** | Very High | Moderate | Low (debugger support) |
| **Hardware Dependency** | 100% (CPU-specific) | 100% (CPU-specific) | **Portable** (with minor changes) |
| **Development Time** | Very High | High | **Low** |
| **Optimization Level** | Manual (perfect control) | Manual (fine control) | Compiler-dependent |
| **Memory Efficiency** | Best | Good | Moderate |
| **Learning Curve** | Steepest | Steep | Gentle |
| **Example Tool** | Hex editor | Keil µVision, ASEM-51 | Keil C51, SDCC |
| **Typical Use** | Bootloaders, BIOS | Time-critical ISR, drivers | Application firmware, IoT |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Language Translation Flow

The following derivation chain shows how code is transformed from human thought to CPU execution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HLL Source} &\xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} \text{Assembly Source} \\[4pt]
\text{Assembly Source} &\xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Object Code (Relocatable)} \\[4pt]
\text{Object Code} &\xrightarrow{\text{Linker + Locator}} \text{HEX File (.hex)} \\[4pt]
\text{HEX File} &\xrightarrow{\text{Programmer}} \text{ROM/Flash on 8051} \\[4pt]
\text{Flash Contents} &\xrightarrow{\text{Fetch by CPU}} \text{Machine Language Execution}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Note:** The term "**mnemonic**" comes from the Greek *mnemonikos* ("of memory"). It is a short, memorable symbol that a programmer can recall easily — e.g., `MOV` for "move", `ADD` for "add", `INC` for "increment".

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Timer/Counter Internal Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

This Mermaid flowchart maps the complete signal flow inside the 8051 timer subsystem, from the crystal oscillator down to the overflow flag.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator f_osc e.g. 11.0592 MHz] --> B[Divide by 12 Prescaler]
    B --> C{Mode Select TMOD M1 M0}
    C -->|Mode 1 16 bit| D1[TH0 8 bits]
    C -->|Mode 1 16 bit| D2[TL0 8 bits]
    C -->|Mode 2 8 bit| E1[TH0 Reload Value]
    C -->|Mode 2 8 bit| E2[TL0 Active Counter]
    C -->|Mode 3 Split| F1[TL0 8 bit Timer]
    C -->|Mode 3 Split| F2[TH0 8 bit Timer]
    D1 --> G[16 bit Counter]
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> H[8 bit Counter]
    E2 --> H
    F1 --> I1[TL0 Overflow Flag]
    F2 --> I2[TH0 Overflow Flag]
    G --> J[Overflow Detection FFFFH to 0000H]
    H --> K[Overflow Detection FFH to 00H]
    J --> L[Set TF0 Flag in TCON]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Interrupt Request to CPU or Polling]
    M --> N[CPU Services Timer Event]
```

### 4.2 Timer Initialization Procedure — Step-by-Step Mermaid Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Configure TMOD Register] --> S2[Step 2 Load THx with High Byte]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Load TLx with Low Byte]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Set TRx bit to 1 Start Timer]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Wait for TFx 1 by Polling JNB TFx or Interrupt]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Clear TFx Flag]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Clear TRx to Stop Timer]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Reload THx TLx for next cycle if Mode 1]
```

### 4.3 Programming Language Translation Hierarchy

This diagram illustrates the **pyramid of abstraction** from hardware to high-level language.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Machine Language 0s and 1s] --> L2[Level 2 Assembly Language Mnemonics MOV ADD]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 High Level Language C C plus plus]
    L1 --> T1[CPU Executes Directly]
    L2 --> T2[Assembler Translates]
    L3 --> T3[Compiler Translates]
    T1 --> E[Executable Code in 8051 ROM]
    T2 --> E
    T3 --> E
    E --> CPU[8051 CPU Fetches and Executes]
```

### 4.4 TMOD Register Bit Mapping (Visual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TMOD[TMOD Register Address 89H] --> B7[GATE1]
    TMOD --> B6[CTbar 1]
    TMOD --> B5[M11]
    TMOD --> B4[M01]
    TMOD --> B3[GATE0]
    TMOD --> B2[CTbar 0]
    TMOD --> B1[M10]
    TMOD --> B0[M00]
    B7 --> T1A[Timer 1 Configuration]
    B6 --> T1A
    B5 --> T1A
    B4 --> T1A
    B3 --> T0A[Timer 0 Configuration]
    B2 --> T0A
    B1 --> T0A
    B0 --> T0A
```

---
