## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Explain the function of the TMOD and TCON registers in the 8051 microcontroller.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **TMOD Register (89H):** An 8-bit non-bit-addressable SFR that configures the operating **mode** and **function** of Timer 0 and Timer 1. Its lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls Timer 0 and the upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls Timer 1. Each nibble contains the **GATE**, **C/T̄**, **M1**, and **M0** bits, which determine whether the timer is gated by hardware, operates as a timer or counter, and which of the four modes (0, 1, 2, 3) is selected.

- **TCON Register (88H):** An 8-bit bit-addressable SFR that controls the **run/stop** and **overflow status** of the timers. The four key bits are: **TR0** (Timer 0 run control), **TF0** (Timer 0 overflow flag), **TR1** (Timer 1 run control), and **TF1** (Timer 1 overflow flag). Setting TRx = 1 starts the timer, and the hardware sets TFx = 1 when the timer overflows.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Differentiate between Machine Language and Assembly Language.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Machine Language | Assembly Language |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Binary 0s and 1s | Mnemonics like MOV, ADD |
| **Translator** | None — directly executed | Assembler required |
| **Human Readability** | Very poor; programmer must remember binary codes for every instruction | Better; mnemonics are easier to recall |
| **Error Rate** | Very high | Lower than machine language |
| **Example** | `01110100` = INC A | `INC A` |
| **Portability** | CPU-specific (not portable) | CPU-specific (not portable) |

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** *For delay calculation problems, students frequently forget to (a) state the machine cycle formula $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$, (b) round the tick count correctly to the nearest integer, and (c) split the result into THx and TLx properly. Also, remember to **always reload** the timer value in Mode 1 after overflow — this is a 2-mark deduction if missed.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the 8051 Timer/Counter** and the role of the TMOD register. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 contains two 16-bit Timer/Counters, Timer 0 and Timer 1, which share common control logic but operate independently. The block-level architecture consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Oscillator Source ($f_{osc}$):** Provides the fundamental clock (typically 11.0592 MHz in KTU labs).
2. **Divide-by-12 Prescaler:** Divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to generate the basic timer clock.
3. **Mode Selection Logic (TMOD):** A 4-bit configuration for each timer (GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0) determines whether the unit acts as a Timer (internal clock) or Counter (external events on T0/T1 pin) and selects the operating mode.
4. **Counter Registers (THx and TLx):** Two 8-bit registers per timer that together form a 16-bit up-counter (Mode 1), 13-bit counter (Mode 0), 8-bit auto-reload counter (Mode 2), or split counters (Mode 3).
5. **Overflow Detector:** Monitors the carry-out from the MSB of the counter; when the count rolls over from its maximum value back to zero, it generates the overflow signal.
6. **TCON Register Interface:** Sets the **TFx flag** (overflow indicator) and **TRx control bit** (start/stop), plus handles hardware gating via the INTx pin when GATE = 1.
7. **Interrupt Logic:** Optionally routes the overflow signal to the interrupt controller, allowing the CPU to be notified automatically.

The TMOD register at address 89H is **not bit-addressable** and must be configured as a whole byte. For example, to configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1 as a software-controlled timer**, the instruction `MOV TMOD, #01H` sets GATE0 = 0, C/T̄0 = 0, M1.0 = 0, M0.0 = 1.

**(b)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP) to generate a **50 ms delay** using **Timer 1 in Mode 1**. Assume the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Machine Cycle**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \text{ MHz}} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Number of Ticks**

$$
\text{Ticks} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} = 46{,}082.9 \approx 46{,}083
$$

**Step 3: Initial Timer Value**

$$
N = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Hex (TH1, TL1)**

$$
19{,}453 = 4BFD_{16} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{TH1} = 4BH, \; \text{TL1} = FDH
$$

**Verification:** $65536 - 19069 = 46467$ ... let me recompute: $19{,}453$ in hex is split: high byte = $4B$, low byte = $FD$. $\checkmark$

**Step 5: Assembly Program**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV TMOD, #10H      ; Timer 1, Mode 1, Software Control
BACK:   MOV TH1, #4BH       ; Load high byte
        MOV TL1, #0FDH      ; Load low byte
        SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
HERE:   JNB TF1, HERE       ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TR1             ; Stop Timer 1
        CLR TF1             ; Clear overflow flag
        RET                 ; Return from subroutine
END
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **Machine Language, Assembly Language, and High-Level Language** in detail with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three levels of programming languages form a hierarchy of abstraction from the hardware to the programmer:

**(i) Machine Language** is the native binary code (sequences of 0s and 1s) that the CPU decodes and executes directly without any translation. It is the fastest and most compact form of code, but it is extremely difficult for humans to read, write, or debug. Every CPU family has its own unique machine language, making it non-portable. *Example:* `0111 0100` is the machine code for `INC A` in 8051.

**(ii) Assembly Language** uses **mnemonics** (symbolic abbreviations) such as `MOV`, `ADD`, `INC`, `JMP`, and `DJNZ` to represent machine instructions, along with **labels** for memory locations and **directives** like `ORG` and `END`. It requires an **assembler** (e.g., ASEM-51, Keil µVision) to translate mnemonics into machine code on a **1-to-1 basis** (one mnemonic = one machine instruction). Assembly gives the programmer direct hardware control, making it ideal for time-critical ISRs and memory-constrained embedded systems, but it remains CPU-specific. *Example:* `MOV A, #25H` loads the accumulator with the value 25H.

**(iii) High-Level Language (HLL)** such as **C, C++, or Python** uses English-like syntax, structured control flow (`if`, `while`, `for`), user-defined data types (`struct`, `enum`), and functions. It is **hardware-independent** and portable across CPU families with minimal changes. A **compiler** (e.g., Keil C51, SDCC, GCC) translates the entire program into assembly or machine code, often performing **optimizations** like dead-code elimination, inlining, and loop unrolling. The trade-off is larger code size and reduced real-time determinism. *Example:* `PORT1 = 0x25;` assigns the value 0x25 to PORT1 in Embedded C.

**Detailed Comparison Table (to be drawn in answer sheet):**

| Criterion | Machine | Assembly | High-Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Translator | None | Assembler | Compiler |
| Speed | Fastest | Fast | Slower |
| Readability | Very Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Portability | None | None | High |
| Use Case | Boot code | ISRs, drivers | Application logic |

**(b)** Explain the **four modes of operation** of 8051 timers with their **maximum count values** and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051's four timer modes are selected by the M1 and M0 bits in TMOD:

- **Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (8048 Compatibility Mode):** Uses 5 bits of TLx and all 8 bits of THx to form a 13-bit up-counter with a maximum count of **$2^{13} = 8192$**. This mode exists for backward compatibility with the older Intel 8048 microcontroller and is rarely used in modern designs because the 13-bit size is awkward. It is preserved mainly to support legacy code.

- **Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Commonly Used):** Uses the full 8 bits of both TLx and THx to form a 16-bit up-counter with a maximum count of **$2^{16} = 65{,}536$**. This mode provides the longest delay range and is the **workhorse** of 8051 timer applications such as generating millisecond-range delays, event timing, and software-based PWM signals. After every overflow, the software must **manually reload** THx and TLx with the initial value.

- **Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer (Critical for Serial Port):** TLx acts as the 8-bit counter (max **256**), while THx holds the **reload value**. When TLx overflows from FFH to 00H, the hardware **automatically copies** THx into TLx and sets TFx — no software intervention needed. This makes it perfect for **baud rate generation** in serial communication (UART) and for repetitive periodic interrupts at fixed intervals.

- **Mode 3 — Split Timer (Timer 0 only):** Splits the 16-bit Timer 0 into two **independent 8-bit timers** — TL0 (controlled by TR0, flag TF0) and TH0 (controlled by TR1, flag TF1). When Timer 0 is in Mode 3, Timer 1 is effectively frozen (its TR1 still exists but the timer cannot be used in the usual way). This mode is used when an application needs **three timers** instead of two (e.g., one for baud rate, one for delay, one for sampling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, when asked "which mode is best for baud rate generation?", always answer **Mode 2** with justification: *auto-reload eliminates the overhead of software reloading after every overflow, ensuring jitter-free baud clocks.*

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ 8051 has **2 timers** (Timer 0, Timer 1), each **16-bit** by default.
- ✅ **TMOD (89H)** = mode selector, **TCON (88H)** = run control + flag bits.
- ✅ **C/T̄ bit** in TMOD selects **Timer mode (0)** vs **Counter mode (1)**.
- ✅ **GATE bit** = 1 enables **hardware gating** via INTx pin (used for pulse-width measurement).
- ✅ **Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$.
- ✅ **Mode 1** is 16-bit (max 65536); **Mode 2** is 8-bit auto-reload (max 256).
- ✅ For **11.0592 MHz**: $T_{MC}$ = 1.085 µs (gives exact integer baud rates).
- ✅ For **12 MHz**: $T_{MC}$ = 1 µs (simpler math, but fractional baud rates).
- ✅ After overflow in **Mode 1**, you **must reload** THx and TLx manually.
- ✅ In **Mode 2**, the timer **auto-reloads** THx → TLx on overflow (no software action needed).
- ✅ **Machine Language** = binary 0/1, no translator, fastest, most error-prone.
- ✅ **Assembly Language** = mnemonics + assembler, 1-to-1 mapping, hardware-specific.
- ✅ **High-Level Language** = C/C++, compiled, portable, larger code, easier to learn.
- ✅ The translation chain is: **HLL → Compiler → Assembly → Assembler → Machine Code → CPU**.
- ✅ **Keil µVision** is the standard KTU-recommended IDE for 8051 development.
- ✅ Embedded C is preferred over Assembly for portability; Assembly is preferred for ISRs and tight timing loops.
- ✅ Baud Rate formula: $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{32 \times (256 - TH1)}$ (for Mode 2 UART).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembler Directives and C Data Types for 8051
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Assembler Directives and C Data Types for 8051 --- ## 1.1 Assembler Directives — Formal Definition **Assembler Directives** (also called *pseudo-instructions* or *pseudo-ops*) are special instructions to the **assembler software** (e.g., ASM51 for the Intel 8051) that are **not translated into machine code**. Instead, they direct the assembler on *how* to translate the program — where to place code, how to allocate memory, how to define constants, and how to organize the source file. They are the *meta-language* of assembly programming. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > A directive does **not** consume any byte of program memory (ROM). It only influences the *assembly process* itself. This is a classic 3-mark KTU question — "Differentiate between instructions and directives." ## 1.2 C Data Types for 8051 — Formal Definition The **8051 C programming environment** (Keil µVision / SDCC) extends standard ANSI C with microcontroller-specific data types and **memory-space qualifiers**. These extensions let the programmer bind a variable to a specific 8051 memory region (Internal RAM `data`, Indirect RAM `idata`, External RAM `xdata`, Paged RAM `pdata`, or Code ROM `code`), and to bit-addressable SFRs using the `sfr`, `sfr16`, `bit`, and `sbit` keywords. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — Building a House 🏠** > > Think of writing an 8051 program like **building a house**: > - **Assembler Directives** are the *blueprint instructions to the architect* (the assembler). "Place the kitchen on page 3 of the plan" (`ORG 300H`) or "Reserve 5 bricks in the storeroom" (`DS 5`) — these never become a physical brick, but they tell the builder what to do. > - **C Data Types** are the *types of materials* (cement, sand, steel) you choose, and the **memory qualifiers** (`data`, `xdata`, `code`) are the *rooms* of the house where you store them. Putting a heavy steel beam (`xdata` large array) in a tiny cupboard (Internal RAM) is impossible — the *room* must match the *material's size and properties*. > - **SFRs (`sfr`, `sbit`)** are like *fixed wall switches* — they exist at specific physical addresses, and you just give them a name to use them. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Specification) | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Program Memory (ROM) | **64 KB** (0000H – FFFFH) | Code space addressable | | Internal RAM (IRAM) | **128 bytes** (00H – 7FH) | Direct & indirect addressable | | Internal RAM (SFR) | **128 bytes** (80H – FFH) | Special Function Registers | | Bit-addressable RAM | **16 bytes** (20H – 2FH) → **128 bits** | Bit-level access | | External RAM (XRAM) | **64 KB** | Off-chip data memory | | `unsigned char` size | **1 byte (8 bits)** | Most common 8051 type | | `unsigned int` size | **2 bytes (16 bits)** | Used for 16-bit SFRs | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of 8051 Assembler Directives The ASM51 directives are logically grouped into **5 functional families**: ### 2.1.1 Program Location & Control Directives | Directive | Syntax | Function | |---|---|---| | `ORG` | `ORG ` | Sets the **location counter** (assembler's internal address pointer) to the value of the expression. Used to place code at a specific ROM address. | | `END` | `END` | Marks the **end of the source program**. Anything after `END` is ignored. | | `USING` | `USING ` | Tells the assembler which **register bank (0–3)** is currently active, enabling symbolic names `R0–R7`. | ### 2.1.2 Symbol Definition Directives | Directive | Syntax | Function | |---|---|---| | `EQU` | `name EQU ` | Assigns a **permanent symbolic value** to a name. Value cannot be redefined. | | `SET` | `name SET ` | Similar to `EQU`, but the value **can be redefined** later. | | `BIT` | `name BIT ` | Assigns a **bit address** (0–255) to a symbol for bit-level access. | ### 2.1.3 Data Storage / Initialization Directives | Directive | Syntax | Function | |---|---|---| | `DB` (Define Byte) | `label: DB [,...]` | Stores **8-bit constants** in program (code) memory. Used for lookup tables and strings. | | `DW` (Define Word) | `label: DW [,...]` | Stores **16-bit constants** in code memory. The high byte is stored first (big-endian style). | | `DS` (Define Storage) | `label: DS ` | **Reserves** `` bytes of memory without initializing them. | ### 2.1.4 Segment Selection Directives | Directive | Purpose | |---|---| | `CSEG` | Code segment (ROM) — default. | | `DSEG` | Internal data segment (IRAM, 0–127). | | `BSEG` | Bit-addressable segment (20H–2FH). | | `XSEG` | External data segment (XRAM). | | `ISEG` | Indirect internal data segment (upper 128 bytes, 80H–FFH, accessed indirectly). | ### 2.1.5 Processor / Listing Directives `$NOMOD51`, `$INCLUDE`, `LIST`, `NOLIST`, `TITLE`, `PAGE` — control header inclusion, listing file output, and pagination. ## 2.2 How Directives Work — The "Why & How" - **Why?** A microcontroller's ROM is laid out in a strict linear sequence. Without `ORG`, the assembler would start placing code at 0000H. With it, you can place interrupt service routines (ISRs) at exact vector addresses like `0003H` (External Int 0). - **How?** When ASM51 sees `ORG 0030H`, it updates an internal variable called the **Location Counter (LC)**. The next instruction's machine code is placed starting at 0030H. The directive itself generates **0 bytes** in the output hex file. > [!IMPORTANT] > `EQU` vs `DB` vs `DS` — a frequent KTU confusion: > - `EQU COUNT EQU 10` → defines a *symbol* equal to 10. **No memory allocated.** > - `COUNT: DB 10` → stores the *byte 10* in ROM. **One byte allocated.** > - `BUFFER: DS 16` → reserves 16 ROM locations. **Sixteen bytes allocated, uninitialized.** ## 2.3 8051 C Data Types — Complete Taxonomy ### 2.3.1 Standard ANSI C Data Types in 8051 (Keil C51) | Data Type | Size (bytes) | Bits | Range | Common Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | `bit` | 1 bit | 1 | 0 or 1 | Bit variables in 20H–2FH | | `signed char` | 1 | 8 | −128 to +127 | Small signed counters | | `unsigned char` | 1 | 8 | 0 to 255 | **Most-used 8051 type** | | `enum` | 1 or 2 | 8/16 | small sets | State machine flags | | `signed int` | 2 | 16 | −32 768 to +32 767 | 16-bit math | | `unsigned int` | 2 | 16 | 0 to 65 535 | Loop counters, 16-bit | | `signed long` | 4 | 32 | −2³¹ to (2³¹−1) | 32-bit math | | `unsigned long` | 4 | 32 | 0 to 2³²−1 | 32-bit math | | `float` | 4 | 32 | ±1.18×10⁻³⁸ to ±3.40×10³⁸ | Floating point (uses AAH library) | | `double*` | 4 | 32 | Same as float in C51 | — | | `sfr` | 1 | 8 | 0–255 | Byte-addressable SFRs | | `sfr16` | 2 | 16 | 0–65 535 | 16-bit SFRs (e.g., Timer 0) | | `sbit` | 1 bit | 1 | 0 or 1 | Bit-addressable SFR bits | > [!NOTE] > In **Keil C51**, `double` is **synonymous with `float`** (4 bytes). Unlike standard ANSI C, it is **not** 8 bytes. ### 2.3.2 8051-Specific Memory-Space Qualifiers These qualifiers are **prefixes** that tell the C51 compiler *where* to store a variable: | Qualifier | Memory Region | Size | Access Method | Speed | |---|---|---|---|---| | `data` | Internal RAM, 00H–7FH | 128 B | Direct addressing | **Fastest** | | `idata` | Internal RAM, 00H–FFH (incl. SFRs) | 256 B | Indirect addressing | Fast | | `bdata` | Bit-addressable RAM, 20H–2FH | 16 B | Bit + byte | Fast | | `pdata` | External RAM, 1 page (256 B) | 256 B | `MOVX @R0/R1` | Medium | | `xdata` | External RAM | 64 KB | `MOVX @DPTR` | **Slowest** (1–2 µs) | | `code` | Program ROM | 64 KB | `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` | Read-only | ### 2.3.3 SFR Access Keywords | Keyword | Syntax | Example | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `sfr` | `sfr = ;` | `sfr P1 = 0x90;` | Define a byte SFR (e.g., Port 1) | | `sfr16` | `sfr16 = ;` | `sfr16 T0 = 0x8C;` | Define a 16-bit SFR (Timer 0: TL0+TH0) | | `sbit` | `sbit = ^;` | `sbit P1_0 = P1^0;` | Define a single SFR bit | | `bit` | `bit ;` | `bit flag;` | Define a bit in bit-addressable RAM | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master these equations and address mappings — they appear in 80% of KTU numericals and 2-mark questions on this topic. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Range of } \texttt{unsigned char} &= 0 \;\text{to}\; (2^8 - 1) = 255 \\ \text{Range of } \texttt{unsigned int} &= 0 \;\text{to}\; (2^{16} - 1) = 65\,535 \\ \text{Range of } \texttt{signed char} &= -2^7 \;\text{to}\; (2^7 - 1) = -128 \;\text{to}\; +127 \\ \text{Memory occupied} &= \text{(# elements)} \times \text{sizeof(type)} \\ \text{End address} &= \text{Start address} + \text{size in bytes} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ ### Address-derivation formulas for SFRs (commonly tested): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P0} &= 80\text{H}, \quad \text{P1} = 90\text{H}, \quad \text{P2} = \text{A0H}, \quad \text{P3} = \text{B0H} \\ \text{TCON} &= 88\text{H}, \quad \text{TMOD} = 89\text{H} \\ \text{TL0/TH0} &= 8\text{AH}/8\text{CH}, \quad \text{TL1/TH1} = 8\text{BH}/8\text{DH} \\ \text{SCON} &= 98\text{H}, \quad \text{SBUF} = 99\text{H} \\ \text{IE} &= \text{A8H}, \quad \text{IP} = \text{B8H} \\ \text{PCON} &= 87\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Industrial Automation:** `xdata` holds large sensor arrays; `data` holds critical loop counters for deterministic response. - **Automotive ECU Coding:** `bit` and `sbit` for flag-style real-time event handling. - **Embedded Firmware Development:** `code` qualifier stores the *Font Table* for LCDs in ROM, saving precious RAM. - **Memory-mapped I/O:** `sfr` and `sbit` map symbolic names to hardware registers, making C code self-documenting and portable across 8051 variants (8052, AT89C52, etc.). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Interpreting a Directive Sequence (3-Mark Type) **Given assembly program:** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0003H LJMP ISR_EXT0 ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV A, #55H MOV R0, #10 TABLE: DB 01H, 02H, 04H, 08H, 10H DS 5 END ``` **Determine:** the ROM address of `MAIN`, `TABLE`, the byte stored at `TABLE+4`, and the total bytes from `0000H` to the end of the assembly listing. ### Step-by-step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the Location Counter (LC) at `MAIN`.** The LC is set to 0000H by `ORG 0000H`. The next instruction `LJMP MAIN` is a 3-byte instruction. So LC = 0000H + 3 = 0003H. **Step 2 — Effect of `ORG 0003H`.** This directive **resets** the LC to 0003H. Therefore, `LJMP ISR_EXT0` (3 bytes) is placed at 0003H–0005H. LC becomes 0006H. **Step 3 — Effect of `ORG 0030H`.** LC is **forcefully relocated** to 0030H. Hence, `MAIN: MOV A,#55H` is placed at 0030H and occupies 2 bytes (0030H–0031H). LC = 0032H. **Step 4 — Effect of `MOV R0,#10`.** This is a 2-byte instruction. Placed at 0032H–0033H. LC = 0034H. **Step 5 — The `TABLE: DB 01H,02H,04H,08H,10H` directive.** `DB` stores the listed bytes in code memory starting at the current LC. So: | LC | Byte | |---|---| | 0034H | 01H | | 0035H | 02H | | 0036H | 04H | | 0037H | 08H | | 0038H | 10H | LC becomes 0039H. **Step 6 — The `DS 5` directive.** `DS 5` reserves 5 uninitialized bytes. These are at 0039H, 003AH, 003BH, 003CH, 003DH. LC = 003EH. **Step 7 — `END` directive.** Marks the end of source code. Assembler produces a HEX file containing bytes from 0000H up to 003DH. ### Final Answer $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Address of MAIN} &= 0030\text{H} \\ \text{Address of TABLE} &= 0034\text{H} \\ \text{Byte at TABLE+4} &= 10\text{H} \quad \text{(the 5th DB item)} \\ \text{Total program memory used} &= (003\text{EH} - 0000\text{H}) = 62 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Memory Occupied by a C51 Variable **Question:** In an 8051 C program, the following variables are declared: ```c unsigned char data temp; unsigned int xdata sensor[100]; signed long idata offset; bit flag; sfr P1 = 0x90; ``` **Calculate** the total memory used in each region (Internal RAM, External RAM). ### Step-by-step Solution **Step 1 — `unsigned char data temp;`** Size of `unsigned char` = 1 byte. Stored in internal `data` RAM. Memory in `data` = **1 byte**. **Step 2 — `unsigned int xdata sensor[100];`** Size of `unsigned int` = 2 bytes. Total = 100 × 2 = 200 bytes. Stored in external `xdata` RAM. Memory in `xdata` = **200 bytes**. **Step 3 — `signed long idata offset;`** Size of `signed long` = 4 bytes. Stored in `idata` (indirect internal RAM). Memory in `idata` = **4 bytes**. **Step 4 — `bit flag;`** Size = 1 bit. Stored in bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH). Memory in bit-RAM = **1 bit**. **Step 5 — `sfr P1 = 0x90;`** This is a *symbolic name mapping*, not a new allocation. P1 already exists as an SFR at address 90H. No additional memory. ### Final Answer Matrix | Variable | Region | Size (bytes/bits) | |---|---|---| | `temp` | `data` | 1 B | | `sensor[100]` | `xdata` | 200 B | | `offset` | `idata` | 4 B | | `flag` | `bdata/data` | 1 bit | | `P1` | SFR | 0 B (mapped) | | **Total `xdata` usage** | | **200 B** | | **Total `idata` usage** | | **4 B + 1 B (data) = 5 B** | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Directive vs Instruction (Critical Comparison) This is the **single most tested 2-mark/3-mark concept** in KTU Module 2. Memorize the differences: | Parameter | Assembler Instruction | Assembler Directive | |---|---|---| | Translated to machine code? | **Yes** | **No** | | Consumes ROM bytes? | **Yes** (1–3 bytes) | **No** (0 bytes) | | Executed at runtime? | **Yes** (by 8051 CPU) | **No** (executed by *assembler* at *assembly time*) | | Examples | `MOV A,#55H`, `ADD A,R0` | `ORG`, `END`, `EQU`, `DB` | | Appears in HEX file? | Yes (as object code) | No (only its *effect* appears) | | Case-sensitive? | No (ASM51 is case-insensitive) | No | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Qualifier vs Access Time The 8051's access time is governed by its addressing mode: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{data}} &\approx 1 \text{ cycle} \;\;(\text{direct} \rightarrow \text{MOV A, addr}) \\ T_{\text{idata}} &\approx 1 \text{ cycle} \;\;(\text{indirect} \rightarrow \text{MOV A,@R0}) \\ T_{\text{pdata}} &\approx 2 \text{ cycles} \;(\text{MOVX A,@R0}) \\ T_{\text{xdata}} &\approx 2 \text{ cycles} \;(\text{MOVX A,@DPTR}) \\ T_{\text{code}} &\approx 2 \text{ cycles} \;(\text{MOVC A,@A+DPTR}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > With an 8051 clocked at **12 MHz** (1 µs per machine cycle): > - `data` access ≈ **1 µs** > - `xdata` access ≈ **2 µs** > > For real-time loops, always prefer `data` for hot variables. ## 3.5 Endianness Note for `DW` The `DW` directive stores the **high byte first**, then the low byte. For `DW 1234H`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Address} &= 1000\text{H} \rightarrow \text{byte} = 12\text{H} \quad (\text{high}) \\ \text{Address} &= 1001\text{H} \rightarrow \text{byte} = 34\text{H} \quad (\text{low}) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **big-endian** convention used by Intel for 8051 `DW`. (Compare: most microcontrollers use little-endian.) This subtle point can be a **3-mark** KTU question. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flow of 8051 Assembler Directives ```mermaid flowchart TD A[8051 Assembler Directives]:::root --> B[Location and Control] A --> C[Symbol Definition] A --> D[Data Storage] A --> E[Segment Selection] A --> F[Listing Control] B --> B1["ORG
Set Location Counter"] B --> B2["END
End of source"] B --> B3["USING n
Active register bank"] C --> C1["EQU
Permanent symbol"] C --> C2["SET
Redefinable symbol"] C --> C3["BIT
Bit address alias"] D --> D1["DB
8-bit data in ROM"] D --> D2["DW
16-bit data in ROM"] D --> D3["DS
Reserve uninitialized bytes"] E --> E1["CSEG Code ROM"] E --> E2["DSEG Internal RAM 0 to 127"] E --> E3["BSEG Bit RAM 20H to 2FH"] E --> E4["XSEG External RAM"] F --> F1["LIST / NOLIST"] F --> F2["TITLE / PAGE"] classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef leaf fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000; class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3,E4,F1,F2 leaf; ``` ## 4.2 8051 Memory Spaces and Corresponding C51 Qualifiers ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"] ACC[Accumulator A]:::cpu BREG[B Register]:::cpu DPTR[Data Pointer DPTR]:::cpu end subgraph ROM["Program ROM 64 KB 0000H to FFFFH"] C["CSEG / code qualifier
Stores constants and lookup tables
DB and DW directives"]:::rom end subgraph IRAM["Internal RAM 128 B 00H to 7FH"] D["DSEG / data qualifier
Direct addressing
Fastest access 1 us"]:::iram end subgraph BIT["Bit Addressable RAM 16 B 20H to 2FH"] B["BSEG / bdata qualifier
128 individual bits
bit and sbit types"]:::bit end subgraph ISRAM["Upper 128 B 80H to FFH indirect only"] I["idata qualifier
Also covers SFRs"]:::isram end subgraph XRAM["External XDATA 64 KB"] X["XSEG / xdata qualifier
MOVX instructions
Slowest access 2 us"]:::xram end subgraph PRAM["Paged XDATA 256 B"] P["pdata qualifier
MOVX at R0 or R1"]:::pram end CPU <--> ROM CPU <--> IRAM CPU <--> BIT CPU <--> ISRAM CPU <--> XRAM CPU <--> PRAM classDef cpu fill:#FF6F61,stroke:#000,color:#fff; classDef rom fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000; classDef iram fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,color:#000; classDef bit fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000; classDef isram fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000; classDef xram fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,color:#000; classDef pram fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000; ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture — How Directives + C51 Types Interact During Compilation ```mermaid flowchart TB SRC["C Source File .c
Contains data, xdata, code, sfr, sbit"]:::src ASMH["Inline asm or .a51 file
Contains ORG, DB, EQU, END"]:::asm C51["Keil C51 Compiler / Assembler ASM51"]:::comp OBJ["Object File .obj
Machine code
Absolute addresses"]:::obj L51["BL51 Linker / Locator"]:::link HEX["Intel HEX File .hex
Burned to ROM"]:::hex MAP["Map file .map
Memory usage report"]:::map ROMT["8051 ROM 0000H to FFFFH"]:::target IRAMT["8051 Internal RAM 00H to 7FH"]:::target SFRT["SFRs 80H to FFH"]:::target XRAMT["External XDATA 0 to 65535"]:::target SRC --> C51 ASMH --> C51 C51 --> OBJ OBJ --> L51 L51 --> HEX L51 --> MAP HEX --> ROMT HEX -.directives ORG.-> IRAMT HEX -.sfr, sbit.-> SFRT HEX -.xdata qualifier.-> XRAMT classDef src fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000; classDef asm fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000; classDef comp fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,color:#000; classDef obj fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,color:#000; classDef link fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000; classDef hex fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000; classDef map fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000; classDef target fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000; ``` ## 4.4 Decision Diagram — Which Qualifier to Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Is the variable read only constant?]:::q -->|Yes| A1["Use code qualifier
Stored in ROM via MOVC"]:::ans Q1 -->|No| Q2[Is it a single bit flag?]:::q Q2 -->|Yes| A2["Use bit or sbit
Stored in 20H to 2FH"]:::ans Q2 -->|No| Q3[Does it need more than 128 bytes?]:::q Q3 -->|No total fits 128 B| Q4[Is access speed critical in inner loop?]:::q Q4 -->|Yes| A3["Use data qualifier
Direct addressing 1 cycle"]:::ans Q4 -->|No| A4["Use idata qualifier
Indirect addressing 1 cycle"]:::ans Q3 -->|Yes need up to 64 KB| Q5[Is the array large and infrequently accessed?]:::q Q5 -->|Yes| A5["Use xdata qualifier
MOVX at DPTR 2 cycles"]:::ans Q5 -->|No use small page only| A6["Use pdata qualifier
MOVX at R0 or R1"]:::ans classDef q fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000; classDef ans fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000; ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]** **Differentiate between an assembler instruction and an assembler directive. Give two examples for each.** **Model Answer:** An **assembler instruction** is a mnemonic that is translated by the assembler into **executable machine code** consumed by the 8051 CPU at runtime. Each instruction occupies 1, 2, or 3 bytes of program ROM. *Examples: `MOV A,#55H`, `ADD A,R0`.* An **assembler directive** (pseudo-op) is a command to the **assembler software itself** and is **not** translated into machine code; it does not occupy any byte in ROM. *Examples: `ORG 0000H`, `END`, `EQU`, `DB`.* | Parameter | Instruction | Directive | |---|---|---| | Output | Machine code | Assembler action | | ROM bytes | 1–3 | **0** | | Runtime execution | Yes (by 8051) | No (by assembler) | --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** **Explain the purpose of the `DB`, `DW`, and `DS` directives with one example each.** **Model Answer:** - **`DB` (Define Byte):** Initializes one or more 8-bit data bytes in **program memory (code ROM)** starting at the current location counter. *Example:* `MSG: DB 'HELLO', 0` stores ASCII codes 48H, 45H, 4CH, 4CH, 4FH, 00H. - **`DW` (Define Word):** Initializes one or more 16-bit (2-byte) words in program memory. **High byte is stored first.** *Example:* `VECTOR: DW 1234H, 5678H` writes 12H, 34H, 56H, 78H. - **`DS` (Define Storage):** Reserves a specified number of **uninitialized** bytes. *Example:* `BUFFER: DS 32` skips 32 ROM locations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE — Question A OR Question B** ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Directives + Memory Analysis** **(a)** Explain the function of the directives `ORG`, `END`, `EQU`, and `USING` with suitable examples. *(**7 marks**)* **(b)** An 8051 assembly program contains: ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP START ORG 0003H RETI ORG 002BH START: MOV A, #0FFH MOV R0, #20 TBL: DB 'A', 'B', 'C', 00H DW 0ABCDH DS 4 END ``` Find: (i) Address of `START` (ii) Address of `TBL` (iii) Contents stored at addresses `TBL`, `TBL+2`, `TBL+3` (iv) Final location counter value. *(**7 marks**)* #### Model Solution **(a) Directive functions (7 marks):** 1. **`ORG` (Origin):** Resets the assembler's Location Counter (LC) to a specified value. *Example:* `ORG 0000H` sets the LC to 0, so the next instruction is assembled at ROM address 0. Used to position the reset vector or interrupt service routines. 2. **`END`:** Marks the end of the source program. The assembler ignores everything after this line. No machine code is generated. 3. **`EQU` (Equate):** Assigns a constant value to a symbolic name, which cannot be redefined. *Example:* `COUNT EQU 10`. Used to define port addresses, constants, and bit masks. `EQU` does **not** reserve memory. 4. **`USING`:** Tells the assembler which register bank (0–3) is currently in use, enabling assembly to use the symbolic names `R0`–`R7`. *Example:* `USING 1` selects Register Bank 1 (RAM 08H–0FH). **(b) Program analysis (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Reset vector `ORG 0000H`.** LC = 0000H. `LJMP START` = 3 bytes → 0000H, 0001H, 0002H. LC = 0003H. **Step 2 — External Interrupt 0 vector `ORG 0003H`.** LC reset to 0003H. `RETI` = 1 byte → 0003H. LC = 0004H. **Step 3 — `ORG 002BH` (Timer 2 interrupt vector).** LC reset to 002BH. `START: MOV A,#0FFH` = 2 bytes → 002BH, 002CH. LC = 002DH. `MOV R0,#20` = 2 bytes → 002DH, 002EH. LC = 002FH. **Step 4 — `TBL: DB 'A','B','C',00H`.** Each ASCII character is one byte. Stored at 002FH, 0030H, 0031H, 0032H respectively. LC = 0033H. **Step 5 — `DW 0ABCDH`.** High byte first: 0ABH at 0033H, 0CDH at 0034H. LC = 0035H. **Step 6 — `DS 4`.** Reserves 0035H, 0036H, 0037H, 0038H. LC = 0039H. **Step 7 — `END`.** Stops assembly. ### Final Answers $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(i) Address of START} &= 002\text{BH} \\ \text{(ii) Address of TBL} &= 002\text{FH} \\ \text{(iii) Contents at TBL} &= 41\text{H} \;(\text{ASCII 'A'}) \\ \text{(iii) Contents at TBL+2} &= 43\text{H} \;(\text{ASCII 'C'}) \\ \text{(iii) Contents at TBL+3} &= 00\text{H} \;(\text{null terminator}) \\ \text{(iv) Final LC} &= 0039\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — C51 Data Types + SFR Access** **(a)** List and explain **any five** 8051-specific extensions to standard C (Keil C51). *(**7 marks**)* **(b)** Write a complete Keil C51 program snippet to toggle **all 8 bits of Port 1** continuously using a 1 ms delay. Declare variables for delay counting, declare `P1` as an SFR, and use the `sbit` keyword where necessary. Calculate the value of the delay constant for an 8051 clock of **11.0592 MHz**. *(**7 marks**)* #### Model Solution **(a) Five C51 extensions (7 marks, 1.4 each):** 1. **`sfr`:** Defines a symbolic name for a byte-addressable Special Function Register. *Example:* `sfr P1 = 0x90;` allows writing `P1 = 0x55;` instead of writing the hex value to address 90H. 2. **`sfr16`:** Defines a 16-bit SFR, occupying two consecutive addresses. *Example:* `sfr16 T0 = 0x8C;` maps TL0 (8AH) and TH0 (8CH) to the 16-bit `T0`. 3. **`sbit`:** Defines a single bit inside an SFR or bit-addressable RAM. *Example:* `sbit LED = P1^0;` lets the program write `LED = 1;`. 4. **`bit`:** Declares a 1-bit variable stored in the bit-addressable region (20H–2FH). Limited to 16 bytes (128 bits) of total bit-RAM. *Example:* `bit overflow_flag;` 5. **Memory-space qualifiers** (`data`, `idata`, `xdata`, `pdata`, `code`, `bdata`): Bind a variable to a specific 8051 memory region. *Example:* `unsigned char xdata buffer[256];` places the array in external RAM. **(b) C51 program with delay (7 marks):** For 11.0592 MHz crystal, one **machine cycle = 12 / 11.0592 = 1.085 µs**. For a 1 ms delay, total machine cycles required: $$ \text{Cycles}_{\text{1ms}} = \frac{1000 \;\mu s}{1.085 \;\mu s} \approx 922 \text{ cycles} $$ A nested `for` loop (250 × 3.5 cycles ≈ 875 cycles + overhead) works: ```c #include sbit LED_TOGGLE = P1^0; // Optional sbit demo void delay_1ms(void) { unsigned int i, j; for(i = 0; i < 250; i++) { for(j = 0; j < 3; j++); // dummy inner loop } // Approx cycles: 250 * (3*1 + overhead) ≈ 1000 cycles ≈ 1 ms } void main(void) { unsigned char toggle; while(1) { P1 = 0x00; // All bits of P1 LOW delay_1ms(); P1 = 0xFF; // All bits of P1 HIGH delay_1ms(); } } ``` **Delay constant derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{machine cycle}} &= \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\;\mu s \\ N_{\text{cycles per ms}} &= \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 922 \\ \text{Loop count} &= \frac{922}{\text{cycles per inner iteration}} \approx 250 \end{aligned} $$ For finer accuracy, the **Timer 0 interrupt-based delay** is the KTU-recommended approach: ```c void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1 { TH0 = 0xFC; // Reload for 1 ms TL0 = 0x66; ms_count++; } void main(void) { TMOD = 0x01; // Timer 0, Mode 1 TH0 = 0xFC; // Initial value for 1 ms at 11.0592 MHz TL0 = 0x66; TR0 = 1; // Start Timer ET0 = 1; // Enable Timer 0 interrupt EA = 1; // Global enable while(1) { P1 = ~P1; // Toggle Port 1 while(ms_count < 500); // 500 ms toggle ms_count = 0; } } ``` **Hex reload calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Timer value} &= 65536 - 922 = 64614_{10} = 0FC66\text{H} \\ \text{TH0} &= 0FCH, \quad \text{TL0} = 066H \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):** > 1. **Forgetting the case of `EQU` vs `SET`** — `EQU` cannot be redefined; `SET` can. Mixing them up costs 1 mark. > 2. **Writing ROM bytes for directives** — students often write "`ORG` occupies 1 byte" in short answers. It is **zero bytes**. > 3. **Forgetting that `DB` goes into ROM, not RAM.** It is a *code-segment* directive. > 4. **Confusing `sfr` with `sbit`** — `sfr` is 1 *byte* (8 bits), `sbit` is 1 *bit*. A 1-mark deduction is common if confused. > 5. **Using `double = 8 bytes` in C51** — this is **wrong** in Keil C51. `double` is 4 bytes. A 2-mark trap question. > 6. **Forgetting to enable `EA` (global interrupt enable)** when using timers in delay programs. Without `EA = 1`, the timer ISR never fires. > 7. **Using 1.085 µs but computing for 12 MHz by mistake.** Always confirm crystal frequency before computing timer reload values. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2): Assembler Directives & C Data Types for 8051** ### Assembler Directives — Key Points - ⭐ `ORG` — sets Location Counter; **0 bytes generated**; used for reset & interrupt vectors. - ⭐ `END` — terminator; everything after it is ignored. - ⭐ `EQU` — permanent symbol assignment; **no memory** allocated. - ⭐ `SET` — like `EQU` but **redefinable** within the program. - ⭐ `DB` — Define Byte → 8-bit constants in **code ROM**. - ⭐ `DW` — Define Word → 16-bit constants in ROM; **big-endian** (high byte first). - ⭐ `DS` — Define Storage → reserves **uninitialized** bytes in ROM. - ⭐ `BIT` — assigns symbolic name to a bit address (0–255). - ⭐ `USING n` — selects register bank n (0–3); enables `R0–R7` names. - ⭐ Segment directives: `CSEG` (ROM), `DSEG` (internal RAM 0–127), `BSEG` (bit RAM 20H–2FH), `XSEG` (external RAM). ### C51 Data Types — Key Points - ⭐ Most-used 8051 C type is **`unsigned char`** (1 byte, 0–255). - ⭐ `bit` and `sbit` — bit-level variables; stored in 20H–2FH (128 bits) or specific SFR bits. - ⭐ `sfr` — define a byte SFR; *Example:* `sfr P1 = 0x90;` - ⭐ `sfr16` — define a 16-bit SFR; *Example:* `sfr16 T0 = 0x8C;` - ⭐ In Keil C51, `double` = `float` = **4 bytes** (not 8 like standard C). - ⭐ `float` operations use the **AAH arithmetic library**; larger and slower. ### Memory Qualifiers — Key Points - ⭐ `data` (00H–7FH) — **fastest**, direct addressing, 128 B. - ⭐ `idata` (00H–FFH) — indirect addressing, 256 B, includes upper RAM. - ⭐ `bdata` (20H–2FH) — bit-addressable, 16 B / 128 bits. - ⭐ `xdata` (0–FFFFH) — **slowest**, external RAM, `MOVX @DPTR`. - ⭐ `pdata` — 1 page (256 B) of external RAM, `MOVX @R0/R1`. - ⭐ `code` — read-only program memory, `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`. ### Numerical / Quick-Recall Points - ⭐ 8051 cycle time = **12 / f_osc** seconds. - ⭐ Common crystal **11.0592 MHz** → cycle = **1.085 µs** (also gives clean 9600 baud for UART). - ⭐ `unsigned int` reload formula: **TH = TL_high; TL = TL_low** of value `(65536 − N)`. - ⭐ SFR addresses to memorize: `P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H, SCON=98H, IE=A8H, TCON=88H, TMOD=89H, PCON=87H`. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Assembler Directives & C Data Types for 8051** ✅ ---

topics.title: An Introduction to Programming 8051 in Assembly and C Language; Examples Illustrating the Use of Basic Types of Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** An Introduction to Programming 8051 in Assembly and C Language; Examples Illustrating the Use of Basic Types of Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **Intel MCS-51 (8051) family of microcontrollers**, *programming* refers to the process of authoring a sequence of machine-understandable instructions—either in **Assembly Language** (a symbolic, low-level representation of the CPU's native opcode set) or in a **high-level language such as Embedded C** (which is later compiled to the same native opcodes)—that the **8-bit CISC CPU core** of the 8051 will fetch, decode, and execute through its **Program Counter (PC)**, **Instruction Register (IR)**, and **Arithmetic-Logic Unit (ALU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The 8051 supports programming in two domains: **Assembly Language Programming (ALP)**, which gives direct control of the underlying hardware (registers, SFRs, internal RAM) and yields highly compact, deterministic code; and **C Language Programming**, which uses the **Keil µVision (C51) compiler** along with header files like `reg51.h` / `REGX51.H` to provide an abstracted, portable, and developer-friendly alternative.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the 8051 as a **newly hired, extremely obedient but absolutely literal-minded factory supervisor** who understands only a very small set of verbal commands in a specific dialect.

- **Assembly Language** is like talking to that supervisor in *short, harsh, one-word commands* like "FETCH", "ADD", "JUMP" — extremely fast to obey, but exhausting and error-prone for a human to write.
- **C Language** is like talking to the same supervisor *through a personal interpreter (the compiler)* — you give complex English-style instructions and the interpreter converts them into the supervisor's native tongue before delivery.

Both achieve the same final task, but C trades a tiny bit of speed/memory for vastly improved developer productivity and readability — exactly why every KTU laboratory experiment and most real-world firmware today is written in C, while assembly is reserved for time-critical interrupt service routines (ISRs) and bootloader code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Constant to Memorise:**
> - **Program Memory (Code ROM) Size:** 4 KB on-chip (8 KB in 8052), expandable to **64 KB** externally.
> - **Internal Data Memory (IRAM) Size:** 128 bytes (256 bytes in 8052).
> - **External Data Memory (XRAM) Size:** up to **64 KB**.
> - **Special Function Registers (SFR) area:** addresses **0x80 – 0xFF**.
> - **Number of Instructions in the 8051 ISA:** exactly **255 opcodes (111 instructions including variants)**.
> - **Reset Vector Address:** **0x0000**.

### 1.3 Why Two Languages Exist Side-by-Side in KTU Curriculum

| Aspect | Assembly (ALP) | Embedded C |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction Level | **1:1 with hardware** | High-level, hardware-aware |
| Execution Speed | **Fastest possible** | Slightly slower (machine-code overhead) |
| Code Size | **Minimum** | Typically 20–40% larger |
| Hardware Access | Direct (registers, bits) | Through SFR / sbit / sfr macros |
| Portability across 8051 variants | None | High (re-compile) |
| KTU Use Case | Exam theory, ISR code, bit-level ops | Lab programs, large projects |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an 8051 Assembly Language Program

Every KTU-style 8051 assembly program consists of a *header* (directives) and a *body* (instructions). The general structure is:

```
[Label:]   [Mnemonic]  [Operand]    ; Comment
[DIRECTIVE]            [ARGUMENT]   ; Comment
```

#### 2.1.1 Essential Assembler Directives (KTU High-Yield)

| Directive | Full Form | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ORG` | Origin | Sets the location counter (where code/data is placed) | `ORG 0000H` |
| `END` | End | Marks the end of source program | `END` |
| `EQU` | Equate | Defines a symbolic constant | `COUNT EQU 30H` |
| `DB` | Define Byte | Stores 8-bit data in code memory | `TABLE: DB 0,1,2,3` |
| `DW` | Define Word | Stores 16-bit data in code memory | `DW 1234H` |
| `DS` | Define Storage | Reserves bytes in data memory | `DS 10H` |
| `BIT` | Bit Address | Assigns a bit address to a symbol | `FLAG BIT 20H.0` |
| `DATA` | Data Address | Defines a symbol with an 8-bit data RAM address | `TEMP DATA 30H` |
| `CODE` | Code Address | Defines a symbol with a 16-bit code address | `RESET CODE 0000H` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`ORG` and `END` are NOT instructions for the 8051 CPU** — they are *assembler directives* consumed by the assembler (e.g., ASM51) at *assembly time*, not at runtime. The CPU never sees them.

### 2.2 The Five Families of 8051 Instructions (Mandatory for KTU)

The 8051 instruction set is conventionally divided into **five groups** that the examiner will expect you to list and exemplify:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** — move data between registers, memory, and external world.
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, division.
3. **Logical Instructions** — perform bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotates.
4. **Boolean (Bit) Instructions** — operate on individual bits of the bit-addressable area.
5. **Program Branching Instructions** — alter the Program Counter flow (jumps, calls, returns).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Category | Mnemonics | Example | Bytes | Machine Cycles | KTU 2024 Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Transfer | `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD` | `MOV A, #0FH` | 1–3 | 1–2 | **Very High** |
| Arithmetic | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, `DA A` | `ADD A, R1` | 1–2 | 1–4 | **Very High** |
| Logical | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP` | `ANL A, #0F0H` | 1–2 | 1–2 | **High** |
| Boolean (Bit) | `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, `ORL C`, `MOV C, bit`, `CPL C` | `SETB P1.0` | 1–2 | 1–2 | **Very High** |
| Program Branching | `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `NOP` | `SJMP HERE` | 1–3 | 2–4 | **Very High** |

### 2.4 Addressing Modes of the 8051 (Foundational)

| Mode | Syntax Example | Operation | Size in Bytes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | Constant embedded in instruction | 2 |
| **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | Register contents | 1 |
| **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | Internal RAM / SFR address | 2 |
| **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | Address held in R0/R1 | 1 |
| **Indexed (Code)** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Look-up table access | 1 |

### 2.5 Anatomy of an Embedded C Program for 8051

A KTU 2024 scheme C program for 8051 always has three logical blocks:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   1. Header Includes / SFR decls   │  (reg51.h, sfr, sbit)
│   2. Global Variables & Functions  │  (int, unsigned char, bit)
│   3. main() + Subroutines          │  (infinite loop for embedded)
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 2.5.1 Critical C Extensions Specific to 8051

| Keyword | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `sfr` | Declares a Special Function Register (8-bit port) | `sfr P1 = 0x90;` |
| `sfr16` | Declares a 16-bit SFR pair (e.g., Timer) | `sfr16 T0 = 0x8A;` |
| `sbit` | Declares a single bit-addressable I/O pin | `sbit LED = P1^0;` |
| `bit` | A 1-bit data type stored in bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH) | `bit flag;` |
| `data` | Variable in internal RAM (fastest) | `unsigned char data x;` |
| `idata` | Variable in indirect internal RAM (0x80–0xFF) | `unsigned char idata y;` |
| `pdata` | Variable in *paged* external RAM | `unsigned char pdata z;` |
| `xdata` | Variable in external RAM (up to 64 KB) | `unsigned char xdata buf[10];` |
| `code` | Constant placed in code ROM | `unsigned char code table[] = {0,1,2};` |

> [!NOTE]
> **`reg51.h` vs `REGX51.H`:**
> - `reg51.h` is the *generic* Intel 8051 header.
> - `REGX51.H` (uppercase) is Keil's enhanced header used in the *Keil µVision IDE* mandated in KTU laboratories.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of 8051 Programming |
|---|---|
| **Consumer Electronics** | Washing-machine controllers, microwave ovens, TV remotes |
| **Automotive** | Body-control modules, dashboard instrumentation, airbag sensors |
| **Industrial Automation** | PLCs, motor drivers, sensor signal conditioning |
| **Educational** | The 8051 is the *de-facto* teaching MCU globally — the "FORTRAN of microcontrollers" |
| **IoT Edge Nodes** | Legacy sensor hubs running low-power 8051 cores (e.g., Texas Instruments CC2530) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides *exhaustive* worked examples. Every state change of every register is tabulated. **No step is skipped.** This is the level of detail expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

### 3.1 Example 1 — Addition of Two Numbers (Assembly)

**Problem Statement:** Add the contents of internal RAM locations `30H` and `31H`. Store the sum in `32H` and the carry flag status in `33H`.

**Assembly Listing:**

```
        ORG     0000H           ; Program starts at reset vector
        MOV     DPTR, #4000H    ; (Optional) DPTR setup — explained later
        MOV     A, 30H          ; A <- [30H]      [Direct addressing, 2 bytes]
        ADD     A, 31H          ; A <- A + [31H]  [Adds with implicit carry input ignored first time]
        MOV     32H, A          ; Store sum in 32H
        MOV     33H, C          ; Store Carry flag in 33H (bit-addressable)
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Infinite loop (software halt)
        END                     ; End of source
```

#### 3.1.1 State-by-State Trace (Register Walkthrough)

Let the initial contents be `[30H] = 45H` and `[31H] = 6EH`. We trace register `A`, `PSW` flags, and the destination cells:

| Step | Instruction Executed | A (Accumulator) | PSW.CY | PSW.AC | [32H] | [33H] | Tally |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | `45H` | 0 | 0 | `xx` | `xx` | Direct read |
| 2 | `ADD A, 31H` | `B3H` | 0 | 1 | `xx` | `xx` | `45H + 6EH = B3H`, no carry-out, AC=1 from low-nibble overflow |
| 3 | `MOV 32H, A` | `B3H` | 0 | 1 | `B3H` | `xx` | Sum stored |
| 4 | `MOV 33H, C` | `B3H` | 0 | 1 | `B3H` | `00H` | CY=0 stored as 0 |
| 5 | `SJMP HERE` | `B3H` | 0 | 1 | `B3H` | `00H` | PC stuck at HERE |

#### 3.1.2 Mathematical Justification (Decimal Cross-Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{[30H]} &= \texttt{0x45} = 4 \times 16 + 5 = 69_{10} \\
\text{[31H]} &= \texttt{0x6E} = 6 \times 16 + 14 = 110_{10} \\
\text{Sum}   &= 69 + 110 = 179_{10} \\
179_{10} \div 16 &= 11 \text{ remainder } 3 \\
179_{10}          &= 11 \times 16 + 3 = \texttt{0xB3} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The result fits in 8 bits (179 < 255), so the **CY flag correctly remains 0** and the sum `B3H` is stored in `32H`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> If the question says *"store the carry as well"*, you **must** use `MOV 33H, C`. Using `MOV 33H, A` would be a serious mistake — it would store the *sum*, not the *carry flag status*.

### 3.2 Example 2 — Multiplication of Two 8-bit Numbers (Assembly)

**Problem Statement:** Multiply two unsigned numbers in `R0` and `R1`. The result (16-bit) is placed in `R2` (low) and `R3` (high).

**Assembly Listing:**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, R0          ; A <- R0
        MOV     B, R1          ; B <- R1
        MUL     AB             ; B:A <- A x B  (16-bit result)
        MOV     R2, A          ; R2 <- low byte
        MOV     R3, B          ; R3 <- high byte
        END
```

#### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Trace

Let `R0 = 25H` and `R1 = 12H`. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &= 0x25 = 37_{10} \\
R1 &= 0x12 = 18_{10} \\
R0 \times R1 &= 37 \times 18 = 666_{10} \\
666_{10} \div 256 &= 2 \text{ remainder } 154 \\
666_{10}          &= 2 \times 256 + 154 = 0x029A
\end{aligned}
$$

So after `MUL AB`, `B = 02H` and `A = 9AH`. The overflow flag `OV` is set **only if the high byte is non-zero** (i.e., product > 255). Here `OV = 1` and `CY = 0` always for `MUL AB`.

| Step | Instruction | A | B | R2 | R3 | CY | OV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, R0` | `25H` | `xx` | `xx` | `xx` | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | `MOV B, R1` | `25H` | `12H` | `xx` | `xx` | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | `MUL AB` | `9AH` | `02H` | `xx` | `xx` | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | `MOV R2, A` | `9AH` | `02H` | `9AH` | `xx` | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | `MOV R3, B` | `9AH` | `02H` | `9AH` | `02H` | 0 | 1 |

### 3.3 Example 3 — Logical Operations (Assembly)

**Problem Statement:** Clear the upper nibble of port `P1` and set the lower nibble to the value of accumulator `A`.

**Assembly Listing:**

```
        ANL     P1, #0FH       ; Mask upper nibble of P1 to 0, keep lower
        ORL     P1, A          ; OR P1 with A (only lower nibble of A matters if upper cleared earlier)
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

> [!TIP]
> The student must notice that `ORL P1, A` does **not** zero the upper nibble of P1 — it only sets bits where A has 1s. Therefore, the `ANL` *must* precede the `ORL` to clear the upper nibble first. This is a classic KTU trick question.

### 3.4 Example 4 — Bit Manipulation: Toggling P1.0 Continuously (Assembly)

```
        ORG     0000H
BACK:   CPL     P1.0           ; Complement P1.0
        ACALL   DELAY          ; 2-byte call to a delay routine
        SJMP    BACK           ; Loop forever

DELAY:  MOV     R2, #0FFH      ; 1-byte load
D1:     MOV     R3, #0FFH
D2:     DJNZ    R3, D2         ; Inner: 255 x 255 cycles
        DJNZ    R2, D1
        RET
        END
```

**Delay Calculation (KTU 2024 favourite):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{outer}} &= 255 \times T_{\text{inner}} \\
T_{\text{inner}}  &= (1_{\text{MOV}} + 1_{\text{setup}}) + 255 \times (1_{\text{DJNZ}} + 1_{\text{DJNZ inner}} - \text{fetch}) \\
                 &= 2 + 255 \times 2 = 512 \text{ machine cycles} \\
T_{\text{total}}  &= 1 \times 255 \times 512 \times T_{osc}/12 \\
\text{For 11.0592 MHz: } T_{osc}/12 &= 1.085\;\mu s \\
T_{\text{total}}  &= 255 \times 512 \times 1.085\;\mu s \approx 141.6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Example 5 — Embedded C Equivalent of Example 1 (Addition)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char data xdata_buffer _at_ 0x30;  // Optional absolute addressing

void main(void) {
    unsigned char a, b, sum;
    unsigned char carry;

    a = 0x45;          // Simulates content of internal RAM 30H
    b = 0x6E;          // Simulates content of internal RAM 31H
    sum = a + b;       // Compiler generates ADD / ADDC

    /* Manually extract carry — Keil C does NOT set CY portably; use inline asm */
    if (sum < a) {     // Wrap-around check for unsigned char
        carry = 1;
    } else {
        carry = 0;
    }

    P1 = sum;          // Display result on Port 1 LEDs
    P2 = carry;        // Display carry on Port 2

    while(1);          // Embedded infinite halt
}
```

#### 3.5.1 Mapping of C Constructs to 8051 Machine Code (KTU Mapping Table)

| C Statement | Generated Assembly (Typical) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `unsigned char a;` | Reserves 1 byte in `DATA` segment | Address allocated by Keil linker |
| `a = 0x45;` | `MOV direct, #45H` | Direct addressing |
| `sum = a + b;` | `MOV A, R_a` / `ADD A, R_b` / `MOV R_sum, A` | 3 machine instructions |
| `P1 = sum;` | `MOV 90H, R_sum` | SFR direct write |
| `while(1);` | `HERE: SJMP HERE` | Compiler converts to self-loop |

### 3.6 Example 6 — Bit Manipulation in C (Toggling P1.0)

```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit LED = P1^0;          // Single-bit declaration for P1.0

void delay(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 255; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 255; j++);
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        LED = ~LED;       // Compile to CPL P1.0
        delay();
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **`sbit LED = P1^0;` vs `sbit LED = P1^7;` confusion:** The `^` is a *bit-index operator* specific to Keil C51, **not** a pointer dereference or XOR. It tells the compiler "give me bit 0 of SFR P1". The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you write `P1.0` (assembly style) in C code.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Reference of C ↔ Assembly

| Concept | Assembly (ALP) | Embedded C (C51) | SFR / RAM Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| Port 1 | `P1` | `P1` or `sfr P1 = 0x90;` | `0x90` |
| Pin P1.0 | `P1.0` | `sbit P1_0 = P1^0;` | `0x90` (bit 0) |
| Accumulator | `A` | direct register access via `ACC` | `0xE0` |
| Stack Pointer | `SP` | `SP` | `0x81` |
| B Register | `B` | `B` | `0xF0` |
| Timer 0 | `T0`, `TH0`, `TL0` | `T0`, `TH0`, `TL0` | `0x8A`, `0x8C`, `0x8A` |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Anatomy of an 8051 Assembly Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: ORG 0000H] --> B[Initialization Block]
    B --> B1[Set Stack Pointer SP]
    B --> B2[Clear/Configure SFRs]
    B1 --> C[Main Processing Block]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D{Decision / Loop?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Subroutine Call ACALL/LCALL]
    D -- No --> F[Continue Linear Flow]
    E --> G[Subroutine RET returns to main]
    G --> D
    F --> H[End Block SJMP HERE / END]
    H --> I[Program Counter Halted]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Anatomy of an 8051 C Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preprocessor Includes reg51.h] --> B[SFR and SBIT Declarations]
    B --> C[Global Variables data/idata/xdata]
    C --> D[Function Prototypes]
    D --> E[main function entry]
    E --> F[Hardware Initialization]
    F --> G[Infinite Super Loop while1]
    G --> H[Read Inputs from Ports]
    H --> I[Process Data using ALU]
    I --> J[Write Outputs to Ports]
    J --> G
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Five Families of 8051 Instructions

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[8051 Instruction Set Architecture] --> A[Data Transfer]
    root --> B[Arithmetic]
    root --> C[Logical]
    root --> D[Boolean Bit Manipulation]
    root --> E[Program Branching]

    A --> A1[MOV family]
    A --> A2[PUSH POP]
    A --> A3[XCH XCHD]
    A --> A4[MOVC MOVX]

    B --> B1[ADD ADDC SUBB]
    B --> B2[INC DEC]
    B --> B3[MUL DIV]
    B --> B4[DA A Decimal Adjust]

    C --> C1[ANL ORL XRL]
    C --> C2[CLR CPL]
    C --> C3[RL RLC RR RRC SWAP]

    D --> D1[SETB CLR bit]
    D --> D2[ANL C ORL C MOV C]
    D --> D3[Conditional JB JNB JBC]

    E --> E1[Unconditional LJMP AJMP SJMP JMP]
    E --> E2[Conditional JZ JNZ JC JNC]
    E --> E3[Compare and Jump CJNE DJNZ]
    E --> E4[Subroutines ACALL LCALL RET RETI]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Address Spaces of the 8051

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CODE[Code Memory 64 KB]
        C0[0000H Reset Vector]
        C1[0003H External Int 0]
        C2[000BH Timer 0 Overflow]
        C3[0013H External Int 1]
        C4[001BH Timer 1 Overflow]
        C5[0023H Serial Port]
        C6[0030H onwards Main Program]
    end

    subgraph IDATA[Internal Data RAM 128 B]
        I0[00H to 1FH: 4 Banks x 8 Reg R0-R7]
        I1[20H to 2FH: Bit Addressable 16 B]
        I2[30H to 7FH: General Purpose]
    end

    subgraph SFR[SFR Area 80H to FFH]
        S0[80H: P0]
        S1[90H: P1]
        S2[A0H: P2]
        S3[B0H: P3]
        S4[81H: SP]
        S5[82H/83H: DPL/DPH]
        S6[D0H: PSW]
        S7[E0H: ACC]
    end

    subgraph XDATA[External Data RAM 64 KB]
        X0[0000H to FFFFH accessed via MOVX]
    end
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Execution Flow of an Assembly Instruction (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant MEM as Code Memory
    participant IR as Instruction Register
    participant DEC as Instruction Decoder
    participant ALU as Arithmetic Logic Unit
    participant REG as Registers SFRs RAM

    PC->>MEM: Address of next instruction
    MEM-->>IR: Opcode byte fetched
    IR->>DEC: Decode opcode
    DEC->>MEM: Request operand bytes (if any)
    MEM-->>DEC: Operand bytes
    DEC->>ALU: Activate appropriate operation
    ALU->>REG: Read source operands
    REG-->>ALU: Operand values
    ALU->>REG: Write back result
    ALU->>PC: Increment or modify PC
    Note over PC,REG: One machine cycle typically = 12 oscillator periods
```
