## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
*List any three assembler directives used in 8051 programming. For each, state its function and write one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`ORG` (Origin) — 1 mark**
   Function: Specifies the starting address of the program or data block in program memory.
   Example: `ORG 0000H`  (places the next instruction at the reset vector)

2. **`END` — 1 mark**
   Function: Marks the termination of the assembly source program. The assembler ignores anything written after `END`.
   Example: `END`

3. **`DB` (Define Byte) — 1 mark**
   Function: Initialises 8-bit constant data in code memory, typically used to build look-up tables.
   Example: `SQUARES: DB 00H, 01H, 04H, 09H`

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between `MOVX` and `MOVC` instructions of 8051 with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `MOVC` (1.5 marks) | `MOVX` (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Move Code byte | Move External memory byte |
| Accesses | Program memory (code ROM) | External data memory (XRAM) |
| Addressing | Only via `@A + DPTR` or `@A + PC` | Only via `@R0`, `@R1`, or `@DPTR` |
| Used for | Look-up tables in ROM | Reading/writing external RAM/I/O ports |
| Example | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) (1 × 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** *Write an 8051 assembly language program to find the sum of the first ten natural numbers stored in internal RAM starting from location `30H`. Store the 16-bit result in locations `40H` (low byte) and `41H` (high byte).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the structure of an 8051 Embedded C program with a suitable example that toggles all bits of Port 1 continuously with a delay.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Algorithm (1 mark):**
1. Initialise a counter `R0` from 00H to 09H (ten iterations).
2. For each iteration, load the value into `A` and add to a 16-bit accumulator.
3. Store the 16-bit sum in `40H` (low) and `41H` (high).

**Assembly Program (5 marks):**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #00H          ; Counter = 0
        MOV     R1, #30H          ; Pointer to source array
        MOV     40H, #00H         ; Low byte of sum = 0
        MOV     41H, #00H         ; High byte of sum = 0
        CLR     C                 ; Clear carry before first add

LOOP:   MOV     A, @R1            ; A <- [R1]
        ADDC    A, 40H            ; A <- A + 40H + C
        MOV     40H, A            ; Update low byte
        JNC     SKIP              ; If no carry, skip high-byte increment
        INC     41H               ; Carry in, increment high byte
SKIP:   INC     R1                ; Point to next source
        INC     R0                ; Increment counter
        CJNE    R0, #0AH, LOOP    ; Loop 10 times
DONE:   SJMP    DONE              ; Halt
        END
```

**Trace (1 mark):** For input `{01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 09, 0A}` (i.e., 1+2+…+10):

$$
1+2+3+\dots+10 = \frac{10 \times 11}{2} = 55_{10} = 0x0037
$$

So `[40H] = 37H` and `[41H] = 00H`. The carry into `41H` is never triggered in this case because the running sum never exceeds 255. The use of `ADDC` and the explicit `JNC SKIP` properly handles the general case (e.g., summing larger numbers where the cumulative sum exceeds 255).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Structure Explanation (3 marks):**

A KTU-style 8051 C program has three logical blocks:

1. **Header / SFR declaration block** — includes `reg51.h` and explicitly declares any `sfr`, `sfr16`, or `sbit` symbols not provided in the header.
2. **Global variables & function prototypes block** — declares module-level state and the signatures of subroutines.
3. **`main()` and subroutine block** — contains the hardware initialisation, an *infinite* `while(1)` super-loop, and any delay / ISR functions.

**Embedded C Program (4 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   /* Tuned for 11.0592 MHz crystal */
}

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;          /* Initialise Port 1 to all zero */
    while(1) {
        P1 = ~P1;       /* Toggle all 8 bits of Port 1 */
        delay_ms(500);  /* 500 ms half-period => ~1 Hz blink */
    }
}
```

**Mark-allocating remarks (included for completeness):** Compiling the loop in Keil µVision produces `CPL 90H` (1 cycle) inside an outer `SJMP`-style infinite branch, with the inner delay loop expanding to roughly **255 × 1275 = 325,125 NOPs** that yield the specified 500 ms on a 11.0592 MHz crystal.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** *Explain the five major groups of 8051 instructions with at least one example for each group.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Write an 8051 assembly program to multiply two 8-bit numbers stored in RAM locations `30H` and `31H`. Store the 16-bit result in `32H` (low) and `33H` (high). Also write the equivalent C program.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| # | Group (1.2 marks each) | Example Instruction (0.2 mark) | Short Note (extra 0.2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Transfer Instructions** | `MOV A, #25H` | Move data between registers, RAM, SFRs |
| 2 | **Arithmetic Instructions** | `ADD A, R1` | Performs 8-bit arithmetic, sets CY/AC/OV |
| 3 | **Logical Instructions** | `ANL A, #0FH` | Bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotates, swap |
| 4 | **Boolean (Bit) Instructions** | `SETB P1.0` | Operate on individual bits of 20H–2FH and bit-addressable SFRs |
| 5 | **Program Branching Instructions** | `SJMP BACK` | Alter PC flow: jumps, conditional branches, calls, returns |

Total 7 marks achievable through structured table + a sentence or two for each group.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Assembly Program (3.5 marks):**

```
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A, 30H          ; A <- [30H]
        MOV     B, 31H          ; B <- [31H]
        MUL     AB              ; B:A = A * B
        MOV     32H, A          ; Low byte
        MOV     33H, B          ; High byte
        END
```

**C Equivalent (3.5 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void) {
    unsigned char a, b;
    unsigned int product;

    a = 0x25;        /* Simulated content of 30H */
    b = 0x12;        /* Simulated content of 31H */

    product = (unsigned int)a * (unsigned int)b;   /* Promotes to 16-bit */
    P1 = (unsigned char)product;        /* Low byte on P1 */
    P2 = (unsigned char)(product >> 8); /* High byte on P2 */

    while(1);
}
```

**Critical Mapping Note (additional credit):** The Keil C51 compiler recognises the `unsigned int` cast of two 8-bit `unsigned char`s and generates exactly the same `MUL AB` instruction in machine code — proving the previous point that *C compiles to native 8051 opcodes*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the `#` symbol.** In assembly, `MOV A, #25H` means *immediate* 25H. Omitting `#` changes it to *direct addressing* and tries to read from RAM address 25H — a completely different operation worth **2 marks** in any sub-part.
> 2. **Do not omit `ORG 0000H`.** The assembler needs the origin directive to know where to place the first instruction. Without it, Keil throws error **A51: missing or improper directive**.
> 3. **In C, do not use `P1.0` syntax.** The C51 bit-index operator is **`P1^0`**, *not* `P1.0`. Writing `P1.0` causes *syntax error C141*.
> 4. **Always end the assembly program with `END`.** Forgetting `END` is the most common reason for assembler to misbehave in KTU lab exams.
> 5. **For `MUL AB`, do not swap B and A carelessly.** `MUL AB` means `A × B → B:A`. The first operand is A, the second is B. Marks are routinely deducted for writing `MUL BA`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The 8051 supports **two programming paradigms**: Assembly (ALP) and Embedded C (C51).
- ✅ The 8051 ISA has **five instruction families**: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, and Program Branching.
- ✅ Every assembly program must begin with **`ORG 0000H`** and terminate with **`END`**.
- ✅ The **number sign `#` denotes immediate addressing** — its absence means *direct* addressing.
- ✅ `MUL AB` stores the 16-bit product in **`B:A`** (high:low). `DIV AB` stores quotient in `A` and remainder in `B`.
- ✅ The header file **`reg51.h`** (or `REGX51.H` in Keil) provides SFR declarations for the standard 8051.
- ✅ For single-bit access in C, use **`sbit`** and the **`^`** index operator: `sbit LED = P1^0;`
- ✅ The C51 storage classes — **`data`, `idata`, `pdata`, `xdata`, `code`** — control which physical memory segment a variable lives in.
- ✅ An embedded C `main()` function should *always* end in an **infinite `while(1)` loop** because the program counter has nowhere meaningful to go afterwards.
- ✅ The **reset vector is `0x0000`**; the first executable instruction is therefore at `ORG 0000H`.
- ✅ Carry flag is set by `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL` (cleared), and `DIV` (cleared). It is *not* affected by `INC`.
- ✅ The **`AC`** (auxiliary carry) flag is set if there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 — used exclusively for BCD arithmetic with `DA A`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming the Ports of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Programming the Ports of 8051**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **8051 microcontroller architecture**, **Programming the Ports** refers to the systematic process of configuring and manipulating the four bidirectional **8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) through their dedicated **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**. Each port is a group of 8 pins that can be individually or collectively written to (output) or read from (input) using a well-defined instruction set of the 8051.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFR Addresses of the 8051 Ports (Must Memorize)**
> - **P0** → SFR Address `0x80` (Bit-addressable)
> - **P1** → SFR Address `0x90` (Bit-addressable)
> - **P2** → SFR Address `0xA0` (Bit-addressable)
> - **P3** → SFR Address `0xB0` (Bit-addressable)

The 8051 contains **32 I/O lines** organized as **4 ports × 8 pins**, all of which can be programmed using simple **MOV**, **SETB**, **CLR**, and **ANL/ORL/XRL** instructions on the corresponding SFR.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Light Switch Panel)

Imagine the 8051 microcontroller as the **electrical control panel of a smart house**:

- Each **port** is a **switchboard panel** containing 8 toggle switches (the 8 pins).
- The **SFR** (Special Function Register) is the **label on the panel** that tells you which switch controls which device.
- **Writing `1` to a pin** = flipping the switch **UP** (e.g., turning ON an LED connected to that pin).
- **Writing `0` to a pin** = flipping the switch **DOWN** (e.g., turning OFF the LED).
- **Reading the port** = checking whether the switch is currently UP or DOWN, OR checking whether an external sensor (like a doorbell button) is pressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why must we program ports?**
> Without programming, the 8051 has no way to "talk" to the outside world. LEDs won't blink, motors won't spin, sensors can't be read. The ports are the **only physical interface** between the CPU's brain and the real world.

---

## 1.3 The Four Ports at a Glance

| Port | Pins (Px.7 → Px.0) | Internal Pull-ups | Special Dual Role | Typical Use |
|------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **P0** | 39–32 | ❌ None (Open Drain) | **AD0–AD7** (Multiplexed Address/Data Bus) | External memory interface, generic I/O |
| **P1** | 1–8 | ✅ Yes (≈ 30 kΩ) | None (Pure I/O) | Simple I/O (LEDs, switches) |
| **P2** | 21–28 | ✅ Yes | **A8–A15** (High Address Byte) | External memory interface, generic I/O |
| **P3** | 10–17 | ✅ Yes | **Alternate Functions** (UART, Interrupts, Timers, RD, WR) | Serial comm., Timers, Interrupts, generic I/O |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The internal pull-up resistors of P1, P2, P3 in the standard 8051 are approximately **~30 kΩ** (weak pull-ups), so they can only source a small current (~ few hundred microamps). For higher current loads, **external drivers** (transistors, ULN2003, etc.) are required.

---

## 1.4 Why Bit-Addressability Matters

A unique feature of all four 8051 ports is that they are **bit-addressable** — meaning you can manipulate any single pin (e.g., P1.3) without disturbing the other 7 pins. This is unlike many other microcontrollers where you must read-modify-write the whole port.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Address Range of Ports (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **P0** bits → addresses `0x80` to `0x87` (P0.0 = 0x80, ..., P0.7 = 0x87)
> - **P1** bits → addresses `0x90` to `0x97`
> - **P2** bits → addresses `0xA0` to `0xA7`
> - **P3** bits → addresses `0xB0` to `0xB7`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single 8051 Port Pin

Every pin of the 8051 port has the following internal structure:

1. **A D-type Latch (1-bit memory cell)** — stores the value written to the pin.
2. **An Output Driver FET** — drives the pin HIGH or LOW externally.
3. **An Input Buffer** — used to read the actual physical state of the external pin.
4. **(For P1, P2, P3) A Weak Internal Pull-up Resistor** — keeps the pin HIGH when no external device is pulling it down.

### The "Two Reads" Problem (Read Pin vs Read Latch)

A critical concept that is **frequently asked in KTU exams**:

- **Read Pin (Read from Physical Pin):** The instruction reads the *actual voltage* on the external pin. Triggered by instructions like `MOV A, P1`.
- **Read Latch (Read from Internal Flip-Flop):** The instruction reads the *value previously written* to the port's internal latch. Triggered by **read-modify-write** instructions like `ANL P1, A`, `ORL P1, A`, `INC P1`, `CPL P1.3`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read-Modify-Write Hazard:** When instructions like `ANL P1, #0FH` execute, the CPU reads the *latch* (not the pin) to perform the AND. If an external switch is currently holding P1.4 LOW, but the latch has P1.4 = 1, the AND operation uses the latch value (1), and the result written back to the latch is 0 — *overwriting* the external switch state. This is a classic KTU exam trap.

---

## 2.2 The Three SFR Instructions Used to Program Ports

### A. The `MOV` Instruction (Atomic Write/Read)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| `MOV P1, A` | Write entire byte of A → P1 | Output 0x55 to P1 |
| `MOV A, P1` | Read entire byte of P1 → A | Read switch states |
| `MOV P1, #0FFH` | Write immediate value to P1 | Initialize P1 HIGH |
| `MOV P1.5, C` | Write single bit (Carry) to P1.5 | Toggle one LED using carry |
| `MOV C, P1.5` | Read single bit P1.5 into Carry | Read one switch |

### B. The `SETB` and `CLR` Instructions (Bit Manipulation)

```
SETB P1.0      ; Set P1.0 = 1 (LED ON)
CLR  P1.0      ; Clear P1.0 = 0 (LED OFF)
```

These are **single-cycle**, **single-byte** instructions — extremely efficient for bit-level port programming.

### C. The Boolean Logic Instructions (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` on Ports)

| Instruction | Function | Use Case |
|-------------|----------|----------|
| `ANL P1, #0F0H` | AND port with mask | Clear lower nibble of P1 |
| `ORL P1, #0FH` | OR port with mask | Set lower nibble of P1 |
| `XRL P1, #0FFH` | XOR port with mask | Toggle all bits of P1 |
| `CPL P1.3` | Complement single bit | Toggle one LED without affecting others |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `CPL` is special:** It is a *true toggle* — it reads the latch, flips that bit, and writes back. It does **not** require the programmer to know the current state.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Symbol | Formula / Value | Notes |
|------------------|-----------------|-------|
| Total I/O pins on 8051 | $N_{IO} = 4 \times 8 = 32$ | Four 8-bit ports |
| SFR address of P0 | `0x80` | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address of P1 | `0x90` | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address of P2 | `0xA0` | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address of P3 | `0xB0` | Bit-addressable |
| Bit n of Port x (address) | $\text{Addr}(P_x.n) = \text{Base}(P_x) + n$ | E.g., P1.3 = 0x90 + 3 = 0x93 |
| Ports requiring external pull-ups | **Only P0** | When used as generic output |
| Ports with alternate functions | **P3** (and **P0**, **P2** if EA=0/1 with ext. mem) | UART, Timers, INT, RD, WR |
| Internal pull-up resistance | $R_{pullup} \approx 30 \text{ k}\Omega$ | P1, P2, P3 only |
| Logic HIGH voltage (TTL) | $V_{IH} \ge 2.0 \text{ V}$ | Standard 8051 (not 8052/LPC) |
| Logic LOW voltage (TTL) | $V_{IL} \le 0.8 \text{ V}$ | Standard 8051 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Programming 8051 ports is the foundation of every embedded system built on this controller:

- **Traffic light controllers** — use P1 to drive LEDs via transistors.
- **Home automation** — use P3 for relay control (using the alternate-function-free pins of P3 not used by UART).
- **Industrial control panels** — use P0 with external latches (74HC573) to drive 7-segment displays.
- **Robotics** — use P1 and P2 for motor driver inputs (L293D, L298N).
- **Sensor interfacing** — read temperature, light, IR sensors via port pins configured as input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even in modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, the concept of "GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) port programming" is directly inherited from the 8051 model. Mastering this topic in 8051 gives you a permanent foundation for **all microcontroller families**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bit Address of any Port Pin

### The General Formula

Every port is an SFR located at a base address. The bit-addressable region for each port occupies **8 consecutive byte addresses** below the SFR byte address. Specifically:

$$
\text{Bit address of } P_x.n \;=\; \text{Base SFR of } P_x \;+\; n
$$

where $n \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 7\}$ and $x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

### Worked Example: Find the bit address of P2.5

**Step 1:** Identify the base SFR address of P2.

$$
\text{Base}(P2) = \text{0xA0} \;=\; 1010\,0000_2
$$

**Step 2:** Add the bit index $n = 5$.

$$
\text{Addr}(P2.5) = \text{0xA0} + 5
$$

$$
\text{Addr}(P2.5) = 0xA5
$$

**Step 3:** Verify by binary addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{0xA0} &= 1010\,0000_2 \\
+ \quad 5 &= 0000\,0101_2 \\
\hline
\text{Result} &= 1010\,0101_2 \;=\; \text{0xA5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $P2.5$ is bit-addressable at memory location `0xA5`, which means it can be directly used as a bit operand in any 8051 bit instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Verification Table (KTU Favorite):**

| Pin | Bit Address | Pin | Bit Address |
|-----|-------------|-----|-------------|
| P0.0 | 0x80 | P2.0 | 0xA0 |
| P0.1 | 0x81 | P2.1 | 0xA1 |
| P0.2 | 0x82 | P2.2 | 0xA2 |
| P0.3 | 0x83 | P2.3 | 0xA3 |
| P0.4 | 0x84 | P2.4 | 0xA4 |
| P0.5 | 0x85 | P2.5 | 0xA5 |
| P0.6 | 0x86 | P2.6 | 0xA6 |
| P0.7 | 0x87 | P2.7 | 0xA7 |
| P1.0 | 0x90 | P3.0 | 0xB0 |
| P1.1 | 0x91 | P3.1 | 0xB1 |
| P1.2 | 0x92 | P3.2 | 0xB2 |
| P1.3 | 0x93 | P3.3 | 0xB3 |
| P1.4 | 0x94 | P3.4 | 0xB4 |
| P1.5 | 0x95 | P3.5 | 0xB5 |
| P1.6 | 0x96 | P3.6 | 0xB6 |
| P1.7 | 0x97 | P3.7 | 0xB7 |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Reading a Switch and Lighting an LED

**Hardware Setup:**
- An active-LOW push-button switch is connected to **P1.7** (one terminal to Vcc via 10 kΩ pull-up, other terminal to GND).
- An LED is connected to **P1.0** (anode via 330 Ω to P1.0, cathode to GND).

**Goal:** When the switch is pressed, the LED should turn ON.

### Assembly Program

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Program starts at code address 0
        MOV     P1, #0FFH       ; Step 1: Configure all P1 pins as INPUT (write 1s)
                                ;         This disables output driver, enables input buffer
LOOP:   MOV     A, P1           ; Step 2: Read the entire P1 port into Accumulator
        ANL     A, #80H         ; Step 3: Mask — keep only bit 7 (1000 0000)
                                ;         If switch is open: bit 7 = 1 (A = 80H)
                                ;         If switch is pressed: bit 7 = 0 (A = 00H)
        JZ      LED_ON          ; Step 4: If A = 0 (switch pressed), jump to LED_ON
        CLR     P1.0            ; Step 5: Switch not pressed → LED OFF
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Step 6: Go back and read switch again
LED_ON: SETB    P1.0            ; Step 7: Switch pressed → LED ON
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Step 8: Go back and read switch again
        END                     ; End of program
```

### Byte-Level State Trace

Let us trace what happens at each step when the switch is **NOT pressed** (open circuit, so P1.7 = 1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After `MOV P1, \#0FFH`} &: \quad P1_{\text{latch}} = 1111\,1111_2 \\
\text{After `MOV A, P1`} &: \quad A = 1111\,1111_2 = \text{0xFF} \\
\text{After `ANL A, \#80H`} &: \quad A = 1111\,1111_2 \,\text{AND}\, 1000\,0000_2 = 1000\,0000_2 = \text{0x80} \\
\text{After `JZ LED_ON`} &: \quad \text{Zero flag} = 0 \quad (\text{since } A \ne 0) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Falls through to `CLR P1.0`} \\
\text{After `CLR P1.0`} &: \quad P1.0 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{LED OFF} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Now trace when the switch **IS pressed** (P1.7 shorted to GND, so P1.7 = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After `MOV A, P1`} &: \quad A = 0111\,1111_2 = \text{0x7F} \\
\text{After `ANL A, \#80H`} &: \quad A = 0111\,1111_2 \,\text{AND}\, 1000\,0000_2 = 0000\,0000_2 = \text{0x00} \\
\text{After `JZ LED_ON`} &: \quad \text{Zero flag} = 1 \quad (\text{since } A = 0) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Jumps to `SETB P1.0`} \\
\text{After `SETB P1.0`} &: \quad P1.0 = 1 \Rightarrow \text{LED ON} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight — Why write `0xFF` to a port to make it an input?**
> In the 8051, any port pin configured as an *input* must have its **latch set to 1**. This turns OFF the lower FET (output driver to GND) and turns ON the upper FET weakly, allowing the pin to be "released" to be driven externally. This is the opposite of many modern microcontrollers where you explicitly set a `DDR` (Data Direction Register).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Categories for Port Programming

| Category | Instructions | Operates On | Read Source | Write Destination | Typical Use |
|----------|--------------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Byte write** | `MOV Px, A` / `MOV Px, #data` | Whole port | — | Port latch | Output a full byte pattern |
| **Byte read** | `MOV A, Px` | Whole port | **Pin** | Accumulator | Read external switches/sensors |
| **Bit write** | `SETB Px.n` / `CLR Px.n` | Single pin | — | Port latch bit | Control one LED/relay |
| **Bit read** | `MOV C, Px.n` | Single pin | **Pin** | Carry flag | Test one switch |
| **Read-Modify-Write (byte)** | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `INC`, `DEC`, `CPL`, `DJNZ` on `Px` | Whole port | **Latch** | Port latch | Manipulate specific bits without disturbing others |
| **Read-Modify-Write (bit)** | `CPL Px.n` | Single pin | Latch | Latch | Toggle one output pin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Read-Modify-Write instruction rule:** Any instruction of the form `<op> Px` (where `<op>` is ANL, ORL, XRL, INC, DEC, CPL, DJNZ) reads from the **internal latch**, not the external pin. This is the source of the famous **"Read-Modify-Write problem"** in 8051 textbooks.

---

## 3.4 Toggle a Single LED Using `CPL` — Exhaustive Trace

**Goal:** Make P1.5 toggle its state every time the program loops.

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        CLR     P1.5            ; Initial state: P1.5 = 0 (LED OFF)
LOOP:   CPL     P1.5            ; Toggle P1.5
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Call delay subroutine
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Repeat forever
```

### State Trace (assuming initial P1.5 = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & P1.5_{\text{before}} = 0 \xrightarrow{\text{CPL}} P1.5_{\text{after}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{LED ON} \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & P1.5_{\text{before}} = 1 \xrightarrow{\text{CPL}} P1.5_{\text{after}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{LED OFF} \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & P1.5_{\text{before}} = 0 \xrightarrow{\text{CPL}} P1.5_{\text{after}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{LED ON} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The output waveform on P1.5 is therefore a **square wave** with period equal to twice the delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **The `CPL` instruction is the only single-instruction toggle available in the 8051** — extremely powerful for generating square waves, PWM, or blink patterns without needing a timer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Architecture of a Single 8051 Port Pin (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BUS["Internal CPU Data Bus"]
        D0["D0 Internal Bus Line"]
        D1["D1 Internal Bus Line"]
    end

    LATCH["D-Type Latch\n(1-bit memory cell)"]
    OUTDRV["Output Driver FET Pair\n(Internal Pull-up)"]
    INBUF["Input Read Buffer\n(Read Pin only)"]
    PIN["External Pin\nP1.0"]

    D0 --> LATCH
    D1 --> LATCH
    LATCH -- "Drives output stage" --> OUTDRV
    OUTDRV -- "Physical voltage" --> PIN
    PIN -- "External voltage level" --> INBUF
    INBUF -- "Returns to bus on 'Read Pin'" --> D0
    INBUF -- "Returns to bus on 'Read Pin'" --> D1

    PIN2["External Device\n(LED/Switch)"] -- "Drives pin" --> PIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The LATCH is read during **Read-Modify-Write** instructions (ANL, ORL, CPL, etc.). The PIN (via the Input Buffer) is read during **simple read** instructions (MOV A, Px). This is the architectural basis of the Read-Modify-Write hazard.

---

## 4.2 Programming Flow for "Read Switch → Control LED"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Power-On Reset\nPC = 0000H"] --> B["MOV P1, #0FFH\n(Configure P1 as Input)"]
    B --> C["MOV A, P1\n(Read P1 into A)"]
    C --> D["ANL A, #80H\n(Mask bit 7 only)"]
    D --> E{"Is A = 0?\n(Switch pressed?)"}
    E -- "Yes (A=0)" --> F["SETB P1.0\n(LED ON)"]
    E -- "No (A=80H)" --> G["CLR P1.0\n(LED OFF)"]
    F --> H["SJMP LOOP\n(Loop back)"]
    G --> H
    H --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Four 8051 Ports

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU8051["8051 CPU Core"]
        ALU["ALU / Accumulator"]
        REG["Internal Registers\n(R0-R7, B, DPTR, PC)"]
    end

    subgraph SFR["Special Function Register Area (80H-FFH)"]
        P0REG["P0 SFR\nAddr 80H\n(8-bit latch)"]
        P1REG["P1 SFR\nAddr 90H\n(8-bit latch)"]
        P2REG["P2 SFR\nAddr A0H\n(8-bit latch)"]
        P3REG["P3 SFR\nAddr B0H\n(8-bit latch)"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["Physical Pins (32 I/O Lines)"]
        P0PINS["P0.0 to P0.7\nPins 32-39\nNo internal pull-up"]
        P1PINS["P1.0 to P1.7\nPins 1-8\nInternal pull-up"]
        P2PINS["P2.0 to P2.7\nPins 21-28\nInternal pull-up"]
        P3PINS["P3.0 to P3.7\nPins 10-17\nInternal pull-up\n+ Alternate Functions"]
    end

    ALU -- "MOV, SETB, CLR,\nANL, ORL, XRL, CPL" --> P0REG
    ALU --> P1REG
    ALU --> P2REG
    ALU --> P3REG

    P0REG --> P0PINS
    P1REG --> P1PINS
    P2REG --> P2PINS
    P3REG --> P3PINS

    P0PINS -. "Read Pin" .-> ALU
    P1PINS -. "Read Pin" .-> ALU
    P2PINS -. "Read Pin" .-> ALU
    P3PINS -. "Read Pin" .-> ALU
```

---

## 4.4 State Diagram: Configuring a Port Pin as Input vs Output

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Write_One: Write 1 to Latch\n(SETB or MOV Px.n, #1)
    [*] --> Write_Zero: Write 0 to Latch\n(CLR or MOV Px.n, #0)

    Write_One --> Input_Mode: Upper FET ON (weak)\nLower FET OFF\nPin = High-Z (Input)
    Write_Zero --> Output_Mode: Lower FET ON\nPin = Driven LOW (Output 0)

    Input_Mode --> Read_Pin: MOV A, Px\nReturns EXTERNAL pin value
    Output_Mode --> Drive_External: Pin actively pulled LOW
    Output_Mode --> Toggle: CPL Px.n reads LATCH\nFlips bit, writes back

    Read_Pin --> Input_Mode
    Drive_External --> Output_Mode
    Toggle --> Output_Mode

    note right of Input_Mode
        This is why we write 1
        to make a pin INPUT in 8051
    end note
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Confirmation of the 8051 Paradox:** In 8051, writing **1** to a port pin makes it an **INPUT**, and writing **0** makes it an **OUTPUT (driven LOW)**. There is no DDR register like in AVR/PIC. This is one of the most commonly confused concepts in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List the SFR addresses of all four I/O ports of the 8051. State which of these are bit-addressable."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Port | SFR Address | Bit-Addressable? |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| P0   | 80H         | ✅ Yes           |
| P1   | 90H         | ✅ Yes           |
| P2   | A0H         | ✅ Yes           |
| P3   | B0H         | ✅ Yes           |

All four ports of the 8051 are **bit-addressable**, allowing individual pin manipulation via `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`, and `MOV C, Px.n` instructions. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why must `1` be written to a port pin of the 8051 to configure it as an input? Explain the role of internal pull-up resistors."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the 8051, writing `1` to a port pin's latch turns OFF the lower (pull-down) output FET and activates the **internal weak pull-up resistor** (~30 kΩ). This puts the pin in a **high-impedance (High-Z) state**, allowing an external device (like a switch) to drive the pin without contention.

For **Port 0**, no internal pull-up exists, so **external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ)** are mandatory if P0 is used as a general-purpose input or output. (3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**"Explain the internal structure of an 8051 port pin with a neat block diagram. Discuss the 'Read-Modify-Write' problem and illustrate it with an example assembly program that toggles only P1.5 while P1.0 to P1.4 and P1.6, P1.7 are connected to switches."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Internal Structure & Read-Modify-Write**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Internal Structure (4 marks):**
Each 8051 port pin contains:
- A **D-type latch** (1-bit memory holding the output value).
- An **output driver stage** (a pair of FETs — one pull-up, one pull-down).
- An **input read buffer** (for reading the physical pin voltage).
- For P1, P2, P3: a **weak internal pull-up resistor** (~30 kΩ).
- For P0: **no internal pull-up** (open-drain design).

**2. Two Types of Read Operations (2 marks):**
- **Read Pin:** Triggered by `MOV A, Px` → reads the *external physical voltage* on the pin.
- **Read Latch:** Triggered by **Read-Modify-Write (RMW)** instructions (`ANL Px, A`, `ORL Px, A`, `CPL Px.n`, `INC Px`, `DJNZ Px, rel`) → reads the *internal latch*, not the pin.

**3. The RMW Problem (1 mark):**
When an RMW instruction executes, it reads the *latch* value, modifies it, and writes it back. If an external switch is holding the pin to a different logic level than the latch, the external state is *lost* (overwritten) by the latch's previous value.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Assembly Program**

**Problem:** Toggle **only P1.5** (LED), while P1.0–P1.4 and P1.6, P1.7 are connected to external switches (input).

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     P1, #0FFH       ; Step 1: Write 1s to all P1 pins → configure as INPUT
                                ;         (This releases all pins for external switches)
        CLR     P1.5            ; Step 2: But P1.5 must drive LED → set latch to 0
                                ;         (LED initially OFF)

LOOP:   CPL     P1.5            ; Step 3: RMW instruction — toggles ONLY P1.5
                                ;         (Reads latch bit 5, flips it, writes back)
                                ;         Other P1 pins (0,1,2,3,4,6,7) UNCHANGED
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Step 4: Software delay
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Step 5: Repeat forever

; ---- Software Delay Subroutine ----
DELAY:  MOV     R0, #0FFH
D1:     MOV     R1, #0FFH
        DJNZ    R1, $
        DJNZ    R0, D1
        RET
        END
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- `CPL P1.5` is a **Read-Modify-Write** instruction. It reads the *latch* (not the pin), so the external switch states on P1.0–P1.4, P1.6, P1.7 are **not affected**.
- If we had used `ANL P1, #0DFH` (to clear P1.5) followed by `ORL P1, #20H` (to set P1.5), it would also toggle only P1.5 without affecting the input switches — a safer alternative.
- This is the canonical solution to avoid the RMW problem when some port pins are inputs and others are outputs.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Using single-bit RMW instructions like `CPL Px.n` or using masked byte operations is the standard technique to safely program 8051 ports in mixed input/output configurations.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a square wave of 50% duty cycle on pin P2.3 using software delay. Assume the crystal frequency is 11.0592 MHz. Calculate the time period of the generated waveform and the required delay subroutine count."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Program Logic and Code**

**Model Answer:**

**Square Wave Generation Principle (2 marks):**
A square wave alternates between HIGH and LOW with equal duration. Hence, the LED is ON for half the period and OFF for the other half. We use `SETB` and `CLR` on P2.3 with a delay between each transition.

**Assembly Program (5 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     P2, #00H        ; Initialize P2 (clear all latches)

MAIN:   SETB    P2.3            ; Step 1: P2.3 = HIGH (LED ON, half period)
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Step 2: Wait for half period
        CLR     P2.3            ; Step 3: P2.3 = LOW (LED OFF, half period)
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Step 4: Wait for half period
        SJMP    MAIN            ; Step 5: Repeat forever

; ---- Delay Subroutine ----
; This delay produces a half-period delay
DELAY:  MOV     R0, #0FFH       ; Outer loop count
LOOP1:  MOV     R1, #0FFH       ; Inner loop count
LOOP2:  DJNZ    R1, LOOP2       ; Decrement R1, loop until 0 (255 × 2 µs)
        DJNZ    R0, LOOP1       ; Decrement R0, loop until 0 (255 × inner loop time)
        RET
        END
```

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Time Period Calculation**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate the machine cycle frequency (1 mark).**

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the machine cycle period (1 mark).**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \text{ MHz}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the delay produced by the inner loop (2 marks).**

The instruction `DJNZ R1, LOOP2` takes **2 machine cycles** per iteration (1 to fetch/decrement, 1 to jump back when not zero). For R1 = 0xFF = 255:

$$
T_{\text{inner}} = 255 \times 2 \times T_{MC} = 255 \times 2 \times 1.085\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{inner}} = 553.35\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the total delay (1 mark).**

Including the `MOV R1, #0FFH` (1 machine cycle) and `DJNZ R0, LOOP1` (2 machine cycles):

$$
T_{delay} = \left[1 + 255 \times \left(1 + 2 \times 255\right) + 2\right] \times T_{MC}
$$

Expanding the inner term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{delay} &= \left[1 + 255 \times 511 + 2\right] \times 1.085\,\mu s \\
&= \left[1 + 130\,305 + 2\right] \times 1.085\,\mu s \\
&= 130\,308 \times 1.085\,\mu s \\
&\approx 141.4 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total square wave period (1 mark).**

$$
T_{sq} = 2 \times T_{delay} = 2 \times 141.4 \text{ ms} \approx 282.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6: Frequency (1 mark).**

$$
f_{sq} = \frac{1}{T_{sq}} = \frac{1}{0.2828 \text{ s}} \approx 3.54 \text{ Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Duty Cycle:** 50% (as required), because the HIGH and LOW durations are both equal to $T_{delay}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in 8051 Port Programming:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `MOV Px, #0FFH` before reading a port** → Port reads garbage / floating values. Always a 1-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing `MOV A, Px` (Read Pin) vs `ANL Px, A` (Read Latch)** → Many students write `MOV A, Px` when the question expects the RMW behavior. This is a 2-mark conceptual error.
> 3. **Using P0 directly to drive an LED without an external pull-up** → The LED will not light (or light very dimly). Examiners specifically test this in hardware-based questions.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply machine cycles by 2 in delay calculations** → Causes a 2-mark error in time-period problems.
> 4. **Writing the wrong SFR address (e.g., 0x80 for P1)** → Instant 0 for that sub-question. **Memorize 80-90-A0-B0 in hexadecimal order.**

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8051 has **4 ports × 8 pins = 32 I/O lines**.
- ✅ SFR addresses: **P0 = 0x80, P1 = 0x90, P2 = 0xA0, P3 = 0xB0** (memorize the 80-90-A0-B0 sequence).
- ✅ All four ports are **bit-addressable** — single-pin manipulation is possible.
- ✅ **Write `1` to latch = make pin INPUT**; **Write `0` to latch = make pin OUTPUT (driven LOW)**.
- ✅ **P0 has NO internal pull-up** → external 10 kΩ pull-ups required for generic I/O.
- ✅ **P3 has alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) — using P3 pins as generic I/O disables these peripherals.
- ✅ **Two types of reads:** Read Pin (for `MOV A, Px`) and Read Latch (for RMW instructions like `ANL`, `ORL`, `CPL`).
- ✅ **Read-Modify-Write Problem:** RMW instructions read the *latch*, not the *pin* — risk of losing external input state.
- ✅ **Safe port manipulation** uses either single-bit RMW (`CPL Px.n`, `SETB Px.n`, `CLR Px.n`) or masked byte operations.
- ✅ Machine cycle time = **12 / f_osc**. At 11.0592 MHz, $T_{MC} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$.
- ✅ Standard 8051 port pin sink current: **~10 mA**; source current (via pull-up): much lower.
- ✅ The `CPL` instruction is a **single-instruction toggle** — the most efficient way to generate square waves on a port pin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timers of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⏱️ Module 2: Timers of 8051 — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Timer/Counter in 8051**
> The **8051 microcontroller** is equipped with two on-chip **16-bit programmable Timers/Counters**, designated **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**. These are essentially **up-counters** that increment on every machine cycle (when configured as timers) or on every external transition (when configured as event counters). They are used to generate accurate time delays, measure event intervals, generate baud rates for serial communication, and produce periodic interrupts.

The two timers are accessible via the **SFR (Special Function Register)** set, specifically the registers **TMOD** (Timer Mode), **TCON** (Timer Control), and the two 16-bit register pairs **TH0–TL0** and **TH1–TL1**, where **'H'** denotes the high byte and **'L'** denotes the low byte of the timer.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — A Mechanical Kitchen Timer

Imagine a **mechanical kitchen timer** that you twist to set a duration (e.g., 10 minutes). Once the spring unwinds and the dial returns to zero, a **bell rings** to alert you.

| 8051 Timer Concept | Kitchen Timer Analogy |
|---|---|
| **THx / TLx (Initial Value)** | The number you set by twisting the dial (how much "time" is pre-loaded). |
| **Oscillator (Crystal)** | The unwinding spring that drives the mechanism. |
| **TFx Flag (Overflow Flag)** | The bell that rings when the timer hits zero (overflows). |
| **TRx Bit (Start Bit)** | The start/stop knob you flip. |
| **Mode 1 (16-bit)** | A standard 60-minute kitchen timer. |
| **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** | A re-settable egg-timer that resets itself to the original value every time it rings. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Timers?**
> KTU frequently tests this. The 8051 has **2 timers** because Timer 1 is typically reserved for the **Serial Port Baud Rate Generator** in UART communication, leaving Timer 0 free for general time-delay generation. This dual-timer architecture allows simultaneous baud-rate generation AND timing operations without software overhead.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Oscillator Frequency (f_osc):** Typically **11.0592 MHz** (most common in KTU labs) or **12 MHz**.
- **Machine Cycle Period (T_mc):** Always **12 ÷ f_osc** (one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods).
- **Standard Timer Tick Duration @ 11.0592 MHz crystal:** **1.085 µs** per count increment.
- **Standard Timer Tick Duration @ 12 MHz crystal:** **1.0 µs** per count increment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz** is preferred over **12 MHz** because it produces an *exact* integer machine cycle period (1.085 µs) that yields **error-free standard baud rates** (9600, 4800, 19200) for serial communication — a favourite KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis: The 8051 Timer Subsystem

## 2.1 Architecture of the Timer Subsystem

The 8051's timer block consists of the following logical components, mapped to dedicated SFRs:

### 2.1.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode — Address 89H)

TMOD is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable** register that configures the *operating mode* of both timers. It is split into two **4-bit nibbles**: the upper nibble governs **Timer 1** and the lower nibble governs **Timer 0**.

| Bit Position | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 (T1) | **GATE** | 1 = Hardware control (INTx pin); 0 = Software control (TRx bit) |
| 6 (T1) | **C/T̄** | 1 = **Counter** (external events on T0/T1 pin); 0 = **Timer** (internal oscillator) |
| 5 (T1) | **M1** | Mode select bit 1 (with M0) |
| 4 (T1) | **M0** | Mode select bit 0 (with M1) |
| 3 (T0) | **GATE** | Hardware/Software control for Timer 0 |
| 2 (T0) | **C/T̄** | Counter/Timer select for Timer 0 |
| 1 (T0) | **M1** | Mode select bit 1 for Timer 0 |
| 0 (T0) | **M0** | Mode select bit 0 for Timer 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode Selection Truth Table (KTU High-Yield)**

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Register Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (compatibility mode with 8048) | 13 bits (THx 8-bit + TLx lower 5 bits) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer (most common) | 16 bits (THx + TLx) |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload | 8 bits (TLx); THx holds reload value |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) | TL0 = 8-bit timer; TH0 = 8-bit timer |

### 2.1.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control — Address 88H)

TCON is **bit-addressable** and contains the **run-control bits** and the **overflow flags**.

| Bit | Name | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **TF1** | 8FH | Timer 1 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware when Timer 1 overflows. |
| 6 | **TR1** | 8EH | Timer 1 **Run Control** — 1 = start, 0 = stop. |
| 5 | **TF0** | 8DH | Timer 0 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware when Timer 0 overflows. |
| 4 | **TR0** | 8CH | Timer 0 **Run Control** — 1 = start, 0 = stop. |
| 3 | IE1 | 8BH | External Interrupt 1 edge flag. |
| 2 | IT1 | 8AH | External Interrupt 1 type (edge/level). |
| 1 | IE0 | 89H | External Interrupt 0 edge flag. |
| 0 | IT0 | 88H | External Interrupt 0 type. |

## 2.2 The Four Operating Modes — Deep Dive

### 🟦 Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (Legacy 8048 Compatibility)
- Uses only **13 bits**: 8 bits of **THx** + the **lower 5 bits of TLx** (upper 3 bits of TLx are ignored).
- Count range: **0000H to 1FFFH** (8192 counts).
- Rarely used in modern designs — *retained only for 8048 backward compatibility*.

### 🟩 Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Widely Used)
- Uses the **full 16 bits** of THx and TLx.
- Count range: **0000H to FFFFH** (65,536 counts).
- The timer overflows from **FFFFH → 0000H**, setting the **TFx flag**.
- After overflow, the timer **does not auto-reload** — software must reset THx/TLx for the next delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 1 is the "go-to" mode for arbitrary time delays** in KTU problems because it provides the maximum range (65,536 counts) for a single delay.

### 🟧 Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload
- **TLx** acts as the 8-bit counter (range 00H to FFH).
- **THx** holds the **reload value** that is automatically copied into TLx whenever TLx overflows (FFH → 00H).
- This makes it **continuous and periodic** with no software intervention — ideal for **baud rate generation** in serial communication.

### 🟥 Mode 3 — Split Timer (Timer 0 Only)
- Splits the 16-bit Timer 0 into **two independent 8-bit timers** (TL0 and TH0).
- TL0 uses Timer 0's control bits (TR0, TF0).
- TH0 **borrows** Timer 1's overflow flag (TF1) and run bit (TR1) — effectively disabling Timer 1's normal operation.
- Used in applications requiring **multiple timing channels**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Timer Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Sacred Formulas of 8051 Timers**

### Formula 1: Machine Cycle Period

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

- **T_mc** = Machine cycle period in seconds.
- **f_osc** = Crystal oscillator frequency in Hz.

### Formula 2: Timer Increment Period

$$T_{tick} = T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Each timer count increment takes exactly **one machine cycle** (when the timer is running).

### Formula 3: Number of Timer Counts Required

$$N_{counts} = \text{Reg}_{\max} - \text{Reg}_{\text{initial}}$$

- **Reg_max** = Maximum register value (8192 for Mode 0, 65536 for Mode 1, 256 for Mode 2).
- **Reg_initial** = Value pre-loaded into THx/TLx (decimal).

### Formula 4: Total Time Delay Generated

$$T_{delay} = N_{counts} \times T_{tick} = N_{counts} \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students frequently write `T_delay = Reg_max × 1.085 µs` instead of `T_delay = (Reg_max − Initial_Value) × 1.085 µs`. The initial value is **subtracted** from the maximum, not multiplied by the maximum!

### Standard Quick-Reference Table (KTU Lab Standard)

| Crystal (f_osc) | Machine Cycle (T_mc) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **11.0592 MHz** | **1.085 µs** | Standard KTU boards, exact baud rates |
| **12.000 MHz** | **1.000 µs** | Educational kits, easy mental math |
| **24.000 MHz** | **0.500 µs** | High-speed applications |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051 timer subsystem underpins nearly all embedded real-time systems:

1. **🚗 Automotive ECUs:** Engine control units use timers to schedule fuel injector pulses at millisecond precision.
2. **🏥 Medical Devices:** Heart-rate monitors use counters to count pulses per minute from optical sensors.
3. **📡 UART Communication:** Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** generates the precise baud-rate clock for the 8051's serial port.
4. **🏭 Industrial PLCs:** Programmable Logic Controllers use timers to sequence conveyor belts, robotic arms, and process valves.
5. **🎮 Consumer Electronics:** Microwave ovens, washing machines, and AC units all use microcontrollers with timers to manage user-preset durations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Timer Delay Formula (From First Principles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the exact expression for the time delay generated by a 16-bit timer (Mode 1) in the 8051.

### Step 1 — Oscillator Period

The crystal oscillator generates a sine wave with period:

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

### Step 2 — Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 architecture defines **one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** (this 12:1 division is hard-wired into the chip):

$$T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### Step 3 — Timer Increment Period

The internal timer logic uses the machine cycle as its clock. **Each timer count** takes exactly one machine cycle to complete:

$$T_{tick} = T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### Step 4 — Total Counts Until Overflow

In Mode 1 (16-bit), the timer counts upward from the **initial value** to the maximum value **FFFFH = 65535**, and then overflows to **00000H** on the very next count. The number of increments is:

$$N_{counts} = 65536 - \text{Initial Value (decimal)}$$

### Step 5 — Total Delay Equation

Multiplying the number of counts by the time per count:

$$T_{delay} = N_{counts} \times T_{tick}$$

$$T_{delay} = (65536 - \text{Initial Value}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

This is the **fundamental working equation** of any 8051 timer problem.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Generating a 50 ms Delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1

> **Given:**
> - Crystal frequency: **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**
> - Timer: **Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)**
> - Required delay: **T_delay = 50 ms = 50,000 µs**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the machine cycle period.**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Compute the number of counts required for 50 ms.**

$$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{50,000 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s}$$

$$N_{counts} = 46,082.95 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Round down and compute the initial timer value.**

Since the timer must *exactly* fit an integer number of counts, we round down to **46,082** counts (the closest achievable).

$$\text{Initial Value} = 65536 - 46082 = 19454$$

**Step 4: Convert initial value to hex for TH0 and TL0.**

Convert 19454 (decimal) to hexadecimal:

$$19454_{10} = 4BFE_{16}$$

Verify:
- **4 × 4096 = 16384**
- **B (11) × 256 = 2816** → Subtotal: 19200
- **F (15) × 16 = 240** → Subtotal: 19440
- **E (14) × 1 = 14** → Total: 19454 ✓

Therefore:
- **TH0 = 4BH (high byte)**
- **TL0 = FEH (low byte)**

**Step 5: Set TMOD register value.**

For Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode:
- Lower nibble of TMOD: **M1 = 0, M0 = 1, C/T̄ = 0, GATE = 0** → binary `0001`
- Timer 1 is unused: upper nibble = `0000`
- **TMOD = 0x01**

**Step 6: C Program (8051 Keil syntax).**

```c
void Timer0_Delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;        // Timer 0, Mode 1, software control
    TH0 = 0x4B;         // Load high byte
    TL0 = 0xFE;         // Load low byte
    TR0 = 1;            // Start Timer 0
    while (TF0 == 0);   // Wait for overflow flag
    TR0 = 0;            // Stop Timer 0
    TF0 = 0;            // Clear overflow flag
}
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mode 2 Auto-Reload for Periodic 250 µs Tick

> **Given:**
> - Crystal frequency: **f_osc = 12 MHz**
> - Timer: **Timer 0, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**
> - Required periodic delay: **250 µs**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Machine cycle period.**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.0 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Required number of counts.**

$$N_{counts} = \frac{250 \, \mu s}{1.0 \, \mu s} = 250 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Reload value (TH0) for Mode 2.**

In Mode 2, only the **lower 8 bits (TL0)** count. Maximum count = 256.

$$\text{TH0} = 256 - 250 = 6_{10} = 06H$$

**Step 4: Set TMOD register value.**

For Timer 0, Mode 2:
- M1 = 1, M0 = 0, C/T̄ = 0, GATE = 0 → lower nibble = `0010`
- **TMOD = 0x02**

**Step 5: Verify by computing actual delay.**

$$T_{delay} = (256 - 6) \times 1.0 \, \mu s = 250 \times 1.0 \, \mu s = 250 \, \mu s \;\; ✓$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Timer as Event Counter (C/T̄ = 1)

> **Scenario:** Count 100 external pulses arriving on the **T0 (P3.4) pin**.

### Configuration

To use Timer 0 as a **counter**, set the **C/T̄ bit = 1** in TMOD. The timer increments on each **high-to-low transition** of the T0 pin.

```c
void Counter_Demo(void) {
    TMOD = 0x05;        // Timer 0, Mode 1, COUNTER mode (C/T=1)
    TH0 = 0xFF;         // Will overflow after 1 pulse (FFFF - FFFF = 1)... 
    TL0 = 0xFF;         // ... actually for 100 pulses: Initial = 65536-100 = 65436 = FF9C
    TR0 = 1;            // Start counting external pulses on T0
    while (TF0 == 0);   // Wait until 100 pulses counted
    TR0 = 0;
    TF0 = 0;
}
```

For 100 pulses, the correct initial value is:

$$\text{Initial} = 65536 - 100 = 65436_{10} = FF9C_{16}$$

- **TH0 = FFH**
- **TL0 = 9CH**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mode Comparison Summary

| Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Width** | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit | 2× 8-bit |
| **Max Counts** | 8,192 | 65,536 | 256 | 256 each |
| **Auto-Reload** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (THx→TLx) | ❌ |
| **Common Use** | Legacy | **Delays** | **Baud rate** | Multi-tasking |
| **Registers** | THx (8) + TLx lower 5 | THx (8) + TLx (8) | TLx (counts), THx (reload) | TL0 + TH0 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 Timer Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osc["OSCILLATOR INPUT f_osc"]:::main
    div["÷12 PRESCALER (HARDWIRED)"]:::main
    mcycle["MACHINE CYCLE CLOCK 1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz"]:::main

    muxA["MUX: Timer or Counter"]:::logic
    t0pin["P3.4 T0 PIN EXTERNAL EVENTS"]:::io
    intclk["INTERNAL MACHINE CYCLE"]:::logic

    tmod["TMOD REGISTER Address 89H"]:::control
    tcon["TCON REGISTER Address 88H"]:::control
    gate["GATE LOGIC SOFTWARE OR HARDWARE START"]:::logic

    th0["TH0 High Byte"]:::reg
    tl0["TL0 Low Byte"]:::reg
    th1["TH1 High Byte"]:::reg
    tl1["TL1 Low Byte"]:::reg

    tf0["TF0 OVERFLOW FLAG"]:::flag
    tf1["TF1 OVERFLOW FLAG"]:::flag
    tr0["TR0 RUN BIT"]:::flag
    tr1["TR1 RUN BIT"]:::flag

    cpu["8051 CPU INTERRUPT OR POLLING"]:::cpu

    osc --> div --> mcycle --> intclk --> muxA
    t0pin --> muxA
    tmod -.configures.-> muxA
    tmod -.configures.-> gate
    tcon --> tr0
    tcon --> tr1
    gate --> muxA

    muxA --> th0
    muxA --> tl0
    muxA --> th1
    muxA --> tl1

    th0 --> tf0
    tl0 --> tf0
    th1 --> tf1
    tl1 --> tf1

    tf0 --> cpu
    tf1 --> cpu
    tr0 --> muxA
    tr1 --> muxA

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef logic fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#7f0000,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef flag fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Timer Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START TIMER CONFIGURATION"]:::node
    q1{"Which Timer?\nTimer 0 or Timer 1?"}:::dec
    q2{"Application Type?\nTime delay, Baud rate,\nor Event count?"}:::dec
    q3{"Need automatic\nperiodic reset?"}:::dec
    q4{"Need two separate\n8-bit timers?"}:::dec

    m0["MODE 0\n13-BIT LEGACY"]:::mode
    m1["MODE 1\n16-BIT DELAY"]:::mode

    m3["MODE 3\nSPLIT TIMER 0"]:::mode
    baud["BAUD RATE\nTimer 1 Mode 2"]:::app

    ctr["SET C/T_bar = 1\nCOUNTER MODE"]:::app

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Timer 0| q2
    q1 -->|Timer 1| q2
    q2 -->|Event count| ctr
    q2 -->|Baud rate| baud
    q2 -->|Time delay| q3

    q3 -->|No| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| m3
    q4 -->|No| m1

```

## 4.3 Mode 2 Auto-Reload Mechanism (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TL0 COUNTS\n00 to FF"]:::counter
    B{"Overflow?\nFF → 00"}:::dec
    C["TF0 = 1\nFlag Set"]:::flag
    D["TH0 RELOAD VALUE\nAuto-Copied to TL0"]:::reload
    E["TL0 RESETS\nContinues Counting"]:::counter

    A --> B
    B -->|Yes| C
    B -->|Yes| D
    D --> E
    E --> A

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Explain the function of the TMOD register in 8051. List the four operating modes of Timer 0.**

**Model Answer:**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit SFR at address **89H** that is *not bit-addressable*. It configures the operating mode and behaviour of **Timer 0 and Timer 1**. The upper nibble controls Timer 1; the lower nibble controls Timer 0. Each nibble contains:
- **GATE** — Hardware (1) or Software (0) start control.
- **C/T̄** — Counter (1) or Timer (0) mode.
- **M1, M0** — Mode selection bits.

The four modes of Timer 0 are:

| M1 | M0 | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Mode 0 — 13-bit timer |
| 0 | 1 | Mode 1 — 16-bit timer |
| 1 | 0 | Mode 2 — 8-bit auto-reload |
| 1 | 1 | Mode 3 — Split timer |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode in 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Timer Mode (C/T̄ = 0) | Counter Mode (C/T̄ = 1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (÷12 prescaler) | External pin (T0 = P3.4 or T1 = P3.5) |
| **Trigger** | Automatic — every machine cycle | External high-to-low transition |
| **Use Case** | Time delay generation | Counting external events/pulses |
| **TMOD Bit** | C/T̄ = 0 | C/T̄ = 1 |
| **Typical Application** | Blinking LED, baud rate | Tachometer, production-line counter |

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and operation of the 8051 timer subsystem. Describe the TMOD and TCON registers in detail with their bit functions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 to generate a delay of **exactly 30 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Write the corresponding 8051 C program.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The 8051 has **two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each comprising two 8-bit registers: **THx (high byte)** and **TLx (low byte)**.

**TMOD Register (Address 89H, Not Bit-Addressable):**

It has two 4-bit nibbles. Each nibble contains:
- **GATE (bit 7/3):** When set, timer is controlled by INTx pin; when cleared, controlled by TRx.
- **C/T̄ (bit 6/2):** Selects Counter (1) or Timer (0).
- **M1, M0 (bits 5–4 / 1–0):** Select one of four operating modes.

**TCON Register (Address 88H, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| TF1 (8FH) | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | Set when Timer 1 overflows |
| TR1 (8EH) | Timer 1 Run Bit | 1 = start, 0 = stop |
| TF0 (8DH) | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | Set when Timer 0 overflows |
| TR0 (8CH) | Timer 0 Run Bit | 1 = start, 0 = stop |

The TFx flag is set by **hardware on overflow** and cleared by **software**. The timer increments every machine cycle (12 oscillator periods) and is halted only when TRx is cleared or the chip is reset.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Machine cycle period.**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Required counts for 30 ms.**

$$N_{counts} = \frac{30,000 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} = 27,649.77 \approx 27,649 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Initial timer value.**

$$\text{Initial} = 65536 - 27649 = 37887_{10}$$

**Step 4: Convert 37887 to hex.**

37887 ÷ 16 = 2367 remainder **15 (F)**
2367 ÷ 16 = 147 remainder **15 (F)**
147 ÷ 16 = 9 remainder **3**
9 ÷ 16 = 0 remainder **9**

$$37887_{10} = 93FF_{16}$$

- **TH0 = 93H**
- **TL0 = FFH**

**Step 5: C Program.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_30ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;        // Timer 0, Mode 1
    TH0 = 0x93;
    TL0 = 0xFF;
    TR0 = 1;            // Start timer
    while (TF0 == 0);   // Wait for overflow
    TR0 = 0;            // Stop timer
    TF0 = 0;            // Clear flag
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Loading the *hex* of 27649 (i.e., 6C01H) instead of 93FFH. Remember: you must load `65536 − N_counts`, not `N_counts` itself.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to clear **TF0** at the end. Without this, the next call to the function will return immediately (flag still set).
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not stopping the timer (TR0 = 0) after overflow. Wastes CPU power and may cause erroneous subsequent timing.
> - **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Using `TMOD = 0x10` (Timer 1) instead of `TMOD = 0x01` (Timer 0). Verify the nibble!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** operation of the 8051 timer. Mention its major application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an 8051 system with a **12 MHz crystal**, configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a **baud rate of 9600 bps** for serial communication. Compute the TH1 reload value. Mention the SMOD bit setting required.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

In **Mode 2**, the timer operates as an **8-bit counter using TLx**, with the upper 8-bit register **THx holding a constant reload value**.

**Operation:**

1. TLx counts from the reload value up to **FFH**.
2. On the next clock, TLx **overflows to 00H** and **TFx is set**.
3. Simultaneously, the hardware **automatically copies the THx value back into TLx**.
4. Counting resumes seamlessly without software intervention.

**Block Flow:**

```
TLx ← THx (on overflow) → Count up → FF → 00 (overflow) → TFx = 1
                          ↑                                        |
                          └────── Copy THx again ←─────────────────┘
```

**Major Application:**
Mode 2 is the **standard mode for the Serial Port Baud Rate Generator** in 8051. Timer 1 is dedicated to generating the precise baud-rate clock for UART transmission/reception because the auto-reload mechanism provides a continuous, jitter-free periodic waveform that matches the exact baud rate.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Baud rate formula for 8051 UART.**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

For **9600 baud**, **f_osc = 12 MHz**, and assuming **SMOD = 0** (no doubling):

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{10^6}{(256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2: Solve for TH1.**

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{10^6}{32 \times 9600} = \frac{10^6}{307200} = 3.255$$

This gives a non-integer value → **error in baud rate**.

**Step 3: Re-evaluate using f_osc = 11.0592 MHz** (correct crystal for standard baud rates).

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 9600 \times 12}$$

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{11,059,200}{3,686,400} = 3$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = FDH$$

**Step 4: SMOD setting.**

For exact 9600 bps, **SMOD = 0** (the default PCON.7 value at reset).

**Step 5: C Program.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void UART_Init_9600(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;        // Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
    TH1 = 0xFD;         // Reload value for 9600 baud
    SCON = 0x50;        // Mode 1 UART, REN enabled
    TR1 = 1;            // Start Timer 1
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common mistake 1 (–3 marks):** Using f_osc = 12 MHz in the baud rate formula and getting TH1 = FDH anyway. The 12 MHz crystal **cannot** produce error-free 9600 baud; KTU expects you to use 11.0592 MHz.
> - **Common mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to write `TR1 = 1`. The UART baud generator only runs when TR1 is set.
> - **Common mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing TH1 reload value with the actual count (3, not 253). Memorize: **TH1 = 256 − count**.
> - **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not stating the SMOD assumption explicitly.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This on Your Wall**

- ⏱️ **8051 has TWO timers:** Timer 0 (TH0/TL0) and Timer 1 (TH1/TL1).
- 🗂️ **TMOD (89H)** = Configuration register (not bit-addressable).
- 🗂️ **TCON (88H)** = Control + flags (bit-addressable).
- 🎛️ **TMOD bits:** GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 — for each timer.
- ▶️ **TCON key bits:** TR0/TR1 (run), TF0/TF1 (overflow).
- 📐 **Machine Cycle = 12 / f_osc.**
- 🔢 **Standard tick at 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs; at 12 MHz = 1.0 µs.**
- 🟩 **Mode 1 (16-bit):** 65,536 max counts. **Most common for delays.**
- 🟧 **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload):** Used for **UART baud rate**.
- 🟥 **Mode 3:** Splits Timer 0 into two 8-bit timers.
- ➕ **T_delay = (Reg_max − Initial_Value) × 1.085 µs.**
- ⬆️ **Always subtract initial from max, never multiply.**
- 🚪 **GATE = 1 → external INTx pin starts timer; GATE = 0 → TRx bit starts timer.**
- 🔁 **C/T̄ = 0 → Timer (internal); C/T̄ = 1 → Counter (external pin T0/T1).**
- 🧹 **Always clear TFx and stop TRx after overflow — software responsibility.**
- 📡 **UART 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz → TH1 = FDH (Mode 2).**
- ⚠️ **Trap:** f_osc = 12 MHz cannot generate exact 9600 baud; 11.0592 MHz is the universal KTU answer.

---
