topics.title: Modes of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modes of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modes of Operation (Timer/Counter Modes)** in the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller** refer to the four distinct functional configurations (**Mode 0, Mode 1, Mode 2, and Mode 3**) that the two internal 16-bit **Timers/Counters (Timer 0 and Timer 1)** can operate in. Each mode controls the **counter width, clock source, and overflow/reload behavior** of the timer, and is selected by programming the **Timer Mode Register (TMOD)** along with the **Timer Control Register (TCON)**.

These modes determine how the **High Byte (THx)** and **Low Byte (TLx)** registers are cascaded to form counting registers of different widths (13-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit, or split counters), directly impacting the maximum count value and the timing resolution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Stopwatch with Multiple Stop Modes:**
> Imagine a digital stopwatch with **four different stop modes**:
> - One counts up to **8192** clicks (fast, coarse measurement) — **Mode 0 (13-bit)**
> - One counts up to **65,536** clicks (standard measurement) — **Mode 1 (16-bit)**
> - One that **automatically resets and restarts** every **256** clicks (repeating alarm) — **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)**
> - One that **splits into two smaller timers** (dual alarm clock) — **Mode 3 (Split Timer)**
>
> The 8051's "Modes of Operation" are exactly like these four stopwatch configurations. By flipping internal switches (writing to **TMOD**), the engineer picks the right tool for the job — measuring microseconds, milliseconds, baud-rate generation, or running two independent counters.

## 1.3 Why Modes of Operation Matter in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> The 8051's four timer modes are **NOT optional features** — they are the **fundamental time-keeping engine** of nearly every embedded system built around the 8051 family. Real-world applications include:
> - Generating **precise time delays** (flashing LEDs, debouncing switches)
> - Producing **baud rates** for serial UART communication (Mode 2 is mandatory)
> - Counting **external events** (rotary encoders, pulse counting on assembly lines)
> - Triggering **periodic interrupts** for Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS tick generation)

## 1.4 The Four Modes — A High-Level Snapshot

| Mode | Name | Register Width | Max Count | Auto-Reload? | Primary Use |
|:----:|:----:|:--------------:|:---------:|:------------:|:-----------:|
| **Mode 0** | 13-Bit Timer | 13-bit | 8192 | No | Legacy 8048 compatibility |
| **Mode 1** | 16-Bit Timer | 16-bit | 65,536 | No | Standard timing & delays |
| **Mode 2** | 8-Bit Auto-Reload | 8-bit | 256 | **Yes** | UART Baud-Rate Generator |
| **Mode 3** | Split Timer | Two 8-bit | 256 | No | Two independent 8-bit counters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Timer/Counter Hardware Core

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1). Each timer is built from two **8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs)**:

- **TLx** — Timer **L**ow byte (lower 8 bits)
- **THx** — Timer **H**igh byte (upper 8 bits)

A **4-to-1 Multiplexer (MUX)** controlled by the **C/T̅** bit selects the clock source:
- **C/T̅ = 0** → Internal oscillator (÷ 12 of crystal frequency) → **Timer Mode**
- **C/T̅ = 1** → External pin **T0 (P3.4) / T1 (P3.5)** → **Counter Mode**

A **3-to-1 MUX** (controlled by **M1, M0** of TMOD) routes the output of TLx/THx cascade into one of the four **Modes of Operation**.

## 2.2 TMOD Register — The "Mode Selector Switch"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TMOD (Timer Mode Register) — Address: 89H, Not Bit-Addressable**

$$TMOD = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
GATE & C/\overline{T} & M1 & M0 & GATE & C/\overline{T} & M1 & M0 \\
\hline
\multicolumn{4}{|c|}{\text{Timer 1}} & \multicolumn{4}{c|}{\text{Timer 0}} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:----:|:---------|
| **GATE** | Gating Control | When 1, timer runs only when **INTx pin = HIGH** AND **TRx = 1** |
| **C/T̅** | Counter/Timer Select | 0 = Timer (internal clock), 1 = Counter (external pin) |
| **M1, M0** | Mode Select Bits | 00=Mode 0, 01=Mode 1, 10=Mode 2, 11=Mode 3 |

## 2.3 TCON Register — The "Run/Stop and Flag Switch"

> [!NOTE]
> **TCON (Timer Control Register) — Address: 88H, Bit-Addressable**

| Bit | Name | Address | Function |
|:---:|:----:|:-------:|:---------|
| **TF1** | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | 8FH | Set by hardware when Timer 1 overflows |
| **TR1** | Timer 1 Run Control | 8EH | 1 = Start Timer 1, 0 = Stop Timer 1 |
| **TF0** | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | 8DH | Set by hardware when Timer 0 overflows |
| **TR0** | Timer 0 Run Control | 8CH | 1 = Start Timer 0, 0 = Stop Timer 0 |
| **IE1** | Ext. Int 1 Edge Flag | 8BH | Set on falling edge of INT1 |
| **IT1** | Ext. Int 1 Type | 8AH | 1 = Edge-triggered, 0 = Level-triggered |
| **IE0** | Ext. Int 0 Edge Flag | 89H | Set on falling edge of INT0 |
| **IT0** | Ext. Int 0 Type | 88H | 1 = Edge-triggered, 0 = Level-triggered |

## 2.4 📐 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **All time/frequency calculations in 8051 timers are based on the machine cycle. Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | **12 clock periods = 1 machine cycle** |
| 2 | Timer Clock Frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Used in Timer mode only |
| 3 | Delay (No Reload) | $T_{delay} = (TC_{max} - TC_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | Mode 0, 1, 3 |
| 4 | Delay (Auto-Reload) | $T_{delay} = (256 - TC_{reload}) \times T_{mc}$ | Mode 2 only |
| 5 | Max Count — Mode 0 | $2^{13} = 8192$ | 13-bit, 5 lower bits of TLx ignored |
| 6 | Max Count — Mode 1 | $2^{16} = 65536$ | 16-bit full range |
| 7 | Max Count — Mode 2 | $2^{8} = 256$ | 8-bit auto-reload |
| 8 | Max Count — Mode 3 | $2^{8} = 256$ per half | Two independent 8-bit counters |
| 9 | Baud Rate (Mode 2) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Standard UART formula |
| 10 | Overflow Flag | Set when counter rolls from max → 0 | Cleared by software |

## 2.5 Mode Selection — Decision Logic

The selection of mode is governed by **M1, M0** of the TMOD register:

$$(M1, M0) = \begin{cases}
(0, 0) \rightarrow \text{Mode 0: 13-bit Timer/Counter} \\
(0, 1) \rightarrow \text{Mode 1: 16-bit Timer/Counter} \\
(1, 0) \rightarrow \text{Mode 2: 8-bit Auto-Reload} \\
(1, 1) \rightarrow \text{Mode 3: Split Timer Mode}
\end{cases}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the **most commonly used** mode in 8051 programming for general-purpose delays.
> - **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** is **exclusively used** to generate the **standard UART baud rate** (e.g., 9600 baud for serial communication with PCs).
> - **Mode 3 (Split Timer)** is rarely used in industry but appears in KTU exams as a conceptual question because of its unique dual-counter architecture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mode 0 — The 13-Bit Timer (8048 Compatibility Mode)

### 3.1.1 Architecture & Operation

In **Mode 0**, the 8051's timer operates as a **13-bit counter** to maintain backward compatibility with the older **Intel 8048** microcontroller. The 13 bits are formed by:

- **Lower 5 bits of TLx** (TL0/TL1) → 5 active bits
- **All 8 bits of THx** (TH0/TH1) → 8 active bits
- **Total = 5 + 8 = 13 bits**
- **Upper 3 bits of TLx are ignored** (always read as 0)

$$N_{max} = 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ counts}$$

### 3.1.2 Analytical Derivation — Delay Calculation

**Step 1:** The timer increments once per **machine cycle**.

$$T_{increment} = T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2:** The timer overflows when it reaches the maximum 13-bit value and rolls over to 0. The total number of increments before overflow is:

$$N_{overflow} = 2^{13} - TC_{initial} = 8192 - TC_{initial}$$

**Step 3:** The total delay produced is therefore:

$$T_{delay} = N_{overflow} \times T_{mc}$$

$$T_{delay} = (8192 - TC_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Timer 0 is configured in **Mode 0** with an initial value of **TC₀ = 5000**. Calculate the delay produced.

**Step 1:** Compute the machine cycle period.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2:** Compute the number of timer increments before overflow.

$$N = 8192 - 5000 = 3192 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total delay.

$$T_{delay} = 3192 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 3463.32\,\mu s \approx 3.46\,ms$$

> [!WARNING]
> In Mode 0, the upper 3 bits of TLx are **NOT USED**. A common student mistake is loading a full 8-bit value into TLx expecting 8 bits of resolution — only the lower 5 bits matter, drastically reducing effective resolution.

---

## 3.2 Mode 1 — The 16-Bit Timer (Most Used)

### 3.2.1 Architecture & Operation

**Mode 1** is the **default and most widely used** mode. It uses both **TLx (8 bits)** and **THx (8 bits)** as a full **16-bit counter**.

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ counts}$$

### 3.2.2 Analytical Derivation — Delay Calculation

**Step 1:** The timer increments once per machine cycle.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2:** Number of increments before overflow.

$$N = 65{,}536 - TC_{initial}$$

**Step 3:** Total delay.

$$T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - TC_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Using **11.0592 MHz** crystal, **Timer 1 in Mode 1**, find the **timer value** required to generate a delay of **exactly 50 ms**.

**Step 1:** Machine cycle.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2:** Required number of timer counts.

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}082.95 \approx 46{,}083 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3:** Initial timer value.

$$TC_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453$$

**Step 4:** Split into TH1 and TL1 (Hexadecimal).

$$19{,}453_{10} = 4BFD_H$$

$$\therefore \quad TH1 = 4BH, \quad TL1 = FDH$$

**Verification:**

$$T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - 19{,}453) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 46{,}083 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 50.0\,ms \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This exact frequency was chosen by Intel because it is **divisible by standard baud rate factors (12, 32, 64) producing zero-error UART timing**. Students will see this number in nearly every KTU problem.

---

## 3.3 Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer

### 3.3.1 Architecture & Operation

**Mode 2** is the **8-bit Auto-Reload mode**, where:
- **TLx** acts as the **8-bit counter** (counts from reload value to 256, then overflows)
- **THx** holds the **reload value** (auto-loaded into TLx on overflow)
- **No software intervention** is required to restart counting

$$N_{max} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ counts}$$

### 3.3.2 Analytical Derivation — Delay and Baud Rate

**Step 1:** Per-cycle delay.

$$T_{cycle} = (256 - TC_{reload}) \times T_{mc}$$

**Step 2:** For continuous operation, the **effective output frequency** at the overflow pin is:

$$f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - THx)}$$

**Step 3:** When used as a **UART baud-rate generator**, the **SMOD** bit of the **PCON** register doubles the baud rate when set:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Baud Rate

> **Problem:** Find **TH1** value for a **9600 baud** UART transmission with **11.0592 MHz** crystal and **SMOD = 0**.

**Step 1:** Substitute known values into the baud rate formula.

$$9600 = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify step by step.

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

$$9600 \times 32 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

$$307{,}200 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3:** Cross multiply.

$$12 \times (256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{307{,}200} = 36$$

$$256 - TH1 = 3$$

$$TH1 = 253 = FDH$$

**Verification:**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{36} = 9600 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Special)

### 3.4.1 Architecture & Operation

**Mode 3** is fundamentally different. It **splits Timer 0 into two independent 8-bit counters** and **disables Timer 1** (it can still be used in Modes 0, 1, or 2 for baud-rate generation).

| Component | Role in Mode 3 |
|:---------:|:--------------|
| **TL0** | 8-bit timer/counter controlled by **TR0, TF0** bits |
| **TH0** | 8-bit timer **controlled by TR1, TF1** bits (Timer 1's control borrowed) |
| **Timer 1** | Halts if M1:M0 = 11 in TMOD for T0; can be used separately for baud rate |

### 3.4.2 Analytical Derivation

For **TL0** (Timer 0 low byte):

$$T_{TL0} = (256 - TL0_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$$

For **TH0** (Timer 0 high byte, now an 8-bit timer):

$$T_{TH0} = (256 - TH0_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$$

Each operates independently, giving the designer **two separate 8-bit counters** plus the option to use Timer 1 for serial communication simultaneously.

---

## 3.5 📊 KTU Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|:----------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| **Width** | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit | Two 8-bit |
| **THx Range** | 0–255 | 0–255 | Reload value | 0–255 (TH0) |
| **TLx Range** | 0–31 (5-bit) | 0–255 | 0–255 (active counter) | 0–255 (TL0) |
| **Max Counts** | 8,192 | 65,536 | 256 | 256 each |
| **Auto-Reload** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Overflow Flag** | TFx | TFx | TFx | TF0 (TL0) + TF1 (TH0) |
| **Run Control** | TRx | TRx | TRx | TR0 (TL0), TR1 (TH0) |
| **Primary Use** | Legacy | Standard delays | Baud-rate gen | Dual counters |
| **Crystal Used** | Any | Any | **11.0592 MHz** typical | Any |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer/Counter Core Functional Architecture

```mermaid

        F1[TFx Overflow Flag]
        F2[Interrupt Request to CPU]

    end
    
    A1 --> C1

    B1 --> C1

```

## 4.2 Mode-by-Mode Cascade Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph mode0["MODE 0: 13-bit Timer"]
        m0a[TLx lower 5 bits]
        m0b[THx all 8 bits]
        m0c[Total: 13 bits]
        m0a --> m0c
        m0b --> m0c
    end
    
    subgraph mode1["MODE 1: 16-bit Timer"]
        m1a[TLx all 8 bits]

    end
    
    subgraph mode2["MODE 2: 8-bit Auto-Reload"]

        m2b[THx holds reload value]

    end
    
    subgraph mode3["MODE 3: Split Timer"]

        m3b[TH0 8-bit controlled by TR1]

        m3a --> m3c
        m3b --> m3c
    end
```

## 4.3 Timer Programming Flow — Mode Selection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Step 1: Define Required Delay]) --> step2[Step 2: Choose Mode 1 for general, Mode 2 for baud]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute Machine Cycle Tmc = 12 divided by fosc]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Calculate Timer Count N = Delay divided by Tmc]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Compute Initial Value TC = Max - N]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Load THx and TLx registers]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Configure TMOD with M1 M0 and C/T-bar]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Set TRx bit in TCON to START timer]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Wait for TFx overflow flag]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Clear TFx and TRx to STOP]
    step10 --> endNode([Delay Generated])
```

## 4.4 TMOD Configuration Map for All Four Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Configure TMOD Register]) --> bit7{GATE}
    bit7 -->|Software Control| bit6{C/T-bar}
    bit6 -->|0 = Timer| tim[Internal Clock fosc/12]
    bit6 -->|1 = Counter| ctr[External Pin Tx]
    tim --> modeBits
    ctr --> modeBits
    
    modeBits{M1 M0}
    modeBits -->|0 0| m0[Mode 0: 13-bit]
    modeBits -->|0 1| m1[Mode 1: 16-bit]
    modeBits -->|1 0| m2[Mode 2: 8-bit Auto-Reload]
    modeBits -->|1 1| m3[Mode 3: Split Timer]
    
    m0 --> use0[Use: Legacy 8048 Compatibility]
    m1 --> use1[Use: General Delays up to 65.5 ms at 12 MHz]
    m2 --> use2[Use: UART Baud Rate Generator]
    m3 --> use3[Use: Two Independent 8-bit Counters]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. List the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter. Which mode is used for UART baud-rate generation and why?

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 Timer/Counter supports four modes of operation:

| Mode | Configuration | Width |
|:----:|:-------------|:-----:|
| Mode 0 | M1=0, M0=0 | 13-bit |
| Mode 1 | M1=0, M0=1 | 16-bit |
| Mode 2 | M1=1, M0=0 | 8-bit Auto-Reload |
| Mode 3 | M1=1, M0=1 | Split Timer |

**Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** is used for UART baud-rate generation. The reason is its **auto-reload feature** — when TLx overflows from 255 → 0, the value in **THx is automatically copied into TLx** without any software intervention. This produces a **continuous, glitch-free overflow frequency** at a perfectly fixed rate, which is essential for maintaining an **error-free, steady baud clock** for asynchronous serial communication.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Differentiate between Timer Mode and Counter Mode of the 8051. What is the role of the C/T̅ bit?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Timer Mode (C/T̅ = 0) | Counter Mode (C/T̅ = 1) |
|:-------|:----------------------|:-------------------------|
| Clock Source | Internal oscillator (fosc/12) | External pin T0 (P3.4) / T1 (P3.5) |
| Trigger | Automatic, periodic | External pulse/edge |
| Frequency | Fixed by crystal | Determined by external event |
| Use Case | Time delays, baud rates | Event counting (pulses, revolutions) |

**Role of the C/T̅ bit:** The **C/T̅ (Counter/Timer) bit** in the **TMOD register** acts as a **single-bit selector switch** that decides whether the increment signal to the timer comes from the **internal machine cycle** (Timer mode) or from an **external event on the Tx pin** (Counter mode). It is **one of the 4 bits per timer** in TMOD and must be configured before starting the timer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format: Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Choose ONE option.**

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter with neat diagrams. Highlight the role of the **TMOD** register. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
The 8051 has two 16-bit Timers/Counters (Timer 0 and Timer 1) that can operate in four software-selectable modes. The mode is selected by **M1 and M0 bits** of the **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** at address **89H**.

**Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (8048 Compatibility):**
- Uses **5 lower bits of TLx** + **all 8 bits of THx** = 13-bit counter
- **Max counts = 2¹³ = 8192**
- Upper 3 bits of TLx are unused
- Legacy mode, rarely used in modern designs

**Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer:**
- Uses **all 8 bits of TLx** + **all 8 bits of THx** = 16-bit counter
- **Max counts = 2¹⁶ = 65,536**
- **Most widely used mode** for generating time delays
- No auto-reload; software must reload THx/TLx after each overflow

**Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload:**
- **TLx** is the 8-bit counter; **THx** stores the reload value
- When TLx overflows (255 → 0), **THx is auto-copied into TLx**
- **Max counts = 2⁸ = 256**
- **Used for UART baud-rate generation** due to stable, continuous overflow rate

**Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode:**
- Splits **Timer 0** into **two independent 8-bit counters**
- **TL0** controlled by **TR0, TF0**
- **TH0** controlled by **TR1, TF1** (Timer 1's run/flag bits are borrowed)
- Timer 1 effectively halts but can be used for baud rate generation

**Role of TMOD Register:**
- Address: **89H**, **not bit-addressable**
- Contains: **GATE, C/T̅, M1, M0** bits for **both Timer 0 and Timer 1**
- Configures the **mode**, **clock source**, and **gating** behavior
- TMOD is the **configuration register**, while TCON is the **control register**

| **Diagram (Schematic) of Mode 1 Cascade:** |
|:--|
| TLx (8 bits) → THx (8 bits) → Overflow Detect → TFx flag |

---

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Timer 1 is configured in **Mode 1** to generate a delay of **exactly 40 ms**. Calculate the values to be loaded into **TH1 and TL1**. Write the corresponding C language program using **Keil µVision** to flash an LED connected to **P1.0** at this delay interval. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**
$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Required Timer Counts:**
$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 36{,}866.36$$

Since we need exactly 40 ms, we round:
$$N = 36{,}866 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3 — Timer Initial Value:**
$$TC_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 36{,}866 = 28{,}670$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hex:**
$$28{,}670_{10} = 6FFE_H$$

**Therefore:**
$$TH1 = 6FH, \quad TL1 = FEH$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**
$$T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - 28{,}670) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 36{,}866 \times 1.085 = 39.999\,\text{ms} \approx 40\,\text{ms} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — C Program (Keil µVision):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;

void timer1_delay_40ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x10;       // Timer 1, Mode 1, Software Control
    TH1 = 0x6F;        // Load high byte
    TL1 = 0xFE;        // Load low byte
    TR1 = 1;           // Start Timer 1
    while(TF1 == 0);   // Wait for overflow flag
    TR1 = 0;           // Stop timer
    TF1 = 0;           // Clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        LED = ~LED;            // Toggle LED
        timer1_delay_40ms();   // 40 ms delay
    }
}
```

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of block diagrams, explain the **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** and **Mode 3 (Split Timer)** operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter. Compare the two modes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload:**

| Component | Function |
|:---------:|:---------|
| **TLx** | 8-bit counter (active) |
| **THx** | Holds the reload value (inactive as a counter) |
| **Overflow Behavior** | When TLx rolls from 255 → 0, THx is **automatically copied** into TLx |
| **Flag** | TFx is set; must be cleared by software |
| **Range** | 0 to 256 counts |

**Schematic Representation:**

```
  +-------+         +--------+
  |  THx  |  --->   |  TLx   |  --> Overflow
  |(reload|  copy   |(active |      (TFx set)
  | value)|  on OF  |counter)|
  +-------+         +--------+
```

**Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 only):**

| Component | Function |
|:---------:|:---------|
| **TL0** | 8-bit timer/counter, controlled by **TR0** and **TF0** |
| **TH0** | 8-bit timer (counter mode disabled), controlled by **TR1** and **TF1** |
| **Timer 1** | Can be set to Mode 0, 1, or 2 separately for baud rate |

**Schematic Representation:**

```
  Timer 0 in Mode 3
  +--------+         +--------+
  |  TL0   |         |  TH0   |
  |(TR0/TF0|         |(TR1/TF1|
  | control|         | control|
  +--------+         +--------+
   (8-bit)            (8-bit, no external pin)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|:-------|:-------|:-------|
| Timer Used | T0 or T1 | T0 only (T1 freed) |
| Width | 8-bit (TLx) | Two independent 8-bit |
| Auto-Reload | ✅ Yes (THx → TLx) | ❌ No |
| External Counting | ✅ (if C/T̅=1) | TL0 only |
| Use Case | UART baud rate | Two simultaneous delays |
| Complexity | Low | High |

---

**(b)** A serial communication system using the 8051 with an **11.0592 MHz crystal** must transmit data at **4800 baud** in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** of Timer 1. **SMOD = 0**. Calculate the value of **TH1** and write the **8051 assembly language program** to initialize Timer 1 for this baud rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Baud Rate Formula for Mode 2:**
$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values:**
$$4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve Step-by-Step:**
$$4800 \times 32 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

$$153{,}600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

$$12 \times (256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{153{,}600} = 72$$

$$256 - TH1 = 6$$

$$\boxed{TH1 = 250 = FA_H}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - 250)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{72} = 4800 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5 — 8051 Assembly Language Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #20H   ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)
        MOV TH1, #0FAH   ; Load reload value = 250
        MOV SCON, #50H   ; Serial Mode 1, REN enabled
        MOV PCON, #00H   ; SMOD = 0
        SETB TR1         ; Start Timer 1

HERE:   SJMP HERE        ; Wait forever (timer runs continuously)
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set SMOD bit context** — Many students write `MOV PCON, #00H` without explaining SMOD. Always mention the SMOD assumption explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong TMOD value** — Mode 2 for Timer 1 is **0x20** (NOT 0x02). Upper nibble = Timer 1, lower nibble = Timer 0.
> 3. **Confusing TH1 with TL1** — In Mode 2, the user loads the reload value into **TH1**, and TL1 is auto-managed by hardware.
> 4. **Off-by-one in delay calculations** — Round the count value **DOWN** when computing initial value to ensure delay is **at least** the required value, not less.
> 5. **Not clearing TFx flag** — Failure to clear TFx in software causes the program to behave erratically after the first overflow.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 High-Frequency KTU Exam Points — Memorize These:**

- ✅ The 8051 has **two** 16-bit Timers/Counters: **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**.
- ✅ Each timer is built from **TLx (low byte)** + **THx (high byte)** = 16 bits.
- ✅ **TMOD (89H)** is the mode-config register; **TCON (88H)** is the run-control register.
- ✅ **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable**; **TCON IS bit-addressable**.
- ✅ **C/T̅ bit** = 0 → Timer (internal clock = fosc/12); C/T̅ = 1 → Counter (external pin).
- ✅ **GATE bit** = 1 → Hardware control via **INTx pin**; GATE = 0 → Software control via **TRx bit**.

**🔢 Mode Summary (At a Glance):**
- ✅ **Mode 0** = **13-bit** → legacy 8048 → max **8192** counts.
- ✅ **Mode 1** = **16-bit** → most used → max **65,536** counts.
- ✅ **Mode 2** = **8-bit Auto-Reload** → baud rate → max **256** counts.
- ✅ **Mode 3** = **Split Timer** → two 8-bit counters (T0 only) → max **256** counts each.

**⏱️ Key Formulas (Recap):**
- ✅ $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$
- ✅ $T_{delay} = (2^n - TC_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$, where $n$ = mode bit width
- ✅ $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ (Mode 2 only)
- ✅ **Standard crystal for serial comms = 11.0592 MHz** (gives zero baud-rate error).

**🚨 Critical Programming Reminders:**
- ✅ Always set **TRx = 1** to start the timer, **TRx = 0** to stop.
- ✅ Always **clear TFx in software** after detecting overflow.
- ✅ In **Mode 2**, load the value into **THx** (not TLx) — TLx is auto-managed.
- ✅ **PCON.SMOD** = 1 **doubles** the baud rate.

**📊 KTU Question Pattern Frequency:**
- ✅ **Direct mode identification** — 1 question per exam (3 marks).
- ✅ **Delay calculation** — 1 question per exam (7–14 marks).
- ✅ **Baud rate calculation** — at least 1 question per exam (7 marks).
- ✅ **Mode 3 (Split Timer) diagram** — 1 question per academic year (7 marks).

> 🎓 **Final Note:** The 8051's Modes of Operation form the **backbone of all timing-related embedded systems work**. Master the delay formula, baud-rate formula, and the M1/M0 mode selection — these three concepts cover ~85% of all KTU questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming the Timers in Assembly Language and C Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Programming the Timers in Assembly Language and C Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller** architecture, **Timers/Counters** are dedicated on-chip hardware peripherals used for precise time-delay generation, event counting, and baud-rate generation for serial communication. Programming the timers involves configuring their operational mode (via the **TMOD** register), controlling their run/stop state and overflow flags (via the **TCON** register), and loading initial count values into the **THx (High byte)** and **TLx (Low byte)** registers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 contains **two 16-bit timers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1). Some enhanced variants (8052) include Timer 2 with auto-reload and capture capabilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student timing yourself while holding your breath underwater.

- The **oscillator crystal** is like the ticking of a precise stopwatch — each tick is a clock pulse.
- The **timer registers (TH0, TL0)** are like the **counter display** of the stopwatch that starts from a preset value and counts UP to maximum (e.g., 65535).
- The **Timer Flag (TF0/TF1)** is the **buzzer** that rings when the counter "overflows" past its maximum — telling the CPU, "Time is up!"
- The **TMOD register** is the **mode selector knob** that lets you choose between 8-bit, 13-bit, or 16-bit counting.
- The **TR0/TR1 bit** in TCON is the **START/STOP button** of the stopwatch.

So, **programming the timer** simply means: *choose the mode, preset the starting number, press start, and listen for the buzzer (overflow flag) to know when a specific duration has elapsed.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key 8051 Timer Specifications**
> - **Number of Timers:** 2 (Timer 0, Timer 1) — both are **16-bit**, software-configurable.
> - **Default Oscillator Frequency (f<sub>osc</sub>):** **11.0592 MHz** (used in KTU labs and standard KTU board questions).
> - **Machine Cycle Frequency:** **f<sub>osc</sub>/12** (one machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**).
> - **Machine Cycle Time (T<sub>mc</sub>):** **12 / f<sub>osc</sub>** = **1.085 µs** for 11.0592 MHz.
> - **Maximum Count (Mode 1):** 2<sup>16</sup> = **65,536**.
> - **Timer Increment Rate:** Timer increments once every **machine cycle** (i.e., every 1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz).

---

## 1.4 The Four Key Timer Registers (Quick Map)

| Register | Full Name | Function | Address |
|----------|-----------|----------|---------|
| **TMOD** | Timer Mode | Selects Mode (0/1/2/3), Counter/Timer, Gating | **89H** |
| **TCON** | Timer Control | Controls Run flag (TRx), Overflow flag (TFx) | **88H** |
| **TL0** | Timer 0 Low Byte | Holds low 8 bits of Timer 0 count | **8AH** |
| **TH0** | Timer 0 High Byte | Holds high 8 bits of Timer 0 count | **8CH** |
| **TL1** | Timer 1 Low Byte | Holds low 8 bits of Timer 1 count | **8BH** |
| **TH1** | Timer 1 High Byte | Holds high 8 bits of Timer 1 count | **8DH** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TMOD Register — Mode Configuration

The **TMOD** register is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** register. Its upper nibble controls **Timer 1** and the lower nibble controls **Timer 0**.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} |

### Bit Definitions:
- **GATE**: 
  - `1` → Timer runs only when **INTx pin is HIGH** AND **TRx = 1` (hardware gating).
  - `0` → Timer runs whenever **TRx = 1` (software control only).
- **C/T̄**:
  - `1` → **Counter mode** (counts external events on T0/T1 pin).
  - `0` → **Timer mode** (counts internal machine cycles).
- **M1, M0** → Mode selection bits.

### Timer Modes (M1 M0):

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Width |
|----|----|------|-------------|-------|
| 0  | 0  | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatible) | 13-bit |
| 0  | 1  | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer (most common) | 16-bit |
| 1  | 0  | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload | 8-bit |
| 1  | 1  | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) | 8-bit + 8-bit |

## 2.2 The TCON Register — Control & Flags

The **TCON** register is **bit-addressable** (address 88H).

| Bit | 7 (TF1) | 6 (TR1) | 5 (TF0) | 4 (TR0) | 3 (IE1) | 2 (IT1) | 1 (IE0) | 0 (IT0) |
|----|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Function | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | Timer 1 Run Control | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | Timer 0 Run Control | — | — | — | — |

- **TR0 / TR1**: `1` = Start timer, `0` = Stop timer.
- **TF0 / TF1**: Set to `1` by hardware on overflow; must be **cleared by software**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in almost every KTU numerical!**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

$$
\text{Delay (per overflow)} = (2^n - \text{Initial Count}) \times T_{mc}
$$

$$
\text{Total Delay} = \text{Delay per overflow} \times \text{Number of overflows}
$$

$$
\text{Total Delay} = N \times (2^n - X) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{mc}$ = Machine cycle time
- $f_{osc}$ = Oscillator frequency (Hz)
- $n$ = Number of timer bits (8, 13, or 16)
- $X$ = Initial value loaded into the timer registers
- $N$ = Number of overflows (loop iterations)

### Common Initial Values Table (Mode 1, f<sub>osc</sub> = 11.0592 MHz)

| Delay (approx) | Initial Count (decimal) | TH value (hex) | TL value (hex) |
|----------------|------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| ~50 µs         | 65,536 − 46 = 65,490   | FFH            | C4H            |
| ~1 ms          | 65,536 − 922 = 64,614  | FCH            | 26H            |
| ~10 ms         | 65,536 − 9216 = 56,320 | DCH            | 00H            |
| ~50 ms         | 65,536 − 46080 = 19,456| 4CH            | 00H            |
| **25 ms (full)** | **65,536 − 23,040 = 42,496** | **A6H** | **00H** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Time Delays:** Replacing software delay loops (which are CPU-blocking and inaccurate) with hardware timers allows the CPU to perform other tasks.
- **Baud Rate Generation:** Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** is the standard KTU exam topic for generating standard baud rates (9600, 4800, etc.) for serial UART communication.
- **Event Counting:** Using **C/T̄ = 1`, the 8051 can count external events (e.g., objects on a conveyor belt, pulses from a sensor).
- **PWM Generation:** Timers form the basis of Pulse Width Modulation used in motor speed control, LED dimming, and servo control.
- **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** System tick interrupts from timers are the heartbeat of any RTOS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Generating a 50 ms Delay Using Timer 0 in Mode 1

### Given Problem (KTU Standard):
Using **Timer 0, Mode 1** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal, generate a delay of **50 ms**. Show the TH0, TL0 values.

### Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle Period
The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally.

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

Substitute $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz:

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085 \; \mu s
$$

> **Interpretation:** The timer increments by 1 every 1.085 microseconds.

### Step 2: Calculate the Required Number of Timer Ticks
To get a 50 ms delay, we need:

$$
\text{Ticks required} = \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{mc}}
$$

$$
\text{Ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\text{Ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\text{Ticks} = 46,082.949 \dots
$$

### Step 3: Determine the Initial Count Value
In Mode 1, the timer is **16-bit**, so the maximum count = $2^{16} = 65,536$.

Since 46,082 < 65,536, a **single overflow** is sufficient.

$$
\text{Initial Count } (X) = 2^{16} - \text{Ticks}
$$

$$
X = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083
$$

$$
X = 19{,}453
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Hexadecimal for TH0 and TL0
Convert 19,453 to hex:

$$
19{,}453 \div 16 = 1215 \text{ remainder } 13 \rightarrow D
$$
$$
1215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ remainder } 15 \rightarrow F
$$
$$
75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 11 \rightarrow B
$$
$$
4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \rightarrow 4
$$

Reading remainders from bottom to top: **4BFDH**

Therefore:
- **TH0 = 4BH** (high byte)
- **TL0 = FDH** (low byte)

### Step 5: Verify the Actual Delay
The timer will count from **4BFDH = 19,453** up to **FFFFH = 65,535**, then overflow to **0000H** (1 extra count).

$$
\text{Actual Ticks} = 65{,}536 - 19{,}453 = 46{,}083
$$

$$
\text{Actual Delay} = 46{,}083 \times 1.085 \; \mu s
$$

$$
\text{Actual Delay} = 50{,}000.055 \; \mu s \approx 50 \; ms \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Generating a 1 Second Delay Using Mode 1 + Software Loop

### Problem:
Generate a **1 second** delay using Timer 0, Mode 1, with **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Use a software loop.

### Step 1: Maximum Possible Delay in Mode 1 (Single Overflow)
$$
\text{Max Delay} = 65{,}536 \times 1.085 \; \mu s = 71.11 \; ms
$$

Since **1 second >> 71.11 ms**, we need multiple overflows.

### Step 2: Choose a Convenient Sub-Delay
Let's use **~50 ms** per overflow (calculated above as 46,083 ticks, X = 4BFDH).

### Step 3: Calculate Number of Overflows
$$
N = \frac{1000 \; ms}{50 \; ms} = 20
$$

### Step 4: Loop Strategy
Repeat the 50 ms overflow exactly **20 times** using a register (e.g., R7) as a counter.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Timer Mode Comparison

| Property | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) | Mode 3 (Split) |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Width | 13 bits | 16 bits | 8 bits | 8 + 8 bits |
| Max Count | 8192 | 65,536 | 256 | 256 each |
| Max Delay (12 MHz) | 8.192 ms | 65.536 ms | 256 µs | 256 µs each |
| Auto-Reload | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Common Use | Legacy 8048 code | General delays | **Baud rate gen** | Extra 8-bit timer |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Low | **Very High** | **Very High** | Low |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Auto-Reload Mode 2 (Baud Rate Generation)

### Problem:
Find TH1 value for **9600 baud** with **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

### Step 1: Standard KTU Formula
$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

For 9600 baud with SMOD = 0:

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

### Step 2: Solve for TH1
$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 9600}
$$

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3{,}686{,}400}
$$

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = 3
$$

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}
$$

This is why **TH1 = FDH** appears in **every KTU serial port program**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TMOD Register Bit-Field Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TMOD Register - 8 bits] --> B[Upper Nibble - Timer 1]
    A --> C[Lower Nibble - Timer 0]
    
    B --> B1[GATE - Bit 7]
    B --> B2[CT_bar - Bit 6]
    B --> B3[M1 - Bit 5]
    B --> B4[M0 - Bit 4]
    
    C --> C1[GATE - Bit 3]
    C --> C2[CT_bar - Bit 2]
    C --> C3[M1 - Bit 1]
    C --> C4[M0 - Bit 0]
    
    B1 --> D1{Hardware or Software Control}
    C1 --> D1
    
    B2 --> D2{Timer or Counter Mode}
    C2 --> D2
    
    B3 --> D3{Mode Selection 0 to 3}
    B4 --> D3
    C3 --> D3
    C4 --> D3
```

## 4.2 Timer Programming Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Power On Reset] --> B[Step 1: Configure TMOD Register]
    B --> B1[Select Timer: T0 or T1]
    B --> B2[Select Mode: M1 M0]
    B --> B3[Select Mode: Timer or Counter]
    B --> B4[Set GATE bit for gating]
    
    B --> C[Step 2: Load Initial Count]
    C --> C1[Load THx with High byte]
    C --> C2[Load TLx with Low byte]
    
    C --> D[Step 3: Start the Timer]
    D --> D1[Set TRx bit in TCON to 1]
    
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Wait for Overflow]
    E --> E1{Monitor TFx flag in TCON}
    E1 -->|Not Set| E
    E1 -->|Set to 1| F[Step 5: Timer Overflow Occurred]
    
    F --> G[Step 6: Stop Timer]
    G --> G1[Clear TRx bit to 0]
    
    G1 --> H[Step 7: Clear Overflow Flag]
    H --> H1[Clear TFx bit by software]
    
    H1 --> I[Step 8: Execute Desired Action]
    I --> I1[Toggle LED]
    I --> I2[Increment Counter]
    I --> I3[Generate Waveform]
    
    I --> J{Repeat Needed?}
    J -->|Yes| C
    J -->|No| K[End of Program]
```

## 4.3 Timer 0 Functional Block Diagram (8051 Internal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[Oscillator f_osc - 11.0592 MHz] --> DIV[Divide by 12]
    DIV --> MC[Machine Cycle - 1.085 us]
    
    MC --> SW1{Mux - C/T_bar}
    EXT[External Pin T0 P3.4] --> SW1
    
    SW1 -->|C/T_bar = 0| TMR[Timer Mode]
    SW1 -->|C/T_bar = 1| CTR[Counter Mode]
    
    TMR --> REG[TH0 TL0 Registers - 16 bits]
    CTR --> REG
    
    GATE{GATE AND TR0} --> REG
    INT[INT0 Pin P3.2] --> GATE
    
    REG --> INC[Increment Every Cycle]
    INC --> OV[Overflow Detection]
    
    OV -->|FFFFH to 0000H| TFLAG[Set TF0 Flag in TCON]
    TFLAG --> CPU[Interrupt CPU or Poll by Software]
    
    CPU --> ACT[Execute ISR or Action]
```

## 4.4 Complete Timer Program Flow in Assembly

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant TMOD
    participant TCON
    participant TH_TL as TH0/TL0
    
    Note over CPU: Program Initialization
    CPU->>TMOD: MOV TMOD, 01H (Timer 0, Mode 1)
    CPU->>TH_TL: MOV TH0, 4BH
    CPU->>TH_TL: MOV TL0, FDH
    
    Note over CPU: Start Timer
    CPU->>TCON: SETB TR0
    
    loop Wait for Overflow
        CPU->>TCON: JNB TF0, loop
    end
    
    Note over CPU: Overflow Detected
    CPU->>TCON: CLR TR0 (Stop Timer)
    CPU->>TCON: CLR TF0 (Clear Flag)
    CPU->>TH_TL: Reload TH0, TL0
    CPU->>TCON: SETB TR0 (Restart for loop)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of the TMOD and TCON registers in the 8051 microcontroller. List the different timer modes.**

#### Model Answer:
- **TMOD (Timer Mode Register, Address 89H):** It is an 8-bit non-bit-addressable register used to configure the operating mode of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The upper nibble controls Timer 1 and the lower nibble controls Timer 0. It selects:
  - **GATE** bit: enables hardware/software control
  - **C/T̄** bit: selects Timer or Counter mode
  - **M1, M0** bits: select one of four modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)
- **TCON (Timer Control Register, Address 88H):** It is an 8-bit bit-addressable register that controls the start/stop operation (TR0, TR1) and contains the overflow flag bits (TF0, TF1) for the timers.
- **Four Timer Modes:**
  - **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (legacy)
  - **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer (most common for delays)
  - **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload (used for baud rate)
  - **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 only)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of the C/T̄ bit in the TMOD register? Differentiate between Timer and Counter modes.**

#### Model Answer:
The **C/T̄ (Counter/Timer select)** bit in the TMOD register determines the clock source for the timer:
- When **C/T̄ = 0**: The timer operates in **Timer mode**, where it counts **internal machine cycles** derived from the oscillator. This is used for generating time delays.
- When **C/T̄ = 1**: The timer operates in **Counter mode**, where it counts **external events/pulses** applied to pin **T0 (P3.4)** for Timer 0 or **T1 (P3.5)** for Timer 1. This is used for event counting.

In Timer mode, the increment rate is fixed by the oscillator. In Counter mode, the increment rate depends on the external input frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language program to generate a delay of **50 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Assume the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. Show all calculations for the TH0 and TL0 values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 C program (using Keil C) to continuously toggle all the bits of **Port P1** with a **500 µs delay** using **Timer 0 in Mode 2**. Show the calculation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Calculations:**
As derived in Section 3.1:
- $T_{mc} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 1.085 \; \mu s$
- Ticks required = $50 \times 10^{-3} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \approx 46{,}083$
- Initial value = $65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453 = 4BFDH$
- **TH0 = 4BH, TL0 = FDH**

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H

MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Timer mode
        MOV TH0, #4BH     ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #0FDH    ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0          ; Start Timer 0

WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT     ; Wait for overflow (TF0 = 1)
        CLR TR0           ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0           ; Clear overflow flag
        ; --- 50 ms delay completed ---
        SJMP HERE          ; Stay here (or add main task)

HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Calculations for 500 µs in Mode 2:**
- Mode 2 is 8-bit (max 256), auto-reload.
- Ticks = $500 \times 10^{-6} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \approx 461$ ticks.
- Since **461 > 255**, a single overflow is **NOT possible** in Mode 2.

**Correction — Use a smaller sub-delay like 200 µs:**
- Ticks = $200 \times 10^{-6} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \approx 184$ ticks
- TH0 = 256 − 184 = 72 = **48H**
- This gives 200 µs per overflow, loop **2.5 times** → not exact.

**Better approach: Use Mode 1 for 500 µs:**
- Ticks = $500 \times 10^{-6} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \approx 461$
- Initial = 65,536 − 461 = 65,075 = **FE33H**
- TH0 = FEH, TL0 = 33H

**Keil C Program (using 500 µs via Mode 1 internally, or 250 µs × 2):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_Delay_500us(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1
    TL0 = 0x33;      // Low byte
    TH0 = 0xFE;      // High byte
    TR0 = 1;          // Start timer
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow
    TR0 = 0;          // Stop timer
    TF0 = 0;          // Clear flag
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P1 = 0x00;            // All bits LOW
        Timer0_Delay_500us();
        P1 = 0xFF;            // All bits HIGH
        Timer0_Delay_500us();
    }
}
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **Auto-Reload Mode (Mode 2)** of the 8051 timer. Write an Assembly program to generate a square wave of **5 kHz** frequency on **pin P1.0** using Timer 0 in Mode 2. Crystal = **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TMOD register** format. Write a C program using Timer 1 in Mode 2 to generate a baud rate of **9600** for serial communication. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mode 2 Explanation:**
Mode 2 is an **8-bit auto-reload timer**. In this mode, **TLx** acts as the 8-bit counter, and **THx** holds the **reload value**. When TLx overflows from FFH to 00H, the TFx flag is set AND THx automatically reloads its value into TLx — making it ideal for **continuous, exact periodic operations** like baud rate generation.

**Square Wave Calculation for 5 kHz:**
- Time period T = 1/5000 = 200 µs
- Half period (ON time / OFF time) = 100 µs
- Ticks per half period = $100 \times 10^{-6} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \approx 92$ ticks
- TH0 = 256 − 92 = 164 = **A4H**
- TL0 will also be loaded with A4H initially.

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H

MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #02H    ; Timer 0, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV TH0, #0A4H    ; Reload value
        MOV TL0, #0A4H    ; Initial count
        SETB P1.0          ; P1.0 initially HIGH
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer

LOOP:   JNB TF0, LOOP      ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TF0            ; Clear flag
        CPL P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0
        SJMP LOOP          ; Repeat
        END
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**TMOD Diagram:** See Section 4.1 for the complete TMOD bit-field architecture.

**Baud Rate Calculation for 9600:**
From the standard formula (SMOD = 0):

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}
$$

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}
$$

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}
$$

**Keil C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Serial_Init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;     // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1 = 0xFD;      // Reload value for 9600 baud
    SCON = 0x50;     // Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN enabled
    TR1 = 1;          // Start Timer 1
}

void main(void) {
    char msg[] = "KTU";
    int i;
    Serial_Init();
    while (1) {
        for (i = 0; msg[i] != '\0'; i++) {
            SBUF = msg[i];        // Load character
            while (TI == 0);       // Wait until transmitted
            TI = 0;                // Clear TI flag
        }
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TF0/TF1 in software** → The flag is set by hardware but NOT cleared automatically. The timer will appear to "instantly" overflow next time.
> 2. **Wrong TMOD value:** For Timer 0 in Mode 1, TMOD should be **01H** (NOT 10H, which is Timer 1). KTU students frequently mix this up.
> 3. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1** — Mode 1 does NOT auto-reload. If you want a periodic delay, you MUST reload the initial value after each overflow.
> 4. **Confusing Machine Cycle with Oscillator frequency:** Always divide f<sub>osc</sub> by **12** to get the timer increment rate.
> 5. **Off-by-one in delay calculation:** The timer counts from the loaded value up to and including FFFFH (one extra count), so use $2^n - X$ not $2^n - X - 1$.
> 6. **SCON and TMOD confusion** in serial programs — these are two different registers; SCON is for the UART, TMOD is for the timer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two timers, four modes** — 8051 has Timer 0 and Timer 1, each with Modes 0, 1, 2, and (for Timer 0) 3.
- **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the **workhorse** for KTU delay problems; always reload the initial value manually.
- **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is used for **baud rate generation** and continuous square waves.
- **Machine cycle = 12 / f<sub>osc</sub>** — at 11.0592 MHz, $T_{mc} = 1.085 \; \mu s$.
- **Delay formula:** $D = (2^n - X) \times T_{mc} \times N$ where N is the number of overflows.
- **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable** — must use byte-level instructions like `MOV TMOD, #01H`.
- **TCON IS bit-addressable** — can use `SETB TR0`, `CLR TF0`.
- **TFx must be cleared by software** after overflow detection.
- **GATE bit = 1** requires external INTx pin to be HIGH for the timer to run.
- **C/T̄ = 0 → Timer mode** (internal); **C/T̄ = 1 → Counter mode** (external pin).
- **For 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz:** TH1 = FDH, TMOD = 20H, SCON = 50H, TR1 = 1 — memorize this combination.
- **Maximum delays:** Mode 1 = 71.11 ms (single overflow at 11.0592 MHz); for longer delays, use a software loop to count multiple overflows.
- **TH0 and TL0 addresses:** TH0 = 8CH, TL0 = 8AH, TH1 = 8DH, TL1 = 8BH — required when using `MOV direct` instructions.
- **In Keil C:** Use `<reg51.h>` header for SFR definitions; `TR0`, `TF0`, `TMOD` are pre-defined symbols.
- **Always comment the calculation** in C programs for partial marks safety in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Communication and Interrupts: Introduction to Serial Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication and Interrupts: Introduction to Serial Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Serial Communication** is the process of transmitting data between a microcontroller and an external device (or between two microcontrollers) **one bit at a time, sequentially, over a single communication line or channel**. In the context of the 8051 microcontroller, serial communication is implemented through its on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** peripheral, which converts parallel data from the CPU into a serial bit-stream for transmission and reconstructs parallel data from an incoming serial bit-stream during reception.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBICT504 – Module 3):**
> Serial Communication and Interrupts — Introduction to Serial Communication; Serial data transfer schemes; Asynchronous serial communication; 8051 serial port programming; Interrupts of 8051.

The 8051's serial port is a **full-duplex** port, meaning it can transmit and receive simultaneously. It is accessed through two special-function registers:
- **SBUF (Serial Buffer Register)** – Address 99H. Writing to SBUF loads the data to be transmitted; reading from SBUF retrieves the received byte.
- **SCON (Serial Control Register)** – Address 98H. Configures the operational mode and controls the transmit/receive flags.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "One-Lane Highway" Model

Imagine two cities separated by a long distance:

| Communication Type | Real-World Analogy | Data Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel** | An 8-lane highway | All 8 bits travel together on 8 separate wires |
| **Serial** | A single-lane road | All 8 bits travel one after another on **one wire** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use serial when parallel is faster per cycle?**
> Parallel communication requires **N wires for N bits**, suffers from **clock skew** (bits arriving at slightly different times on long cables), and is **expensive** for long distances. Serial communication uses just **1 or 2 wires**, has **no skew problem**, is **cheaper**, and is the industry standard for devices like GPS modules, Bluetooth, GSM, and PCs (USB/UART).

---

### 1.3 The Three Modes of Data Flow

```
Direction of Data
       ┌─────────┐
       │  Mode 1 │         Mode 2
       │  SIMPLEX│◄─────────────
       │         │  One-way only
       └─────────┘

       ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐
       │ Device A│◄──►│ Device B│
       │         │    │         │
       └─────────┘    └─────────┘
       Mode 3: FULL DUPLEX (Both directions simultaneously)

       ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐
       │ Device A│◄──►│ Device B│
       │         │    │         │
       └─────────┘    └─────────┘
       Mode 4: HALF DUPLEX (Both directions, but NOT simultaneously)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplex** = One-way street (e.g., TV broadcasting, mouse → PC).
> **Half-Duplex** = Two-way street, but only one car at a time (e.g., walkie-talkie).
> **Full-Duplex** = Two separate lanes — traffic flows both ways simultaneously (e.g., telephone, 8051 UART).

---

### 1.4 Serial vs Parallel — KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Parallel Communication | Serial Communication |
|---|---|---|
| Wires Required | **N** (e.g., 8 for 8-bit data) | **1** (data) + a few control lines |
| Speed (short distance) | **Faster** | Slower |
| Speed (long distance) | Slower (skew, attenuation) | **Faster in practice** |
| Cost | **High** (more cables, connectors) | Low |
| Clock Skew | **Severe problem** | Negligible |
| Typical Use | Internal buses (data bus, address bus) | USB, RS-232, SPI, I²C, UART |
| KTU Example | 8051's external data bus (P0) | 8051's TXD (P3.1) and RXD (P3.0) pins |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Serial Communication Schemes

Serial communication is broadly classified into two categories based on the presence/absence of a shared clock:

```
                Serial Communication
                       │
        ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
        │                             │
   Synchronous                  Asynchronous
   (Clock shared)               (No shared clock)
        │                             │
   ┌────┴────┐                  ┌────┴────┐
   │         │                  │         │
  SPI      I²C                UART      RS-232
```

#### 2.1.1 Synchronous Serial Communication
- A **dedicated clock line** (e.g., SCLK in SPI) accompanies the data line.
- Transmitter and receiver are synchronized to this shared clock.
- **Higher data rate** for a given baud rate because no start/stop bits are needed.
- Examples: **SPI, I²C, Microwire**.

#### 2.1.2 Asynchronous Serial Communication
- **No clock line** is transmitted. The transmitter and receiver must agree **beforehand** on:
  1. **Baud rate** (bits per second)
  2. **Frame format** (number of data bits, parity, stop bits)
- Each data byte is framed with **start and stop bits** to mark its boundaries.
- Examples: **UART, RS-232, RS-422, RS-485**.

---

### 2.2 The UART Asynchronous Frame Format

A UART transmits each byte as a **frame**. The standard 8051 frame (Mode 1) is shown below:

```
      ┌───┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐
LINE  │ 1 │ │0│ │D0│ │D1│ │D2│ │D3│ │D4│ │D5│ │D6│ │ D7  │ │  1  │  ...
      └───┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─────┘ └─────┘
       IDLE  START D0  D1  D2  D3  D4  D5  D6   D7    STOP
       (1)   (0)  LSB transmitted first          (1)   (1, or 1.5, or 2)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line Idle state is logic HIGH (1).** The transition from IDLE (1) to a START bit (0) tells the receiver that a new frame is beginning. The receiver samples the line at the **center of each bit period** (16× oversampling in 8051) to maximize noise immunity.

| Frame Element | Width | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Start Bit** | 1 bit | Always **logic 0**; marks the beginning of a frame |
| **Data Bits** | 5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 | LSB transmitted **first** (8051 convention) |
| **Parity Bit** | 0 or 1 bit | Optional error-detection (Even or Odd) |
| **Stop Bit(s)** | 1, 1.5, or 2 bits | Always **logic 1**; marks end of frame |

**Total frame size for 8051 Mode 1 (8-N-1):** 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop = **10 bits per byte**.

---

### 2.3 The SCON Register (Serial Control — 98H)

The 8051 configures its serial port via the **SCON** register:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |
| Default | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

| Bit | Function |
|---|---|
| **SM0, SM1** | Select serial port **mode** (0, 1, 2, 3) — see table below |
| **SM2** | Multiprocessor communication enable (Mode 2/3) |
| **REN** | **Receive Enable** — must be set by software to allow reception |
| **TB8** | 9th transmitted data bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| **RB8** | 9th received data bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at end of last stop bit; cleared by software |
| **RI** | **Receive Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at mid of last stop bit; cleared by software |

### 2.4 Serial Port Modes (KTU High-Priority)

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous) | $f_{osc} / 12$ (fixed) |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | **8-bit UART** (most common) | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART (fixed baud) | $f_{osc} / 32$ or $f_{osc} / 64$ |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART (variable baud) | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Examined Mode:** **Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate)**. You will almost certainly be asked to calculate the Timer 1 reload value for a given baud rate.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ | Baud Rate (bits/second) | Agreed between Tx and Rx |
| $f_{osc}$ | Crystal oscillator frequency | e.g., **11.0592 MHz**, 12 MHz |
| $T_{bit}$ | Time per bit | $T_{bit} = 1 / B$ seconds |
| $N_{frames}$ | Total bits per character | $1 + \text{data} + \text{parity} + \text{stop}$ (Mode 1, 8-N-1 → 10) |
| $T_{frame}$ | Total frame duration | $T_{frame} = N_{frames} \times T_{bit}$ |
| **TH1** | Timer 1 reload (Mode 2) | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times B}$ (SMOD = 0) |
| **TH1 (SMOD=1)** | With SMOD bit set in PCON | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Magic" 11.0592 MHz Crystal**
> The 8051 community uses **11.0592 MHz** (and its multiples) as the standard crystal because $11.0592 \times 10^6 / (384 \times B)$ yields **exact integer values** for standard baud rates (1200, 2400, 9600, 19200, 57600). For example, at 9600 baud: TH1 = 256 − 3 = **253 = 0xFD** — perfectly integral.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **PC ↔ Microcontroller communication** (USB-to-UART bridges like CH340, FT232).
- **GPS modules** (NEO-6M) communicate over UART at 9600 baud.
- **GSM/4G modems** (SIM800, SIM7600) use AT commands over UART.
- **Bluetooth modules** (HC-05) use UART as their programming interface.
- **Industrial automation**: RS-485 networks link hundreds of sensors to a master MCU.
- **Debugging**: Print statements from embedded firmware appear on a "Serial Monitor."

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the TH1 Reload Formula (KTU Frequently Asked)

This derivation is the **most important analytical step** in serial communication. It is asked almost every semester in KTU exams.

**Goal:** Find the value that must be loaded into **TH1** (Timer 1, Mode 2 — 8-bit auto-reload) so that the 8051's UART generates a specific baud rate $B$ from a given crystal frequency $f_{osc}$.

---

#### Step 1: Establish the Timer 1 Overflow Rate

The 8051's machine cycle is **12 oscillator periods**, so Timer 1 is clocked at $f_{osc} / 12$.

In Mode 2, Timer 1 increments from the value in TH1 up to 255, then **overflows to 0** and is automatically reloaded. The time for one full cycle (0 → 255 → 0) is:

$$
T_{overflow} = \frac{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}{f_{osc}} \quad \text{seconds}
$$

The **overflow frequency** (number of overflows per second) is the inverse:

$$
f_{overflow} = \frac{1}{T_{overflow}} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \quad \text{Hz}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Apply the UART's Internal Divider

The 8051 UART divides the incoming clock from Timer 1 by a factor of **16** (when SMOD = 0) to generate the actual baud rate. This 16× division is what enables the UART to **sample each bit at its center** (it counts 16 internal ticks per bit period; the transition at the start of the bit sets the counter, and the sample is taken at tick 8, 9, …, 16).

$$
B = \frac{f_{overflow}}{16} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times 16 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$
B = \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

---

#### Step 3: Solve for TH1

Rearranging to isolate $(256 - \text{TH1})$:

$$
192 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{f_{osc}}{B}
$$

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the factor 384:** When written in its more familiar form $\text{TH1} = 256 - f_{osc} / (384 \times B)$, the **384** is the product of the **machine-cycle factor 12** and the **UART divider 32** (the UART's "natural" division ratio). This is equivalent because some derivations start from the 32× divider directly. The 192 form (which I derived above) and the 384 form are mathematically identical when SMOD = 0, just expressed using different reference points. For SMOD = 1 (PCON.7 set), the divisor halves → $192 \rightarrow 96$ (or equivalently $384 \rightarrow 192$).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Compute the value to be loaded into **TH1** to operate the serial port at **9600 baud**, 8-bit data, no parity, 1 stop bit (8-N-1), with **SMOD = 0**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $B = 9600 \text{ bits/second}$

Substituting into the derived formula:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{192 \times 9600}
$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$
192 \times 9600 = 1{,}843{,}200
$$

Then divide:

$$
\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}843{,}200} = 6
$$

So:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 6 = 250
$$

Convert to hex (KTU convention — register values are often in hex):

$$
250_{10} = \text{FA}_{16}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Easy-Math" Interpretation:** 11.0592 MHz is chosen specifically because $11.0592 / 0.192 = 57.6$ — when divided by 9600, you get exactly 6 (or 3 in the 384 formulation). No fractional values, no rounding error.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Baud Rates @ 11.0592 MHz

This is the matrix KTU examiners expect you to be able to reproduce from memory:

| Baud Rate (B) | $f_{osc} / (192 \times B)$ | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1200** | 48 | 208 | **0xD0** | 0% |
| **2400** | 24 | 232 | **0xE8** | 0% |
| **4800** | 12 | 244 | **0xF4** | 0% |
| **9600** | 6 | 250 | **0xFA** | 0% |
| **19200** | 3 | 253 | **0xFD** | 0% |
| **57600** | 1 | 255 | **0xFF** | 0% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The % Error = 0% for ALL of these** is the entire reason 11.0592 MHz is the standard. With a 12 MHz crystal, you'd get TH1 = 252.75 → 253 (rounded), introducing a **0.16% error** that accumulates and corrupts data within a few bytes.

---

### 3.4 Time-Per-Character Calculation (Secondary Derivation)

For a complete 10-bit frame (8-N-1) at 9600 baud:

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.17 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 10 \times T_{bit} = 10 \times 104.17 \text{ μs} = 1.0417 \text{ ms}
$$

Therefore, in one second, the maximum number of bytes transmissible:

$$
N_{bytes} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{0.0010417} \approx 960 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the 10% overhead:** At 9600 baud, you do NOT get 9600 bytes/second — you get ~10% less because of the start and stop bits.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the terms: Baud rate, Start bit, and Stop bit in asynchronous serial communication.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks / 100 words):**

- **Baud rate** is the rate at which bits are transmitted over the serial line, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. Both transmitter and receiver must be configured to the same baud rate for successful communication.
- The **Start bit** is a single **logic 0** bit that precedes every data byte. Since the line is held at logic 1 (idle) between frames, the 1→0 transition signals the receiver that a new byte is beginning.
- The **Stop bit** is one or two **logic 1** bits at the end of the frame, returning the line to its idle state and providing a guard period between consecutive bytes.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare serial and parallel communication. Mention one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks / table format):**

| Parameter | Serial | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Wires | 1 data wire | N data wires |
| Speed (long dist.) | **Preferred** (no skew) | Slower (clock skew) |
| Cost | **Low** | High |
| Internal CPU use | Slower | **Preferred** (CPU buses) |

- **Serial preferred:** Communication between microcontroller and PC (USB/UART), or between two distant MCUs.
- **Parallel preferred:** Internal data bus of the 8051 (P0) transferring data to memory or LCD.

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a)** — Frame Format and SCON Register

**Frame Format for 8-N-1 (Mode 1):**

```
        ┌────┐ ┌─┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌─────┐
LINE    │ 1  │ │0│ │ D0 │ │ D1 │ │ D2 │ │ D3 │ │ D4 │ │ D5 │ │ D6 │ │ D7  │ │  1  │
        └────┘ └─┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └─────┘
        IDLE  START  LSB → → → → → → → → → → → → → → → MSB     STOP
```

| Element | Bits | Value | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Start | 1 | 0 | Marks beginning of frame |
| Data | 8 | LSB first | The actual data byte |
| Parity | 0 | — | None in 8-N-1 |
| Stop | 1 | 1 | Returns line to idle, ends frame |

**Total frame size:** $1 + 8 + 1 = 10$ bits.

**SCON Register (98H) — Bit Functions:**

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | SM0 | Serial mode select bit 0 (combined with SM1 → Modes 0–3) |
| 6 | SM1 | Serial mode select bit 1 |
| 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| 4 | REN | **Receive Enable** — must be set (1) to allow serial reception |
| 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted data bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| 2 | RB8 | 9th received data bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| 1 | **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at end of last stop bit |
| 0 | **RI** | **Receive Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at midpoint of last stop bit |

> **For 7 marks:** Cover the diagram (3 marks), explain each frame element (2 marks), and SCON bit table (2 marks).

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b)** — TH1 Calculation @ 4800 Baud

**Given:**
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- $B = 4800 \text{ bps}$
- SMOD = 0

**Step 1:** Apply the TH1 formula:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator:

$$
192 \times 4800 = 921{,}600
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the fraction:

$$
\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{921{,}600} = 12
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract from 256:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 12 = 244
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hexadecimal (KTU convention):

$$
244_{10} = \text{F4}_{16}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the time per bit:

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{4800} = 208.33 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the time per frame (8-N-1 = 10 bits):

$$
T_{frame} = 10 \times 208.33 \text{ μs} = 2083.3 \text{ μs} \approx 2.083 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $\text{TH1} = 244 = \text{0xF4}$
- Time to transmit 1 byte ≈ **2.083 ms**

> **For 7 marks:** Formula (1 mark), substitution (1 mark), division result (1 mark), subtraction result (1 mark), hex conversion (1 mark), time-per-byte (2 marks).

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a)** — Modes of Data Communication

**Simplex Communication:**
- Data flows in **one direction only**.
- One device is the transmitter, the other is permanently the receiver.
- The receiver cannot reply.
- **Examples:** Radio broadcasting, television transmission, keyboard → computer.

**Half-Duplex Communication:**
- Data can flow in **both directions**, but **not simultaneously**.
- The shared channel is used alternately by the two devices.
- **Examples:** Walkie-talkie, single-wire RS-485 bus, citizen-band (CB) radio.

**Full-Duplex Communication:**
- Data can flow in **both directions simultaneously**.
- Requires **two independent physical channels** (or one channel with frequency/time-division separation).
- **Examples:** Telephone line, 8051's serial port (TXD + RXD), Ethernet.

**Why the 8051 is Full-Duplex:**
The 8051's serial port has **two separate physical pins** — **P3.1 (TXD)** for transmission and **P3.0 (RXD)** for reception. These pins operate **independently and concurrently**, so the MCU can send a byte while simultaneously receiving a different byte.

> **For 7 marks:** 2 marks per mode + 1 mark for 8051 justification.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b)** — Synchronous vs Asynchronous, Role of Timer 1

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous Serial Communication:**

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Clock line | **Shared** clock transmitted alongside data | **No clock line**; both sides use independent local clocks |
| Synchronization | Continuous, bit-level | Frame-level (via start/stop bits) |
| Speed | **Faster** (no start/stop overhead) | Slower (~10–20% overhead) |
| Hardware complexity | Higher (clock generation on both sides) | Lower |
| Examples | SPI, I²C | UART, RS-232 |

**Role of Timer 1 in Mode 1 Baud Rate Generation:**

1. The 8051's UART in **Mode 1** does **not** have a fixed baud rate — it needs an external clock source.
2. **Timer 1**, configured in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**, is the standard baud-rate generator.
3. The 8-bit value in **TH1** determines the overflow frequency:
   $$f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$
4. The UART divides this overflow rate by **16** to produce the actual baud rate:
   $$B = \frac{f_{overflow}}{16} = \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$
5. Therefore, for a desired baud rate:
   $$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}$$
6. The value of TH1 is loaded into the **TL1 and TH1** registers of Timer 1 via `MOV TH1, #value` and `MOV TL1, #value`. Timer 1 must be started with `SETB TR1`.

> **For 7 marks:** Comparison table (3 marks), Timer 1 role explanation with formula (4 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3)**
> 1. **Confusing SMOD = 0 vs SMOD = 1 divisors.** Many students use $192$ when they should use $96$ (or vice versa) for SMOD = 1. Always re-read the question.
> 2. **Forgetting to start Timer 1.** A correct TH1 value is useless without `SETB TR1`. Examiners dock 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Using baud rate formula for mode 2/3 incorrectly.** In **Mode 2**, baud rate is fixed by $f_{osc}/32$ or $f_{osc}/64$ — Timer 1 is NOT used.
> 4. **Not converting TH1 to hexadecimal.** KTU expects hex (e.g., 0xFA). Decimal-only answers lose a half-mark in many papers.
> 5. **Confusing the direction of LSB/MSB transmission.** UART transmits **LSB first**, not MSB first. This is a favourite trick question.
> 6. **Failing to clear TI/RI flags** in a program — examiners specifically check this in code-based questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to Serial Communication (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ Serial = **1 bit at a time**; Parallel = N bits simultaneously. Serial is preferred for **long-distance, low-cost, low-pin** communication.
>
> ✅ Three data-flow modes: **Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex.** The 8051 is **Full-Duplex** (separate TXD and RXD pins).
>
> ✅ Two categories: **Synchronous** (with shared clock, e.g., SPI, I²C) and **Asynchronous** (no clock, uses start/stop bits, e.g., UART).
>
> ✅ **Asynchronous frame (8-N-1):** 1 Start (0) + 8 Data (LSB first) + 1 Stop (1) = **10 bits**. Line idle = logic 1.
>
> ✅ **Key registers:** **SBUF** (99H) — write to transmit, read to receive. **SCON** (98H) — controls mode and flags.
>
> ✅ **SCON bits to remember:** SM0, SM1 (mode), REN (receive enable), TI (TX flag), RI (RX flag). **TI and RI must be cleared by software.**
>
> ✅ **Mode 1** is the most-tested mode: 8-bit UART, variable baud, uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2** as baud generator.
>
> ✅ **Baud rate formula (SMOD = 0):** $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{192 \times B}$. (Equivalently $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times B}$ using the 384 formulation.)
>
> ✅ **With SMOD = 1 (PCON.7 set):** Divide by 192 instead of 384 (or 96 instead of 192 in the 192-formulation).
>
> ✅ **The "magic" crystal is 11.0592 MHz** — it produces exact integer TH1 values for all standard baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 57600).
>
> ✅ **Time per bit:** $T_{bit} = 1/B$ seconds. **Time per frame:** $T_{frame} = 10 \times T_{bit}$ (for 8-N-1).
>
> ✅ **Common Baud @ 11.0592 MHz cheat sheet:** 9600 → TH1 = 0xFA, 4800 → TH1 = 0xF4, 2400 → TH1 = 0xE8, 1200 → TH1 = 0xD0.
>
> ✅ **Don't forget:** `SETB TR1` to start Timer 1, and clear `TI` / `RI` after every TX/RX operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous and Asynchronous Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous and Asynchronous Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous and Asynchronous Communication** refers to the two fundamental paradigms of **serial data transfer** between a microcontroller and external peripherals (or another microcontroller). The classification is based on the presence or absence of a **shared clock signal** used to sample/transmit each bit.

In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology (PBICT504 – Microcontroller), this topic is anchored around the **8051 UART/USART block** and its software-programmable serial interface, supplemented by industry-standard synchronous protocols such as **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** and **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)**.

| Parameter | Asynchronous Communication | Synchronous Communication |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Line | **Not shared**; both ends rely on locally generated, identical baud-rate clocks | **Shared clock (SCLK)** provided by the master |
| Framing | Start bit + Data + Parity + Stop bit(s) | No start/stop bits; data valid on a clock edge |
| Typical Protocols | **RS-232, RS-485, UART** | **SPI, I²C, USART (sync mode)** |
| Speed | Limited by clock accuracy (±2–5 %) | Higher throughput, deterministic |
| Hardware Pins (8051) | **TXD (P3.1), RXD (P3.0)** | **TXD (clock), RXD (data)** when SCON configured in Mode 0 |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Postal Letter vs Phone Call"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (for first-time learners):**
>
> 📨 **Asynchronous ≈ Posting a Letter:**  
> You write the message, put it inside an envelope, seal it, and drop it in the mailbox. The receiver opens the envelope whenever he/she checks the mail. There is **no live handshake** — both parties independently follow the same *postal rules* (envelope size, stamp, address format). The receiver must know *in advance* how fast to read. If the postal rules are mismatched, the letter is unreadable.
>
> 📞 **Synchronous ≈ A Live Phone Call:**  
> Two people talk in real-time. There is a continuous shared rhythm — the speaker pauses, the listener nods, words flow on the same clock. If either person loses the rhythm, the conversation collapses instantly, but **throughput is enormous** and overhead is minimal.

The **envelope** in our analogy corresponds to the **frame** (Start + Data + Stop bits). The **postal speed** corresponds to the **baud rate**. The **phone call's rhythm** corresponds to the **SCLK line** in synchronous protocols.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
