> *We round up the count to 46,083 to ensure the delay is at least 50 ms (rounding down would produce a shorter delay than required).*

**Step 5 — Convert 19,453 to hexadecimal.**

$$19{,}453 \div 16 = 1215\,\text{remainder}\,13 \rightarrow D$$

$$1215 \div 16 = 75\,\text{remainder}\,15 \rightarrow F$$

$$75 \div 16 = 4\,\text{remainder}\,11 \rightarrow B$$

$$4 \div 16 = 0\,\text{remainder}\,4$$

Reading remainders bottom-to-top: **4BFDH**

**Step 6 — Split into TH0 and TL0.**

$$\text{TH0} = \text{High Byte} = 4\text{BH}$$

$$\text{TL0} = \text{Low Byte} = \text{FDH}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of the achieved delay.**

$$T_{achieved} = (65{,}536 - 19{,}453) \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$T_{achieved} = 46{,}083 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_{achieved} = 50{,}000.055\,\mu s \approx 50.00\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mode Comparison Table

| Property | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Width** | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit | Two × 8-bit |
| **Register Used** | TH + 5 bits of TL | Full TH + Full TL | TL (counts) / TH (reload) | TL0 + TH0 (separate) |
| **Max Count** | 8192 | 65,536 | 256 | 256 each |
| **Auto-Reload?** | No (manual) | No (manual) | **Yes (from TH)** | No (manual) |
| **Overflow Flag Set On** | 1FFFH → 0000H | FFFFH → 0000H | FFH → 00H (TL) | FFH → 00H (each) |
| **Available For** | T0 and T1 | T0 and T1 | T0 and T1 | **T0 only** |
| **Typical Use** | Legacy 8048 code | General delays | Baud rate gen | Extra timer channel |

## 3.3 Derivation: Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) — Computing TH1 for 9600 Baud

**Given:** 8051 UART, 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz crystal, Timer 1 in Mode 2.

**Step 1 — Recall the baud rate formula for 8051 UART in Mode 2:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Assuming SMOD = 0 (standard):

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for (256 − TH1).**

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 9600}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3{,}686{,}400}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute TH1.**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times 3} = \frac{921{,}600}{36} = 9600 \quad \checkmark$$

> This is precisely why the **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred in 8051 systems — it produces **exact, error-free baud rates** like 9600, 4800, 1200.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Timer/Counter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator Circuit 11.0592 MHz] --> B[Divide by 12 Machine Cycle Generator]
    B --> C{Switch C/T-bar 0=Timer, 1=Counter}
    C -- Timer Mode --> D[Internal Pulse Stream]
    C -- Counter Mode --> E[External Pin T0 P3.4 or T1 P3.5]
    D --> F[Mode Selector M1 M0 Logic]
    E --> F
    F --> G[13-bit Latch THx + 5bits TLx]
    F --> H[16-bit Latch THx and TLx]
    F --> I[8-bit TLx with Auto-Reload from THx]
    F --> J[Split Mode TL0 and TH0 Independent]
    G --> K[Increment Logic]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Overflow Detection}
    L -- Yes --> M[Set TEx Flag in TCON]
    L -- No --> K
    M --> N[Interrupt Request to CPU if Enabled]
```

## 4.2 State Diagram: Mode 2 Auto-Reload Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateDiagram
    [*] --> LoadTHx
    LoadTHx: Software Loads THx with Reload Value
    LoadTHx --> CopyTHxToTLx
    CopyTHxToTLx: Initial Copy THx to TLx
    CopyTHxToTLx --> CountUp
    CountUp: TLx Increments on Each Tick
    CountUp --> CountUp: TLx less than FFH
    CountUp --> Overflow
    Overflow: TLx Reaches FFH and Rolls Over
    Overflow --> SetTFx
    SetTFx: Hardware Sets TFx Flag
    SetTFx --> AutoReload
    AutoReload: THx Value Automatically Copies Back to TLx
    AutoReload --> CountUp
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart: Choosing the Right Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a Timer or Counter] --> Q1{What is the application?}
    Q1 -- Generate exact delay --> M1[Use Mode 1 16-bit]
    Q1 -- Generate serial baud rate --> M2[Use Mode 2 Auto-Reload]
    Q1 -- Need 3 independent timers --> M3[Use Mode 3 Split on Timer 0]
    Q1 -- Migrate old 8048 code --> M0[Use Mode 0 13-bit]
    M1 --> Note1[Manual reload required after overflow]
    M2 --> Note2[Automatic reload from THx]
    M3 --> Note3[Timer 1 must be stopped or used carefully]
    M0 --> Note0[Legacy mode rarely used today]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**List the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter. Which mode is used for baud rate generation, and why?**

**Model Answer:**
The 8051 Timer/Counter has **four modes of operation**:
1. **Mode 0** – 13-bit Timer/Counter (8048 compatible)
2. **Mode 1** – 16-bit Timer/Counter (most commonly used)
3. **Mode 2** – 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer/Counter
4. **Mode 3** – Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 only)

**Mode 2** is used for baud rate generation in serial communication. **Reason:** In Mode 2, when TLx overflows, it is **automatically reloaded with the value from THx**, producing a **continuous, jitter-free clock signal** — which is exactly what the 8051's UART hardware needs to maintain accurate bit timing.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Explain the role of the TMOD register with a neat bit-format diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** at address **89H** is an 8-bit non-bit-addressable SFR that configures both Timer 0 and Timer 1. Its bit layout is:

| GATE1 | C/T̄1 | M1_1 | M0_1 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | M1_0 | M0_0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|

- **GATE** – Hardware gating control. When set, timer runs only if INTx pin is HIGH and TRx is set.
- **C/T̄** – `0` selects **Timer mode** (internal clock); `1` selects **Counter mode** (external pin T0/T1).
- **M1, M0** – Mode select bits. Values `00`, `01`, `10`, `11` correspond to Modes 0, 1, 2, 3.

**Example:** `TMOD = 0x20` → Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (auto-reload), C/T̄ = 0 (timer), GATE = 0 (software start).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the four modes of operation of the 8051 Timer with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 C program to generate a delay of **25 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**, assuming a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Seven Marks Breakdown

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):** The 8051 has two 16-bit Timer/Counters, each with 4 operating modes selected via TMOD bits M1 and M0.

**(ii) Mode 0 — 13-bit Timer (1.5 marks):**
- Uses THx (8 bits) + lower 5 bits of TLx.
- Range: `0000H` to `1FFFH` (8192 counts).
- Legacy mode, compatible with 8048.
- **Diagram:** Show THx feeding into a 5-bit portion of TLx.

**(iii) Mode 1 — 16-bit Timer (1.5 marks):**
- Uses full THx and full TLx (16 bits).
- Range: `0000H` to `FFFFH` (65536 counts).
- Most widely used; requires **manual reload** after overflow.
- **Diagram:** THx and TLx cascaded as one 16-bit register.

**(iv) Mode 2 — 8-bit Auto-Reload (1.5 marks):**
- TLx is the counter; THx holds the reload value.
- On overflow, TLx is **automatically reloaded** from THx.
- Range: 00H to FFH (256 counts).
- **Diagram:** Show TLx receiving reload arrow from THx.

**(v) Mode 3 — Split Timer (1.5 marks):**
- Available for **Timer 0 only**.
- TL0 and TH0 act as **two independent 8-bit timers**.
- Timer 1 in this mode loses its flags (TR1, TF1 borrowed by TH0).
- Used when three timing channels are required.
- **Diagram:** Show TL0 and TH0 as parallel 8-bit blocks.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Seven Marks Breakdown

**Step 1 — Machine cycle frequency (1 mark):**

$$f_{MC} = \frac{11.0592\,\text{MHz}}{12} = 0.9216\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Tick duration (1 mark):**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{0.9216\,\text{MHz}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Required counts (1 mark):**

$$N = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 23{,}041.47 \approx 23{,}042 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4 — Initial value (1 mark):**

$$\text{Timer Value} = 65{,}536 - 23{,}042 = 42{,}494$$

**Step 5 — Hex conversion (1 mark):**

$$42{,}494 = \text{A5FEH}$$

$$\therefore \text{TH0} = \text{A5H}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{FEH}$$

**Step 6 — 8051 C Program (2 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_Delay_25ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0 = 0xA5;      // Load high byte
    TL0 = 0xFE;      // Load low byte
    TR0 = 1;         // Start Timer 0
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow flag
    TR0 = 0;         // Stop Timer 0
    TF0 = 0;         // Clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P1 = 0x00;             // LED ON
        Timer0_Delay_25ms();
        P1 = 0xFF;             // LED OFF
        Timer0_Delay_25ms();
    }
}
```

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TMOD and TCON registers** of the 8051. Discuss how the **GATE bit** affects timer operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses an **8051 with 12 MHz crystal**. Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a **4800 baud** rate for serial communication. Calculate the value of **TH1**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Seven Marks Breakdown

**TMOD Register (3 marks):**
- Address 89H, **not bit-addressable**, must be loaded as a byte.
- Two identical nibbles for Timer 1 (upper) and Timer 0 (lower).
- Bits: **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0**.

**TCON Register (2.5 marks):**
- Address 88H, **bit-addressable**.
- Upper 4 bits (TF1, TR1, TF0, TR0) relate to timers.
- Lower 4 bits (IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0) relate to external interrupts.

**GATE bit functionality (1.5 marks):**
- When **GATE = 0**: Timer is started/stopped purely by software using the **TRx bit** (software control).
- When **GATE = 1**: Timer is **hardware-gated** — it runs only when **both** the TRx bit is set **AND** the corresponding INTx pin (P3.2 or P3.3) is HIGH. Used to measure **pulse width** of external signals.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Seven Marks Breakdown

**Step 1 — Baud rate formula for UART (1 mark):**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2 — Take SMOD = 0 (1 mark):**

$$4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (1 mark):**

$$4800 = \frac{10^6}{32 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for (256 − TH1) (1 mark):**

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{10^6}{32 \times 4800} = \frac{10^6}{153{,}600} = 6.51$$

Since TH1 must be an integer, the closest standard value gives an error. **Use 11.0592 MHz instead in practice.** For the given 12 MHz, the answer is approximate.

**Step 5 — TH1 value (1 mark):**

$$\text{TH1} \approx 256 - 6 = 250 = \text{FAH}$$

(The actual baud rate achieved = $\frac{1}{32} \times \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \times 6} = 5208$ baud — an 8.5% error, which is why **11.0592 MHz** is preferred for UART.)

**Step 6 — 8051 C code snippet for Mode 2 setup (2 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void UART_Init_4800(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;     // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFA;     // Reload value for ~4800 baud (approx with 12 MHz)
    SCON = 0x50;     // 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    TR1  = 1;        // Start Timer 1
}

void main(void) {
    UART_Init_4800();
    SBUF = 'A';                // Transmit character 'A'
    while (TI == 0);           // Wait until transmission complete
    TI = 0;                    // Clear TI flag
    while (1);
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TFx flag** after overflow — code gets stuck in an infinite loop. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Wrong hex splitting** — writing TH0 = 0xFE and TL0 = 0xA5 instead of TH0 = 0xA5 and TL0 = 0xFE. Always re-check which is the high byte. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Using 12 MHz in UART problems** without stating the resulting baud rate error. Examiners expect you to mention that **11.0592 MHz is the standard crystal** for UART applications. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing Mode 0 and Mode 1** — Mode 0 is **13-bit**, not 12-bit and not 16-bit. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Not specifying "Mode 2 of Timer 1"** when explaining baud rate generation — the answer must mention *both* the mode and the timer used. (Lose 0.5 mark)

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱️ **8051 has 2 Timer/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each **16-bit**, with **4 modes** of operation.
- 🎛️ **TMOD register (89H)** controls the **mode (M1, M0)**, **Counter/Timer (C/T̄)**, and **Gating (GATE)**.
- 🏃 **TCON register (88H)** controls the **run bits (TR0, TR1)** and **overflow flags (TF0, TF1)**.
- 🔢 **Mode 0** = 13-bit (legacy); **Mode 1** = 16-bit (general purpose); **Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload (baud rate); **Mode 3** = split timer (Timer 0 only).
- 🕒 **Tick time = 12 / f_osc** → For 11.0592 MHz, tick = 1.085 μs; for 12 MHz, tick = 1 μs.
- 📐 **Delay (Mode 1) = (65536 − N) × T_tick**, where N is the decimal equivalent of the TH:TL value.
- 🔁 **Delay (Mode 2) = (256 − THx) × T_tick**, automatic reload — no software intervention needed.
- 📡 **Baud rate generation** uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2**; preferred crystal is **11.0592 MHz** for error-free standard baud rates.
- ⚠️ **Always clear the TFx flag** in software after handling an overflow.
- 🚦 **GATE = 1** enables hardware-controlled timing — useful for pulse-width measurement on the INTx pin.
- 📚 **Most expected KTU questions:** "Explain the 4 modes with diagrams" (14 marks) and "Generate X ms delay using Mode 1" (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Communication Programming in Assembly and C Language; Introduction to Interrupts in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication Programming in Assembly and C Language; Introduction to Interrupts in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Serial Communication in 8051 — Formal Definition

**Serial Communication** is a data transmission technique in which bits of a data word are transmitted sequentially over a *single communication line*, in contrast to parallel communication where multiple bits travel simultaneously across multiple lines. The 8051 microcontrollers contain a built-in **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** hardware module that supports full-duplex serial communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051's on-chip serial port can operate in **4 modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)**, where Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate) is the most frequently tested mode in KTU university examinations because it is the standard PC-to-MCU communication mode.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Single-File Conveyor Belt"

Imagine two factory workers (the 8051 and a PC) standing at opposite ends of a long, narrow **single-file conveyor belt**:
- They can only send **one item (bit) at a time** over the belt.
- Before sending data, the sender rings a **bell (Start bit)** to alert the receiver.
- The receiver listens at a fixed **rhythm (Baud Rate)** to pick up each bit.
- After all data bits are sent, the sender raises a **flag (Stop bit)** to indicate completion.
- If they agreed on different rhythms, the bits will be misread — this is why **Baud Rate matching is critical**.

## 1.2 Interrupts in 8051 — Formal Definition

An **Interrupt** is an external or internal asynchronous event that temporarily halts the main program execution, forces the CPU to execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then returns control back to the main program using the `RETI` instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 provides **six interrupt sources**: Reset, two external hardware interrupts (`INT0`, `INT1`), two timer overflow interrupts (`TF0`, `TF1`), and one serial port interrupt (`TI`/`RI`).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Worker's Doorbell"

Consider a busy office worker (the CPU) absorbed in paperwork (the main program):
- The worker doesn't keep checking the door every second (this would waste time — called **polling**).
- Instead, a **doorbell (Interrupt signal)** alerts the worker only when something urgent arrives.
- The worker **pauses** the current task, **saves their place** (PC pushed onto stack), and opens the door.
- After handling the visitor (executes the ISR), the worker **returns to the exact same page** (PC popped from stack).
- Some visitors are VIPs (High Priority) and are attended to before normal ones (Low Priority).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards in Serial Communication

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Baud Rates | **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 115200** bps |
| Preferred Crystal for 8051 | **11.0592 MHz** (gives exact standard baud rates) |
| Standard Data Frame | 1 Start + **8/9 Data bits** + 1 Stop bit |
| SBUF Register Address | **0x99** |
| SCON Register Address | **0x98** |
| PCON Register Address | **0x87** |
| Serial Interrupt Vector | **0x0023** |
| Standard Mode for PC Communication | **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SCON Register (Serial Control — 0x98, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 (with SM1 selects Mode 0/1/2/3) |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (Mode 2/3 only) |
| 4 | **REN** | Receive Enable — must be set to receive data |
| 3 | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (Mode 2/3) |
| 2 | **RB8** | 9th received bit (Mode 2/3) |
| 1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte |
| 0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware when byte received |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `TI` and `RI` must be cleared by software:** The 8051 hardware sets these flags but never clears them. Forgetting to clear them causes the program to think transmission/reception is complete when it is not — a common KTU exam pitfall.

## 2.2 SCON Mode Selection Table

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 8-bit Shift Register | $f_{osc}/12$ (fixed) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 8-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1) |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 9-bit UART | $f_{osc}/32$ or $f_{osc}/64$ |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | 9-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1) |

## 2.3 PCON Register (Power Control — 0x87, NOT Bit-Addressable)

Only the **SMOD** bit (bit 7) is relevant for serial communication. When `SMOD = 1`, the baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3 is **doubled**.

## 2.4 SBUF Register (Serial Buffer — 0x99)

Physically, **SBUF is two separate registers**:
- **Writing** to SBUF loads the **transmit register**.
- **Reading** from SBUF reads the **receive register**.

Both share the same address 0x99 — the operation determines which register is accessed.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Serial Communication & Baud Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Baud Rate for Mode 1 (Most Tested in KTU):**
> $$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Timer 1 Reload Value (TH1) for Mode 1:**
> $$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times 32 \times 2^{-SMOD} \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Baud Rate for Mode 2:**
> $$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{64} \times f_{osc}$$

| Concept | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Mode 1 Baud Rate | $\frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12(256-TH1)}$ | Timer 1 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) |
| Mode 2 Baud Rate | $\frac{2^{SMOD}}{64} \times f_{osc}$ | Fixed (no timer) |
| Timer 1 Overflow Rate | $\frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256-TH1)}$ | Timer Mode 2 |
| TH1 Reload Value | $256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times 2^{-SMOD} \times \text{Baud}}$ | For Mode 1 |
| Maximum Baud Rate | $\frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ (Mode 0) | No timer required |
| Interrupt Vector Address | $8 \times (\text{Interrupt Number}) + 3$ | For all 8051 interrupts |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial PLCs and SCADA systems** use 8051 UART for sensor data logging at 9600 baud.
- **GPS modules** (like NEO-6M) communicate at 9600 baud — exactly why the 11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred.
- **GSM modules (SIM800/900)** exchange AT commands via UART at 9600 baud.
- **Bluetooth HC-05** modules default to 9600 baud serial communication.
- **Interrupt-driven I/O** is the foundation of real-time systems, RTOS kernels, and modern embedded firmware.

## 2.7 8051 Interrupt Structure

The 8051 supports **6 interrupt sources** with fixed vector addresses:

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | — | 0x0000 | Highest |
| **External 0 (INT0)** | IE0 | 0x0003 | 1 |
| **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | 0x000B | 2 |
| **External 1 (INT1)** | IE1 | 0x0013 | 3 |
| **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | 0x001B | 4 |
| **Serial Port** | TI / RI | 0x0023 | 5 (Lowest) |

## 2.8 IE Register (Interrupt Enable — 0xA8, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | **EA** | Global Enable — must be set to enable any interrupt |
| 6 | — | Reserved |
| 5 | — | Reserved |
| 4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port Interrupt |
| 3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 Interrupt |
| 2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| 1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 Interrupt |
| 0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

## 2.9 IP Register (Interrupt Priority — 0xB8, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7-5 | — | Reserved |
| 4 | **PS** | Serial Port Priority (1 = High) |
| 3 | **PT1** | Timer 1 Priority (1 = High) |
| 2 | **PX1** | External 1 Priority (1 = High) |
| 1 | **PT0** | Timer 0 Priority (1 = High) |
| 0 | **PX0** | External 0 Priority (1 = High) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Tier Priority in 8051:** Each interrupt can be set to **High** (priority bit = 1) or **Low** (priority bit = 0). A High-priority interrupt can interrupt a Low-priority ISR. This is **NOT** the same as the fixed natural priority (Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TH1 Reload Value for Standard Baud Rate (11.0592 MHz, 9600 baud, SMOD=0)

> **Problem:** Find TH1 value for 9600 baud using 11.0592 MHz crystal, Mode 1, SMOD = 0.

**Step 1:** Start with the Mode 1 baud rate equation.
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values: Baud = 9600, $f_{osc} = 11{,}059{,}200$, $SMOD = 0$ (so $2^{SMOD} = 1$).
$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11059200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by 32.
$$9600 \times 32 = \frac{11059200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$
$$307200 = \frac{11059200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 4:** Invert the fraction and multiply.
$$12 \times (256 - TH1) = \frac{11059200}{307200}$$
$$12 \times (256 - TH1) = 36$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by 12.
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{36}{12} = 3$$

**Step 6:** Solve for TH1.
$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Verification:** $TH1 = 0xFD$ is the universal value for **9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0** — the most commonly used setting in KTU practical exams and real-world embedded projects.

## 3.2 Derivation: TH1 Value for 4800 Baud (11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0)

**Step 1:** Using the same base equation, with Baud = 4800.
$$4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11059200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by 32 × 12.
$$4800 \times 32 \times 12 = \frac{11059200}{(256 - TH1)}$$
$$1843200 = \frac{11059200}{(256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply.
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11059200}{1843200} = 6$$

**Step 4:** Solve for TH1.
$$TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0xFA$$

## 3.3 Common TH1 Reference Table (for 11.0592 MHz Crystal)

| Baud Rate | TH1 (Hex) | TH1 (Decimal) | SMOD | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9600 | **0xFD** | 253 | 0 | 0.00% |
| 4800 | **0xFA** | 250 | 0 | 0.00% |
| 2400 | **0xF4** | 244 | 0 | 0.00% |
| 1200 | **0xE8** | 232 | 0 | 0.00% |
| 19200 | **0xFD** | 253 | 1 | 0.00% |
| 9600 (Timer Mode 1) | **0xFFE8** | — | 0 | 0.00% |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Interrupt Processing Sequence

**When an interrupt occurs, the 8051 hardware performs these steps automatically:**

1. **Completes the current instruction** (no mid-instruction interruption).
2. **Pushes the current PC (Program Counter)** onto the stack — this is how the CPU remembers where to return.
3. **Loads the PC with the interrupt vector address** (e.g., 0x0023 for serial port).
4. **Executes the ISR** (Interrupt Service Routine) at that vector.
5. **Encountering the `RETI` instruction** pops PC from stack, restoring original execution point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Difference between `RET` and `RETI`:** Both pop the PC from the stack, but only `RETI` tells the interrupt controller hardware that the ISR has ended. Using `RET` instead of `RETI` will cause subsequent interrupts of the same or lower priority to fail — a classic KTU valuation mistake.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Assembly Program for Serial Transmission of "A" (Mode 1)

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0023H          ; Serial ISR vector
        LJMP SERIAL_ISR

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)
        MOV TH1, #0FDH    ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        MOV SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB TR1          ; Start Timer 1
        MOV SBUF, #'A'    ; Load 'A' into transmit buffer

WAIT:   SJMP WAIT         ; Wait here — TI flag is set when done

        ORG 0100H
SERIAL_ISR:
        CLR TI             ; MUST clear TI flag
        RETI
        END
```

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: C Program for Interrupt-Driven Serial Communication

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char received_byte;

void serial_init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;      // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;      // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    SCON = 0x50;      // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN=1
    TR1  = 1;         // Start Timer 1
    ES   = 1;         // Enable serial interrupt
    EA   = 1;         // Enable global interrupt
}

void serial_ISR(void) interrupt 4 {
    if (TI) {
        TI = 0;       // Clear transmit flag
        // Transmit complete — load next byte if any
    }
    if (RI) {
        RI = 0;       // Clear receive flag
        received_byte = SBUF;  // Read received byte
    }
}

void main(void) {
    serial_init();
    SBUF = 'A';       // Start transmission
    while (1);        // Main loop — ISR handles the rest
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use interrupt numbers (like `interrupt 4`) in Keil C51?** The `interrupt` keyword tells the compiler which vector address to use. The mapping is: **0=Reset, 1=INT0, 2=T0, 3=INT1, 4=T1, 5=Serial Port**. This is a direct C-language abstraction of the hardware vector table.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Serial Communication Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[8051 CPU]
        A[Accumulator]
        B[SBUF Register 0x99]
    end

    subgraph SControl[SCON Register 0x98]
        S0[SM0]
        S1[SM1]
        S2[REN]
        S3[TI Flag]
        S4[RI Flag]
    end

    subgraph TXPath[Transmit Path]
        T1[Load SBUF]
        T2[Shift Register]
        T3[TXD Pin P3.1]
    end

    subgraph RXPath[Receive Path]
        R1[RXD Pin P3.0]
        R2[Receive Shift Register]
        R3[SBUF Read]
    end

    subgraph Timer[Baud Rate Generator]
        TM1[Timer 1 Mode 2]
        TM2[TH1 Reload]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> T1 --> T2 --> T3
    T3 -.External Line.-> R1
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> A
    S0 --> T2
    S1 --> T2
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> R3
    TM1 --> T2
    TM1 --> R2
    TM2 --> TM1
```

## 4.2 Serial Data Frame Structure (Mode 1)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Idle High] --> B[Start Bit 0]
    B --> C[D0 LSB]
    C --> D[D1]
    D --> E[D2]
    E --> F[D3]
    F --> G[D4]
    G --> H[D5]
    H --> I[D6]
    I --> J[D7 MSB]
    J --> K[Stop Bit 1]
    K --> L[Idle High]
```

## 4.3 8051 Interrupt System Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Main Program Running] --> CheckEA{EA = 1?}
    CheckEA -->|No| Continue1[Continue Main Loop]
    CheckEA -->|Yes| CheckIE{IE bit set for source?}

    CheckIE -->|No| Continue1
    CheckIE -->|Yes| CheckIP{Priority Check}

    CheckIP --> HighPri{High Priority Interrupt?}
    HighPri -->|Yes| SavePC1[Push PC on Stack]
    HighPri -->|No| CheckCurr{Low priority ISR running?}

    CheckCurr -->|Yes| Continue1
    CheckCurr -->|No| SavePC2[Push PC on Stack]

    SavePC1 --> LoadVec1[Load Vector Address]
    SavePC2 --> LoadVec2[Load Vector Address]

    LoadVec1 --> ExecuteISR[Execute ISR]
    LoadVec2 --> ExecuteISR

    ExecuteISR --> RETI[RETI Instruction]
    RETI --> PopPC[Pop PC from Stack]
    PopPC --> Resume[Resume Main Program]
    Resume --> Start
```

## 4.4 8051 Interrupt Vector Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[0x0000 Reset] --> V1[0x0003 External INT0]
    V1 --> V2[0x000B Timer 0 Overflow]
    V2 --> V3[0x0013 External INT1]
    V3 --> V4[0x001B Timer 1 Overflow]
    V4 --> V5[0x0023 Serial Port TI or RI]
    V5 --> EndMain[0x002B onwards User Program]
```

## 4.5 Serial Port Initialization Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Begin Serial Init] --> B[Set TMOD for Timer 1 Mode 2]
    B --> C[Load TH1 with calculated value]
    C --> D[Set SCON for Mode 1 and REN = 1]
    D --> E[Set TR1 to start Timer 1]
    E --> F{Use Interrupts?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Set ES and EA]
    F -->|No| H[Polling TI or RI in loop]
    G --> I[Initialization Complete]
    H --> I[Initialization Complete]
```

## 4.6 Interrupt Service Routine Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt Occurs] --> B[Hardware pushes PC]
    B --> C[Jump to Vector Address]
    C --> D[Save registers on stack if used]
    D --> E[Check interrupt source flag]
    E --> F{Which flag set?}
    F -->|TI| G[Clear TI - handle transmit]
    F -->|RI| H[Clear RI - read SBUF]
    F -->|TF0 or TF1| I[Clear TF - timer handling]
    F -->|IE0 or IE1| J[Handle external event]
    G --> K[Execute RETI]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Return to Main Program]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the function of the SCON register in 8051. List its bits and describe Mode 0 and Mode 1.**

**Model Answer:**

The **SCON (Serial Control) register** at address 0x98 is a bit-addressable register that controls the 8051's serial port. It contains the following bits:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | SM0 | Serial Mode bit 0 |
| 6 | SM1 | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor enable (Mode 2/3) |
| 4 | REN | Receive enable |
| 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit |
| 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit |
| 1 | TI | Transmit interrupt flag |
| 0 | RI | Receive interrupt flag |

**Mode 0 (SM0=0, SM1=0):** 8-bit shift register mode. Serial data enters and exits through **RXD (P3.0)**, while **TXD (P3.1)** outputs the shift clock. Baud rate is fixed at $f_{osc}/12$.

**Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1):** 8-bit UART mode. Frame = 1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit. Data is transmitted via TXD and received via RXD. Baud rate is **variable** and generated by **Timer 1 overflow** — this is the most commonly used mode.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the six interrupts of 8051 along with their vector addresses and default priorities.**

**Model Answer:**

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | — | **0x0000** | Highest |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0x0003** | 1 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **0x000B** | 2 |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0x0013** | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **0x001B** | 4 |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | **0x0023** | 5 (Lowest) |

The vector address spacing is **8 bytes** between successive interrupts, which is exactly sufficient for short ISRs. For larger ISRs, a **LJMP** to a far routine is required.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain Mode 1 serial communication in 8051 with a neat diagram. Calculate the value of TH1 required to generate a baud rate of 4800 using a 11.0592 MHz crystal, with SMOD = 0.

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 1 Operation:**
- 8-bit UART with **variable baud rate** from Timer 1.
- Frame: 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop = **10 bits total**.
- **TXD (P3.1)** transmits, **RXD (P3.0)** receives.
- Full-duplex operation.
- Timer 1 must be in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** for baud generation.
- TI flag is set at the **start of the stop bit** time — indicating end of transmission.
- RI flag is set at the **end of the stop bit** — indicating a complete byte received.

**Baud Rate Calculation:**

Step 1: Use the Mode 1 baud rate equation.
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

Step 2: Substitute values: Baud = 4800, $f_{osc} = 11{,}059{,}200$, $SMOD = 0$.
$$4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11059200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

Step 3: Multiply both sides by $32 \times 12$.
$$4800 \times 384 = \frac{11059200}{(256 - TH1)}$$
$$1843200 = \frac{11059200}{(256 - TH1)}$$

Step 4: Cross-multiply and solve.
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11059200}{1843200} = 6$$
$$TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0xFA$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** `TH1 = 0xFA` (250 decimal) gives 4800 baud with 0% error.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to transmit the byte "K" continuously at 9600 baud using 11.0592 MHz crystal in Mode 1. Explain the program.

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 0023H           ; Serial Port ISR Vector
SERIAL_ISR:
        CLR TI              ; MUST clear TI (hardware does not clear it)
        MOV SBUF, #'K'      ; Reload next 'K' for continuous transmission
        RETI                ; Return from ISR

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)
        MOV TH1, #0FDH      ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0
        MOV SCON, #50H      ; 0101 0000b: Mode 1, REN=1
        SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1 (baud generator)
        MOV IE, #90H        ; 1001 0000b: EA=1, ES=1 (enable serial interrupt)
        MOV SBUF, #'K'      ; Load first 'K' to start transmission

HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Infinite wait — ISR handles rest
        END
```

**Explanation:**
1. **TMOD = 0x20:** Configures Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), which is mandatory for stable baud rate generation.
2. **TH1 = 0xFD:** The reload value for 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal, SMOD = 0.
3. **SCON = 0x50:** Sets SM0=0, SM1=1 (Mode 1) and REN=1 (receive enable).
4. **TR1 = 1:** Starts Timer 1 — now the baud rate generator is active.
5. **IE = 0x90:** Sets EA=1 (global enable) and ES=1 (serial port interrupt enable).
6. **First SBUF write:** Triggers transmission of the first 'K'.
7. **In ISR:** When TI=1 (set by hardware at end of stop bit), the ISR clears TI and reloads 'K' into SBUF — this gives **continuous transmission**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the Interrupt structure of 8051. Discuss the role of IE and IP registers.

**Model Solution:**

**Interrupt Structure:**
The 8051 supports **6 interrupt sources** with fixed vector addresses. The interrupt logic in 8051 has three main blocks: the **interrupt source flags**, the **masking logic (IE register)**, and the **priority logic (IP register)**.

**Role of IE Register (Interrupt Enable — 0xA8):**
- It is a bit-addressable register.
- **EA (bit 7):** Global enable — must be 1 to enable any interrupt.
- **ES (bit 4):** Enables serial port interrupt.
- **ET1, ET0 (bits 3, 1):** Enable Timer 1 and Timer 0 interrupts.
- **EX1, EX0 (bits 2, 0):** Enable external interrupts INT1 and INT0.
- A disabled interrupt source cannot interrupt the CPU even if its flag is set.

**Role of IP Register (Interrupt Priority — 0xB8):**
- Bit-addressable register that sets each interrupt to **High** (bit=1) or **Low** (bit=0) priority.
- **PS (bit 4):** Serial port priority.
- **PT1, PT0 (bits 3, 1):** Timer 1 and 0 priority.
- **PX1, PX0 (bits 2, 0):** External interrupt priority.
- **Two-tier priority rule:** A High-priority interrupt can interrupt a Low-priority ISR. Two High-priority interrupts are resolved by the natural priority order (Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial).

**Interrupt Processing Steps:**
1. Finish the current instruction.
2. Hardware pushes PC onto stack.
3. Load PC with vector address.
4. Execute ISR.
5. `RETI` instruction pops PC and returns.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Write an 8051 C Program to receive bytes via serial port at 9600 baud and store them in an array. Use interrupt-driven reception.

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char buffer[10];
unsigned char index = 0;
unsigned char count = 0;

void serial_init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;        // Timer 1, Mode 2
    TH1  = 0xFD;        // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    SCON = 0x50;        // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN=1
    TR1  = 1;           // Start Timer 1
    ES   = 1;           // Enable serial interrupt
    EA   = 1;           // Global interrupt enable
}

void serial_ISR(void) interrupt 4 {
    if (RI) {
        RI = 0;                   // Clear RI flag
        if (index < 10) {
            buffer[index] = SBUF; // Store received byte
            index++;
        }
    }
    // TI handling if needed
    if (TI) {
        TI = 0;
    }
}

void main(void) {
    serial_init();
    while (1) {
        // Main program can do other tasks
        // Reception happens in background via interrupt
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
1. **serial_init()** configures Timer 1 for 9600 baud, sets SCON for Mode 1 with receive enable, and enables the serial interrupt.
2. The **ISR runs automatically** when a byte is received (RI = 1).
3. The ISR reads SBUF and stores it in the buffer.
4. The main program is **free to do other work** — this is the key advantage of interrupt-driven I/O over polling.
5. The `interrupt 4` keyword tells the Keil C51 compiler to place the function's entry code at the **0x0023 vector address** (serial port ISR).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in 8051 Serial & Interrupt Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI flags** in the ISR — hardware sets them, software MUST clear them. Failure to do so locks the serial port permanently.
> 2. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** at the end of an ISR — this prevents further interrupts of equal or lower priority. Always use `RETI`.
> 3. **Setting EA=0 (or not setting it at all)** — without the global enable bit, no interrupt works, even if individual bits are set.
> 4. **Incorrect TH1 calculation** — using a non-standard crystal (like 12 MHz) gives **non-integer timer values**, producing baud rate errors. Always use **11.0592 MHz** for clean standard baud rates.
> 5. **Confusing SCON modes** — Mode 1 is 8-bit UART (most common), Mode 2 is 9-bit with fixed baud, Mode 3 is 9-bit with variable baud. Read the SCON bits carefully.
> 6. **Not enabling REN** for reception — if REN=0, the receiver is disabled and no data can be received.
> 7. **Wrong interrupt number in C** — the `interrupt n` keyword uses **0=Reset, 1=INT0, 2=T0, 3=INT1, 4=T1, 5=Serial**. Writing `interrupt 4` for serial is WRONG; it should be `interrupt 5` in some compilers, but Keil C51 uses `interrupt 4` for serial port (sequence: 0=Reset, 1=IE0, 2=TF0, 3=IE1, 4=TF1, 5=Serial+TI+RI combined... verify with your compiler documentation).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SCON (0x98)** is the master control register for the 8051's serial port — it selects the mode, enables receive, and houses TI/RI flags.
- **SBUF (0x99)** is two registers in one — write to it to transmit, read from it to receive.
- **Mode 1** is the standard 8-bit UART mode used for PC communication at 9600 baud.
- **Baud Rate Formula:** $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256-TH1)}$
- **TH1 = 0xFD** is the magic value for 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal and SMOD=0.
- **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** is the only correct configuration for baud rate generation.
- **PCON bit 7 (SMOD)** doubles the baud rate when set to 1.
- **8051 has 6 interrupts** with 8-byte vector spacing: 0x00, 0x03, 0x0B, 0x13, 0x1B, 0x23.
- **EA must be 1** to enable any interrupt (global master switch).
- **RETI** (not RET) must terminate every ISR — this signals the hardware that the ISR is complete.
- **Two-tier priority:** Each interrupt is High or Low priority based on IP register; ties are broken by natural priority.
- **Interrupt C keyword:** `void isr(void) interrupt n` where n corresponds to the vector position.
- **Always clear TI and RI in software** — the 8051 hardware only sets them, never clears them.
- **REN = 1** is required for any reception — a common oversight in KTU practical exams.
- **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred over 12 MHz because it gives exact integer timer reload values for all standard baud rates.
- **Modes 0, 1, 2, 3** correspond to (Shift Register, 8-bit UART, 9-bit fixed, 9-bit variable) — remember this sequence.
- **Full-duplex communication** is supported — the 8051 can transmit and receive simultaneously because TXD and RXD are physically separate pins.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Priority of Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 MODULE 3: PRIORITY OF INTERRUPTS — SECTION 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, the **Priority of Interrupts** refers to the predefined and programmable hierarchy that determines the **order in which the CPU services multiple simultaneous or pending interrupt requests**. The 8051 interrupt controller employs a **two-tier priority resolution mechanism** combining a **fixed natural (default) priority** with a **programmable priority structure** controlled by the **Interrupt Priority (IP) register** at SFR address `0xB8`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The 8051 supports **six (6) interrupt sources** — `Reset`, `INT0`, `Timer 0`, `INT1`, `Timer 1`, and `Serial Port`. Five of these (excluding Reset) are **user-maskable** and **priority-programmable**.

The official, syllabus-grade definition is as follows:

> **Interrupt Priority** is the mechanism by which the 8051 CPU resolves conflicts when two or more interrupt flags are set simultaneously, or when a new interrupt request arrives while another ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) is already executing. The system uses both a **hardware-encoded natural priority sequence** and a **software-configurable priority register** to deliver deterministic, real-time response.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine an **Emergency Room (ER) in a multi-specialty hospital**. Patients arrive simultaneously — some with mild fever, others with chest pain, and one in critical cardiac arrest.

| Hospital Analogy | 8051 Equivalent | Function |
|:---|:---|:---|
| 🚨 Cardiac Arrest patient | `Reset` (Highest) | Immediate, non-maskable life-saving action |
| 🫀 Heart Attack (INT0) | External Interrupt 0 | Urgent hardware event from outside |
| ⏱️ Scheduled checkup (T0) | Timer 0 Overflow | Periodic, time-bound task |
| 🤕 Fracture case (INT1) | External Interrupt 1 | Less-urgent external event |
| 💊 Routine medicine (T1) | Timer 1 Overflow | Background timing task |
| 📞 Phone call (Serial) | Serial Port (TI/RI) | Communication activity (lowest) |

Just as the **head of the ER (IP register)** decides whether a particular doctor (interrupt) gets **VIP (High Priority) or General (Low Priority)** treatment status, the 8051's IP register lets the programmer elevate any interrupt above its natural rank. A patient already being treated (an executing ISR) can be **interrupted mid-treatment** if a higher-priority emergency (a higher-priority interrupt) arrives — this is **nested interrupt handling**.

---

## 1.3 The Six Interrupt Sources — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Reset is special.** It is the **highest-priority interrupt** of all, and it is **NOT maskable** and **NOT vectored through the IP register**. It clears all pending interrupts and restarts the CPU from address `0x0000`.

The five remaining interrupts each have:

1. A **dedicated flag bit** in the **TCON** register (for `INT0`, `INT1`, `T0`, `T1`) and the **SCON** register (for `Serial Port`).
2. A **dedicated enable bit** in the **IE (Interrupt Enable) register** at SFR address `0xA8`.
3. A **dedicated priority bit** in the **IP (Interrupt Priority) register** at SFR address `0xB8`.
4. A **fixed vector address** in program memory where its ISR must begin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two-Tier Priority Resolution Logic

The 8051's interrupt controller resolves priority using **three sequential decision layers** every time it samples the interrupt flags:

### Layer 1 — Global Mask Check
The CPU first inspects the **EA (Enable All)** bit in the IE register. If `EA = 0`, **all interrupts (except Reset) are globally disabled** — the system is said to be in *polled mode* by default.

$$\text{If } EA = 0 \implies \text{No interrupt accepted (except Reset)}$$

### Layer 2 — Individual Mask Check
For each pending interrupt source $i$, the corresponding enable bit $E_i$ in the IE register must be set:

$$\text{Interrupt } i \text{ is serviced} \iff EA = 1 \;\land\; E_i = 1 \;\land\; \text{Flag}_i = 1$$

### Layer 3 — Priority Arbitration
When multiple interrupt flags are simultaneously set **and** all their enable bits are active, the priority logic kicks in using this two-step process:

**Step A — Programmable Priority (IP Register):** Each interrupt is classified as either **High Priority (HP)** or **Low Priority (LP)** based on its bit in the IP register.

> **A High-Priority interrupt can interrupt a Low-Priority ISR (nesting).**
> **A Low-Priority interrupt CANNOT interrupt a High-Priority ISR.**
> **Two High-Priority interrupts are resolved by natural priority.**

**Step B — Natural Priority (Default Sequence):** If two interrupts fall in the **same programmable priority class**, the natural priority sequence applies:

$$\text{Natural Priority (High} \rightarrow \text{Low):} \quad \overline{\text{Reset}} > \overline{\text{INT0}} > \overline{\text{T0}} > \overline{\text{INT1}} > \overline{\text{T1}} > \overline{\text{Serial Port}}$$

---

## 2.2 IP (Interrupt Priority) Register — Bit Architecture

The IP register at SFR address `0xB8` is **bit-addressable**, meaning any single bit can be modified using instructions like `SETB` and `CLR`.

| Bit | Name | Address | Reset Value | Controls |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 7 | — | `0xBF` | `x` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 6 | — | `0xBE` | `x` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 5 | — | `0xBD` | `x` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 4 | **PS** | `0xBC` | `0` | Serial Port priority (1 = HP) |
| 3 | **PT1** | `0xBB` | `0` | Timer 1 priority (1 = HP) |
| 2 | **PX1** | `0xBA` | `0` | External INT1 priority (1 = HP) |
| 1 | **PT0** | `0xB9` | `0` | Timer 0 priority (1 = HP) |
| 0 | **PX0** | `0xB8` | `0` | External INT0 priority (1 = HP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The IP register only defines High vs. Low priority. The natural priority sequence (Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial) is HARDWIRED and cannot be reordered within the same priority class.**

The default IP register value after reset is `0x00`, meaning **all five maskable interrupts are at Low Priority by default** — and the natural priority sequence becomes the operative sequence.

---

## 2.3 Vector Addresses of the Six Interrupts

Each interrupt has a **fixed, 8-byte vector area** in program memory starting at a predefined address. The CPU pushes the current PC onto the stack and jumps to this address upon accepting the interrupt.

| Interrupt Source | Flag Bit | Vector Address | ISR Entry Point |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Reset (System) | — | `0x0000` | `ORG 0000H` |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | `IE0` (TCON.1) | `0x0003` | `ORG 0003H` |
| Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` (TCON.5) | `0x000B` | `ORG 000BH` |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | `IE1` (TCON.3) | `0x0013` | `ORG 0013H` |
| Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` (TCON.7) | `0x001B` | `ORG 001BH` |
| Serial Port (TI or RI) | `TI`/`RI` (SCON.0/1) | `0x0023` | `ORG 0023H` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Only 8 bytes of code space** are reserved between vector addresses. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, a **LJMP** instruction must be placed at the vector to redirect execution to the actual ISR code located elsewhere in ROM.

---

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

> **Canonical reference for 8051 Interrupt Priority Resolution:**

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Constraint |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Global enable condition | $EA = 1$ | Must be set for any interrupt |
| Individual enable | $E_i = 1$ (IE register) | Set per source |
| Service condition | $EA \;\land\; E_i \;\land\; \text{Flag}_i$ | All three must be true |
| Programmable priority | $IP_i = 1$ implies High Priority | $IP_i = 0$ implies Low Priority |
| Nesting rule | HP can preempt LP; LP cannot preempt HP | Enforced in hardware |
| Natural priority order | Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial | Hardwired, fixed |
| Vector address spacing | $0x0008$ bytes apart (8 bytes) | $0x08 = 8$ bytes |
| Polling cycle time | One machine cycle $= 12$ oscillator periods | $T_{cycle} = 12 / f_{osc}$ |
| Flag clearing | Hardware clears TF0, TF1, IE0, IE1 | Software must clear TI and RI |
| Edge-triggered flag | $IT0 = 1$ / $IT1 = 1$ → falling edge | Sets IE0/IE1 hardware-flagged |

> **Edge vs. Level Triggering for INT0 / INT1:**
> $$\text{If } ITx = 0 \implies \text{Level-triggered (Low)} \quad \text{\textbar} \quad \text{If } ITx = 1 \implies \text{Edge-triggered (falling)}$$
> The `ITx` bit resides in the **TCON register** at `0x88`.

---

## 2.5 Why Priority Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility

In embedded systems design, deterministic interrupt handling is the **cornerstone of real-time performance**. The 8051's priority structure directly enables:

- 🚗 **Automotive ECUs** — Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) interrupts must dominate climate-control interrupts.
- 🏥 **Medical Devices** — Pacemaker timing interrupts must override display-update interrupts.
- 🏭 **Industrial PLCs** — Emergency-stop interrupts must dominate routine sensor polling.
- 📡 **Communication Gateways** — Serial reception must be serviced before transmission in half-duplex protocols.
- 🤖 **Robotics** — Motor stall detection must override position-update logging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Priority Resolution Decision Tree

The interrupt controller executes this **decision sequence** at the end of every instruction cycle. Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\} = \{\text{INT0}, \text{T0}, \text{INT1}, \text{T1}, \text{Serial}\}$ represent the set of maskable interrupt sources.

### Decision Tree (Truth-Table Form)

| Step | Condition | Action | Outcome |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 0 | Reset asserted? | Jump to `0x0000` immediately | **Reset wins always** |
| 1 | $EA = 0$? | Disable all maskable interrupts | CPU continues current code |
| 2 | Exactly one flag set, $E_i = 1$? | Service that interrupt | Direct vector to ISR |
| 3 | Multiple flags set, all in same priority class? | Apply natural priority $\rightarrow$ service highest | Vector to that ISR |
| 4 | Multiple flags set, **mixed classes**? | Service all HP first, defer LP | HP interrupts preempt LP |
| 5 | ISR executing, new HP request arrives? | Push PC, service new HP ISR | **Nested interrupt** |
| 6 | ISR executing, new LP request arrives? | Wait until current ISR finishes via `RETI` | LP request is **held pending** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Resolving a Multi-Interrupt Scenario

### Problem Statement
A 8051 system has the following configuration after initialization:

$$\text{IE} = 0x9F \quad ; \quad \text{IP} = 0x0A \quad ; \quad \text{TCON} = 0xC5$$

Decode this configuration and determine: **(a)** which interrupts are enabled, **(b)** which are at High Priority, **(c)** the final service order if **all five** flag bits become set simultaneously.

### Step 1 — Decode the IE Register (`0x9F`)

The IE register at `0xA8` has the bit layout `[EA - - ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0]`.

$$IE = 0x9F = 1001\,1111_2$$

Converting bit-by-bit from MSB to LSB:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 7 (EA)}  &= 1 &\text{(Global enable ON)} \\
\text{Bit 6 (--)}  &= 0 &\text{(Reserved)} \\
\text{Bit 5 (--)}  &= 0 &\text{(Reserved)} \\
\text{Bit 4 (ES)}  &= 1 &\text{(Serial Port enabled)} \\
\text{Bit 3 (ET1)} &= 1 &\text{(Timer 1 enabled)} \\
\text{Bit 2 (EX1)} &= 1 &\text{(External INT1 enabled)} \\
\text{Bit 1 (ET0)} &= 1 &\text{(Timer 0 enabled)} \\
\text{Bit 0 (EX0)} &= 1 &\text{(External INT0 enabled)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** All five maskable interrupts are individually enabled, and the global enable is active.

### Step 2 — Decode the IP Register (`0x0A`)

The IP register at `0xB8` has the bit layout `[-- -- -- PS PT1 PX1 PT0 PX0]`.

$$IP = 0x0A = 0000\,1010_2$$

Bit-by-bit decoding:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 7-5} &= 0\,0\,0 &\text{(Reserved)} \\
\text{Bit 4 (PS)}  &= 1 &\text{(Serial Port} = \text{High Priority)} \\
\text{Bit 3 (PT1)} &= 0 &\text{(Timer 1} = \text{Low Priority)} \\
\text{Bit 2 (PX1)} &= 1 &\text{(External INT1} = \text{High Priority)} \\
\text{Bit 1 (PT0)} &= 0 &\text{(Timer 0} = \text{Low Priority)} \\
\text{Bit 0 (PX0)} &= 0 &\text{(External INT0} = \text{Low Priority)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**
- **High Priority group:** `Serial Port`, `External INT1`
- **Low Priority group:** `External INT0`, `Timer 0`, `Timer 1`

### Step 3 — Decode the TCON Register (`0xC5`)

The TCON register at `0x88` has the bit layout `[TF1 TR1 TF0 TR0 IE1 IT1 IE0 IT0]`.

$$TCON = 0xC5 = 1100\,0101_2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TF1} &= 1 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Timer 1 overflow flag SET} \\
\text{TR1} &= 1 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Timer 1 running} \\
\text{TF0} &= 0 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Timer 0 flag NOT set} \\
\text{TR0} &= 0 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Timer 0 stopped} \\
\text{IE1} &= 0 \;\rightarrow\; \text{External INT1 flag NOT set} \\
\text{IT1} &= 1 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Edge-triggered mode} \\
\text{IE0} &= 1 \;\rightarrow\; \text{External INT0 flag SET} \\
\text{IT0} &= 1 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Edge-triggered mode}
\end{aligned}$$

For the serial port, we additionally need `SCON`. The problem says **all five flag bits are set**, so we assume:
$$\text{TF1} = 1,\; \text{TF0} = 1,\; \text{IE0} = 1,\; \text{IE1} = 1,\; \text{RI/TI} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Priority Resolution Algorithm

We now have two priority queues:

$$\text{HP Queue} = \{ \text{Serial Port},\; \text{External INT1} \}$$
$$\text{LP Queue} = \{ \text{External INT0},\; \text{Timer 0},\; \text{Timer 1} \}$$

The CPU first services **all** High Priority interrupts in **natural order**, then services **all** Low Priority interrupts in **natural order**.

$$\text{HP order (natural):} \quad \text{INT1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port}$$

$$\text{LP order (natural):} \quad \text{INT0} \rightarrow \text{T0} \rightarrow \text{T1}$$

**Final service order:**

$$\boxed{\text{INT1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port} \rightarrow \text{INT0} \rightarrow \text{Timer 0} \rightarrow \text{Timer 1}}$$

---

## 3.3 Programming the IP Register — Step-by-Step Assembly

To make `Timer 0` a **High Priority** interrupt while keeping all others Low Priority:

### Required Final IP Value

$$IP = ?\,?\,?\,\underbrace{0}_{PS}\underbrace{0}_{PT1}\underbrace{0}_{PX1}\underbrace{1}_{PT0}\underbrace{0}_{PX0} = 0x02$$

### Assembly Code Implementation

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Skip ISR vector area

        ORG     000BH           ; Timer 0 vector
        LJMP    T0_ISR          ; Redirect to actual ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     IP,    #02H     ; Set PT0 = 1 → Timer 0 HP
        MOV     IE,    #82H     ; EA=1, ET0=1 → enable Timer 0
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Wait loop

T0_ISR: ; <-- actual interrupt service routine code -->
        RETI
        END
```

**Verification of IE Register `0x82`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
0x82 &= 1000\,0010_2 \\
\text{EA} &= 1,\; \text{ET0} = 1,\; \text{All others} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always end ISRs with `RETI`, not `RET`.** The `RETI` instruction performs **two critical functions**: (1) it pops the return address from the stack back into the PC, and (2) it notifies the interrupt controller that the ISR has completed, thereby **unblocking any nested interrupts** of equal or lower priority.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Vector Address Spacing

Each interrupt vector occupies **8 bytes** of code memory. We can derive this spacing from the system design:

$$\text{Starting address of vector } n = 0x0003 + (n-1) \times 0x0008$$

For $n = 1$ (INT0): $0x0003 + 0 \times 8 = 0x0003$ ✓
For $n = 2$ (T0): $\;\;0x0003 + 1 \times 8 = 0x000B$ ✓
For $n = 3$ (INT1): $0x0003 + 2 \times 8 = 0x0013$ ✓
For $n = 4$ (T1): $\;\;\;0x0003 + 3 \times 8 = 0x001B$ ✓
For $n = 5$ (Serial): $0x0003 + 4 \times 8 = 0x0023$ ✓

$$\Delta_{\text{vector}} = 0x0008 = 8 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(Universal constant for 8051)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exactly 8 bytes?** This was an Intel design choice balancing ROM efficiency and ISR length. A `LJMP` instruction is 3 bytes, leaving **5 bytes** of code space in the vector itself — typically just enough for very short ISRs (e.g., setting a flag bit).

## 4.2 Priority Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0["START: End of Instruction Cycle"]:::start
    s1["Reset Pin Active?"]:::check
    s2["Vector to 0x0000"]:::resetaction
    s3["EA = 1?"]:::check
    s4["Continue Polling Loop"]:::reject
    s5["Any Flag Set with E_i = 1?"]:::check
    s6["No Pending Interrupt - Continue"]:::reject

    s10["Service HP First via Vector"]:::serve
    s11["After HP Done - Service LP via Vector"]:::serve
    s12["Single Interrupt - Direct Vector"]:::serve
    s13["RETI: Return and Unblock"]:::finish

    s0 --> s1
    s1 -- YES --> s2 --> s13
    s1 -- NO --> s3
    s3 -- NO --> s4
    s3 -- YES --> s5
    s5 -- NO --> s6
    s5 -- YES --> s7
    s7 -- YES --> s10 --> s11 --> s13
    s7 -- NO --> s8
    s8 -- YES --> s9 --> s13
    s8 -- NO --> s12 --> s13
```

## 4.3 Nested Interrupt Scenario — Time-Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MAIN as Main Program
    participant T0 as Timer 0 ISR (LP)
    participant INT1 as External INT1 ISR (HP)

    MAIN->>MAIN: Executing main loop
    Note over MAIN: TF0 = 1 detected
    MAIN->>T0: Push PC; Jump to 0x000B
    T0->>T0: Execute LP ISR body
    Note over T0,INT1: IE1 = 1 detected (HP request!)
    T0->>INT1: Push PC; Jump to 0x0013
    INT1->>INT1: Execute HP ISR body
    Note over INT1: HP work complete
    INT1-->>T0: RETI; Pop PC
    T0->>T0: Resume LP ISR body
    Note over T0: LP work complete
    T0-->>MAIN: RETI; Pop PC
    Note over MAIN: Main loop resumes
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Explain the natural priority sequence of interrupts in 8051 microcontroller. Why is Reset given the highest priority?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **natural priority** (also called **default priority**) is a hardwired, fixed sequence used by the 8051 to resolve simultaneous interrupt requests that fall in the **same programmable priority class**. The sequence, from highest to lowest, is:

| Rank | Interrupt | Vector Address |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Reset** | `0x0000` |
| 2 | External Interrupt 0 (`INT0`) | `0x0003` |
| 3 | Timer 0 Overflow (`TF0`) | `0x000B` |
| 4 | External Interrupt 1 (`INT1`) | `0x0013` |
| 5 | Timer 1 Overflow (`TF1`) | `0x001B` |
| 6 | Serial Port (`TI`/`RI`) | `0x0023` |

**Reset is highest priority** because it represents a **catastrophic system-level event** (power-up, brown-out, watchdog timeout). It must override all other activities to **reinitialize the CPU to a known state** at address `0x0000`. Reset is also **non-maskable** — it cannot be disabled by software, ensuring the system can always recover from a fault condition.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**What is the role of the IP register in 8051? List its bit addresses.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **IP (Interrupt Priority) register**, located at SFR address `0xB8`, is used to **elevate specific interrupts to High Priority (HP)** above the default Low Priority (LP) class. Setting a bit to `1` makes the corresponding interrupt HP; clearing it to `0` makes it LP.

| Bit | Name | Address | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 7–5 | — | `0xBF`–`0xBD` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 4 | **PS** | `0xBC` | Serial Port priority |
| 3 | **PT1** | `0xBB` | Timer 1 priority |
| 2 | **PX1** | `0xBA` | External INT1 priority |
| 1 | **PT0** | `0xB9` | Timer 0 priority |
| 0 | **PX0** | `0xB8` | External INT0 priority |

**Key rule:** A High Priority interrupt can interrupt a Low Priority ISR (nested interrupts), but a Low Priority interrupt **cannot** interrupt a High Priority ISR. Default value after reset is `0x00`.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Convention:** Students answer **ONE** full question (either A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**a) With neat diagrams, explain the priority structure and vector addresses of the 8051 interrupts.** **(7 Marks)**

**b) An 8051 system is configured with `IE = 0xAF` and `IP = 0x06`. All five maskable interrupt flags become set simultaneously. Determine the exact order in which the CPU services these interrupts. Show all decoding steps.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The 8051 interrupt controller implements a **two-tier priority structure**:

**Tier 1 — Programmable Priority (via IP register):** Each maskable interrupt is classified as either **High Priority (HP)** or **Low Priority (LP)**. HP interrupts can interrupt LP ISRs.

**Tier 2 — Natural Priority (Hardwired):** Within the same priority class, this fixed order applies:

$$\text{Reset} \rightarrow \text{INT0} \rightarrow \text{T0} \rightarrow \text{INT1} \rightarrow \text{T1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port}$$

**Vector Addresses:**

| Interrupt | Vector |
|:---|:---:|
| Reset | `0x0000` |
| INT0 | `0x0003` |
| T0 | `0x000B` |
| INT1 | `0x0013` |
| T1 | `0x001B` |
| Serial | `0x0023` |

[Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.4 diagrams for visual reference.]

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Decode IE Register (`0xAF`)**

$$IE = 0xAF = 1010\,1111_2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EA &= 1 \quad \text{(Global enable ON)} \\
ES &= 1 \quad \text{(Serial enabled)} \\
ET1 &= 1 \quad \text{(Timer 1 enabled)} \\
EX1 &= 1 \quad \text{(External INT1 enabled)} \\
ET0 &= 1 \quad \text{(Timer 0 enabled)} \\
EX0 &= 1 \quad \text{(External INT0 enabled)}
\end{aligned}$$

All five maskable interrupts are enabled.

**Step 2: Decode IP Register (`0x06`)**

$$IP = 0x06 = 0000\,0110_2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
PS &= 0 \quad \text{(Serial} = \text{LP)} \\
PT1 &= 0 \quad \text{(Timer 1} = \text{LP)} \\
PX1 &= 1 \quad \text{(External INT1} = \text{HP)} \\
PT0 &= 1 \quad \text{(Timer 0} = \text{HP)} \\
PX0 &= 0 \quad \text{(External INT0} = \text{LP)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Form Priority Queues**

$$\text{HP Queue} = \{ \text{INT1},\; \text{Timer 0} \}$$
$$\text{LP Queue} = \{ \text{INT0},\; \text{Timer 1},\; \text{Serial Port} \}$$

**Step 4: Apply Natural Priority within each queue**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HP Service Order} &: \text{INT1 (0x0013)} \rightarrow \text{Timer 0 (0x000B)} \\
\text{LP Service Order} &: \text{INT0 (0x0003)} \rightarrow \text{Timer 1 (0x001B)} \rightarrow \text{Serial (0x0023)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{INT1} \rightarrow \text{T0} \rightarrow \text{INT0} \rightarrow \text{T1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**a) Explain the concept of nested interrupts in 8051. Under what conditions is nesting possible?** **(7 Marks)**

**b) Write an 8051 assembly program to configure `External Interrupt 1` as a High Priority edge-triggered interrupt and `Timer 1` as a Low Priority interrupt. Include the ISR stubs.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Nested interrupts** occur when a higher-priority interrupt request is acknowledged **while another ISR is still executing**. The CPU then:

1. **Completes the current instruction** of the running ISR.
2. **Pushes the current PC** (return address) onto the stack.
3. **Jumps to the vector** of the newly acknowledged higher-priority interrupt.
4. **Executes the new ISR**.
5. On `RETI`, **returns to the interrupted ISR**, which then resumes.

**Conditions for Nesting:**

$$\text{Nesting occurs} \iff \text{New interrupt} = \text{HP} \;\land\; \text{Current ISR} = \text{LP}$$

- An HP interrupt **can preempt** an LP ISR. ✅
- An HP interrupt **can preempt** another HP ISR **only if** the new HP has a higher natural priority ranking. ✅
- An LP interrupt **CANNOT preempt** an HP ISR. ❌
- An LP interrupt **CANNOT preempt** another LP ISR (would also need higher natural priority). ❌

**Critical Hardware Rule:** The interrupt controller hardware automatically handles the priority comparison and preemption logic. The programmer only needs to set the IP register bits correctly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often write that "any interrupt can nest any other interrupt." This is **wrong** for the 8051. The two-tier structure means **only HP→LP nesting is unconditional**. HP→HP nesting requires a higher natural rank, and LP→anything nesting is impossible.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Required Configuration:**
- `PX1 = 1` (External INT1 → High Priority)
- `PT1 = 0` (Timer 1 → Low Priority)
- `IT1 = 1` (INT1 → Edge-triggered, falling)
- `EX1 = 1`, `ET1 = 1` (Both enabled)
- `EA = 1` (Global enable)

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0013H           ; External INT1 vector
        LJMP    EXTINT1_ISR

        ORG     001BH           ; Timer 1 vector
        LJMP    TIMER1_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     IE,    #00H     ; Disable all interrupts first
        MOV     TCON,  #04H     ; Set IT1 = 1 (edge-triggered)
        MOV     IP,    #04H     ; PX1 = 1, others = 0
        MOV     IE,    #8CH     ; EA=1, ES=0, ET1=1, EX1=1, ET0=0, EX0=0
        MOV     TMOD,  #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,   #0F3H    ; 2400 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        SETB    TR1             ; Start Timer 1
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Idle loop

EXTINT1_ISR:
        ; <-- HP ISR code here -->
        RETI                    ; Return and signal controller

TIMER1_ISR:
        ; <-- LP ISR code here -->
        CLR     TF1             ; Clear overflow flag (defensive)
        RETI

        END
```

**Verification of IP = 0x04:**

$$0x04 = 0000\,0100_2 \implies \text{PX1} = 1, \text{others} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification of IE = 0x8C:**

$$0x8C = 1000\,1100_2 \implies EA=1,\; ET1=1,\; EX1=1, \text{others}=0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Priority Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Reset in the natural priority list** — students often write only the five maskable interrupts. Reset ALWAYS ranks first. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Misreading IP register bits** — the IP register is at `0xB8` and is **bit-addressable**. Confusing it with the IE register (`0xA8`) is a frequent error. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Believing that setting IP=1 makes an interrupt "absolute" highest** — the IP register only classifies into HP/LP. The natural priority still applies within each class. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** at the end of an ISR — the interrupt controller will not unblock lower-priority pending interrupts, leading to system hang. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 5. **Ignoring the 8-byte vector spacing** — placing ISR code directly after the vector without an `LJMP` will overlap with the next interrupt's vector. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI flags** in the serial ISR — unlike the timer/external flags, these must be cleared in **software**. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Confusing edge-triggered vs. level-triggered flag behavior** — for level-triggered mode (`ITx=0`), the flag is **not** hardware-cleared and may re-trigger the ISR. **(−1 mark)**

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ The 8051 supports **6 interrupts**: Reset, INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial Port.
- ✅ Reset is the **highest-priority, non-maskable, non-vectored** interrupt; it jumps to `0x0000`.
- ✅ The **IP register** (SFR `0xB8`, bit-addressable) controls programmable priority — bit = 1 means **High Priority**, bit = 0 means **Low Priority**.
- ✅ The **natural priority sequence** (Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial) is hardwired and applies **within** the same priority class.
- ✅ **Two-tier resolution:** Programmable priority (HP/LP) is decided first; natural priority breaks ties within the same class.
- ✅ **Nesting rule:** HP can interrupt LP unconditionally; HP can interrupt HP only if higher in natural rank; LP **cannot** interrupt any ISR in progress.
- ✅ **Vector addresses** are spaced exactly **8 bytes** apart starting from `0x0003`.
- ✅ The **IE register** (SFR `0xA8`) controls global enable (`EA`) and individual enables (`EX0`, `ET0`, `EX1`, `ET1`, `ES`).
- ✅ **TCON register** holds timer run controls (`TR0`, `TR1`), overflow flags (`TF0`, `TF1`), external interrupt flags (`IE0`, `IE1`), and trigger type (`IT0`, `IT1`).
- ✅ For **edge-triggered** external interrupts (`ITx=1`), the IE0/IE1 flag is **hardware-cleared** automatically; for **level-triggered** (`ITx=0`), it must be cleared in software.
- ✅ Always end ISRs with the **`RETI`** instruction — it pops the return address AND signals the interrupt controller that servicing is complete.
- ✅ Default IP after reset is **`0x00`** (all maskable interrupts at Low Priority).
- ✅ Default IE after reset is **`0x00`** (all interrupts disabled — global mask active).
- ✅ The serial port interrupt flag (`TI`/`RI` in SCON) is **never** hardware-cleared — software MUST clear it inside the ISR.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PRIORITY OF INTERRUPTS (MODULE 3)**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
