<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔔 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Timer Interrupt (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Timer Interrupt** is a hardware-triggered asynchronous event generated by the internal Timer/Counter module of the 8051 microcontroller when the timer register (**TLx/THx**) overflows from its maximum value (`FFFFH` for Mode 1) or reaches a pre-loaded terminal count. The overflow sets the corresponding **Timer Flag bit (TF0 for Timer 0 / TF1 for Timer 1)** in the **TCON** register. If the corresponding interrupt is unmasked (via the **IE** register) and the **global enable flag EA** is set, the 8051 core **suspends** the main program execution, **saves the Program Counter (PC)** on the stack, and transfers control to a **predefined vector address** (Timer 0 → `000BH`, Timer 1 → `001BH`) where the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** resides.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, timer interrupts belong to the **"Internal / Vectored Interrupts"** family — a class of interrupts whose source, vector address, and priority are **hard-wired** inside the silicon die of the 8051 and cannot be changed by firmware.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef and the Cooking Timer" 🍳

Imagine a chef (the **CPU**) is busy chopping vegetables (running the **main program**). Simultaneously, a **kitchen timer** (the **hardware timer**) is counting down on the oven.

- The chef does **not** have to keep checking ("polling") the timer every second.
- When the timer hits zero, it **rings a bell** (sets the **TFx flag**) on the chef's ear.
- The chef then **pauses chopping** (the CPU finishes the current instruction — completes its **instruction cycle**).
- He **writes down where he stopped** on a notepad (saves the **PC** onto the **stack**).
- He **walks to the oven** (the CPU jumps to the **ISR vector address**).
- He **handles the dish** (executes the **Interrupt Service Routine**).
- When done, he **reads the notepad** to find where he was (executes the **RETI** instruction → pops PC back from stack).
- He **resumes chopping** exactly from where he left off.

This is **precisely** how a timer interrupt works in the 8051 — it frees the CPU from wasteful polling and enables **multitasking on a single core**.

## 1.3 Why Timer Interrupts? (KTU Board Favourite Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three key engineering reasons** timer interrupts are mandatory in 8051-based systems:
> 1. **Elimination of Polling Overhead** — CPU bandwidth is conserved; no `JNB TF0, $` busy-wait loops.
> 2. **Deterministic Timing** — Time-critical tasks (e.g., ADC sampling, UART baud generation, PWM) run at **guaranteed** fixed intervals.
> 3. **Concurrent Task Handling** — A single-core 8051 can appear to "do many things at once" by time-slicing via a timer ISR.

## 1.4 Key Hardware Building Blocks Involved

| Hardware Block | SFR Address | Role in Timer Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| **TCON** (Timer Control) | `88H` | Holds the **TF0 / TF1 overflow flags** that *trigger* the interrupt |
| **IE** (Interrupt Enable) | `A8H` | **Unmasks** the timer interrupt via **ET0 / ET1** and global **EA** |
| **IP** (Interrupt Priority) | `B8H` | Assigns **high / low priority** via **PT0 / PT1** |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | `81H` | Stores the **return address (PC)** when ISR is entered |
| **TMOD** | `89H` | Configures the **timer mode** (0, 1, 2, 3) and operation |
| **THx / TLx** | `8CH/8AH` (T0) | The **counting registers** that physically overflow to set TFx |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Logical Phases of Timer Interrupt Handling

The 8051 hardware executes an **exact, deterministic sequence** the instant a timer interrupt is recognized. Mastering these five phases is the single highest-yield KTU topic in Module 3.

### Phase 1 — Hardware Event Detection (The "Trigger")
- The 16-bit counter (THx + TLx) increments on every **machine cycle** (1 µs at 11.0592 MHz crystal, 1.085 µs at 12 MHz).
- On overflow from `FFFFH → 0000H`, the hardware **automatically sets the TFx bit** in TCON. No software intervention is needed.
- This is a **level-triggered, internal, edge-evaluated** event — invisible to the user until sampled.

### Phase 2 — Interrupt Request Latching
- The TFx flag acts as the **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line to the interrupt controller block inside the 8051.
- The controller checks two conditions: **Is EA = 1?** AND **Is the specific ETx = 1?**
- If both true → the request is **forwarded to the priority resolver**.

### Phase 3 — Priority Arbitration
- If a **higher-priority interrupt** is already in service (or simultaneously pending), the timer interrupt is **held**.
- The 8051 uses a **two-level priority scheme** (set via IP register). Default is **low priority** for all interrupts.
- Setting **PT0 = 1** (for Timer 0) or **PT1 = 1** (for Timer 1) promotes the timer to **high priority**, allowing it to preempt a low-priority ISR.

### Phase 4 — Vectored Call to ISR (The "Save & Jump")
> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Hardware-Atomic Steps inside Phase 4:**
> 1. The current instruction finishes execution completely (no mid-instruction interruption is allowed).
> 2. The **Program Counter (PC)** is automatically **pushed onto the stack** (as a 16-bit value — high byte first, then low byte).
> 3. The **TFx flag is CLEARED automatically by hardware** (for Timer 0/1 interrupts, the flag is cleared on the vector fetch).
> 4. The PC is loaded with the **fixed vector address**: `000BH` for Timer 0, `001BH` for Timer 1.
> 5. Execution begins at the ISR. The ISR typically starts with **register bank push** (`PUSH PSW` / `PUSH ACC`) to save context.

### Phase 5 — Return from Interrupt (The "Restore")
- The ISR ends with the **RETI** instruction (not `RET`!).
- `RETI` performs **two distinct operations**:
  1. **Pops PC from stack** → restores execution flow.
  2. **Signals the interrupt controller** that this priority level is now free to accept a new interrupt of equal/lower priority.
- Using `RET` instead of `RETI` is one of the **most common KTU board mistakes** — the system "appears" to work, but nested interrupts fail silently.

## 2.2 The IE Register — Interrupt Enable (SFR @ `A8H`)

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **EA** | **Global Enable** — Master switch. Must be `1` for ANY interrupt. |
| Bit 6 | — | Reserved |
| Bit 5 | — | Reserved |
| Bit 4 | **ES** | Enable **Serial Port** interrupt |
| Bit 3 | **ET1** | Enable **Timer 1** overflow interrupt |
| Bit 2 | **EX1** | Enable **External Interrupt 1** |
| Bit 1 | **ET0** | Enable **Timer 0** overflow interrupt |
| Bit 0 | **EX0** | Enable **External Interrupt 0** |

**Typical KTU line of code:** `IE = 0x82;` → EA=1, ET0=1 (Timer 0 enabled, all others off). `IE = 0x8A;` → enables Timer 0 + Timer 1.

## 2.3 The TCON Register — Timer/Counter Control (SFR @ `88H`)

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **TF1** | **Timer 1 Overflow Flag** — set by HW, cleared by HW on ISR entry or by SW. |
| Bit 6 | **TR1** | **Timer 1 Run Control** — `1` = start, `0` = stop (must be set in SW). |
| Bit 5 | **TF0** | **Timer 0 Overflow Flag** — analogous to TF1. |
| Bit 4 | **TR0** | **Timer 0 Run Control**. |
| Bit 3 | IE1 | External Int 1 edge flag |
| Bit 2 | IT1 | External Int 1 trigger type |
| Bit 1 | IE0 | External Int 0 edge flag |
| Bit 0 | IT0 | External Int 0 trigger type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TRx vs. TFx — The Most Confused Pair in KTU Papers:**
> - **TRx** is the *software-controlled* "start button" for the timer.
> - **TFx** is the *hardware-set* "overflow alarm bell".
> - Setting TR0 = 1 **does NOT** generate an interrupt. It only starts counting. The interrupt only fires when the count rolls over and TF0 = 1.

## 2.4 Timer Interrupt Vector Addresses (Hard-Wired)

| Interrupt Source | Flag | Vector Address | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | `000BH` | "T-zero" |
| **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | `001BH` | "T-one" |

> [!NOTE]
> The vector address is **fixed in silicon**. The `LJMP` instruction at this address redirects execution to wherever the programmer has placed the actual ISR in code memory (typically at `0x0030`, `0x0050`, etc.).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Timer Tick Count | $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}}$ | dimensionless integer |
| 3 | Timer Reload Value (Mode 1) | $Reload = 65536 - N$ | hex (16-bit) |
| 4 | THx Byte | $THx = \dfrac{Reload}{256}$ | high byte |
| 5 | TLx Byte | $TLx = (Reload) \mod 256$ | low byte |
| 6 | Max Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{max} = 65536 \times T_{mc}$ | ≈ 65.536 ms @ 11.0592 MHz |
| 7 | Max Delay (Mode 2) | $T_{max} = 256 \times T_{mc}$ | ≈ 256 µs @ 11.0592 MHz |
| 8 | Interrupt Latency | $Latency = (2 \text{ to } 8) \times T_{mc}$ | machine cycles |
| 9 | Stack Growth (8051) | $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ on PUSH | stack grows **upward** |
| 10 | Polling Check Pattern | `while (TF0 == 0);` | bad practice — use ISR |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where timer interrupts shine in industry:**
> - **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** — A periodic timer ISR is the **heartbeat / tick** that drives task scheduling (e.g., FreeRTOS port to 8051).
> - **Digital Clock Displays** — Timer ISR updates the 7-segment display every 1 second.
> - **UART Baud Rate Generators** — Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload) generates the baud rate clock for the on-chip serial port.
> - **PWM Generation** — A fast timer ISR toggles a pin to synthesize any duty cycle.
> - **Debouncing** — A timer ISR re-samples a noisy mechanical switch every 5 ms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Generating a 50 ms Interrupt Tick (Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate an **interrupt every 50 ms** using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Write the C initialization code, the ISR skeleton, and the main program. Assume the ISR toggles Port Pin P1.0.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Period
The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Required Tick Count
The timer must count up for 50 ms. Number of timer ticks needed:

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N = 46080 \text{ ticks (decimal)}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the 16-bit Reload Value
In Mode 1, the timer is a 16-bit counter that rolls over from `FFFFH` to `0000H`. To get exactly 46080 ticks, we preload:

$$Reload = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 \text{ (decimal)}$$

Converting 19456 to hexadecimal:

$$19456 \div 16 = 1216 \text{ remainder } 0$$

$$1216 \div 16 = 76 \text{ remainder } 0$$

$$76 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 12 \rightarrow C$$

$$4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4$$

Reading remainders **bottom-up**:

$$19456_{10} = 4C00_{H}$$

### Step 4 — Split into High and Low Bytes

$$TH0 = \frac{Reload}{256} = \frac{0x4C00}{0x100} = 0x4C$$

$$TL0 = (Reload) \bmod 256 = 0x4C00 \bmod 0x100 = 0x00$$

### Step 5 — Configure TMOD for Timer 0, Mode 1
TMOD bits: `GATE1 | C/T1 | M1_1 | M0_1 | GATE0 | C/T0 | M1_0 | M0_0`
- Gate = 0 (software start)
- C/T̄ = 0 (timer mode, not counter)
- M1 = 0, M0 = 1 → Mode 1
- Lower nibble = `0001` = `0x01`

$$TMOD = 0x01$$

### Step 6 — Set Up the IE Register
Enable Timer 0 + Global enable. Other interrupts off.

$$IE = 0x82 \quad \text{(binary: 10000010)}$$

### Step 7 — Load the Timer, Start It, and Write the ISR

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;
unsigned int tick_count = 0;

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1   // vector 1 = 000BH = Timer 0
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;     // reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;     // reload low byte
    tick_count++;
    if (tick_count >= 20)   // 20 × 50 ms = 1 second
    {
        LED = ~LED;         // toggle LED every 1 sec
        tick_count = 0;
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;     // T0, Mode 1, software start
    TH0  = 0x4C;     // load high byte
    TL0  = 0x00;     // load low byte
    IE   = 0x82;     // enable Timer 0 interrupt
    TR0  = 1;        // START Timer 0
    while(1)
    {
        // main program can do anything here
        // the LED will be toggled "in the background" by the ISR
    }
}
```

### Step 8 — Verification of Timing
Per interrupt = 50 ms. 20 interrupts = 1000 ms = **1 second** ✓. The LED blinks once per second.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Timer 1 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) for a 100 µs Tick

**Problem Statement:**
Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to overflow every 100 µs. Crystal = 12 MHz.

### Step 1 — Machine Cycle
$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Tick Count
$$N = \frac{100 \, \mu s}{1 \, \mu s} = 100 \text{ ticks}$$

### Step 3 — Reload Value (Mode 2 uses only TL1; TH1 is the auto-reload register)
$$Reload = 256 - N = 256 - 100 = 156_{10}$$

$$156_{10} = 9C_{H}$$

### Step 4 — Register Configuration
$$TH1 = TL1 = 0x9C$$

$$TMOD = 0x20 \quad \text{(T1, Mode 2)}$$

$$IE = 0x88 \quad \text{(EA=1, ET1=1)}$$

```c
void timer1_ISR(void) interrupt 3   // vector 3 = 001BH = Timer 1
{
    // No manual reload needed in Mode 2!
    // Hardware automatically copies TH1 → TL1 on overflow
    P1 ^= 0x01;   // toggle P1.0 every 100 µs
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode 2 is the preferred mode for periodic interrupts** because the hardware auto-reload eliminates any reload jitter caused by the few machine cycles of ISR entry latency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Timer Modes for Interrupt Use

| Property | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit AR) | Mode 3 (Split T0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Counter Width | 13 bits | **16 bits** | 8 bits | Two 8-bit |
| Max Tick Count | 8192 | **65536** | 256 | 256 each |
| Auto-Reload | ❌ Manual | ❌ Manual | ✅ **Hardware** | ❌ Manual |
| Reload Jitter | High | High | **None** | High |
| KTU Use Case | Legacy | Long delays | **UART baud, periodic** | Dual-baud (rare) |
| Recommended for ISR? | No | Yes (with reload) | **Yes (best)** | No |

## 3.4 Interrupt Latency Calculation

The worst-case latency is the time from TFx setting to the first instruction of the ISR executing:

$$Latency = T_{mc} \times (1 \text{ to } 2) \text{ [current instr.]} + T_{mc} \times 2 \text{ [call latency]} + \text{ISR entry overhead}$$

$$\boxed{Latency_{max} \approx 8 \times T_{mc} = 8 \, \mu s \text{ @ 12 MHz}}$$

This 8 µs ceiling is **why** timer values must be reloaded immediately inside the ISR (especially in Mode 1) — otherwise the next interrupt is delayed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Timer Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power-On Reset: PC=0000H, SP=07H] --> B[Initialize TMOD, THx, TLx]
    B --> C[Set IE register: EA=1, ETx=1]
    C --> D[Set TRx=1: Start Timer Counting]
    D --> E[Main Loop executing]
    E --> F{TFx Overflow?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes - Hardware sets TFx| G[CPU finishes current instruction]
    G --> H[Push PC onto Stack]
    H --> I[Hardware clears TFx flag]
    I --> J[Load PC with Vector Address 000BH or 001BH]
    J --> K[Execute ISR: PUSH ACC, B, PSW]
    K --> L[Reload THx, TLx if Mode 0/1]
    L --> M[Execute ISR body / user task]
    M --> N[POP PSW, B, ACC]
    N --> O[Execute RETI instruction]
    O --> P[Pop PC from Stack]
    P --> Q[Signal Priority Controller: ISR done]
    Q --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Timer Interrupt Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_8051[8051 CPU Core]
        ALU[ALU and Accumulator]
        PC_REG[Program Counter PC]
        SP_REG[Stack Pointer SP]
    end

    subgraph TIMER_BLOCK[Timer 0 Module]
        TH0_REG[TH0 8-bit High]
        TL0_REG[TL0 8-bit Low]
        MUX[Mode MUX M0 M1]
        INCR[1 to Tmc Incrementer]
    end

    subgraph CTRL_BLOCK[Control and Status SFRs]
        TCON_REG[TCON: TR0 TF0 TR1 TF1]
        TMOD_REG[TMOD: Mode and Source]
        IE_REG[IE: EA ET0 ET1]
        IP_REG[IP: PT0 PT1 Priority]
    end

    OSC[Crystal Oscillator 11.0592 MHz] --> DIV12[Divide by 12]
    DIV12 --> INCR
    INCR --> TH0_REG
    TH0_REG --> TL0_REG
    MUX --> TIMER_BLOCK
    TMOD_REG --> MUX
    TL0_REG --> OVF_CHECK{Overflow FFFFH to 0000H}
    OVF_CHECK -->|Yes| TCON_REG
    OVF_CHECK -->|No| INCR
    TCON_REG --> IRQ_LOGIC[Interrupt Request Logic]
    IE_REG --> IRQ_LOGIC
    IP_REG --> IRQ_LOGIC
    IRQ_LOGIC --> VECT_CTRL[Vector Address Generator]
    VECT_CTRL -->|000BH or 001BH| PC_REG
    PC_REG --> SP_REG
    SP_REG --> ISR_MEM[ISR Code in ROM]
    ISR_MEM --> RETI_INST[RETI Instruction]
    RETI_INST --> CPU_8051
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Interrupt Priority Arbitration

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: CPU running main code
    Idle --> Pending: TFx set and IE masked
    Pending --> Servicing: Priority arbitration won
    Servicing --> Blocked: Higher priority ISR active
    Blocked --> Servicing: Higher ISR completes
    Servicing --> Idle: RETI executed
    Pending --> Idle: Flag cleared before arbitration
```

## 4.4 Memory Map — Interrupt Vector Locations in 8051 Code Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["0000H - RESET Vector"] --> A1["0003H - External Int 0"]
    A1 --> A2["000BH - TIMER 0 Interrupt"]
    A2 --> A3["0013H - External Int 1"]
    A3 --> A4["001BH - TIMER 1 Interrupt"]
    A4 --> A5["0023H - Serial Port Interrupt"]
    A5 --> A6["002BH onwards - User Code and ISRs"]
    A2 -.-> ISR_T0["Typical ISR placement: 0030H via LJMP from 000BH"]
    A4 -.-> ISR_T1["Typical ISR placement: 0050H via LJMP from 001BH"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between polling and interrupt-based timer handling in 8051. State two advantages of the interrupt method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Polling | Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| CPU activity | Repeatedly checks TFx flag in tight loop | Executes main code; ISR runs only on overflow |
| CPU efficiency | Wasted cycles | Fully utilized |
| Response time | Software-controlled, variable | Hardware-triggered, deterministic |

**Advantages of interrupt method:**
1. CPU bandwidth is freed for other tasks (e.g., display update, keypad scan).
2. Multiple tasks can be "time-sliced" by a single timer ISR, creating an illusion of multitasking on a single core.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the SFR bits that must be set to enable Timer 0 interrupt in 8051. What is its vector address?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `EA = 1` (bit 7 of IE register @ A8H) — global enable.
- `ET0 = 1` (bit 1 of IE register) — Timer 0 specific enable.
- `TR0 = 1` (bit 4 of TCON register @ 88H) — start the timer.
- **Vector address: `000BH`**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Module-based Part B question offers an **internal choice**. Two fully-solved alternatives are provided below.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Timer 0 Interrupt, Mode 1, Multi-Second Timing**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the complete sequence of events that occur inside the 8051 hardware when a Timer 0 overflow interrupt is recognized. Clearly state the role of the IE, TCON, and IP registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Timer 0 is to be configured in **Mode 1** to generate an interrupt **every 25 ms**, which will toggle pin **P2.3**. Compute the timer reload value, write the full C program, and explain how to extend this to obtain a **1 Hz** blinking rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hardware Sequence (7 marks):**
1. The timer registers TH0-TL0 increment on every machine cycle (`12 / f_osc`).
2. When TH0-TL0 rolls over from `FFFFH` to `0000H`, hardware **sets TF0 = 1** in TCON.
3. The interrupt controller checks: `EA == 1` AND `ET0 == 1`. If both true, the request is forwarded.
4. The **IP register's PT0 bit** determines if this is a high- or low-priority request. If a higher-priority ISR is active, the controller waits.
5. The CPU finishes its current instruction (no mid-instruction preemption).
6. The 16-bit **PC is pushed** to the stack (high byte first, then low byte); SP increments by 2.
7. **TF0 is cleared automatically** by hardware on vector fetch.
8. **PC is loaded with `000BH`** (Timer 0 vector).
9. Execution begins at the ISR. The ISR saves ACC, B, PSW as needed.
10. On `RETI`, the saved PC is popped, and the priority controller is notified that this priority level is free.

**(b) Numerical & Code Solution (7 marks):**

**Calculation:**
$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

$$N = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 23040 \text{ ticks}$$

$$Reload = 65536 - 23040 = 42496_{10} = A600_H$$

$$TH0 = 0xA6, \quad TL0 = 0x00$$

**For 1 Hz**: Need 40 interrupts of 25 ms each (40 × 25 ms = 1000 ms = 1 s). Use a software counter inside the ISR.

**Complete C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit TOGGLE_PIN = P2^3;
unsigned char count = 0;

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0xA6;
    TL0 = 0x00;
    count++;
    if (count >= 40)
    {
        TOGGLE_PIN = ~TOGGLE_PIN;
        count = 0;
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;
    TH0  = 0xA6;
    TL0  = 0x00;
    IE   = 0x82;
    TR0  = 1;
    while(1);
}
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Timer 1 Mode 2, UART-Style Application**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3 Alternate]**

**(a)** Compare the four Timer modes (0, 1, 2, 3) of the 8051 with respect to their suitability for interrupt-driven applications. Justify why **Mode 2** is preferred for generating periodic ticks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system with a **12 MHz crystal** uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a **200 µs periodic interrupt**. Calculate the TH1/TL1 value, configure all relevant SFRs, and write the ISR that increments a global variable `tick_ms` once every **5 interrupts** (i.e., every 1 ms). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mode Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Mode | Width | Auto-Reload | Jitter | Verdict for ISR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 13-bit | No | High | Obsolete; rarely used |
| 1 | 16-bit | No | High (needs manual reload) | OK for long delays |
| **2** | **8-bit** | **Yes (THx → TLx)** | **Zero** | **Best for periodic ISR** |
| 3 | Two 8-bit | No | High | Special dual-baud only |

**Justification for Mode 2:** Hardware auto-reload copies TH1 to TL1 on overflow in a single machine cycle, eliminating the few µs of jitter introduced when software must reload TH/TL. This makes Mode 2 ideal for **baud-rate generation** and **high-precision periodic interrupts**.

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$

$$N = \frac{200 \, \mu s}{1 \, \mu s} = 200 \text{ ticks}$$

$$Reload = 256 - 200 = 56_{10} = 38_H$$

$$\boxed{TH1 = TL1 = 0x38}$$

$$TMOD = 0x20 \quad \text{(T1 Mode 2, software start)}$$

$$IE = 0x88 \quad \text{(EA=1, ET1=1)}$$

**C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
unsigned char tick_ms = 0;
unsigned char cnt = 0;

void timer1_ISR(void) interrupt 3
{
    cnt++;
    if (cnt >= 5)   // 5 × 200 µs = 1 ms
    {
        tick_ms++;
        cnt = 0;
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x20;
    TH1  = 0x38;
    TL1  = 0x38;
    IE   = 0x88;
    TR1  = 1;
    while(1);
}
```

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks on timer-interrupt questions (read carefully!):**
> 1. **Writing `RET` instead of `RETI` at the end of the ISR** — `RET` does NOT signal the priority controller; nested interrupts will hang. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** specifically for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1 inside the ISR** — the next period will be ~65 ms instead of the intended 25 ms. Always show the reload as the first two lines of the ISR.
> 3. **Setting `ET0 = 1` but forgetting `EA = 1`** — interrupts are masked globally. The board will mark this as "interrupt not enabled".
> 4. **Using `C/T̄ = 1` (counter mode) by mistake** — the timer will then count external pulses on T0/T1 pin, not machine cycles. Verify the lower nibble of TMOD carefully.
> 5. **Confusing `TR0` and `TF0`** — TR0 is your *start* switch; TF0 is the *alarm*. Both are needed: TR0 = 1 to begin, TF0 = 1 to interrupt.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Quick-Recall Bullet Box — Revise in 60 seconds:**
- **Two Timer Interrupts**: Timer 0 @ `000BH`, Timer 1 @ `001BH` — both are **internal, vectored** interrupts.
- **Enable Formula**: `IE = 0x80 | (1 << 1)` for Timer 0, `IE = 0x80 | (1 << 3)` for Timer 1.
- **Start the timer**: `TR0 = 1` or `TR1 = 1` (in TCON).
- **TFx is hardware-cleared** on vector fetch for timer interrupts; you don't need to clear it in ISR.
- **Reload math**: `Reload = 65536 - (Delay / T_mc)` for Mode 1; `Reload = 256 - (Delay / T_mc)` for Mode 2.
- **ISR must end with `RETI`**, never `RET`.
- **Mode 2 is best for periodic interrupts** (no reload jitter).
- **Mode 1 is best for long single delays** (up to ~65 ms @ 11.0592 MHz).
- **Save context**: At ISR start, `PUSH ACC`, `PUSH PSW`; restore at end with `POP PSW`, `POP ACC` (LIFO order).
- **Stack grows upward** in 8051 — `PUSH` increments SP, `POP` decrements SP.
- **Priority**: Default = low for all. Set PT0/PT1 = 1 in IP register for high priority → allows preemption of low-priority ISRs.
- **Latency**: ~8 machine cycles from TFx set to first ISR instruction.
- **Jitter in Mode 1**: ~3–8 µs due to reload timing — acceptable for ≥1 ms ticks, problematic for sub-100 µs tasks.
- **Clock frequency gotcha**: 11.0592 MHz is preferred over 12 MHz because it produces **exact integer baud rates** for the UART (Timer 1 in Mode 2).
- **Typical KTU line of code** that appears in 90% of answers: `IE = 0x82; TR0 = 1;` — memorize this.
- **Most-asked KTU sub-question**: "Calculate the timer reload value for X ms delay using Y MHz crystal." — practice at least 5 such problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External and Serial Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** External and Serial Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Interrupt?

An **interrupt** is an asynchronous event (either internal or external to the microcontroller) that temporarily halts the main program execution so that the CPU can service a higher-priority request. After servicing, the CPU returns to the exact point in the main program where it was interrupted, and resumes execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PBICT504 - Module 3):**
> An interrupt is a hardware- or software-generated signal that causes the microcontroller to suspend its current sequence of instruction execution and branch to a special routine known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Upon completion of the ISR, the microcontroller resumes normal program flow from the interrupted instruction's next address.

### 1.2 The 8051 Interrupt System — Overview

The 8051 microcontroller supports **five interrupt sources**, out of which **two are external** and **one is serial**. The other two are timers (Timer 0 and Timer 1 overflow).

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Pin / Source |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** | IE0 | `0003H` | **P3.2** |
| **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | `000BH` | Internal |
| **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** | IE1 | `0013H` | **P3.3** |
| **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | `001BH` | Internal |
| **Serial Port (TI/RI)** | TI/RI | `0023H` | Internal (TX/RX) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bell in a Library"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition: The Librarian's Bell**
> Imagine a librarian quietly reading a book (the **main program**). Suddenly, a patron at the door rings a bell (an **interrupt request**). The librarian pauses, marks the page with a bookmark (saves the **Program Counter** on the stack), and goes to attend to the patron (executes the **ISR**). Once done, the librarian returns to the bookmarked page and resumes reading (executes **RETI**).
> - The **bell button** is the interrupt pin.
> - The **bookmark** is the stack-pushed return address.
> - The **bell enable switch** is the IE (Interrupt Enable) register.
> - The **priority of multiple patrons** is the IP (Interrupt Priority) register.

### 1.4 External Interrupts — Core Definition

**External Interrupts (INT0 and INT1)** are hardware-triggered interrupts initiated by an external signal applied to pins **P3.2 (INT0)** and **P3.3 (INT1)** of the 8051. They can be configured as either **level-triggered** (low) or **edge-triggered** (falling edge) using the bits **IT0** and **IT1** in the **TCON** register.

### 1.5 Serial Interrupt — Core Definition

The **Serial Port Interrupt** is triggered by either of two flags:
- **TI (Transmit Interrupt)** — set when a byte transmission is complete.
- **RI (Receive Interrupt)** — set when a byte has been completely received.

Both flags reside in the **SCON** register. They are logically **OR'd** internally; software must explicitly check which flag caused the interrupt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must understand the role of the **IE (Interrupt Enable)**, **IP (Interrupt Priority)**, **TCON** (for external interrupt triggering), and **SCON** (for serial interrupt) special function registers (SFRs) at the bit level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Interrupt Handling Sequence (Universal for 8051)

The 8051 follows a strict, automatic sequence whenever an interrupt is accepted:

1. **Detection:** The interrupt flag (e.g., IE0, TI/RI) becomes set.
2. **Enabling Check:** The corresponding enable bit in the **IE register** must be set to `1`.
3. **Priority Check:** If multiple interrupts are pending, the **IP register** decides which is served first.
4. **Hardware Response:**
   - The current instruction finishes execution.
   - The 16-bit **Program Counter (PC)** is automatically pushed onto the **stack** (saved).
   - The **Interrupt Flag** is automatically **cleared** (for edge-triggered external interrupts) or remains as-is (for level/serial).
5. **Vector Fetch:** CPU loads the PC with the **Interrupt Vector Address**.
6. **ISR Execution:** The Interrupt Service Routine begins.
7. **Return (RETI):** The saved PC is popped from the stack, and execution resumes.

### 2.2 The IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address `0xA8`

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Default | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **EA (Enable All):** Must be `1` to enable *any* interrupt. Global master switch.
- **ES (Enable Serial):** Enables the serial port interrupt (TI/RI).
- **ET1, ET0:** Enable Timer 1 and Timer 0 overflow interrupts.
- **EX1, EX0:** Enable External Interrupts 1 and 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students forget to set `EA = 1`. Without it, the IE register's other enable bits are meaningless.

### 2.3 The IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address `0xB8`

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |

Setting a bit to `1` raises that interrupt to **high priority**. A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority one. On reset, all bits are `0` (low priority), and the **default polling order** is: `INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial`.

### 2.4 TCON Register (Timer/Counter Control) — Address `0x88` (External Interrupt Bits)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **IT0, IT1 (Interrupt Type):** `0` = **Level-triggered** (low), `1` = **Edge-triggered** (falling edge).
- **IE0, IE1 (Interrupt Edge/Level Flag):** Set by hardware; **auto-cleared** if edge-triggered, but must be **manually cleared** if level-triggered.

### 2.5 SCON Register (Serial Control) — Address `0x98` (Serial Interrupt Bits)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |

- **TI (Transmit Interrupt):** Set by hardware at the **end of the stop bit** time of the 8th bit. **Must be cleared by software.**
- **RI (Receive Interrupt):** Set by hardware at the **end of the 8th bit received**. **Must be cleared by software.**

### 2.6 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vector Address Computation | `Vector = 8 \times (Interrupt\_Number + 1) + 3` | Hexadecimal; for INT0: $8 \times 1 + 3 = 11 = 0003H$ |
| INT0 Vector | `0003H` | Bytes 0003H–000AH (8 bytes) |
| Timer 0 Vector | `000BH` | Bytes 000BH–0012H |
| INT1 Vector | `0013H` | Bytes 0013H–001AH |
| Timer 1 Vector | `001BH` | Bytes 001BH–0022H |
| Serial Vector | `0023H` | Bytes 0023H–002AH |
| Stack Pointer After INT | `SP` increases by **2** | Because PC (16-bit) is pushed |
| Timer Reload for 50 ms @ 11.0592 MHz | $T = \frac{(65536 - X) \times 12}{11059200}$ | Yields value for TH/TL |
| Polling Order (Default) | `INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial` | By vector address order |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **External Interrupts:** Used for emergency stop buttons, real-time sensor events (e.g., zero-crossing detector in motor control), and external peripheral handshaking.
- **Serial Interrupts:** Enable **buffered, non-blocking communication** with PCs, GSM modules, and other MCUs. Without it, the CPU is stuck in polling loops.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** In RTOS-less designs, interrupts are the **only** mechanism for deterministic response to multiple events.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vector Address Calculation

The 8051 has a fixed **8-byte slot** for each ISR in program memory. The vector address is derived from the interrupt's natural position in the polling sequence.

$$
\text{Vector Address} = 3 + (8 \times n)
$$

where $n$ is the position of the interrupt in the polling order, starting from $n = 0$ for a hypothetical 0th interrupt (which doesn't exist, hence the offset of 3).

**Step-by-step for INT0 (n=0):**
$$
\text{Vector} = 3 + (8 \times 0) = 3 = 0003H
$$

**For Timer 0 (n=1):**
$$
\text{Vector} = 3 + (8 \times 1) = 3 + 8 = 11 = 000BH
$$

**For INT1 (n=2):**
$$
\text{Vector} = 3 + (8 \times 2) = 3 + 16 = 19 = 0013H
$$

**For Timer 1 (n=3):**
$$
\text{Vector} = 3 + (8 \times 3) = 3 + 24 = 27 = 001BH
$$

**For Serial (n=4):**
$$
\text{Vector} = 3 + (8 \times 4) = 3 + 32 = 35 = 0023H
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** 8 bytes is only enough for very short ISRs. If an ISR is longer, it must use an **ORG (origin) directive** to jump to a separate code area.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: External Interrupt — Edge vs. Level Triggering

The behavior of the IE0/IE1 flag in TCON depends on IT0/IT1:

**Case 1: IT0 = 1 (Edge-Triggered, Falling Edge)**

$$
\text{On falling edge of P3.2:} \quad IE0 \leftarrow 1 \text{ (hardware set)}
$$
$$
\text{On ISR entry:} \quad IE0 \leftarrow 0 \text{ (hardware auto-cleared)}
$$

**Case 2: IT0 = 0 (Level-Triggered, Active Low)**

$$
\text{While P3.2 is LOW:} \quad IE0 \leftarrow 1 \text{ (continuously held)}
$$
$$
\text{On ISR entry:} \quad IE0 \text{ is NOT auto-cleared. Software MUST clear it.}
$$
$$
\text{Condition: P3.2 must go HIGH, then LOW again to retrigger.}
$$

**Analytical Matrix for External Interrupt Triggering:**

| ITx Bit | Trigger Type | Flag Set By | Flag Cleared By | Retrigger Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **0** | Low Level | External pin low | **Software** (must clear) | Pin goes high then low |
| **1** | Falling Edge | Falling edge | **Hardware** (auto on ISR) | Another falling edge |

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Serial Interrupt — TI/RI Logic

The serial interrupt is unique because it has **two causes** under one vector (`0023H`).

$$
\text{Interrupt Generated} = (TI = 1) \lor (RI = 1)
$$

**Software Must Disambiguate Inside the ISR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } TI = 1 \text{ and } RI = 0 &: \text{ Transmission Complete} \rightarrow \text{Clear } TI \\
\text{If } RI = 1 \text{ and } TI = 0 &: \text{ Byte Received} \rightarrow \text{Clear } RI \text{ and read SBUF} \\
\text{If } TI = 1 \text{ and } RI = 1 &: \text{ Simultaneous TX Done and RX Done (rare)} \rightarrow \text{Clear BOTH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**SBUF Register Behavior:**

$$
\text{Writing to SBUF} \rightarrow \text{Starts transmission}
$$
$$
\text{Reading from SBUF} \rightarrow \text{Reads the last received byte}
$$

The two SBUF operations share address `0x99` but are physically separate in silicon.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Interrupt Latency and Stack Behavior

The latency (delay) between interrupt request and ISR execution is fixed:

$$
\text{Latency (cycles)} = \text{Cycles of current instruction} + 2 \text{ (for pushing PC)}
$$

For the 8051 (12 clocks per machine cycle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min Latency} &= 1 \text{ machine cycle (for a 1-byte, 12-clock instruction)} + 2 \text{ cycles} \\
&= 3 \text{ machine cycles} = 36 \text{ oscillator clocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stack Pointer Update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP (before)} &= \text{Initial Value} \\
\text{After ISR entry: } SP_{new} &= SP_{old} + 2 \\
\text{After RETI: } SP_{new} &= SP_{old} \text{ (restored)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use External vs. Serial Interrupts

| Requirement | Use External Interrupt | Use Serial Interrupt |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Emergency hardware signal (e.g., fault) | ✅ **Best Choice** | ❌ Inappropriate |
| Asynchronous byte arrival on UART | ❌ Wrong | ✅ **Best Choice** |
| Frequency measurement via edges | ✅ Good | ❌ Inappropriate |
| CPU-to-CPU message passing | ❌ Wrong | ✅ Good |
| Wake-up from sleep via event | ✅ Good (edge) | ❌ Limited |
| Multiple concurrent triggers | ✅ With IP register | ✅ With IP register |

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    VECINT1["Vector 0013H ISR INT1"]
    VECT1["Vector 001BH ISR Timer1"]
    VECSER["Vector 0023H ISR Serial"]

    P32 --> TCONReg
    P33 --> TCONReg

    Timer1 --> TCONReg

    TCONReg --> IEReg
    SCONReg --> IEReg
    IEReg --> IPReg

    CPUCore --> VECT1

```

### 4.2 External Interrupt Triggering Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN["External Pin P3.2 or P3.3"]
    CHECKIT{"Check ITx bit in TCON"}
    LEVEL["Level Triggered Mode ITx = 0"]
    EDGE["Edge Triggered Mode ITx = 1"]
    LEVELFLAG["Flag follows pin state"]
    EDGEDET["Detect falling edge"]
    SETFLAG["Set IEx flag to 1"]
    ISRENTRY["Enter ISR"]
    LEVELCLEAR["Software must clear IEx"]
    EDGECLEAR["Hardware auto-clears IEx"]
    RETI["Execute RETI"]

    PIN --> CHECKIT
    CHECKIT -->|ITx = 0| LEVEL
    CHECKIT -->|ITx = 1| EDGE
    LEVEL --> LEVELFLAG --> SETFLAG
    EDGE --> EDGEDET --> SETFLAG
    SETFLAG --> ISRENTRY
    ISRENTRY --> LEVELCLEAR
    ISRENTRY --> EDGECLEAR
    LEVELCLEAR --> RETI
    EDGECLEAR --> RETI
```

### 4.3 Serial Interrupt Disambiguation Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Serial Interrupt Fires at 0023H"]
    CHECKRI{"Check RI flag in SCON"}
    CHECKTI{"Check TI flag in SCON"}
    READSBUF["Read received byte from SBUF"]
    CLEARRI["Clear RI to 0"]
    TRANSMITDONE["Transmit buffer empty"]
    CLEARTI["Clear TI to 0"]
    BOTH["Both flags set - Clear both TI and RI"]
    RETI["Execute RETI"]

    START --> CHECKRI
    CHECKRI -->|RI = 1| READSBUF --> CLEARRI
    CHECKRI -->|RI = 0| CHECKTI
    CHECKTI -->|TI = 1| TRANSMITDONE --> CLEARTI
    CHECKTI -->|TI = 0| RETI
    CLEARRI --> CHECKTI
    CLEARTI --> RETI
    TRANSMITDONE -.->|TI and RI both set| BOTH --> RETI
```

```mermaid
    V4["0023H to 002AH Serial ISR Slot 8 bytes"]
    MAIN["0030H Onwards Main Program"]

    MEM --> RST
    MEM --> V0

    V0 -.->|If ISR is long| ORG

    V2 -.->|If ISR is long| ORG
    V3 -.->|If ISR is long| ORG
    V4 -.->|If ISR is long| ORG
```
