topics.title: Examples of Interfacing Data Memory and Code Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Interfacing Data Memory and Code Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller**, memory is organized using the **Modified Harvard Architecture**, which physically separates **Program (Code) Memory** and **Data Memory**. The 8051 can address a maximum of **64 KB** of external code memory (16-bit program counter) and **64 KB** of external data memory (16-bit data pointer / MOVX instructions). 

**Memory Interfacing** is the process of connecting external memory chips (ROM/EPROM/Flash for code, RAM for data) to the 8051's multiplexed bus system using address latches, address decoders, and the correct control signals — namely **PSEN (Program Store Enable)**, **RD**, **WR**, and **ALE (Address Latch Enable)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The 8051 has a **16-bit address bus** for code memory and a **16-bit address** capability for data memory, but only an **8-bit data bus**. Hence external interfacing requires careful bus demultiplexing and address decoding — this is a frequently asked 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large municipal library**:
- The **main reading room** contains *reference textbooks* (Code Memory / ROM) — these are **read-only**, organized sequentially, and accessed frequently by a librarian (**Program Counter**).
- The **study cubicles** contain *notebooks and writing pads* (Data Memory / RAM) — these are **read/write**, and students (**CPU**) scribble temporary calculations here.
- The **librarian's announcement system** (**ALE**) grabs the student's *hall ticket number* (low-byte address) once, latches it, and uses it for the entire transaction.
- The **librarian's stamp** (**PSEN**) validates only *textbook* access, while the **borrowing slip** (**RD/WR**) validates only *notebook* access.

This separation is precisely why the 8051 needs distinct control signals and cannot use a single unified memory bus.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Maximum external code memory | **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** |
| Maximum external data memory | **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** |
| Internal RAM | **128 bytes** (8051) / **256 bytes** (8052) |
| Data bus width | **8 bits** |
| Address bus width | **16 bits** (A0–A15) |
| Multiplexed bus lines | **AD0–AD7** (Port 0) |
| Latch IC used | **74LS373** (8-bit D-latch) |
| Decoder IC used | **74LS138** (3-to-8 line decoder) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 8051 External Bus Signals — The Role of Each Pin

When interfacing external memory, the following signals must be correctly routed:

1. **Port 0 (P0.0 – P0.7):** Acts as the **multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)**. The low-byte address is sent first, followed by data. A **74LS373 octal latch** (with ALE as its enable) is required to *freeze* the low-byte address.

2. **Port 2 (P2.0 – P2.7):** Provides the **high-byte address (A8–A15)**, which remains stable throughout the bus cycle.

3. **ALE (Pin 30):** *Address Latch Enable* — goes HIGH during the low-byte address phase, then falls to latch the address into 74LS373.

4. **PSEN (Pin 29):** *Program Store Enable* — **active LOW** signal asserted by the 8051 only during an external **code fetch**. It is connected to the **OE (Output Enable)** pin of the external ROM/EPROM.

5. **EA (Pin 31):** *External Access* — when tied LOW, **all** code fetches (even below 4 KB) come from external ROM. When tied HIGH, internal ROM is used up to 4 KB, and external ROM is used above 4 KB.

6. **RD (P3.7) and WR (P3.6):** *Read* and *Write* strobes for **external data memory** (MOVX instructions). RD is connected to the RAM's **OE**, and WR to the RAM's **WE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PSEN and RD are different signals:**
> This is the hallmark of the *Modified Harvard* architecture. The 8051 distinguishes a code read from a data read by selecting PSEN (for code) versus RD (for data). This dual control prevents accidental corruption of program memory during data operations.

### 2.2 The Address Demultiplexing Mechanism

The 8051 sends the **low-byte address** and **data** on the *same physical pins* (P0). The handshake is:

| Phase | ALE | P0 carries | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 (Address) | **HIGH** | A0–A7 (low-byte address) | 74LS373 latches the address |
| Phase 2 (Data) | **LOW** | D0–D7 (actual data) | Latch output is held; P0 is freed for data transfer |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Memory capacity of an IC | $N = 2^n$ bytes | $n$ = number of address lines |
| Address range of a chip | $[B, \ B + N - 1]$ | $B$ = base address, $N$ = size in bytes |
| 8051 code space | $0000\text{H} - \text{FFFFH}$ | Total **64 KB** |
| 8051 external data space | $0000\text{H} - \text{FFFFH}$ | Total **64 KB**, accessed via MOVX |
| 74LS138 output $Y_k$ range | $[k \times 2000\text{H}, \ k \times 2000\text{H} + 1\text{FFFH}]$ | Each $Y_k$ = **8 KB** segment |
| Bytes per 74LS138 output | $2^{13} = 8192$ | $2^{15} / 2^{3} = 2^{12} = 4\text{K}$... **re-derive below** |
| Bytes per 74LS138 output (corrected) | $2^{13} = 8192$ bytes (8 KB) | With A13, A14, A15 as inputs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** A 74LS138 decoder splits the 64 KB address space into **8 blocks of 8 KB each** (since 3 address lines = $2^3 = 8$ outputs, and 3 remaining address lines contribute the within-block addressing: $2^{13} = 8\text{ KB}$). Never confuse this with a 4 KB or 16 KB segmentation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

External memory interfacing is the foundation of every **embedded product** built around the 8051 family:
- **Industrial PLCs** use external EPROM for firmware and external SRAM for stack/log buffers.
- **Automotive ECUs** historically used 8051 derivatives with external Flash for calibration data.
- **Consumer electronics** (washing machines, microwave ovens) use external EEPROM for user settings.

Mastering this topic allows a KTU engineer to design a **complete 8051-based system-on-board** with predictable memory maps — a critical skill for VLSI/embedded system interviews.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through **four canonical KTU-style interfacing problems** with full step-by-step derivations.

---

### **Example 1: Interfacing a 16 KB EPROM (27128) to 8051 — Full Address Range Derivation**

**Given:** Memory IC = **27128** (16 K × 8 EPROM), connected to the 8051 starting at address **0000H**.

**Step 1 — Identify the IC pin count.**
The 27128 has **14 address lines** ($A_0$ to $A_{13}$), since:

$$
N = 2^n \implies 16384 = 2^n \implies n = 14
$$

**Step 2 — Map 8051 address lines to the EPROM.**

- Low-byte address lines **A0–A7** go through the 74LS373 latch to the EPROM's **A0–A7**.
- High-byte address lines **A8–A13** (six pins) go directly from Port 2 to the EPROM's **A8–A13**.
- The remaining **A14, A15** must be used for *chip selection* logic.

**Step 3 — Determine chip select condition.**
The chip is active when the base address starts at **0000H**, i.e., A15 = 0, A14 = 0, and A13 = 0.

**Step 4 — Derive the CS expression using a 74LS138 decoder.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{We need: } &\overline{Y_0} \text{ (active when A15=A14=A13=0)} \\
\text{Inputs: } &C = A_{15}, \quad B = A_{14}, \quad A = A_{13} \\
\text{Condition: } &C=B=A=0 \implies \overline{Y_0} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Connect $\overline{Y_0}$ of 74LS138 to the $\overline{CE}$ (Chip Enable) of 27128.

**Step 5 — Connect control signal.**

$$
\text{PSEN} \rightarrow \overline{OE} \text{ of 27128}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the address range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum address: } & A_{15..0} = 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_b = 0000\text{H} \\
\text{Maximum address: } & A_{15..0} = 0111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_b = 7\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Range: } & 0000\text{H} - 7\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Verification: } & 7\text{FFFH} - 0000\text{H} + 1 = 8000\text{H} = 32\text{ K...}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction above:** With 14 address lines tied directly to the IC and 3 upper lines (A13, A14, A15) used for the decoder, the within-chip addressing is provided by **A0–A12** (13 lines = 8 K). The address range for $\overline{Y_0}$ is therefore **8 KB (0000H–1FFFH)**, not 32 KB.

**Corrected Step 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range: } & 0000\text{H} - 1\text{FFFH} \quad (\text{8 KB segment}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

To obtain a full 16 KB, the EPROM's $\overline{CE}$ must be tied to the **OR-combination** of $\overline{Y_0}$ and $\overline{Y_1}$:

$$
\overline{CE}_{27128} = \overline{Y_0} + \overline{Y_1} \quad \text{(using a 2-input OR gate)}
$$

This widens the range to **0000H–3FFFH (16 KB)**.

---

### **Example 2: Interfacing an 8 KB RAM (6264) at Address Range 4000H–5FFFH**

**Given:** Memory IC = **6264** (8 K × 8 SRAM), base address = 4000H.

**Step 1 — Pin count of 6264.**

$$
N = 2^n \implies 8192 = 2^n \implies n = 13
$$

So 6264 has 13 address lines ($A_0$ to $A_{12}$).

**Step 2 — Identify the decoder output.**

We need the segment starting at 4000H. In binary:

$$
4000\text{H} = 0100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_b
$$

So **A15 = 0, A14 = 1, A13 = 0**. In 74LS138, this combination activates **$\overline{Y_2}$**.

**Step 3 — Address mapping.**

- A0–A7 (latched) → 6264's A0–A7
- A8–A12 (from Port 2) → 6264's A8–A12
- $\overline{CE1}$ of 6264 ← $\overline{Y_2}$ of 74LS138
- $\overline{OE}$ of 6264 ← $\overline{RD}$ (P3.7)
- $\overline{WE}$ of 6264 ← $\overline{WR}$ (P3.6)

**Step 4 — Verify the address range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum: } & 0100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_b = 4000\text{H} \\
\text{Maximum: } & 0101\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_b = 5\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Size check: } & 5\text{FFFH} - 4000\text{H} + 1 = 2000\text{H} = 8192 = 8\text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Example 3: Interfacing 32 KB EPROM (27256) and 32 KB RAM (62256) — Full 64 KB System**

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark design problem**.

**Given:** 27256 (32 K × 8 EPROM) + 62256 (32 K × 8 RAM). Both must be interfaced.

**Step 1 — Determine the address pin count.**

For 27256:

$$
32\text{K} = 2^{15} \implies 15 \text{ address lines } (A_0 \text{ to } A_{14})
$$

For 62256: also 15 address lines.

**Step 2 — Allocate address space using A15 as the chip-select line.**

| A15 | Device Selected | Address Range |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 27256 (EPROM) | **0000H – 7FFFH** |
| 1 | 62256 (RAM) | **8000H – FFFFH** |

**Step 3 — Wiring.**

- **A0–A14** from 8051 → A0–A14 of **both** memory ICs.
- **A15** → $\overline{CE}$ of 27256 (through an inverter, since A15=0 must select ROM).

  Mathematically:
  $$
  \overline{CE}_{27256} = \overline{A_{15}}
  $$

- **A15** → $\overline{CE1}$ of 62256 (direct, since A15=1 must select RAM).

  $$
  \overline{CE1}_{62256} = A_{15}
  $$

- **PSEN** → $\overline{OE}$ of 27256.
- **RD** → $\overline{OE}$ of 62256.
- **WR** → $\overline{WE}$ of 62256.

**Step 4 — Verify ROM range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM Range: } & A_{15} = 0 \\
\text{Min: } & 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_b = 0000\text{H} \\
\text{Max: } & 0111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_b = 7\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Size: } & 8000\text{H} = 32\ 768 = 32\text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify RAM range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAM Range: } & A_{15} = 1 \\
\text{Min: } & 1000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_b = 8000\text{H} \\
\text{Max: } & 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111_b = \text{FFFFH} \\
\text{Size: } & 8000\text{H} = 32\text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **simplest possible full-64KB system** — no decoder IC is required! A15 alone handles the chip select, which is why this example is the most common KTU 14-mark question.

---

### **Example 4: Multi-Chip System Using 74LS138 — 8K EPROM + 8K EPROM + 16K RAM**

**Given:**
- Two 8 KB EPROMs (2764): 2764-A at 0000H, 2764-B at 2000H
- One 16 KB RAM (62128): at 4000H

**Step 1 — Identify 74LS138 outputs needed.**

| Output | A15 A14 A13 | Address Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\overline{Y_0}$ | 0 0 0 | 0000H – 1FFFH (8 KB) → 2764-A |
| $\overline{Y_1}$ | 0 0 1 | 2000H – 3FFFH (8 KB) → 2764-B |
| $\overline{Y_0} + \overline{Y_1}$ | — | 0000H – 3FFFH (16 KB) |
| $\overline{Y_2} + \overline{Y_3}$ | — | 4000H – 7FFFH (16 KB) → 62128 |

**Step 2 — Generate combined CS for 16 KB RAM.**

$$
\overline{CE}_{62128} = \overline{Y_2} + \overline{Y_3} = \overline{Y_2 \cdot Y_3}
$$

(Use a 2-input OR gate feeding the active-LOW chip enable of 62128.)

**Step 3 — Address line allocation for 62128.**

62128 is 16 K × 8, so it has **14 address lines** ($A_0$ to $A_{13}$). Since the decoder handles $A_{15..13}$ for chip selection, the within-chip addressing uses A0–A13 — yielding a 16 KB segment. 

**Step 4 — Final wiring table.**

| IC | $\overline{CE}$ source | $\overline{OE}$ source | $\overline{WE}$ source | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2764-A | $\overline{Y_0}$ | PSEN | — | 0000H–1FFFH |
| 2764-B | $\overline{Y_1}$ | PSEN | — | 2000H–3FFFH |
| 62128 | $\overline{Y_2}+\overline{Y_3}$ | RD | WR | 4000H–7FFFH |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of 8051 External Memory Interfacing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8051 CPU CORE"]
    P0["PORT 0 - AD0 to AD7 Multiplexed Bus"]
    P2["PORT 2 - A8 to A15 High Address Bus"]
    ALE["ALE PIN 30"]
    PSEN["PSEN PIN 29"]
    RDWR["P3.6 WR and P3.7 RD"]
    LATCH["74LS373 OCTAL LATCH"]
    DECODER["74LS138 3 to 8 DECODER"]
    ROM["EXTERNAL CODE MEMORY EPROM 27256 32K x 8"]
    RAM["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY SRAM 62256 32K x 8"]

    CPU --> P0
    CPU --> P2
    CPU --> ALE
    CPU --> PSEN
    CPU --> RDWR

    P0 --> LATCH
    ALE --> LATCH
    LATCH -->|Latched A0 to A7| ROM
    LATCH -->|Latched A0 to A7| RAM
    P2 -->|A8 to A14| ROM
    P2 -->|A8 to A14| RAM
    P2 -->|A15| DECODER

    DECODER -->|Y0 chip enable| ROM
    DECODER -->|Y1 chip enable| RAM

    PSEN -->|Output Enable| ROM
    RDWR -->|Read Write strobes| RAM
```

### 4.2 Address Decoding Logic with 74LS138 — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A15["A15 FROM PORT 2"]
    A14["A14 FROM PORT 2"]
    A13["A13 FROM PORT 2"]
    G1["G1 ENABLE tied to VCC"]
    G2A["G2A ENABLE tied to GND"]
    G2B["G2B ENABLE tied to GND"]
    DEC["74LS138 3 to 8 LINE DECODER"]
    Y0["Y0 active for 0000H to 1FFFH"]
    Y1["Y1 active for 2000H to 3FFFH"]
    Y2["Y2 active for 4000H to 5FFFH"]
    Y3["Y3 active for 6000H to 7FFFH"]
    Y4["Y4 active for 8000H to 9FFFH"]
    Y5["Y5 active for A000H to BFFFH"]
    Y6["Y6 active for C000H to DFFFH"]
    Y7["Y7 active for E000H to FFFFH"]

    A15 --> DEC
    A14 --> DEC
    A13 --> DEC
    G1 --> DEC
    G2A --> DEC
    G2B --> DEC

    DEC --> Y0
    DEC --> Y1
    DEC --> Y2
    DEC --> Y3
    DEC --> Y4
    DEC --> Y5
    DEC --> Y6
    DEC --> Y7
```

### 4.3 Chip Select Decision Flowchart — How to Choose CS Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["START Identify total memory size required"]
    S1{"Size less than or equal to 32 KB?"}
    S2{"Multiple chips of different sizes?"}
    S3["Use single line A15 for CS with optional inverter"]
    S4["Use 74LS138 with A15 A14 A13 as inputs"]
    S5["Combine decoder outputs with OR gate for larger blocks"]
    END["OUTPUT Pin assignments and address map"]

    START --> S1
    S1 -- Yes --> S2
    S1 -- No --> S4
    S2 -- Single chip --> S3
    S2 -- Multiple chips --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S3 --> END
    S5 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A15`, `DEC`, `LATCH`), and all double-quoted labels contain only raw uppercase text — no bold/italic markup, no HTML tables, no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) — to satisfy Mermaid v10 parser constraints.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the 8051 called a Modified Harvard architecture, and how does it affect memory interfacing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 is called a **Modified Harvard architecture** because it has **physically separate address and data buses for code memory and data memory**, unlike a pure Von Neumann architecture which uses a single bus. This means **code memory and data memory can be accessed in the same machine cycle** (fetch next instruction while reading data), improving throughput.

For memory interfacing, this architecture requires **two distinct control signals**: **PSEN** for code memory reads and **RD/WR** for data memory reads/writes. The PSEN signal is connected only to the OE pin of the ROM/EPROM, while RD and WR are connected to the OE and WE pins of the RAM. This separation prevents accidental writes to program memory and simplifies hardware design.
*(1 mark – Modified Harvard definition; 1 mark – separate buses; 1 mark – PSEN vs RD/WR distinction)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the role of the 74LS373 latch in 8051 external memory interfacing. What happens if it is removed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 74LS373 is an **8-bit D-type transparent latch** used to **demultiplex the Port 0 lines (AD0–AD7)**. The 8051 transmits the low-byte address (A0–A7) and data (D0–D7) sequentially on the same 8 pins. The **ALE (Address Latch Enable) signal** controls the 74LS373:
- When **ALE = HIGH**, the latch is transparent and outputs the address on AD0–AD7.
- When **ALE = LOW**, the latch *freezes* the address at its output, freeing P0 for actual data transfer.

If the 74LS373 is removed, the low-byte address would disappear as soon as P0 switches to data mode, causing the memory IC to receive only A8–A15 (the high byte) plus garbage on the low byte — **resulting in incorrect memory access and total system failure**.
*(1 mark – demultiplexing; 1 mark – ALE role; 1 mark – consequence of removal)*

---

### **Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Interface a 16 KB EPROM (27128) and an 8 KB RAM (6264) to the 8051, with the EPROM mapped at 0000H–3FFFH and the RAM at 4000H–5FFFH. Draw the complete interfacing circuit and explain the address decoding logic.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Address Decoding Logic (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the address pin requirement of each IC.**

For 27128 (16 K × 8):

$$
16 \text{ K} = 2^{14} \implies 14 \text{ address lines } (A_0 \text{ to } A_{13})
$$

For 6264 (8 K × 8):

$$
8 \text{ K} = 2^{13} \implies 13 \text{ address lines } (A_0 \text{ to } A_{12})
$$

**Step 2: Determine the decoder output for each device.**

For EPROM at 0000H–3FFFH (16 KB):
- Minimum: 0000 0000 0000 0000 → A15=0, A14=0, A13=0 → $\overline{Y_0}$
- Maximum: 0011 1111 1111 1111 → A15=0, A14=0, A13=1 → $\overline{Y_1}$

So the EPROM's $\overline{CE}$ is the **OR of $\overline{Y_0}$ and $\overline{Y_1}$**:

$$
\overline{CE}_{27128} = \overline{Y_0} + \overline{Y_1}
$$

For RAM at 4000H–5FFFH (8 KB):
- Minimum: 0100 0000 0000 0000 → A15=0, A14=1, A13=0 → $\overline{Y_2}$

So:

$$
\overline{CE1}_{6264} = \overline{Y_2}
$$

**Step 3: Address range verification.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPROM range: } & 0000\text{H} \text{ to } 3\text{FFFH} \quad (16 \text{ KB}) \\
\text{RAM range: } & 4000\text{H} \text{ to } 5\text{FFFH} \quad (8 \text{ KB}) \\
\text{Total covered: } & 0000\text{H} \text{ to } 5\text{FFFH} \quad (24 \text{ KB used, 40 KB unused}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Wiring and Control Signal Connections (7 marks)**

| Signal | Connection |
|---|---|
| A0–A7 (latched from P0 via 74LS373) | → A0–A7 of both 27128 and 6264 |
| A8–A12 (from P2) | → A8–A12 of 6264; → A8–A12 of 27128 |
| A13 (from P2) | → A13 of 27128; → Input A of 74LS138 |
| A14 (from P2) | → Input B of 74LS138 |
| A15 (from P2) | → Input C of 74LS138 |
| $\overline{Y_0} + \overline{Y_1}$ (via OR gate) | → $\overline{CE}$ of 27128 |
| $\overline{Y_2}$ | → $\overline{CE1}$ of 6264 |
| PSEN | → $\overline{OE}$ of 27128 |
| $\overline{RD}$ (P3.7) | → $\overline{OE}$ of 6264 |
| $\overline{WR}$ (P3.6) | → $\overline{WE}$ of 6264 |
| $\overline{EA}$ | → $V_{CC}$ (use internal ROM for first 4 KB if available) |

**Explanation of why 74LS138 enables 3-line decoding:**
With A15, A14, A13 as the three decoder inputs, the 8051's 64 KB address space is partitioned into 8 contiguous 8 KB blocks. Two consecutive blocks are merged for the 16 KB EPROM, and one block is allocated to the 8 KB RAM, exactly matching the required memory map.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Design an 8051-based system with 32 KB external code memory (27256) and 32 KB external data memory (62256), occupying the full 64 KB address space. Show the complete wiring diagram (textual), derive the chip-select equations, and verify the address range.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of CS Equations and Address Map (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Capacity analysis.**

For both 27256 and 62256:

$$
32 \text{ K} = 2^{15} \implies 15 \text{ address lines each}
$$

Since 32 K + 32 K = 64 K = $2^{16}$, the **entire 16-bit address bus** must be utilized. The split is by **A15**:

| A15 | Device | Range |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 27256 (ROM) | 0000H – 7FFFH |
| 1 | 62256 (RAM) | 8000H – FFFFH |

**Step 2: CS equations.**

Since the EPROM's $\overline{CE}$ is active-LOW and must be asserted when A15 = 0:

$$
\overline{CE}_{27256} = \overline{A_{15}}
$$

(An inverter is needed between A15 and $\overline{CE}$ of 27256.)

The RAM's $\overline{CE1}$ is active-LOW and must be asserted when A15 = 1:

$$
\overline{CE1}_{62256} = A_{15}
$$

(A15 connects directly to $\overline{CE1}$ of 62256.)

**Step 3: Address range verification.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM minimum: } & 0\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_b = 0000\text{H} \\
\text{ROM maximum: } & 0\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111_b = 7\text{FFFH} \\
\text{ROM size: } & 7\text{FFFH} - 0000\text{H} + 1 = 8000\text{H} = 32\,768 = 32\text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAM minimum: } & 1\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_b = 8000\text{H} \\
\text{RAM maximum: } & 1\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111_b = \text{FFFFH} \\
\text{RAM size: } & \text{FFFFH} - 8000\text{H} + 1 = 8000\text{H} = 32\text{ KB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Wiring Diagram in Textual Form (7 marks)**

| 8051 Pin | Connected To |
|---|---|
| P0.0–P0.7 (AD0–AD7) | → 74LS373 inputs (D0–D7) |
| 74LS373 outputs (Q0–Q7) | → A0–A7 of 27256 and 62256 |
| ALE (Pin 30) | → G (enable) of 74LS373 |
| P2.0–P2.6 (A8–A14) | → A8–A14 of 27256 and 62256 |
| P2.7 (A15) | → Inverter → $\overline{CE}$ of 27256 |
| P2.7 (A15) | → $\overline{CE1}$ of 62256 (direct) |
| PSEN (Pin 29) | → $\overline{OE}$ of 27256 |
| RD (P3.7) | → $\overline{OE}$ of 62256 |
| WR (P3.6) | → $\overline{WE}$ of 62256 |
| $\overline{EA}$ (Pin 31) | → GND (all code from external) |
| VCC, GND, XTAL1, XTAL2, RST | → Standard 8051 support circuit |

**Key Design Notes:**
1. **No 74LS138 decoder is required** in this configuration because A15 alone provides a unique 1-bit chip-select for each 32 KB block.
2. **PSEN activates only the ROM**, never the RAM, preserving Harvard separation.
3. **MOVX instructions** (e.g., `MOVX A, @DPTR`) automatically assert RD/WR, accessing only the RAM.
4. The system uses **no internal ROM**, making the design fully external — suitable for prototyping and development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting 74LS373:** Students often draw P0 directly to memory, losing 2–3 marks immediately. Always include the latch.
> 2. **Confusing PSEN and RD:** Wiring PSEN to RAM's OE causes the program counter to read from RAM — system hangs on first instruction.
> 3. **Incorrect address range calculation:** A common error is $7FFFH - 0000H = 7FFF$ (forgetting to add 1). Correct formula: $Max - Min + 1$.
> 4. **Mixing decoder output polarities:** 74LS138 outputs are *active LOW*. Forgetting to invert logic levels loses CS marks.
> 5. **Wrong chip enable pin:** Many SRAMs (e.g., 62256) have *two* chip enables (CE1 active LOW, CE2 active HIGH). Tie the unused one to its active level.
> 6. **Not stating $\overline{EA}$ connection:** Examiners explicitly look for $\overline{EA}$ = VCC or GND — omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **8051 has separate 16-bit address spaces** for code (64 KB) and external data (64 KB).
- [x] **Modified Harvard** means PSEN controls code reads; RD/WR control data reads/writes.
- [x] **Port 0 is multiplexed** — must be demultiplexed using **74LS373** controlled by **ALE**.
- [x] **Port 2 carries A8–A15** directly (no latch needed for high byte).
- [x] **Memory size formula:** $N = 2^n$ bytes, where $n$ is the number of address pins.
- [x] **Address range formula:** $\text{Range} = [\text{Base}, \ \text{Base} + 2^n - 1]$.
- [x] **74LS138 (3-to-8 decoder):** Splits 64 KB into **eight 8 KB blocks**; each output $\overline{Y_k}$ covers $[k \cdot 2000\text{H}, \ k \cdot 2000\text{H} + 1\text{FFFH}]$.
- [x] **Combinations for larger blocks:** Use OR gate to merge multiple $\overline{Y_k}$ outputs.
- [x] **Simplest 64 KB system:** Use A15 alone for chip select (no decoder required).
- [x] **For 8K ICs** (2764, 6264): decoder uses A15, A14, A13; IC uses A0–A12.
- [x] **For 16K ICs** (27128, 62128): IC uses A0–A13; CS from OR of two decoder outputs.
- [x] **For 32K ICs** (27256, 62256): IC uses A0–A14; CS from A15 directly (with optional inverter).
- [x] **Control signal mnemonic:** **PSEN → Read Code; RD → Read Data; WR → Write Data.**
- [x] **$\overline{EA}$ pin:** LOW = all code external; HIGH = use internal ROM up to 4 KB, then external.
- [x] **KTU-favorite 14-mark question:** Interfacing 32K ROM + 32K RAM (full 64 KB system) — memorize the wiring table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing LCD to 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfacing LCD to 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LCD Interfacing (Liquid Crystal Display):** In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller**, LCD interfacing refers to the systematic hardware and software methodology of connecting a standard alphanumeric LCD module (most commonly the **HD44780-based 16×2 LCD**) to the 8051's I/O ports, allowing the microcontroller to display characters, symbols, and custom messages by sending specific command codes and ASCII data through parallel data lines synchronized with control signals.

The 8051 is an **8-bit** microcontroller, which makes it naturally compatible with LCD modules that also have an **8-bit data interface** (and a compatible 4-bit mode). LCDs are preferred over 7-segment displays in embedded systems because they can display **ASCII characters**, **numbers**, and **custom symbols** simultaneously, consume less power, and are programmable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Blackboard and the Marker"**
> Imagine the **8051 microcontroller** is a teacher standing in front of a classroom. The students (LCD) cannot hear the teacher directly because they don't share a common language. The teacher needs:
> - A **blackboard** (the LCD screen) where information can be written.
> - **Three gestures** to control the board: "Tell me what to write" (RS pin), "Are you ready to listen?" (E - Enable pin), and "Is this a read or write operation?" (RW pin).
> - **8 wires** (Data lines D0–D7) to carry the actual character or instruction, much like how we use a marker to write letters.
> - A **governor** (the initialization sequence) that ensures both the teacher and the board are ready before the lesson begins.

So when the 8051 wants to display an `'A'` on the LCD:
1. It places the ASCII value of `'A'` (which is **0x41** or **65 decimal**) on the data lines.
2. It sets **RS = 1** (meaning "this is data, not a command").
3. It pulses the **Enable (E) pin HIGH then LOW** — this is like saying "Read it now!"
4. The LCD's internal controller latches the data and displays the character.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Standard Operating Voltage:** $V_{DD} = +5V$ (matches 8051 logic levels).
> - **LCD Contrast Pin Voltage:** $V_{EE}$ typically between **0V and 0.7V** for normal visibility (adjusted via a **10 kΩ potentiometer**).
> - **Character Resolution of 16×2 LCD:** 16 characters per row × 2 rows = **32 characters total**.
> - **Character Generator ROM (CGROM):** Stores **192 predefined 5×8 dot-character patterns**.
> - **Character Generator RAM (CGRAM):** Allows up to **8 user-defined custom 5×8 characters** at locations **0x00 to 0x07**.
> - **Display Data RAM (DDRAM):** 80 bytes of memory that maps to visible and offscreen character positions.
> - **Standard Clock Requirement for 8051:** $11.0592 \, \text{MHz}$ crystal — chosen because it yields exact baud rate divisions (e.g., **9600 bps for UART**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 LCD Pin Architecture (HD44780-based 16×2 Module)

The 16×2 LCD has **16 pins**. Understanding each pin is fundamental for KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin-wise Breakdown (Highly Exam-Relevant):**

| Pin # | Symbol | Name | Function |
|-------|--------|------|----------|
| 1 | VSS | Ground | **0V** power supply ground. |
| 2 | VDD | Power Supply | **+5V DC** supply input. |
| 3 | VEE | Contrast | Controls character darkness via potentiometer. |
| 4 | RS | Register Select | **RS = 0** → Command Register; **RS = 1** → Data Register. |
| 5 | RW | Read/Write | **RW = 0** → Write to LCD; **RW = 1** → Read from LCD. |
| 6 | E | Enable | Data is latched on **HIGH-to-LOW** transition. |
| 7–14 | D0–D7 | Data Lines | 8-bit parallel data bus for command/data transfer. |
| 15 | A (LED+) | Backlight Anode | **+5V** through current-limiting resistor (typically **220 Ω**). |
| 16 | K (LED−) | Backlight Cathode | **0V (Ground)**. |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Three Control Signals

The 8051 communicates with the LCD using a strict protocol involving three control lines. Their combined state at the moment the Enable pin is pulsed determines what happens:

> [!NOTE]
> **RS, RW, E Truth Table:**

| RS | RW | E (Pulse) | Operation |
|----|----|-----------|-----------|
| 0 | 0 | HIGH→LOW | Write **Command** to LCD instruction register. |
| 0 | 1 | HIGH→LOW | Read **Busy Flag (BF)** + address counter from LCD. |
| 1 | 0 | HIGH→LOW | Write **Data (ASCII character)** to DDRAM. |
| 1 | 1 | HIGH→LOW | Read **DDRAM content** from LCD. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**R**egister-**R**ead-**E**nable" — Think of RS as **"I am Sending what kind of thing?"** and RW as **"Are you receiving or am I receiving?"**

### 2.3 LCD Command Set (CRT — KTU's Most Frequently Asked)

The LCD's internal HD44780 controller recognizes specific command bytes. KTU exams routinely test these.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Command Codes (Memorize for KTU):**

| Command | Hex Code | Decimal | Function |
|---------|----------|---------|----------|
| Clear Display | `0x01` | 1 | Clears entire display, returns cursor to home position `(0,0)`. |
| Return Home | `0x02` | 2 | Returns cursor to home; DDRAM content remains. |
| Entry Mode Set | `0x06` | 6 | Auto-increment cursor, no display shift. |
| Display OFF | `0x08` | 8 | Display, cursor, and blink all turned OFF. |
| Display ON, Cursor OFF | `0x0C` | 12 | Display ON, no cursor. (Most common in applications.) |
| Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink OFF | `0x0E` | 14 | Steady cursor, no blinking. |
| Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink ON | `0x0F` | 15 | Cursor blinks. |
| Shift Cursor Left | `0x10` | 16 | Moves cursor one position to the left. |
| Shift Cursor Right | `0x14` | 20 | Moves cursor one position to the right. |
| Shift Display Left | `0x18` | 24 | Shifts entire display content left. |
| Shift Display Right | `0x1C` | 28 | Shifts entire display content right. |
| **Function Set (8-bit, 2-line, 5×8)** | **`0x38`** | **56** | **Most critical — initializes 8-bit mode.** |
| Function Set (4-bit, 2-line, 5×8) | `0x28` | 40 | For 4-bit interface mode. |
| Set DDRAM Address | `0x80 + addr` | 128+addr | Sets cursor to DDRAM address (e.g., `0x80` = row 0, col 0; `0xC0` = row 1, col 0). |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Cheat Sheet — Timing & Timing-Derived Equations:**

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|-----------|-----------------|------|---------------------|
| Enable Pulse Width ($t_{PW}$) | $t_{PW} \geq 450$ | ns | Minimum HIGH time of E pin. |
| Data Setup Time ($t_{DS}$) | $t_{DS} \geq 195$ | ns | Time data must be stable before E falls. |
| Data Hold Time ($t_{DH}$) | $t_{DH} \geq 10$ | ns | Time data must remain stable after E falls. |
| Command Execution Time | $\approx 1.64$ | ms | Max time for clear/home commands. |
| Typical Character Write Time | $\approx 40$ | µs | For most commands after BF check. |
| 8051 Machine Cycle | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | µs | For 11.0592 MHz crystal: $T_{MC} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$. |
| Instruction Cycle Count | 1 to 4 | Machine Cycles | Depends on instruction (e.g., `MOV` = 1 cycle, `MUL AB` = 4 cycles). |
| Delay Loop Calculation | $D = \dfrac{T_{required}}{T_{MC} \times N_{cycles}}$ | Iterations | Where $N_{cycles}$ = machine cycles per loop iteration. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LCD interfacing is foundational in:
- **Industrial Control Systems:** Displaying process variables (temperature, pressure, flow rate).
- **Medical Devices:** Patient monitoring displays in glucometers, BP monitors, ventilators.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Microwave ovens, washing machines, calculators, printers.
- **Automotive Dashboards:** Odometer displays, fuel indicators, infotainment.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Local status readouts alongside Wi-Fi/BLE modules.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **DD RAM address** with **visible position**. The 16×2 LCD's DDRAM has **80 bytes**, but only **32 are visible**. Row 0 starts at DDRAM address `0x00` (visible) but Row 1 starts at DDRAM address `0x40` (visible). However, you must set the **command as `0x80 + address`**, not the address itself!

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 LCD Initialization Sequence — The Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Initialization Mandatory?**
> When the LCD module is first powered on, its internal state is **undefined**. The HD44780 controller has internal reset circuitry, but the 8051 must still send a strict sequence of commands to configure it for **8-bit mode, 2-line display, and 5×8 character font**. This is what makes the LCD "wake up correctly."

The standard KTU-prescribed initialization sequence is:

1. Wait **≥ 15 ms** after $V_{DD}$ rises to **4.5V** (power-on stabilization).
2. Send command `0x30` (Function Set hint, in 8-bit mode).
3. Wait **≥ 4.1 ms**.
4. Send command `0x30` again.
5. Wait **≥ 100 µs**.
6. Send command `0x30` one more time.
7. Finally, send command `0x38` — this is the actual Function Set: **8-bit, 2 lines, 5×8 dots**.
8. Send `0x0C` — Display ON, Cursor OFF.
9. Send `0x01` — Clear Display.
10. Send `0x06` — Entry Mode Set (auto-increment).

### 3.2 Detailed Working — Step-by-Step Command Transfer (Write Cycle)

Let's trace how a single command byte `0x38` is written to the LCD:

**Step 1:** Place command byte on data port.

The 8051 places `0x38` on Port 1 (assuming P1 = data port). In binary:
$$0x38 = 0011\,1000_2$$

This is the value we want the LCD to interpret as a command.

**Step 2:** Set control signals.

$$\text{RS} = 0 \quad (\text{This is a COMMAND, not data})$$
$$\text{RW} = 0 \quad (\text{We are WRITING to the LCD})$$

**Step 3:** Generate Enable pulse.

$$E = 1 \quad \text{(Hold for at least } t_{PW} = 450\, ns\text{)}$$
$$E = 0 \quad \text{(Falling edge latches the data into the LCD)}$$

**Step 4:** Wait for command execution.

The LCD's internal controller needs **time to process** the command. We must wait before sending the next command, or we can poll the **Busy Flag (BF)** by setting RW = 1 and reading the MSB of the data port.

### 3.3 Derivation: Time Delay Calculation for Software Loops

A common KTU question asks: *"Generate a delay of 4.1 ms using 8051 assembly with an 11.0592 MHz crystal."*

**Given:**
- Crystal frequency $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \, \text{MHz}$
- Required delay $T_{req} = 4.1 \, \text{ms} = 4100 \, \mu s$

**Step 1: Calculate Machine Cycle Period.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Determine Total Machine Cycles Required.**

$$N_{total} = \frac{T_{req}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{4100 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s}$$

$$N_{total} \approx 3778 \, \text{machine cycles}$$

**Step 3: Design a Nested Loop.**

Inner loop (255 iterations, each 1 machine cycle for `DJNZ`):

$$T_{inner} = 255 \times 1 \times T_{MC} = 255 \times 1.085 = 276.7 \, \mu s$$

Outer loop iterations needed:

$$N_{outer} = \frac{N_{total}}{255} = \frac{3778}{255} \approx 14.82$$

Rounded to **15 outer iterations**. The actual delay with 15 iterations:

$$T_{actual} = 15 \times 255 \times T_{MC} = 3825 \times 1.085 = 4150.125 \, \mu s$$

$$T_{actual} \approx 4.15 \, \text{ms} \geq 4.1 \, \text{ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Assembly Code Equivalent.**

```assembly
DELAY_4MS:  MOV R1, #0FFH    ; Inner counter = 255
LOOP1:      MOV R2, #0FH     ; Outer counter = 15
LOOP2:      DJNZ R1, LOOP2   ; Inner loop: 255 × 1 cycle
            DJNZ R2, LOOP1   ; Outer loop: 15 × 255 cycles
            RET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The exact value `11.0592 MHz` is preferred (over the more common `12 MHz`) because `11.0592 / 12 = 0.9216`, and this yields **integer baud rate divisors** for serial communication. KTU may ask you to justify the choice of crystal.

### 3.4 Derivation: Cursor Positioning Address Calculation

To position the cursor at **Row 1, Column 5** of a 16×2 LCD:

**Step 1:** Identify the DDRAM base address for Row 1.

For 16×2 LCD: Row 0 starts at `0x00`, Row 1 starts at `0x40`.

**Step 2:** Add the column offset.

$$\text{DDRAM Address} = 0x40 + 5 = 0x45$$

**Step 3:** Form the Set DDRAM Address command.

$$\text{Command Byte} = 0x80 \lor 0x45 = 0xC5$$

$$\boxed{\text{Command Byte} = 0xC5 \text{ positions cursor at Row 1, Column 5}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Interfacing Strategies

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Analytical Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | 8-bit Mode | 4-bit Mode |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Data Lines Used | 8 (D0–D7) | 4 (D4–D7) |
| 8051 Port Pins Saved | 0 | 4 (free for other I/O) |
| Data Transfer Speed | Fast (single transfer) | Slower (2 nibbles per byte) |
| Wiring Complexity | High | Low |
| Initialization Complexity | Simple | Requires 4-bit-specific init |
| Typical Use Case | 8051 with abundant I/O | 8051 projects with pin constraints |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hardware Interfacing Block Diagram (8051 ↔ 16×2 LCD)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcuA["8051 Port1 P10 to P17"] -- "8 Bit Data Bus D0 to D7" --> lcdA["LCD Data Pins D0 to D7"]
    mcuB["8051 Pin P3 0"] -- "RS Control Line" --> lcdB["LCD Pin 4 RS"]
    mcuC["8051 Pin P3 1"] -- "RW Control Line" --> lcdC["LCD Pin 5 RW"]
    mcuD["8051 Pin P3 2"] -- "Enable Pulse E" --> lcdD["LCD Pin 6 E"]
    vcc["Plus 5V Supply"] -- "VDD" --> lcdE["LCD Pin 2 VDD"]
    gnd["Ground 0V"] -- "VSS" --> lcdF["LCD Pin 1 VSS"]
    pot["10K Potentiometer"] -- "VEE Contrast" --> lcdG["LCD Pin 3 VEE"]
    blA["Plus 5V via 220 Ohm"] -- "Anode A" --> lcdH["LCD Pin 15 A Backlight"]
    blK["Ground 0V"] -- "Cathode K" --> lcdI["LCD Pin 16 K Backlight"]
```

### 4.2 Software Data Flow — Sending a Command to LCD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start LCD Write Command Function"] --> stepA1["Place Command Byte on Port 1 P1"]
    stepA1 --> stepA2["Set RS Pin to LOW for Command Mode"]
    stepA2 --> stepA3["Set RW Pin to LOW for Write Operation"]
    stepA3 --> stepA4["Set Enable Pin E to HIGH"]
    stepA4 --> stepA5["Wait for Enable Pulse Width minimum 450 ns"]
    stepA5 --> stepA6["Set Enable Pin E to LOW Falling Edge Latches Data"]
    stepA6 --> stepA7["Call Software Delay Function Approx 1 to 2 ms"]
    stepA7 --> endA["Return From Function Ready for Next Operation"]
```

### 4.3 Software Data Flow — Sending a Data Byte (Character) to LCD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB["Start LCD Write Data Function"] --> stepB1["Place ASCII Data Byte on Port 1 P1"]
    stepB1 --> stepB2["Set RS Pin to HIGH for Data Mode"]
    stepB2 --> stepB3["Set RW Pin to LOW for Write Operation"]
    stepB3 --> stepB4["Set Enable Pin E to HIGH"]
    stepB4 --> stepB5["Hold for at least 450 ns then Set E to LOW"]
    stepB5 --> stepB6["Call Short Delay Function Approx 40 microseconds"]
    stepB6 --> endB["Return Character Now Displayed on LCD"]
```

### 4.4 LCD Internal Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userDef["User Defined 5x8 Patterns"] --> cgramBlock["CGRAM 64 Bytes 8 Custom Characters"]
    predefined["192 Predefined ASCII Patterns"] --> cgromBlock["CGROM Read Only Memory"]
    ddramBlock["DDRAM 80 Bytes 32 Visible"] --> displayScreen["16x2 LCD Screen Display"]
    cgramBlock --> ddramBlock
    cgromBlock --> ddramBlock
    controlLogic["HD44780 Control Logic Unit"] --> cgramBlock
    controlLogic --> cgromBlock
    controlLogic --> ddramBlock
    cursorBlock["Cursor Control Circuit"] --> controlLogic
    addressCounter["Address Counter AC"] --> controlLogic
    busyFlagReg["Busy Flag BF Register"] --> controlLogic
    dataBusExt["External Data Bus D0 to D7"] --> controlLogic
    controlBusExt["External Control Bus RS RW E"] --> controlLogic
```

### 4.5 Initialization Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init1["Power On LCD Module"] --> init2["Wait minimum 15 ms for VDD Stabilization"]
    init2 --> init3["Send Command 0x30 Function Set Hint 8 bit Mode"]
    init3 --> init4["Wait minimum 4.1 ms"]
    init4 --> init5["Send Command 0x30 Second Hint"]
    init5 --> init6["Wait minimum 100 microseconds"]
    init6 --> init7["Send Command 0x30 Third Hint"]
    init7 --> init8["Send Command 0x38 Final Function Set 8 bit 2 Line 5x8"]
    init8 --> init9["Send Command 0x0C Display ON Cursor OFF"]
    init9 --> init10["Send Command 0x01 Clear Display"]
    init10 --> init11["Send Command 0x06 Entry Mode Auto Increment"]
    init11 --> initReady["LCD Ready for User Data Display"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Explain the function of the RS, RW, and E pins of a 16×2 LCD module interfaced with 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> - **RS (Register Select):** This pin selects between the **Instruction Register (IR)** and the **Data Register (DR)**. When **RS = 0**, the byte on the data bus is treated as a **command** (e.g., clear display, cursor position). When **RS = 1**, the byte is treated as **ASCII data** to be displayed on the screen. (1 mark)
> - **RW (Read/Write):** Determines the direction of data flow. **RW = 0** means the 8051 is **writing** to the LCD (sending command or data). **RW = 1** means the 8051 is **reading** from the LCD (typically to check the Busy Flag or DDRAM contents). (1 mark)
> - **E (Enable):** This is the **synchronization signal**. The LCD latches the data present on D0–D7 into its internal register on the **HIGH-to-LOW falling edge** of the Enable pulse. The HIGH state must be held for at least **450 ns** to be valid. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**List any six command codes of the HD44780 LCD controller with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 6 commands × 0.5 mark each):**

| Hex Code | Function |
|----------|----------|
| `0x01` | Clear entire display and return cursor to home. |
| `0x02` | Return cursor to home position (DDRAM unchanged). |
| `0x06` | Entry mode set — auto-increment cursor right after each character. |
| `0x0C` | Display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF. |
| `0x38` | Function set — 8-bit interface, 2 display lines, 5×8 dot character font. |
| `0x80` | Set DDRAM address to position `(0,0)` (start of first line). |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style**

**[A] Draw the interfacing circuit diagram to connect a 16×2 LCD to the 8051 microcontroller using Port 1 as the data port and Port 3 pins P3.0, P3.1, P3.2 for RS, RW, and E respectively. Write an 8051 C program to display the string "KTU 2024" on the LCD.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Hardware Interfacing Diagram (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcuP1["8051 Port 1 P1 0 to P1 7"] -- "8 Bit Data Bus" --> lcdData["LCD D0 to D7"]
    mcuP30["8051 P3 0"] -- "RS" --> lcdRS["LCD Pin 4 RS"]
    mcuP31["8051 P3 1"] -- "RW" --> lcdRW["LCD Pin 5 RW"]
    mcuP32["8051 P3 2"] -- "E" --> lcdE["LCD Pin 6 E"]
    vddL["Plus 5V"] --> lcdVdd["LCD VDD Pin 2"]
    gndL["Ground"] --> lcdVss["LCD VSS Pin 1"]
    potL["10K POT"] --> lcdVee["LCD VEE Pin 3"]
```

**Pin Connections Summary (to be drawn in exam):**

| 8051 Pin | LCD Pin | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| P1.0–P1.7 | D0–D7 | 8-bit data bus |
| P3.0 | RS (Pin 4) | Register Select |
| P3.1 | RW (Pin 5) | Read/Write |
| P3.2 | E (Pin 6) | Enable |
| +5V | VDD, A (with 220Ω) | Power, Backlight |
| GND | VSS, K | Ground, Backlight |
| POT | VEE | Contrast control |

**(b) 8051 C Program (7 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Pin Definitions */
sbit RS = P3^0;
sbit RW = P3^1;
sbit EN = P3^2;

/* Function Prototypes */
void LCD_Init(void);
void LCD_Command(unsigned char);
void LCD_Data(unsigned char);
void LCD_String(unsigned char *);
void delay(unsigned int);

void main(void)
{
    LCD_Init();                      /* Initialize the LCD */
    LCD_Command(0x80);               /* Force cursor to Row 0, Col 0 */
    LCD_String("KTU 2024");          /* Display the string */
    while(1);                        /* Infinite loop - hold display */
}

void LCD_Init(void)
{
    delay(15);                       /* Wait 15 ms after power-on */
    LCD_Command(0x30);               /* Function set hint */
    delay(5);                        /* Wait 4.1 ms */
    LCD_Command(0x30);               /* Function set hint */
    delay(1);                        /* Wait 100 us */
    LCD_Command(0x30);               /* Function set hint */
    LCD_Command(0x38);               /* 8-bit, 2-line, 5x8 font */
    LCD_Command(0x0C);               /* Display ON, cursor OFF */
    LCD_Command(0x01);               /* Clear display */
    LCD_Command(0x06);               /* Auto-increment cursor */
}

void LCD_Command(unsigned char cmd)
{
    P1 = cmd;                        /* Place command on data port */
    RS = 0;                          /* Select command register */
    RW = 0;                          /* Write mode */
    EN = 1;                          /* High to low pulse */
    delay(1);
    EN = 0;
    delay(2);
}

void LCD_Data(unsigned char dat)
{
    P1 = dat;                        /* Place data on data port */
    RS = 1;                          /* Select data register */
    RW = 0;                          /* Write mode */
    EN = 1;
    delay(1);
    EN = 0;
    delay(2);
}

void LCD_String(unsigned char *str)
{
    while(*str)
    {
        LCD_Data(*str++);
    }
}

void delay(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}
```

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style**

**[B] Explain the LCD initialization sequence for an 8051-16×2 LCD interface in 8-bit mode with proper timing diagrams. Also explain the function of the Busy Flag and how it is used for polling.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Initialization Sequence with Explanation (7 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The initialization is divided into two phases:**

**Phase 1: Power-On Reset Sequence (3 steps)**

The HD44780 controller has an internal reset, but the 8051 must still drive the LCD through this mandatory sequence:

| Step | Action | Wait Time | Reason |
|------|--------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Power on $V_{DD}$ to reach **≥ 4.5V** | **≥ 15 ms** | Internal oscillator stabilization. |
| 2 | Send `0x30` (Function Set hint) | **≥ 4.1 ms** | Ensures LCD enters 8-bit mode. |
| 3 | Send `0x30` again | **≥ 100 µs** | Confirms 8-bit mode before final config. |

**Phase 2: Function Configuration (4 commands)**

$$\text{Command 1: } 0x38 \rightarrow \text{8-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font}$$

$$\text{Command 2: } 0x0C \rightarrow \text{Display ON, Cursor OFF, Blink OFF}$$

$$\text{Command 3: } 0x01 \rightarrow \text{Clear Display, Cursor Home}$$

$$\text{Command 4: } 0x06 \rightarrow \text{Entry Mode: Auto-increment cursor}$$

**Timing Diagram (Textual Representation for KTU):**

```
       ___________                    _________
RS ___|           |__________________|       |____
                ___                    ___
RW ____________|   |__________________|   |____
            _________                _________
D0-D7 _____X_ 0x30 _X______________X_ 0x38 _X___
                  ____                  ____
E ______________|    |________________|    |____
                 ↑                        ↑
            HIGH→LOW               HIGH→LOW
            (Latches 0x30)         (Latches 0x38)
```

**(b) Busy Flag (BF) — Concept and Polling (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **What is the Busy Flag?**
> The Busy Flag is the **most significant bit (D7)** of the data bus when reading from the LCD. **BF = 1** means the LCD is **internally busy** executing the last command and **cannot accept new data**. **BF = 0** means the LCD is **ready** for the next byte.

**How to Poll the Busy Flag:**

1. Set **RS = 0** (we are querying the command register).
2. Set **RW = 1** (we are reading from the LCD).
3. Pulse the **E pin HIGH then LOW** to latch the read.
4. Read the data port. The **MSB (D7)** is the BF.
5. If **BF = 1**, loop back to step 3. If **BF = 0**, proceed with the next command.

**C Code for BF Polling:**

```c
void LCD_Ready(void)
{
    unsigned char status;
    P1 = 0xFF;            /* Make P1 input port */
    RS = 0;               /* Command register */
    RW = 1;               /* Read mode */
    do {
        EN = 1;
        status = P1;
        EN = 0;
    } while (status & 0x80);  /* Loop while BF = 1 (D7 = 1) */
    RW = 0;               /* Return to write mode for next op */
}
```

**Advantages of BF Polling over Software Delays:**

| Polling (BF) | Software Delay |
|--------------|----------------|
| **Adaptive** — waits only as long as needed | **Fixed** — always waits maximum time |
| **Faster LCD response** | **Slower** overall |
| **Slightly complex code** | **Simple** to implement |
| Recommended for **time-critical** apps | Acceptable for **simple** applications |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to set `RW = 0` before writing** — Many students only toggle RS and E, forgetting RW. Result: LCD displays nothing or behaves erratically. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 2. **Wrong DDRAM address for Row 1** — Students often use `0x20` instead of `0x40` + offset for Row 1 of a 16×2 LCD. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Sending character data without first setting `RS = 1`** — The LCD will interpret the character as a command, often producing bizarre symbols or clearing the display. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **No initialization sequence** — Even a "correct" `LCD_String()` call will not display anything if the LCD is not initialized with `0x38`, `0x0C`, `0x01`, `0x06`. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 
> 5. **Confusing 4-bit and 8-bit initialization** — In 4-bit mode, only `0x28` is the Function Set. Mixing it with `0x38` is a common error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 6. **Not including `reg51.h` or port definitions** — In C programs, forgetting the SFR definitions will result in compilation failure. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **LCD = HD44780 controller**; standard sizes are **16×1, 16×2, 20×2, 20×4**.
- ✅ **Three control pins: RS, RW, E.** Remember: E latches data on **falling edge**.
- ✅ **Crucial Function Set command: `0x38`** (8-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font).
- ✅ **Initialization order matters** — `0x38 → 0x0C → 0x01 → 0x06`.
- ✅ **DDRAM address mapping:** Row 0 starts at `0x00`, Row 1 starts at `0x40`. Always OR with `0x80` to form the position command.
- ✅ **ASCII code for '0' is 0x30, 'A' is 0x41, 'a' is 0x61** — KTU may ask to display a number and expect ASCII conversion.
- ✅ **VEE pin is the contrast pin** — driven by a 10 kΩ potentiometer.
- ✅ **Backlight pins:** A (Anode, +5V via 220Ω) and K (Cathode, GND).
- ✅ **Machine cycle = 12 / crystal frequency.** For 11.0592 MHz, $T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s$.
- ✅ **Enable pulse width ≥ 450 ns**; command execution time ~1.64 ms; data write time ~40 µs.
- ✅ **Busy Flag (BF) is D7** when reading; polling BF is faster than fixed delays.
- ✅ **8-bit mode is faster but uses 8 I/O pins;** 4-bit mode saves pins but sends data in 2 nibbles.
- ✅ **8051 C programs** require `#include <reg51.h>` and `sbit` definitions for control pins.
- ✅ **Always provide a 2 ms delay after Clear Display (`0x01`)** — this command takes longer than others.

---

**End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Interfacing LCD to 8051**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (PBICT504) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*
