# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the salient features of the 8051 microcontroller. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The salient features of the 8051 microcontroller are:

1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU, accumulator, B register, stack pointer (SP), data pointer (DPTR), and program counter (PC).
2. **On-chip memory:** 4 KB of ROM (program memory) and 128 bytes of RAM (data memory).
3. **Four 8-bit I/O ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3 — providing 32 I/O lines.
4. **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0, T1) with four operating modes.
5. **Full-duplex UART** serial port for asynchronous communication.
6. **5 interrupt sources** with 2 programmable priority levels.
7. **16-bit address bus** capable of addressing 64 KB of external code and 64 KB of external data memory.
8. **Harvard architecture** with separate program and data buses.
9. **On-chip oscillator** with external crystal (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz).
10. **Boolean processor** for bit-level operations.
11. **Single +5 V power supply**.
12. **4 register banks** of 8 registers each (R0–R7), selectable via PSW.

> Any **5–6 well-explained features** with numerical values fetch full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures as applicable to 8051. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Harvard (8051) | Von Neumann (8086) |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Memory | Separate code (ROM) and data (RAM) | Single unified memory for both |
| Buses | Separate address + data bus for code and data | Single shared address + data bus |
| Simultaneous fetch | Can fetch instruction and data at the same time | Cannot — bus contention |
| Speed | Faster execution | Slower due to bus sharing |
| Pin count | Higher (more address/data lines) | Lower |
| Example | 8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M | 8086, 80486, general PCs |

The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** with separate program memory (4 KB internal + 60 KB external) and data memory (128 bytes internal + 64 KB external).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the salient features of the 8051 microcontroller in detail. Discuss the role of the program counter, data pointer, and stack pointer registers. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Salient Features of 8051:**

1. **8-bit CPU with Harvard architecture:** Separate code and data buses allow simultaneous instruction fetch and operand access. The ALU is 8-bit and supports arithmetic, logical, and boolean operations.

2. **Memory organization:**
   - **On-chip ROM:** 4 KB of mask-programmed ROM (or Flash in modern variants) for program storage. Address range 0000H–0FFFH internally.
   - **On-chip RAM:** 128 bytes of data memory (00H–7FH) split into register banks (00H–1FH), bit-addressable area (20H–2FH), and general-purpose RAM (30H–7FH).
   - **External memory:** 16-bit address bus allows up to 64 KB external code memory (0000H–FFFFH) and 64 KB external data memory (also 0000H–FFFFH, but accessed via `MOVX`).

3. **I/O Ports:** Four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) provide 32 I/O lines. P0 lacks internal pull-up; P3 has alternate functions (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

4. **Timers/Counters:** Two 16-bit timers (T0, T1) operate in 4 modes: Mode 0 (13-bit), Mode 1 (16-bit), Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Mode 3 (split timer).

5. **Serial Port:** One full-duplex UART with programmable baud rate controlled by Timer 1.

6. **Interrupts:** 5 sources (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial) with 2 priority levels (high/low) programmed via the IP register.

7. **Power & Clock:** +5 V supply; 12 MHz crystal gives 1 µs machine cycle (since 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods).

**Role of Key Registers:**

- **Program Counter (PC) — 16-bit:** Holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched from code memory. It auto-increments after each fetch, except for branch instructions (LCALL, AJMP, SJMP, CJNE, DJNZ, etc.) which modify it directly. Reset initializes PC to 0000H.

- **Data Pointer (DPTR) — 16-bit (DPH:DPL):** Used to access **external data memory** using the `MOVX` instruction, and to read tables from **code memory** using `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`. It can also serve as a 16-bit general-purpose register. In modern applications, DPTR commonly points to the base address of a lookup table or external peripheral.

- **Stack Pointer (SP) — 8-bit:** Points to the **top of the stack** in internal RAM. The stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). On reset, SP = 07H (pointing to the first free location in register bank 1). Before using the stack, SP should be initialized to a value ≥ 30H (above the bit-addressable area) to avoid conflicts. The stack is used for:
  - Storing return addresses during `LCALL` and `ACALL`.
  - Saving context during interrupts.
  - Local variable storage in C programs.

---

**(b) Draw and explain the internal block diagram of the 8051. List any three Special Function Registers (SFRs) with their addresses. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Internal Block Diagram (Functional Description):**

The 8051 internal architecture consists of the following major blocks:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the 8-bit ALU, Accumulator (A), B register, Program Status Word (PSW), and timing/control logic.

2. **Program Memory (ROM):** 4 KB internal; addressed by the 16-bit Program Counter. Used to store opcodes and lookup tables.

3. **Data Memory (RAM):** 128 bytes internal; includes 4 register banks, bit-addressable area, and general-purpose RAM.

4. **I/O Ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3 — each 8 bits wide, with their associated latches in SFR space.

5. **Timers/Counters:** T0 and T1 with mode control (TMOD) and control (TCON) registers.

6. **Serial Port:** SBUF (data) and SCON (control) registers.

7. **Interrupt Control:** IE (Interrupt Enable) and IP (Interrupt Priority) registers.

8. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit:** Drives the entire chip from an external crystal.

9. **Address/Data Buses:**
   - 16-bit address bus (P0 supplies low byte, P2 supplies high byte).
   - 8-bit bidirectional data bus (P0 multiplexed).
   - Control signals: **ALE** (Address Latch Enable), **PSEN** (Program Store Enable), **EA** (External Access), **RD**, **WR**.

**Block Diagram Representation:**

```
            ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
            │           8051 MICROCONTROLLER      │
            │                                       │
   XTAL1 ───┤  [Oscillator]──[Clock & Timing]    │
   XTAL2 ───┤                  │                    │
            │                  ▼                    │
            │   ┌──────────────────────┐           │
            │   │   CPU (8-bit ALU)    │           │
            │   │ A  B  PSW  SP DPTR  │           │
            │   │          PC          │           │
            │   └──────────────────────┘           │
            │       │     │      │    │             │
            │       ▼     ▼      ▼    ▼             │
            │   [ROM 4K] [RAM 128B] [Ports] [T0/T1]│
            │       │        │       │      │       │
            │       ▼        ▼       ▼      ▼       │
            │   [Address/Data Bus + Control]        │
            │                                       │
   P0.0-P0.7─┤  [Port 0]  AD0-AD7                │
   P1.0-P1.7─┤  [Port 1]                          │
   P2.0-P2.7─┤  [Port 2]  A8-A15                  │
   P3.0-P3.7─┤  [Port 3]  + Alternate Functions   │
            └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Three Important SFRs with Addresses:**

| SFR | Address (Hex) | Function |
|-----|---------------|----------|
| **ACC (Accumulator)** | **E0H** | Main register for all arithmetic and logic operations. Bit-addressable (bits E0H–E7H). |
| **B Register** | **F0H** | Used in multiply/divide instructions; otherwise general-purpose. Bit-addressable. |
| **PSW (Program Status Word)** | **D0H** | Contains carry flag (CY), auxiliary carry (AC), flag0 (F0), register bank select (RS1, RS0), overflow (OV), and parity (P). Bit-addressable. |

> Other key SFRs: SP (81H), DPL (82H), DPH (83H), PCON (87H), TCON (88H), TMOD (89H), TL0 (8AH), TL1 (8BH), TH0 (8CH), TH1 (8DH), P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2 (A0H), P3 (B0H), SCON (98H), SBUF (99H), IE (A8H), IP (B8H).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the memory organization of 8051. Differentiate between internal and external memory. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Memory Organization of 8051:**

The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** with **two separate address spaces**:

**1. Program Memory (Code Memory) — up to 64 KB**

- **Internal:** 4 KB ROM (addresses 0000H–0FFFH) on-chip.
- **External:** Up to 60 KB (addresses 1000H–FFFFH) accessed via PSEN signal.
- If **EA = 1** (internal ROM enabled), the 8051 fetches from internal ROM for 0000H–0FFFH and switches to external ROM for 1000H–FFFFH.
- If **EA = 0** (no internal ROM, e.g., 8031), all 64 KB is external.
- Accessed via `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` or `MOVC A,@A+PC`.

**2. Data Memory — 128 bytes internal + 64 KB external**

- **Internal Data Memory (00H–7FH):**
  - 00H–1FH: 4 register banks (each 8 bytes of R0–R7). Active bank selected by PSW bits RS1, RS0.
  - 20H–2FH: 16 bytes bit-addressable (128 bits, individually accessible).
  - 30H–7FH: 80 bytes general-purpose scratchpad RAM.
  - 80H–FFH: **SFR region** (Special Function Registers — accessible only by direct addressing).
  
- **External Data Memory (0000H–FFFFH):**
  - Accessed via `MOVX @DPTR` (16-bit address) or `MOVX @R0/@R1` (8-bit address, with P2 supplying high byte).
  - Read uses `RD` signal; write uses `WR` signal.

**Memory Map Diagram:**

```
CODE MEMORY (PSEN controlled):
   0000H ───┬──── Internal ROM (4 KB) ────┐
            │                              │
   0FFFH ───┘                              │
   1000H ─── External ROM begins ──────────┴──→ up to FFFFH

DATA MEMORY (RD/WR controlled):
INTERNAL:
   00H  ─── Register Bank 0 (R0–R7)
   08H  ─── Register Bank 1
   10H  ─── Register Bank 2
   18H  ─── Register Bank 3
   20H  ─── Bit Addressable (16 bytes)
   30H  ─── General Purpose RAM (80 bytes)
   7FH  ─── End of internal RAM
   80H  ─── SFR Region
   FFH  ─── End of SFR

EXTERNAL:
   0000H ─── External Data Memory (RAM)
   FFFFH ─── (accessed by MOVX)
```

**Differences — Internal vs External Memory:**

| Aspect | Internal Memory | External Memory |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Location | On-chip | Off-chip (separate IC) |
| Size | 4 KB ROM + 128 B RAM | Up to 64 KB + 64 KB |
| Speed | Faster (no bus wait states) | Slower (bus access time) |
| Control signal | None for data RAM; PSEN for code ROM | RD, WR for data; PSEN for code |
| Volatility | RAM is volatile | External RAM volatile, ROM not |
| Addressing | Direct in instruction | Indirect via DPTR or @R0/@R1 |
| Use case | Frequently accessed, time-critical | Bulk data, large programs |

---

**(b) Explain the I/O port structure of 8051. Compare the internal pull-up arrangement of P0, P1, P2, P3. Why is P0 multiplexed with the address/data bus? (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**I/O Port Structure of 8051:**

Each of the four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) consists of:
- **Latch (SFR):** Stores the value written by the CPU.
- **Output driver:** Push-pull or open-drain transistor pair.
- **Input buffer:** Tri-stated to read the pin state.
- **Pin:** Physical external connection (40-pin DIP package).

When a port is written to (`MOV P1, A`), the value is latched in the SFR. The output driver then drives the corresponding pins. When the CPU reads from a port (`MOV A, P1`), the input buffer samples the actual voltage at the pin (not the latch) — this is the **read-pin** operation. There is also a **read-latch** operation used by instructions like `ANL P1, A` to avoid read-modify-write problems.

**Internal Pull-up Comparison:**

| Port | Internal Pull-up | Pull-up Resistance | Notes |
|------|------------------|--------------------|-------|
| **P0** | **No internal pull-up** | None | Open-drain; requires **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistors** when used as I/O. |
| **P1** | **Yes** | ~30–40 kΩ (weak) | Can source limited current; for driving loads use external transistors. |
| **P2** | **Yes** | ~30–40 kΩ | Similar to P1. |
| **P3** | **Yes** | ~30–40 kΩ | Same as P1, P2. |

**Why P0 is Multiplexed with the Address/Data Bus:**

The 8051 has 40 pins in its standard DIP package. To address 64 KB of external memory, it needs:
- **16 address lines** (A0–A15), AND
- **8 data lines** (D0–D7).

If all were separate pins, the package would need 24+ pins just for memory interfacing, leaving very few for I/O. To solve this, the designers **multiplexed the lower 8 address lines (A0–A7) with the 8-bit data bus (D0–D7) onto Port 0**.

**How the multiplexing works (controlled by ALE signal):**

1. When **ALE = 1**, the lower address byte (A0–A7) appears on P0. An **external latch** (e.g., 74HC373) captures this address on the falling edge of ALE.
2. When **ALE = 0**, P0 carries the actual data (D0–D7) to be read from/written to external memory.
3. The high address byte (A8–A15) is **not multiplexed** — it is driven by **Port 2** directly, because P2 has internal pull-ups and remains stable throughout the bus cycle.

This multiplexing reduces the pin count from 24 (separate) to 16 (8 multiplexed + 8 high-address), saving 8 pins for I/O use.

> **ALE** = Address Latch Enable (pin 30). Used by external latch to demultiplex the address.

> **PSEN** = Program Store Enable (pin 32). Active low signal to read from external code memory (ROM). It is **not activated** for external data memory access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes / Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Writing "P0 has internal pull-up" — **WRONG**. P0 has NO internal pull-up; external resistors (typically 10 kΩ) are required.
> 2. Confusing PSEN and RD — PSEN is for code memory, RD is for data memory.
> 3. Forgetting that P0 cannot source current to drive LEDs directly without external pull-ups.
> 4. Saying "8051 has 64 KB RAM" — **WRONG**. It has 128 bytes internal + 64 KB external data memory; ROM is 4 KB internal + 60 KB external.
> 5. Forgetting that internal data RAM and SFRs share the address space 80H–FFH, but they are accessed by *different addressing modes* (direct for SFR, direct or indirect for RAM).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Architecture:** Harvard, 8-bit ALU, 16-bit address bus.
- **On-chip ROM:** 4 KB (Flash in modern variants).
- **On-chip RAM:** 128 bytes (00H–7FH) + SFRs (80H–FFH).
- **External Memory:** 64 KB code (MOVC, PSEN) + 64 KB data (MOVX, RD/WR).
- **I/O:** 4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines. P0 = no pull-up, P1/P2/P3 = have pull-up.
- **P0 multiplexing:** Address/Data bus — requires external latch (74HC373) + ALE signal.
- **P3 alternate functions:** RxD (P3.0), TxD (P3.1), INT0 (P3.2), INT1 (P3.3), T0 (P3.4), T1 (P3.5), WR (P3.6), RD (P3.7).
- **Timers:** T0 and T1, each 16-bit, 4 modes. Mode 1 = 16-bit, Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload.
- **Timer delay formula:** $T = (65536 - \text{THTL}) \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$ (for 11.0592 MHz) or $1\ \mu\text{s}$ (for 12 MHz).
- **Baud rate formula:** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$.
- **TH1 for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz:** 0xFD (= 253).
- **Interrupts:** 5 sources (INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial) + Reset = 6 total. Two priority levels.
- **Interrupt vector addresses:** 0003H (INT0), 000BH (T0), 0013H (INT1), 001BH (T1), 0023H (Serial).
- **Registers:** A, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, PC, R0–R7 (in 4 banks).
- **Register bank selection:** PSW bits RS1, RS0 — 00 = Bank 0, 01 = Bank 1, 10 = Bank 2, 11 = Bank 3.
- **Bit-addressable RAM:** 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits). Bit addresses 00H–7FH.
- **Bit-addressable SFRs:** ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), TCON (88H), SCON (98H), IE (A8H), IP (B8H), P0–P3.
- **Crystal:** 11.0592 MHz is preferred (exact baud rates); 12 MHz gives 1 µs machine cycle.
- **Machine cycle:** 12 oscillator periods. Instruction cycle = 1 or 2 or 4 machine cycles.
- **Power supply:** +5 V DC; pin 40 = Vcc, pin 20 = Vss.
- **Package:** Standard 40-pin DIP (also available in 44-pin PLCC/QFP).
- **Boolean processor:** bit-level instructions (SETB, CLR, MOV C, ANL C, ORL C, CPL C, JC, JNC, JB, JNB, JBC).
- **EA pin:** When low, all code memory is external (used in 8031-based systems).
- **ALE pin:** Outputs a pulse at 1/6 of oscillator frequency (so ~2 MHz for 12 MHz crystal). Can be used as a clock source for external circuitry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The General-Purpose Registers in the AVR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The General-Purpose Registers in the AVR # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **AVR (Alf and Vegard's RISC processor)** architecture, the **General-Purpose Registers (GPRs)** constitute the **Register File**, a high-speed, on-chip **Static RAM (SRAM)** block consisting of **32 general-purpose 8-bit registers**, conventionally named **R0 through R31**. These registers form the *working area* of the CPU and are directly accessible by nearly every AVR instruction in a **single clock cycle**, embodying the **Load-Store RISC paradigm** where most arithmetic and logical operations *must* occur on data already present in these registers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The AVR Register File is a **32 × 8-bit** SRAM block located at the *lowest* addresses of the internal SRAM data memory (typically mapped to addresses `$0000` to `$001F`). It is the *only* data memory space directly accessible by the ALU in a single cycle. The first **six registers (R0–R5)** are unrestricted, while the **last six registers (R26–R31)** are *aliased* (multiplexed) to support the **X, Y, and Z pointer registers** used for indirect SRAM addressing. Registers **R0–R15** cannot be used with the immediate-load (`LDI`) instruction due to AVR's compact instruction encoding; only **R16–R31** are usable as `LDI` targets. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Desk-Top Toolbox Metaphor:** > Imagine a carpenter's workbench. The **workbench surface** = the **Register File** (32 slots, R0–R31). The **carpenter's hands** = the **ALU**. The carpenter can only hammer, saw, or glue (perform ALU operations) on materials (data) lying *on the desk*. The **toolbox drawer below** = the rest of **SRAM**, and the **outdoor lumber store** = **Flash/EEPROM**. Every time the carpenter needs new wood, he must *fetch* it onto the desk first (a `LDS`/load), and finished pieces must be *put away* (a `STS`/store). The 32 registers are the carpenter's *simultaneous reach*: 8 tools, both hands, and a small tray — fast, limited, and constantly in use. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Register width:** **8 bits** (data), but pairs can form **16-bit pointers** (X, Y, Z). - **Number of GPRs:** **32** registers. - **Access time:** **1 CPU clock cycle** (single-cycle ALU access). - **Address range in data SRAM:** `$0000` – `$001F`. - **Total register file size:** **32 bytes (256 bits)**. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Register File Architecture The AVR Register File is partitioned by function: | Register Group | Register Names | Primary Role | Special Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Lower Group** | **R0 – R15** | Basic data manipulation, ALU operands | **Cannot be used as destination of `LDI` (Load Immediate)** | | **Upper Group** | **R16 – R31** | General data + immediate loads + `MUL` operations | None — full instruction set support | | **Pointer Group (Aliased)** | **R26 & R27** | Form the **X-register** (16-bit pointer) | Used for indirect addressing | | **Pointer Group (Aliased)** | **R28 & R29** | Form the **Y-register** (16-bit pointer) | Used for indirect addressing with displacement | | **Pointer Group (Aliased)** | **R30 & R31** | Form the **Z-register** (16-bit pointer) | Used for indirect addressing, also reads Flash via `LPM` | > [!NOTE] > The *aliasing* is a hardware-level feature: writing to `R26` simultaneously updates the low byte of the X-pointer. This means **X, Y, Z are not separate registers** — they are *projections* of R26:R27, R28:R29, R30:R31. ## 2.2 Why the Split Between R0–R15 and R16–R31? The AVR uses a **fixed 16-bit instruction word**. To support a 32-register file in 16 bits, the encoding for `LDI` uses only **4 bits for the register field** plus a **4-bit condition-code region (dddd)**, giving a 4-bit register address (16 possible destinations) — therefore only **R16–R31** can be loaded with an 8-bit immediate. This is a classic *RISC instruction-encoding trade-off*. $$ \text{Number of LDI-accessible registers} = 2^{4} = 16 = \text{R16 to R31} $$ ## 2.3 The X, Y, Z Pointer Registers | Pointer | Composed Of | Address Pointer Width | Special Capability | |---|---|---|---| | **X** | **R27 (high) : R26 (low)** | **16 bits** $\rightarrow$ addresses `$0000` to `$FFFF` | Indirect read/write: `LD Rd, X` / `ST X, Rr` | | **Y** | **R29 (high) : R28 (low)** | **16 bits** | Indirect + **6-bit displacement** (`LDD Rd, Y+q`) | | **Z** | **R31 (high) : R30 (low)** | **16 bits** | Indirect + displacement + **Flash read via `LPM`** | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | Register file size | $N_{regs} = 32$ | Fixed by AVR ISA | | Addressable SRAM via pointer | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | Full 16-bit pointer range | | Effective address from Y + q | $EA = Y + q$ | $q \in [0, 63]$ | | Pointer pointer size | $W = 16 \text{ bits}$ | Two 8-bit GPRs concatenated | | LDI register constraint | $R_{dst} \in [16, 31]$ | `dddd` field width = 4 bits | | MUL operand constraint | $R \in [0, 31]$ | `MUL` allows R0–R15, unlike `LDI` | | 8-bit range | $-128 \le N \le 127$ | Two's complement | | Unsigned 8-bit range | $0 \le N \le 255$ | Hex: `$00` – `$FF` | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility The GPR-centric design is what makes the AVR exceptionally **deterministic and fast** for embedded real-time control (motor drives, sensor sampling, automotive ECUs). Because all ALU operands reside in registers, **interrupt latency is bounded** and C compilers (avr-gcc) can map local variables directly into these registers, achieving near-assembler performance in motor-control loops, ADC pipelines, and PWM signal generation. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Registers From Instruction Encoding The AVR's **single 16-bit instruction word** is partitioned for an `LDI` (Load Immediate) as: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction word (16 bits)} &= \underbrace{1110}_{\text{opcode (4 bits)}} \;\; \underbrace{KKKK}_{\text{high nibble of imm (4 bits)}} \;\; \underbrace{KKKK}_{\text{low nibble of imm (4 bits)}} \;\; \underbrace{dddd}_{\text{register (4 bits)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Identify the destination field width:** The `dddd` field has a width of $n = 4$ bits. **Step 2 — Compute the number of unique register codes:** $$ N = 2^{n} = 2^{4} = 16 $$ **Step 3 — Map the codes to register names (AVR convention):** The codes `0000` through `1111` map to registers **R16 through R31** (i.e., code `dddd` = `0000` → R16, code = `1111` → R31). $$ \boxed{R_{dst} = 16 + \text{(binary value of dddd)}} $$ **Conclusion:** Only **16 of the 32** general-purpose registers can be destinations of `LDI`; the other 16 (R0–R15) require a **two-step** load (clear upper half, then `LDI` lower, or load from SRAM). ## 3.2 Derivation: Concatenation of X, Y, Z Pointers Given the AVR's **little-endian** byte ordering, the low byte occupies the *lower-numbered* register: $$ \begin{aligned} X_{16} &= \big(R27 \cdot 2^{8}\big) + R26 \\ Y_{16} &= \big(R29 \cdot 2^{8}\big) + R28 \\ Z_{16} &= \big(R31 \cdot 2^{8}\big) + R30 \end{aligned} $$ **Example — Evaluate Y after `LDI R28, 0x55` and `LDI R29, 0x33`:** $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{16} &= (R29 \cdot 256) + R28 \\ &= (0x33 \cdot 256) + 0x55 \\ &= (51 \times 256) + 85 \\ &= 13056 + 85 \\ &= 13141 \\ \therefore Y_{16} &= 0x3355 \end{aligned} $$ This is the address in data SRAM the Y pointer will reference during a `LD Rd, Y` operation. ## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Address With Displacement The `LDD Rd, Y+q` instruction computes an *effective address* without altering the Y-pointer itself: $$ \begin{aligned} EA = Y + q, \quad \text{where } q \in [0, 63] \end{aligned} $$ **Example — `LDD R16, Y+8` with Y = `0x0200`:** $$ \begin{aligned} EA &= 0x0200 + 0x08 = 0x0208 \\ \therefore \text{R16} &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[0x0208] \end{aligned} $$ Y remains at `0x0200` afterward; only the *load* is affected. This is a powerful construct for accessing **struct/array fields** in C-compiled AVR code. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Register Usage Patterns | Operation | Assembly Snippet | Affected Registers | |---|---|---| | Load immediate | `LDI R16, 0x25` | **R16 ← 0x25** | | Register copy | `MOV R17, R16` | **R17 ← R16** | | Add registers | `ADD R18, R16` | **R18 ← R18 + R16** | | Add with carry | `ADC R19, R16` | **R19 ← R19 + R16 + C** | | Indirect load | `LD R20, X` | **R20 ← Memory[X]** | | Indirect store | `ST X, R20` | **Memory[X] ← R20** | | Post-increment | `LD R20, X+` | **R20 ← Mem[X]; X ← X+1** | | Pre-decrement | `ST -X, R20` | **X ← X-1; Mem[X] ← R20** | | Initialize Z | `LDI R30, low(0x1234)`
`LDI R31, high(0x1234)` | **Z ← 0x1234** | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AVR Data Memory Map (Register File Region) ```mermaid flowchart TB sramTop["SRAM Top 0x08FF End of SRAM"] extIO["I/O Registers 0x0020 to 0x005F 64 bytes"] gpr["General Purpose Registers 0x0000 to 0x001F 32 bytes R0 R1 ... R31"] sramTop --> extIO --> gpr r0["R0 0x0000"] r1["R1 0x0001"] r2["R2 0x0002"] r25["R25 0x0019"] r26["R26 0x001A X low"] r27["R27 0x001B X high"] r28["R28 0x001C Y low"] r29["R29 0x001D Y high"] r30["R30 0x001E Z low"] r31["R31 0x001F Z high"] gpr --> r0 gpr --> r1 gpr --> r2 gpr --> r25 gpr --> r26 gpr --> r27 gpr --> r28 gpr --> r29 gpr --> r30 gpr --> r31 ``` ## 4.2 X, Y, Z Pointer Aliasing Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR reg26["R26 8-bit storage cell"] reg27["R27 8-bit storage cell"] reg28["R28 8-bit storage cell"] reg29["R29 8-bit storage cell"] reg30["R30 8-bit storage cell"] reg31["R31 8-bit storage cell"] aliasA["X pointer 16-bit wide bus to SRAM"] aliasB["Y pointer 16-bit wide bus to SRAM"] aliasC["Z pointer 16-bit wide bus to SRAM and Flash via LPM"] reg26 --- aliasA reg27 --- aliasA reg28 --- aliasB reg29 --- aliasB reg30 --- aliasC reg31 --- aliasC ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Register File in the AVR Data Path ```mermaid flowchart LR pc["Program Counter"] flash["Flash Memory Instruction Store"] idec["Instruction Decoder"] gprFile["Register File R0 to R31 32 x 8-bit SRAM"] alu["ALU 8-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit"] sram["Data SRAM 0x0060 to 0x08FF"] io["I/O and Peripherals 0x0020 to 0x005F"] x["X pointer R27 R26"] y["Y pointer R29 R28"] z["Z pointer R31 R30"] sreg["Status Register SREG 8 flags"] pc --> flash flash --> idec idec -->|"opcode"| alu idec -->|"register addresses"| gprFile gprFile -->|"operand A"| alu gprFile -->|"operand B"| alu alu -->|"result"| gprFile alu --> sreg gprFile --> x gprFile --> y gprFile --> z x -->|"address bus"| sram y -->|"address bus"| sram z -->|"address bus"| sram x -->|"address bus"| io y -->|"address bus"| io z -->|"address bus"| io z -->|"address bus for LPM"| flash ``` ## 4.4 Why R0–R15 Cannot Receive `LDI` — Encoding Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR instr["16-bit LDI instruction word"] opc["1110 opcode 4 bits"] immH["KKKK imm high 4 bits"] immL["KKKK imm low 4 bits"] dddd["dddd register 4 bits only 16 codes"] out["Result destination R16 to R31 only"] instr --> opc instr --> immH instr --> immL instr --> dddd dddd --> out ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. List the General-Purpose Registers in the AVR architecture and explain why R26–R31 are called pointer registers.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Model Answer:** The AVR contains **32 general-purpose 8-bit registers** named **R0 through R31**, mapped to data SRAM addresses `0x0000`–`0x001F`. Registers **R26:R27**, **R28:R29**, and **R30:R31** are *aliased* to form the **16-bit pointer registers X, Y, and Z** respectively. They are called pointer registers because they hold the **16-bit address** used by indirect-load (`LD`) and indirect-store (`ST`) instructions to access the entire **64 KB** data SRAM space. The Z-pointer is additionally used by the **`LPM` (Load Program Memory)** instruction to read from Flash. (3 marks) --- ### **Q2. Why is the instruction `LDI R5, 0x25` illegal in the AVR? How can the value 0x25 be loaded into R5 using only registers?** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Model Answer:** The `LDI` instruction uses a **4-bit `dddd` field** to encode the destination register, supporting only **R16–R31**. R5 is in the range R0–R15 and therefore **cannot be the destination of `LDI`** — the assembler will flag it as an *illegal opcode* (or fold it into `SUBI`/`ANDI` families with constraints). To load `0x25` into R5, a two-step *clearing* approach is used: ```assembly CLR R5 ; R5 ← 0 (R5 = R5 XOR R5) LDI R16, 0x25 ; R16 ← 0x25 (legal) MOV R5, R16 ; R5 ← R16 ``` (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Recommended]** #### (a) Draw and explain the architecture of the AVR General-Purpose Register File. Discuss the roles of R0–R15 and R16–R31. **(7 marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Model Solution:** The **AVR Register File** is a **32 × 8-bit SRAM block** located at the *bottom* of the data-memory map (addresses `$0000`–`$001F` in SRAM). It is the **only memory space directly accessible by the ALU in a single clock cycle**, embodying the RISC Load-Store philosophy. **Architecture Diagram (textual representation):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Register File} &= \begin{bmatrix} R0 \; (\$00) \\ R1 \; (\$01) \\ \vdots \\ R15 \; (\$0F) \\ \hline R16 \; (\$10) \\ \vdots \\ R25 \; (\$19) \\ R26 \; (\$1A) \rightarrow X_{low} \\ R27 \; (\$1B) \rightarrow X_{high} \\ R28 \; (\$1C) \rightarrow Y_{low} \\ R29 \; (\$1D) \rightarrow Y_{high} \\ R30 \; (\$1E) \rightarrow Z_{low} \\ R31 \; (\$1F) \rightarrow Z_{high} \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Roles of R0–R15:** - Serve as ALU **source and destination operands** for arithmetic, logical, and bit-manipulation instructions. - Can be used with instructions such as `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`, `COM`, `NEG`, `MUL`, `MOV`. - **Cannot** be loaded directly with an 8-bit constant using `LDI` (only R16–R31 may be `LDI` destinations). - Often used by the C compiler for **scratch/working registers**. **Roles of R16–R31:** - Full instruction set support, including `LDI`, `SUBI`, `ANDI`, `ORI`, `SBR`, `CBR`, and bit-test instructions. - Privileged upper half of the register file. - Pair-wise (R16:R17, etc.) usable for **16-bit operations** with instructions like `MOVW`, `ADIW`, `SBIW`. (7 marks) #### (b) Explain the X, Y, and Z pointer registers. With a numerical example, show how a 16-bit address is formed. How are they used in indirect addressing? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The X, Y, Z pointers are **16-bit address registers** formed by concatenating two GPRs each: $$ \begin{aligned} X &= \{R27, R26\} \quad (\text{high byte} = R27,\; \text{low byte} = R26) \\ Y &= \{R29, R28\} \\ Z &= \{R31, R30\} \end{aligned} $$ Because the AVR is **little-endian**, the lower-numbered register holds the **low byte** of the address. Each pointer is **16 bits wide** and can therefore address $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes of data SRAM. **Numerical Example:** To set Z to address `$3F4A`: ```assembly LDI R30, 0x4A ; low byte of Z ($4A = 74) LDI R31, 0x3F ; high byte of Z ($3F = 63) ; Z = R31·256 + R30 ; = 63·256 + 74 ; = 16128 + 74 ; = 16202 = 0x3F4A ``` **Indirect addressing uses:** | Instruction | Meaning | Pointer Used | |---|---|---| | `LD Rd, X` | Load from address X | X | | `LD Rd, X+` | Load, then X ← X+1 | X | | `LD Rd, -X` | X ← X-1, then load | X | | `ST X+, Rr` | Store, then X ← X+1 | X | | `LDD Rd, Y+q` | Load from Y + q (q=0..63) | Y | | `LPM Rd, Z+` | Load Program Memory (Flash) from Z, then Z ← Z+1 | Z | This is the *cornerstone* of array/pointer manipulation in AVR C, and the underlying mechanism for `pgm_read_byte()` used to read Flash-resident constants. (7 marks) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]** #### (a) Discuss the difference between Register Direct, Register Indirect, and Register Indirect with Displacement addressing modes in the AVR. Give one example instruction for each. **(7 marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Model Solution:** | Mode | Description | Example | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Register Direct** | The instruction explicitly names the GPR holding the operand. | `ADD R16, R17` (R16 ← R16 + R17) | Single-cycle, 1-word instruction | | **Register Indirect** | The instruction names one of the pointer registers (X, Y, or Z); the pointer holds the *effective address* of the operand in SRAM. | `LD R20, Z` (R20 ← Mem[Z]) | Requires 2-word instruction, 2 cycles | | **Register Indirect with Displacement** | The effective address is **Pointer + constant q** (0 ≤ q ≤ 63). | `LDD R18, Y+6` (R18 ← Mem[Y+6]) | Y and Z only; X does not support displacement | **Key Distinctions:** - *Register Direct* operates on data **already in the register file** — fastest, single-cycle. - *Register Indirect* uses the **pointer as an address** into the wider SRAM — required when data lives outside the register file. - *Displacement mode* allows **fixed-offset struct access** (e.g., `obj.field3`) without disturbing the pointer itself — extremely useful in C-compiled code. (7 marks) #### (b) Write an AVR assembly program to copy a block of 5 bytes from SRAM starting at address `$0200` to address `$0300` using the X and Y pointer registers. Comment on each instruction. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```assembly ; --- Initialize X (source pointer) to $0200 --- LDI R26, 0x00 ; X_low = 0x00 LDI R27, 0x02 ; X_high = 0x02 → X = 0x0200 ; --- Initialize Y (destination pointer) to $0300 --- LDI R28, 0x00 ; Y_low = 0x00 LDI R29, 0x03 ; Y_high = 0x03 → Y = 0x0300 ; --- Set up loop counter R16 = 5 --- LDI R16, 5 ; R16 = 5 (counter) LOOP: LD R17, X+ ; R17 ← Mem[X]; X ← X + 1 (post-increment) ST Y+, R17 ; Mem[Y] ← R17; Y ← Y + 1 DEC R16 ; R16 ← R16 - 1 BRNE LOOP ; Branch if R16 ≠ 0 → repeat DONE: RJMP DONE ; Infinite loop (program end) ``` **Instruction Commentary:** | Instruction | Function | |---|---| | `LDI R26, 0x00` | Loads low byte of X-pointer (R16–R31 constraint satisfied for `LDI`; R26 is *loaded directly* via its alias identity in the assembler) | | `LDI R27, 0x02` | Loads high byte of X; now X = `0x0200` | | `LDI R28, 0x00` | Low byte of Y | | `LDI R29, 0x03` | High byte of Y; now Y = `0x0300` | | `LDI R16, 5` | Loop counter | | `LD R17, X+` | **Post-increment** load: reads Mem[X], then auto-increments X by 1 | | `ST Y+, R17` | **Post-increment** store: writes R17 to Mem[Y], then auto-increments Y | | `DEC R16` | Decrement counter | | `BRNE LOOP` | Branch back to LOOP if Z-flag = 0 (counter not yet zero) | **Execution Trace (first iteration):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{X} &= 0x0200,\; \text{Y} = 0x0300 \\ \text{After LD R17, X+}: \quad \text{R17} &= \text{Mem}[0x0200],\; \text{X} \to 0x0201 \\ \text{After ST Y+, R17}: \quad \text{Mem}[0x0300] &= \text{R17},\; \text{Y} \to 0x0301 \\ \text{After DEC R16}: \quad \text{R16} &= 4 \\ \text{After BRNE LOOP}: \quad \text{PC} &\to \text{LOOP} \end{aligned} $$ After 5 iterations, all 5 bytes are copied and the program enters the idle loop at `DONE`. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **The `LDI` constraint is the #1 mark-loser.** Students routinely write `LDI R5, 0x25` and lose 1–2 marks for not explaining *why* it's illegal. Always cite the 4-bit `dddd` field. > 2. **Endianness mistake:** When concatenating X, Y, Z, students often write `X = R26 : R27` (high:low) — this is **backwards**. The AVR is **little-endian**: R26 is the *low* byte. > 3. **Confusing "Register Direct" with "Direct SRAM addressing":** `LDS R16, 0x0200` is *direct SRAM*, **not** register-direct. Register-direct uses a register name as the operand. > 4. **Forgetting to initialize both bytes of the pointer.** A common bug: setting only R26 = `0x00` and forgetting R27 → X = `0x0000`, not `0x0200`. Always show *both* `LDI` instructions. > 5. **Using `BRNE` semantics:** Many students write `BRNE LOOP` thinking it branches "if not equal" — clarify it actually branches when the **Z flag = 0** (i.e., result of `DEC` was not zero). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Register File = 32 × 8-bit SRAM**, addresses `$0000`–`$001F`, **single-cycle access**. - **Two functional groups:** **R0–R15** (basic) and **R16–R31** (full + immediate). - **LDI destination is restricted to R16–R31** (4-bit field). - **R26:R27 = X**, **R28:R29 = Y**, **R30:R31 = Z** — *aliased*, little-endian, **16-bit pointers** to the **64 KB** SRAM. - **X** → simple indirect addressing. - **Y** → indirect + 6-bit displacement `q ∈ [0,63]`. - **Z** → indirect + displacement + **Flash access via `LPM`**. - The Register File is the **fastest storage tier** in the AVR memory hierarchy: **Registers → I/O → SRAM → EEPROM → Flash** (descending speed, ascending capacity). - **Load-Store RISC architecture** mandates that all ALU operations operate on register data; SRAM must be loaded in/out via `LD`/`ST`/`LDS`/`STS`. - **Post-increment / pre-decrement** forms (`LD R, X+`, `ST -X, R`) enable efficient **stack/array traversal** in just one instruction. - **Compiler behaviour:** `avr-gcc` allocates 32-byte register window per function — running out of GPRs causes *register spill* to the *stack frame* in SRAM, increasing code size and cycles. - **Interview/KTU viva favourite:** *"Why are the X, Y, Z registers made of GPRs instead of separate dedicated registers?"* — Answer: To **save silicon area** (no extra 16-bit register hardware) and to **allow flexible use** as general 8-bit registers when pointer functionality is not needed. - **Exam mnemonic — "LLZ, X first, Z for flash":** **L**ow **L**ittle-endian, then **Z** = flash, **X** = first (alphabetical), **Y** = middle with displacement.

topics.title: AVR Data Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AVR Data Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **AVR Data Memory** is a volatile, read/write memory space used by the AVR microcontroller to store temporary data, variables, the stack, the heap, and the I/O/peripheral register mappings during program execution. It is implemented using **Static RAM (SRAM)** technology and is organized as a linear, byte-addressable memory space of up to **64 KB (65,536 bytes)**.

The AVR (Advanced Virtual RISC) uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture**, meaning the data memory is physically separate from the Program Memory (Flash), but the CPU can access data memory using five distinct addressing modes. The data memory map is partitioned into distinct functional regions:

- **General Purpose Register File** (32 × 8-bit registers)
- **I/O Register Space** (64 × 8-bit registers, directly accessible via IN/OUT)
- **Extended I/O Register Space** (160 × 8-bit registers)
- **Internal SRAM** (typically 1 KB to 4 KB depending on variant)
- **External SRAM** (optional, up to 64 KB total)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The data memory addresses the entire mapped region from `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`, but only specific blocks are physically implemented. The first **96 bytes (`0x0000`–`0x005F`)** and the **Internal SRAM block** are the most frequently tested regions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Office Filing Cabinet System"**
>
> Imagine the AVR data memory as a **large corporate office filing cabinet** where every drawer is numbered sequentially from `0x0000` to `0xFFFF` (a total of **65,536** drawers).
>
> - **Drawers 0–31 (`0x0000`–`0x001F`)**: The 32 "personal working files" on your desk — the **General Purpose Registers**. These are the **fastest** to access (just one CPU clock cycle), so you keep your most frequently used variables here.
> - **Drawers 32–95 (`0x0020`–`0x005F`)**: The "office equipment control panel" — the **I/O Registers**. Each drawer directly controls a peripheral (e.g., drawer 0x25 controls Port B's data direction).
> - **Drawers 96–255 (`0x0060`–`0x00FF`)**: The "extended equipment control panel" — **Extended I/O Registers** for advanced peripherals like ADC, Timer, UART.
> - **Drawers 256+ (`0x0100` onwards)**: The "general archive room" — **Internal SRAM** for large variables, arrays, the stack, and the heap.
> - **Higher drawers**: An optional "off-site storage warehouse" — **External SRAM** (only in AVRs with external memory interface like ATmega64/128).

The key insight: the **CPU does not differentiate** between these regions during execution — it sees one continuous linear memory space. The "regions" are a logical/functional mapping, not physical boundaries.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Total Linear Data Space | **64 KB (0x0000 – 0xFFFF)** |
| General Purpose Registers | **32 × 8-bit** |
| I/O Register Space | **64 bytes** |
| Extended I/O Register Space | **160 bytes** |
| Internal SRAM (ATmega32) | **2 KB (2048 bytes)** |
| Internal SRAM (ATmega16) | **1 KB (1024 bytes)** |
| Internal SRAM (ATmega328P) | **2 KB** |
| Data Bus Width | **8 bits** |
| Data Address Bus Width | **16 bits** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Memory Map of AVR Data Memory

The AVR data memory is logically divided into **five distinct regions**. Understanding the address boundaries of each region is critical for both assembly programming (LD/ST instructions) and embedded C programming (pointers, SFR access).

### Region 1: General Purpose Register File (R0 – R31)

- **Address Range:** `0x0000` to `0x001F`
- **Size:** **32 bytes (32 × 8-bit registers)**
- **Purpose:** The fastest storage accessible in a single clock cycle. Used for arithmetic, logic, and intermediate computations.
- **Naming Convention:** Registers are named **R0, R1, R2, ..., R31** in assembly.
- **Special Roles:**
  - **R0**: Used implicitly in MUL (multiplication) instruction.
  - **R1**: Used to store the **Zero Flag** result after multiplication; always assumed to be zero by the C runtime.
  - **R26:R27** → Form the **X-pointer register pair** (16-bit address pointer).
  - **R28:R29** → Form the **Y-pointer register pair**.
  - **R30:R31** → Form the **Z-pointer register pair** (used for LPM — Load Program Memory).

### Region 2: I/O Register Space

- **Address Range:** `0x0020` to `0x005F`
- **Size:** **64 bytes (64 × 8-bit registers)**
- **Purpose:** Direct control and status registers of built-in peripherals.
- **Access Methods:**
  - **IN** instruction: `IN Rd, A` (where A is `0x00` to `0x3F` — *offsets from 0x20*)
  - **OUT** instruction: `OUT A, Rr`
  - **CBI/SBI** instruction: `SBI PORTB, 3` (set bit 3 of PORTB)
- **Common Registers:**
  - `0x0023` (PORTA offset `0x03`)
  - `0x0025` (PORTB offset `0x05`)
  - `0x0028` (PORTC offset `0x08`)
  - `0x002B` (PORTD offset `0x0B`)

### Region 3: Extended I/O Register Space

- **Address Range:** `0x0060` to `0x00FF`
- **Size:** **160 bytes**
- **Purpose:** Advanced peripherals such as ADC, USART, Timers, SPI, TWI, EEPROM.
- **Access Method:** Only accessible using **LD/LDS/STS/ST** instructions (data memory addressing), **NOT** by IN/OUT.

### Region 4: Internal SRAM

- **Address Range:** Starts at `0x0100`, ends at the top of internal SRAM.
- **For ATmega32**: `0x0100` to `0x08FF` (2048 bytes).
- **Purpose:** Static variables, the **stack**, the **heap**, large buffers, and arrays.

### Region 5: External SRAM (Optional)

- **Available In:** ATmega128, ATmega2560, ATmega1280, etc.
- **Address Range:** From the end of internal SRAM up to `0xFFFF`.
- **Configuration:** Controlled by the **MCUCR (MCU Control Register)**, specifically the **SRE (SRAM Enable)** bit.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Address → I/O Offset | $\text{I/O Offset} = \text{Data Address} - 0x0020$ | Used to convert between LD/ST and IN/OUT syntax |
| SRAM Start Address | $A_{\text{SRAM, start}} = 0x0100$ | First byte of user-accessible SRAM |
| SRAM End Address (ATmega32) | $A_{\text{SRAM, end}} = 0x08FF$ | Top of 2 KB internal SRAM |
| SRAM Size Calculation | $S_{\text{SRAM}} = A_{\text{end}} - A_{\text{start}} + 1$ | In bytes |
| X-Pointer Formation | $X = (R27 \ll 8) \cup R26$ | 16-bit pointer |
| Y-Pointer Formation | $Y = (R29 \ll 8) \cup R28$ | 16-bit pointer |
| Z-Pointer Formation | $Z = (R31 \ll 8) \cup R30$ | 16-bit pointer |
| Register File Size | $N_{\text{regs}} = 32$ | R0 to R31 |
| I/O Space Size | $S_{\text{IO}} = 64 \text{ bytes}$ | Data addresses 0x20 to 0x5F |
| Extended I/O Size | $S_{\text{ExtIO}} = 160 \text{ bytes}$ | Data addresses 0x60 to 0xFF |
| Memory Regions Combined (before SRAM) | $96 + 160 = 256 \text{ bytes} = 0x100$ | Occupies `0x0000` to `0x00FF` |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Memory Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale:**
> The reason the General Purpose Register File is placed at the **lowest addresses** (`0x0000`–`0x001F`) is **not arbitrary**. In AVR instruction encoding, register operands are encoded using only **5 bits** (allowing values 0–31). By placing the most frequently accessed 32 registers at the base, the instruction set remains compact (most instructions are a single 16-bit word). Larger addresses (16-bit) require longer `LDS`/`STS` instructions (2 words / 32 bits).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Data Memory Space Calculation for ATmega32

We want to compute the total data memory size for the ATmega32 by summing the contributions of each region.

### Step 1 — Identify the Regions
The ATmega32 has four functional regions in its data memory map:
- **Region A:** General Purpose Registers
- **Region B:** I/O Registers
- **Region C:** Extended I/O Registers
- **Region D:** Internal SRAM

### Step 2 — Calculate the Size of Each Region

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= 0x001F - 0x0000 + 1 \\
&= 31 - 0 + 1 \\
&= 32 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= 0x005F - 0x0020 + 1 \\
&= 95 - 32 + 1 \\
&= 64 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= 0x00FF - 0x0060 + 1 \\
&= 255 - 96 + 1 \\
&= 160 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= 0x08FF - 0x0100 + 1 \\
&= 2303 - 256 + 1 \\
&= 2048 \text{ bytes (2 KB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum All Regions

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Total}} &= S_A + S_B + S_C + S_D \\
&= 32 + 64 + 160 + 2048 \\
&= 2304 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 2.25 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of this calculation:** Each region occupies a **contiguous, non-overlapping** block of the linear data space. Summing the sizes of all blocks gives the total accessible data memory footprint of the device. Note that 0x0000 to 0x00FF (the first 256 bytes) is **shared** by registers and I/O — these are the SFR/Register file region.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: I/O Offset Conversion

When writing AVR assembly, students often confuse whether to use **IN/OUT** (which uses offsets `0x00`–`0x3F`) or **LD/ST** (which uses full data addresses `0x20`–`0x5F`). The relationship is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{data}} &= A_{\text{IO offset}} + 0x0020
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example: Accessing PORTB

PORTB is the data register for port B. Its **I/O address** is `0x05` and its **data memory address** is `0x0025`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{data}} &= 0x05 + 0x0020 \\
&= 0x0025
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the following two instructions are **equivalent** (and produce identical machine code when optimized by the assembler):

```assembly
OUT 0x05, R16     ; Uses I/O offset 0x05
STS 0x0025, R16   ; Uses full data address 0x0025
```

---

## 3.3 Derivation: X, Y, Z Pointer Formation

The 16-bit pointer registers are formed by concatenating two 8-bit general purpose registers.

### X-Pointer

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= (R27 \ll 8) \cup R26 \\
&= (\text{High Byte}) \ll 8 \ \vert\ (\text{Low Byte})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example: If R26 = `0x5A` and R27 = `0xA5`

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= (0xA5 \ll 8) \cup 0x5A \\
&= 0xA500 \cup 0x005A \\
&= 0xA55A
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The high byte is shifted left by 8 bits (placed in the upper 8 bits of the 16-bit word), then the low byte is OR-ed in. This produces the full 16-bit address used for indirect memory access via `LD R0, X+` (load with post-increment).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Memory Access Methods

| Instruction Type | Address Range Accessible | Address Width | Execution Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instructions with register operands (R0–R31) | `0x0000` – `0x001F` | 5 bits (encoded in opcode) | **1 cycle** |
| `IN` / `OUT` / `SBI` / `CBI` | `0x0020` – `0x005F` (offset `0x00` – `0x3F`) | 6 bits (I/O offset) | **1 cycle** |
| `LD` / `ST` with X/Y/Z pointer | `0x0000` – `0xFFFF` (full 64 KB) | 16 bits (in registers) | **2 cycles** |
| `LDS` / `STS` (direct) | `0x0000` – `0xFFFF` | 16 bits (in instruction) | **2 cycles** |
| `PUSH` / `POP` (stack) | `0x0000` – `0xFFFF` (via SP) | Indirect via SP | **2 cycles** |
| `LPM` (Load Program Memory) | Program Flash only (Z-pointer) | 16 bits | **3 cycles** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AVR Data Memory Map (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the block-level functional architecture of the AVR Data Memory space, showing each region, its size, and its address range.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startPoint([Data Memory Space 0x0000 to 0xFFFF]) --> regFile
    startPoint --> ioSpace
    startPoint --> extIO
    startPoint --> intSRAM
    startPoint --> extSRAM

    regFile[REGION 1 General Purpose Register File R0 to R31 Size 32 bytes Address 0x0000 to 0x001F Access 1 cycle]

    ioSpace[REGION 2 I/O Register Space Size 64 bytes Address 0x0020 to 0x005F Access via IN OUT SBI CBI]

    extIO[REGION 3 Extended I/O Register Space Size 160 bytes Address 0x0060 to 0x00FF Access via LD ST only]

    intSRAM[REGION 4 Internal SRAM Size 2 KB for ATmega32 Address 0x0100 to 0x08FF Holds variables stack heap]

    extSRAM[REGION 5 External SRAM OPTIONAL Address above 0x08FF up to 0xFFFF Available only on ATmega128 family]

    regFile -- Pointer Pairs --> ptrPair
    ptrPair[Pointer Registers X R26 R27 Y R28 R29 Z R30 R31]

    ioSpace --> periphA[PORTA DDRA PINA etc]
    ioSpace --> periphB[PORTB DDRB PINB etc]
    extIO --> periphAdv[ADC USART Timers SPI TWI]

    style regFile fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style ioSpace fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    style extIO fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style intSRAM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    style extSRAM fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - **Orange block** = General Purpose Registers (fastest, 1-cycle access)
> - **Blue block** = Standard I/O Registers (1-cycle access via IN/OUT)
> - **Green block** = Extended I/O Registers (2-cycle access via LD/ST)
> - **Purple block** = Internal SRAM (general storage)
> - **Yellow dashed block** = Optional External SRAM

---

## 4.2 Detailed Address Map (Linear Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[0x0000 to 0x001F R0 to R31 General Purpose Registers 32 bytes] --> A1[0x0020 to 0x005F I O Registers 64 bytes]
    A1 --> A2[0x0060 to 0x00FF Extended I O Registers 160 bytes]
    A2 --> A3[0x0100 to 0x08FF Internal SRAM 2048 bytes for ATmega32]
    A3 --> A4[0x0900 to 0xFFFF External SRAM or Unused Optional up to 59136 bytes]

    style A0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px
    style A1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.3 I/O vs. Data Address Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    cpu[CPU Core] --> instrType{Instruction Type}
    instrType -- IN OUT SBI CBI --> ioOffset[Uses I O Offset 0x00 to 0x3F]
    instrType -- LD ST LDS STS --> dataAddr[Uses Full Data Address 0x20 to 0xFFFF]
    ioOffset --> calc[Add 0x0020 to Offset]
    calc --> physAddr[Same Physical Address in Memory]
    dataAddr --> physAddr
    physAddr --> reg[General Purpose Register File]
    physAddr --> ioreg[I O Register Space]
    physAddr --> extIoreg[Extended I O Register Space]
    physAddr --> sram[Internal SRAM]

    style cpu fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ioOffset fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style dataAddr fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style physAddr fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **How to Read this Diagram:** The CPU issues an instruction. If the instruction is `IN`/`OUT`/`SBI`/`CBI`, the assembler/cpu treats the operand as an **I/O offset** (0–63) and automatically adds `0x0020` to obtain the physical data memory address. If the instruction is `LD`/`ST`/`LDS`/`STS`, the operand is the **full 16-bit data address** directly.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the different regions of the AVR data memory map with their address ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

The AVR data memory map is divided into the following regions:

1. **General Purpose Register File**: `0x0000` to `0x001F` (32 bytes, R0–R31)
2. **I/O Register Space**: `0x0020` to `0x005F` (64 bytes)
3. **Extended I/O Register Space**: `0x0060` to `0x00FF` (160 bytes)
4. **Internal SRAM**: `0x0100` to top of SRAM (e.g., `0x08FF` for ATmega32 = 2 KB)
5. **External SRAM** (optional): From end of internal SRAM up to `0xFFFF` (only on devices with external memory interface)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the X, Y, and Z pointer register pairs in AVR data memory access.**

**Model Answer:**

The X, Y, and Z pointer register pairs are formed by concatenating pairs of general purpose registers:

- **X-Pointer**: R26 (low byte) : R27 (high byte) — Used for indirect addressing with `LD`/`ST` and post-increment/decrement modes.
- **Y-Pointer**: R28 (low byte) : R29 (high byte) — Supports all X-pointer modes **plus** displacement addressing (e.g., `LDD Rd, Y+q`).
- **Z-Pointer**: R30 (low byte) : R31 (high byte) — Supports all Y-pointer modes **plus** the special `LPM` (Load Program Memory) instruction to read from Flash.

These 16-bit pointers allow the CPU to access the entire 64 KB data memory space using indirect addressing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **AVR data memory organization** in detail. List all regions with their sizes and address ranges for the **ATmega32** microcontroller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **I/O Register Space** and **Extended I/O Register Space**. Explain the different instruction formats used to access each region with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The AVR data memory is a linear, byte-addressable space of up to 64 KB. For the ATmega32, the memory map is divided as follows:

| Region | Address Range | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| General Purpose Registers (R0–R31) | `0x0000` – `0x001F` | 32 | Fastest scratch storage |
| I/O Registers | `0x0020` – `0x005F` | 64 | Standard peripheral control |
| Extended I/O Registers | `0x0060` – `0x00FF` | 160 | Advanced peripheral control |
| Internal SRAM | `0x0100` – `0x08FF` | 2048 | Variables, stack, heap |

**Total data memory = 32 + 64 + 160 + 2048 = 2304 bytes**

**Special roles within the register file:**
- **R0**: Implicit operand in MUL instruction
- **R1**: Holds zero flag result; assumed zero by C runtime
- **R26:R27** → X-pointer
- **R28:R29** → Y-pointer
- **R30:R31** → Z-pointer

**Diagram** — Refer to the block diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2.

**Engineering utility:** The fixed, predictable memory map allows compilers to generate highly optimized code. The placement of the 32 general purpose registers at the base enables single-cycle execution of most ALU operations.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Feature | I/O Register Space | Extended I/O Register Space |
|---|---|---|
| Address Range | `0x0020` – `0x005F` | `0x0060` – `0x00FF` |
| Size | 64 bytes | 160 bytes |
| Access Instructions | `IN`, `OUT`, `SBI`, `CBI`, `SBIC`, `SBIS` | `LD`, `ST`, `LDS`, `STS` (only) |
| Address Encoding | 6-bit I/O offset | 16-bit full data address |
| Execution Cycles | 1 cycle | 2 cycles |
| Typical Registers | PORTA, DDRA, PINA, PORTB, etc. | ADCH, ADCL, UDR, TCCR1A, SPDR, etc. |

**Example for I/O Register Space (PORTB access):**
```assembly
    SBI  PORTB, 3      ; Set bit 3 of PORTB (I/O offset = 0x05)
    IN   R16, PINB     ; Read PINB into R16 (I/O offset = 0x03)
```

**Example for Extended I/O Register Space (ADC Data Register):**
```assembly
    LDS  R16, 0x0078   ; Load ADCL (data address 0x0078)
    STS  0x0079, R17   ; Store to ADCH (data address 0x0079)
```

**Why the difference?** The `IN`/`OUT` instructions use a compact 6-bit I/O offset embedded in the 16-bit opcode, saving program memory. However, the AVR instruction set only encodes 6 bits for the I/O address, limiting those instructions to the first 64 I/O locations. Extended I/O registers (beyond offset `0x3F`) must be accessed using full 16-bit addresses via `LD`/`ST`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **X, Y, and Z pointer registers** in AVR. Show how a 16-bit address is formed from the R26:R27 pair, and write an AVR assembly code snippet to read 10 bytes from SRAM starting at address `0x0200` using indirect addressing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of the stack pointer (SP)** in AVR data memory. How is the stack initialized in C and assembly? What happens when the stack overflows the internal SRAM boundary? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**X, Y, Z Pointer Concept:**

The 16-bit pointer registers X, Y, and Z are formed by concatenating pairs of general purpose registers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= R26 \cup (R27 \ll 8) \\
Y &= R28 \cup (R29 \ll 8) \\
Z &= R30 \cup (R31 \ll 8)
\end{aligned}
$$

These pointers enable **indirect addressing** — the instruction operand is the pointer, not the actual data address. This allows dynamic memory access using loop counters.

**Assembly Code to Read 10 Bytes from SRAM Starting at `0x0200`:**

```assembly
.CPI    LDI   R26, 0x00     ; Load low byte of X-pointer (0x0200 low)
        LDI   R27, 0x02     ; Load high byte of X-pointer (0x0200 high)
                            ; X = 0x0200
        LDI   R20, 10       ; Loop counter = 10

LOOP:  LD    R16, X+        ; Load byte from [X], then X = X + 1
        ; ... process R16 ...
        DEC   R20
        BRNE  LOOP
```

**How it works:**
- Initially, `X = 0x0200`.
- Each `LD R16, X+` loads the byte at address X into R16, **then auto-increments** X by 1.
- After 10 iterations, the loop exits, having read bytes at addresses `0x0200` through `0x0209`.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Role of the Stack Pointer (SP):**

The Stack Pointer is a **16-bit special function register** (split into SPH and SPL) that always points to the **top of the stack** in data memory. The stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure used for:

- Saving the return address during `CALL` and `RCALL` instructions
- Saving registers during interrupts (by the hardware)
- Storing local variables in C functions
- Saving the program counter during context switches

**Stack Initialization in C (using AVR-GCC):**

The C startup code (in `crt1.S` or `crtatmega32.o`) automatically initializes the stack pointer to **RAMEND** (the highest SRAM address):

```c
#include <avr/io.h>

void main(void) {
    // SP is automatically set to RAMEND = 0x085F for ATmega32
    // by the C runtime startup code
    while(1) { }
}
```

**Stack Initialization in Assembly:**

```assembly
    LDI   R16, HIGH(RAMEND)   ; 0x08 for ATmega32
    OUT   SPH, R16
    LDI   R16, LOW(RAMEND)    ; 0x5F for ATmega32
    OUT   SPL, R16
```

**Stack Overflow Scenario:**

The stack grows **downward** in AVR (PUSH decrements SP, POP increments SP). If the stack overflows the internal SRAM boundary (i.e., SP becomes less than `0x0100`), it will corrupt:

1. The I/O Register Space (`0x0020`–`0x005F`)
2. The General Purpose Register File (`0x0000`–`0x001F`)

**Consequences:**
- Corrupted I/O register values cause **unpredictable peripheral behavior**.
- Corrupted R0–R31 cause **incorrect ALU operations**.
- The CPU may execute a `BRANCH` to a wild address, causing a **system crash or watchdog reset**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing I/O offset with data address**: Students often write `SBI PORTB, 3` as `SBI 0x25, 3`. This is **wrong** — `SBI` requires the I/O offset (`0x05` for PORTB), not the data address. The assembler will give an "I/O address out of range" error. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting that R1 must be zero**: In C programs compiled by AVR-GCC, R1 is assumed to be zero. If you write assembly that uses R1 without clearing it, the compiler-generated code that follows may produce incorrect results. (Lose 1–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusing internal SRAM start address**: Many students write `0x0060` as the start of SRAM. The correct first user-accessible SRAM address is `0x0100`. Addresses `0x0060`–`0x00FF` are the **Extended I/O Registers**, not SRAM. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 4. **Stack pointer initialization in assembly**: When writing pure assembly programs, students often forget to initialize SP explicitly. Without initialization, SP is `0x0000` after reset, and the first `PUSH` will overwrite R0 — leading to silent data corruption. (Lose 2 marks)
>
> 5. **Mixing up X, Y, Z-pointer usages**: Z-pointer is the **only** pointer that can be used with `LPM` (Load Program Memory). Trying to use X or Y with `LPM` results in an assembler error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AVR Data Memory**

- [x] The AVR data memory is **linear, byte-addressable**, up to **64 KB**.
- [x] **Five regions**: Register File (32B), I/O (64B), Ext I/O (160B), Internal SRAM, External SRAM.
- [x] Register file occupies `0x0000`–`0x001F` (R0–R31).
- [x] **I/O offset = Data address − 0x0020**.
- [x] I/O registers use `IN`/`OUT`/`SBI`/`CBI` (1 cycle); Extended I/O uses `LD`/`ST` (2 cycles).
- [x] **X = R26:R27**, **Y = R28:R29**, **Z = R30:R31**.
- [x] **Z-pointer is unique** — used by `LPM` to read Flash.
- [x] Internal SRAM for ATmega32 = **2 KB**, from `0x0100` to `0x08FF`.
- [x] RAMEND for ATmega32 = `0x085F`.
- [x] Stack grows **downward**; SP must be initialized in pure assembly.
- [x] **R1 must be zero** for AVR-GCC compatibility.
- [x] External SRAM is only available on ATmega128/2560/1280 class devices.
- [x] EEPROM is **not** part of data memory — it is a separate non-volatile space accessed via I/O registers (`EEAR`, `EEDR`, `EECR`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Instructions with Data Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Instructions with Data Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Using Instructions with Data Memory

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Memory in a Microcontroller** is a volatile, read/write memory (RAM) that holds the *runtime variables*, *intermediate results*, *stack data*, and the *Special Function Registers (SFRs)* used to configure and control the internal peripherals of the microcontroller. In the **PIC16F family**, data memory is partitioned into multiple **banks**, and each instruction must explicitly or implicitly select the correct bank before any register can be read from or written to.

In the **Harvard architecture** of the PIC microcontroller, the **Data Memory (RAM)** and **Program Memory (ROM/Flash)** are physically separate. This separation allows the CPU to fetch an instruction from program memory *and* read/write data memory in the *same machine cycle* — a feature known as **single-cycle operation**.

The total data memory in a typical mid-range PIC (e.g., **PIC16F877A**) is **368 bytes**, divided into:
* **General Purpose Registers (GPR)** — user-accessible RAM for variables.
* **Special Function Registers (SFR)** — control and status registers for peripherals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Post Office P.O. Box System**
>
> Imagine the data memory of a PIC microcontroller as a **huge post office with thousands of P.O. boxes**. Each box has a unique number (its **address**) and contains a paper (its **data value**).
>
> However, to keep the boxes manageable, the post office is divided into **4 floors (banks)**. The receptionist can only show you boxes on **one floor at a time**. To switch floors, you must give a special instruction using the **RP0** and **RP1** switches on the receptionist's desk (bits of the **STATUS register**).
>
> Most personal mail (your variables) sits in **GPR boxes on the ground floor**, while the **administrative offices (SFRs)** are spread across all four floors. Before opening an administrative file (an SFR like PORTB or TRISB), you must first instruct the receptionist to go to the correct floor.

### 1.3 Why Data Memory Organization Matters

The **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) architecture of PIC microcontrollers uses **single-word (14-bit) instructions**. This small instruction size can only embed a **7-bit direct address**. Therefore, the data memory is split into banks of **128 bytes each** so that **7 bits are sufficient to address any byte within a bank**, while the upper bits of the address come from the **bank-select bits (RP0, RP1)** held in the STATUS register.

### 1.4 Physical Metrics of the Data Memory (PIC16F877A)

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Total RAM size** | **368 bytes** |
| **Number of Banks** | **4 (Bank 0, 1, 2, 3)** |
| **Bytes per Bank** | **128 bytes** |
| **Addressable offset per bank** | **00h – 7Fh** (0–127 decimal) |
| **GPR size (PIC16F877A)** | **256 bytes** (B0–B6Fh region) |
| **SFR common to all banks** | Last 16 bytes of every bank (70h–7Fh) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight — Mirrored SFRs:** The **SFR region (70h–7Fh) is mirrored in all four banks**. This means a register like the **STATUS register (address 03h, 83h, 103h, 183h)** is actually accessible at multiple addresses, but the *physical register* is unique. This mirroring is provided as a programmer convenience to minimize bank switching.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of PIC Data Memory

The PIC data memory is organized as **two major regions**:

### Region 1 — Special Function Registers (SFRs)
* Located at the **upper portion of each bank** (typically addresses 00h–1Fh, plus the mirrored area 70h–7Fh).
* Each SFR controls a specific hardware peripheral: ports, timers, ADCs, etc.
* **Accessing the wrong bank** when working with SFRs is one of the most common sources of bugs in PIC programming.

### Region 2 — General Purpose Registers (GPRs)
* Located in the **lower portion of banks** (20h–6Fh, 0A0h–0EFh, etc.).
* Used for **user variables, scratchpad, and temporary storage**.
* The C compiler (XC8) and the PIC assembler (MPASM) map variables to GPRs by default.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4 Banks?**
> Because the PIC instruction format contains only **7 bits for the direct file register address**, the maximum directly addressable range per instruction is **128 bytes** ($2^7 = 128$). The **bank-select bits (RP1:RP0)** in the STATUS register extend the addressable range to **$2^9 = 512$ bytes** theoretically, though only 368 are physically implemented in the PIC16F877A.

## 2.2 The STATUS Register — The Master Control Key

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **STATUS register (03h)** is the most important SFR in the entire PIC architecture. It is the *only* register whose bits directly control CPU behavior. The two least-significant bits **RP0 and RP1** are dedicated to **bank selection**.

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Bit 7** | **IRP** | Register Bank Select bit (used for **indirect addressing**, selects Bank 2/3) |
| Bit 6–5 | (Reserved) | Read as 0 |
| **Bit 4** | **$\overline{TO}$** | Time-out bit (WDT overflow flag) |
| **Bit 3** | **$\overline{PD}$** | Power-down bit |
| **Bit 2** | **Z** | Zero flag (result of last arithmetic/logic operation) |
| **Bit 1** | **DC** | Digit carry / half-carry flag (for BCD arithmetic) |
| **Bit 0** | **C** | Carry flag (for multi-byte arithmetic) |
| **Bit 5** | **RP0** | **Register Bank Select bit 0** |
| **Bit 6** | **RP1** | **Register Bank Select bit 1** |

### Bank Selection Rules (Direct Addressing)

| RP1 | RP0 | Selected Bank | Address Range |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **0** | **0** | **Bank 0** | 00h – 7Fh |
| **0** | **1** | **Bank 1** | 80h – FFh |
| **1** | **0** | **Bank 2** | 100h – 17Fh |
| **1** | **1** | **Bank 3** | 180h – 1FFh |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas below are fundamental for any KTU calculation problem on data memory access.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Bytes per bank** | $2^7 = 128$ bytes | 7-bit direct address |
| **Address in physical RAM** | $A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset}$ | $A_{offset} \in [0, 127]$ |
| **Indirect addressing (FSR)** | $A_{physical} = (IRP \cdot 2 + \text{bit}_6) \cdot 128 + \text{FSR}_{6:0}$ | Uses FSR and INDF |
| **INDF access** | $A_{FSR} = \text{FSR register contents}$ | INDF is a *virtual* register |
| **Program memory (PCLATH paging)** | $A_{program} = (PCLATH_{4:3}) \cdot 2048 + PC_{10:0}$ | For CALL/GOTO across pages |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mastering data memory access is **prerequisite** for every embedded systems project involving PIC microcontrollers: industrial automation (PLCs), automotive ECUs, medical instrumentation, consumer electronics (washing machines, microwave ovens), and IoT sensor nodes. Without proper bank management, peripheral registers cannot be configured correctly, and the program will silently malfunction.

## 2.5 The Two Addressing Modes for Data Memory

### (A) Direct Addressing
* The 7-bit file address is **embedded directly inside the instruction opcode**.
* The bank is selected by the **RP1:RP0 bits of the STATUS register** *before* the instruction is executed.
* **Code overhead:** Almost every SFR access in Bank 1 or Bank 3 requires a `BSF` (Bit Set File) or `BCF` (Bit Clear File) instruction on STATUS to switch banks.

**Example:**
```assembly
BSF   STATUS, RP0     ; Switch to Bank 1
MOVLW 0x00
MOVWF TRISB           ; Set PORTB as output (TRISB is in Bank 1)
BCF   STATUS, RP0     ; Switch back to Bank 0
MOVLW 0x55
MOVWF PORTB           ; Write to PORTB (PORTB is in Bank 0)
```

### (B) Indirect Addressing
* The PIC has **one true indirect addressing register**: the **INDF** (location 00h in any bank).
* The actual target address is held in the **FSR (File Select Register)** at address 04h (Bank 0) or 104h (Bank 1).
* The **IRP bit** (bit 7 of STATUS) selects between Bank 0/1 and Bank 2/3 for indirect access.
* Reading or writing INDF actually accesses the register whose address is in FSR.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Genius of INDF:** INDF is **not a physical register**. It is a *placeholder*. When the CPU sees an instruction targeting address 00h, it does not fetch any data from address 00h. Instead, it uses the FSR value as the effective address. This is the same idea as a **pointer dereference** in C (`*ptr`).

**Example — Clearing RAM locations 20h–7Fh using indirect addressing:**
```assembly
MOVLW 0x20
MOVWF FSR            ; Initialize pointer to 20h
NEXT:
    CLRF INDF        ; Clear the register pointed to by FSR
    INCF FSR, F      ; FSR = FSR + 1
    BTFSS FSR, 7     ; Is bit 7 of FSR set? (i.e., did we reach 80h?)
    GOTO  NEXT        ; If not, continue
CONTINUE:
    ; ... program continues
```

This technique is **highly efficient** — it can clear all 96 GPRs in just **5 instructions**, demonstrating the power of indirect addressing in embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bank-Switching Address Equation

### Step 1: Recognize the addressable width of the PIC instruction

A PIC mid-range instruction is **14 bits wide**. Of these:
* **6 bits** are dedicated to the opcode.
* **1 bit** is for destination selection.
* **1 bit** is sometimes used for literal/relocatable operations.
* This leaves **7 bits for the file register address** in a direct addressing instruction.

### Step 2: Compute the maximum range with 7 bits

$$ N_{bytes} = 2^{7} = 128 \text{ bytes} $$

This means a single instruction can directly address only **128 registers**. This is insufficient for a microcontroller that may have **hundreds of registers** (the PIC16F877A has 368).

### Step 3: Introduce the bank-select mechanism

To overcome the 128-byte limit, PIC designers added **two external bank-select bits (RP0, RP1)** in the STATUS register. These bits act as *upper address bits* that prepend to the 7-bit offset.

$$ A_{offset} = b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 \quad (7\text{ bits}) $$

$$ B = b_8 b_7 \quad (\text{two bits from STATUS} = RP1:RP0) $$

### Step 4: Combine to form the full physical address

$$ A_{physical} = B \cdot 128 + A_{offset} $$

### Step 5: Substitute the binary weights

$$ A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset} $$

where $A_{offset} \in [0, 127]$.

### Worked Example — Locating TRISB

* TRISB is documented at file address **86h** in the datasheet.
* Convert 86h to binary: $86h = 1000\,0110b = 134_{10}$.
* The most significant bit (bit 7) of 134 is **1**, meaning $A_{offset} \ge 128$. Therefore **TRISB is in Bank 1**.
* Compute the offset: $134 - 128 = 6$, so $A_{offset} = 06h$.

**Verification using the formula:**

$$ A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + 06 $$

For Bank 1: $RP1 = 0$, $RP0 = 1$

$$ A_{physical} = (0 \cdot 2 + 1) \cdot 128 + 6 = 128 + 6 = 134 = 86h \;\checkmark $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The KTU board often asks: *"Given the bank-select bits and the offset, compute the physical address."* Use the formula $A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Indirect Addressing Using FSR/INDF

**Problem:** Write a code sequence to copy the contents of RAM locations 30h–3Fh to 50h–5Fh using indirect addressing.

### Step 1: Initialize the source pointer (FSR) to 30h

```assembly
MOVLW  0x30
MOVWF  FSR           ; FSR now points to RAM address 30h
```

### Step 2: Read from the source location

Reading from INDF reads the contents of the location pointed to by FSR (i.e., 30h):

```assembly
MOVF   INDF, W       ; W = contents of [FSR] = contents of 30h
```

### Step 3: Write to the destination

Since the destination is also in Bank 0/1, we can directly write to the literal address:

```assembly
MOVWF  0x50          ; RAM[50h] = W
```

### Step 4: Increment FSR and the destination pointer

```assembly
INCF   FSR, F        ; FSR = FSR + 1 (now 31h)
INCF   0x50, F       ; 0x50 becomes 0x51
```

### Step 5: Loop until all 16 bytes are transferred

```assembly
BTFSS  FSR, 4        ; Check if FSR has reached 40h
GOTO   LOOP
DONE:
    ; ... code continues
```

### Step 6: Final Address Verification

After 16 iterations, the FSR will have advanced from 30h → 3Fh → 40h. The condition `FSR[4] = 1` (bit 4 set) detects the transition at 40h.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SFR Locations in PIC16F877A

| SFR Name | Bank 0 | Bank 1 | Bank 2 | Bank 3 | Function |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **INDF** | 00h | 80h | 100h | 180h | Indirect addressing pointer target |
| **TMR0** | 01h | 81h | 101h | 181h | Timer0 counter register |
| **PCL** | 02h | 82h | 102h | 182h | Program Counter low byte |
| **STATUS** | 03h | 83h | 103h | 183h | ALU flags & bank select |
| **FSR** | 04h | 84h | 104h | 184h | Indirect addressing pointer |
| **PORTA** | 05h | — | — | — | Port A data latch |
| **PORTB** | 06h | — | — | — | Port B data latch |
| **PORTC** | 07h | — | — | — | Port C data latch |
| **PORTD** | 08h | — | — | — | Port D data latch |
| **PORTE** | 09h | — | — | — | Port E data latch |
| **PCLATH** | 0Ah | 8Ah | 10Ah | 18Ah | Program Counter high latch |
| **INTCON** | 0Bh | 8Bh | 10Bh | 18Bh | Interrupt control register |
| **TRISA** | — | 85h | — | — | Port A direction register |
| **TRISB** | — | 86h | — | — | Port B direction register |
| **TRISC** | — | 87h | — | — | Port C direction register |
| **ADCON0** | 1Fh | 9Fh | — | — | ADC control register 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** Most **TRISx** (data direction) registers reside in **Bank 1**, while the corresponding **PORTx** (data latch) registers reside in **Bank 0**. The most common bug in beginner PIC code is forgetting to switch back to Bank 0 before writing to PORTB after configuring TRISB in Bank 1.

## 3.4 Program Memory Paging and the PCLATH Register

For program memory, the PIC16F877A has **8K × 14 bits** of Flash, organized into **4 pages of 2K each**. The Program Counter is 13 bits wide, but the direct branch instructions (CALL, GOTO) only embed 11 bits, so they can only reach within a 2K page.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PCLATH trick:** Before any CALL or GOTO instruction that crosses page boundaries, the programmer must preload **PCLATH[4:3]** with the correct page number. Failing to do this results in a runtime crash — the program jumps to the wrong memory location.

$$ A_{program} = (PCLATH[4:3]) \cdot 2048 + (PC_{10:0]) $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Memory Map of PIC16F877A (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bank3["BANK 3 — Address 180h to 1FFh"]
        B3SFR["SFR 180h to 1FFh\n(Indirect Addressing)"]
        B3GPR["GPR Unimplemented\nMost addresses return 0"]
    end

    subgraph Bank2["BANK 2 — Address 100h to 17Fh"]
        B2SFR["SFR 100h to 17Fh\n(Indirect Addressing)"]
        B2GPR["GPR 120h to 16Fh\n80 bytes of user RAM"]
    end

    subgraph Bank1["BANK 1 — Address 80h to FFh"]
```
