```mermaid
        B1SFR["SFR 80h to FFh\nTRISA, TRISB, OPTION_REG, etc."]
        B1GPR["GPR 0A0h to 0EFh\n80 bytes of user RAM"]
    end

    subgraph Bank0["BANK 0 — Address 00h to 7Fh"]
        B0SFR["SFR 00h to 1Fh\nPORTA, PORTB, STATUS, FSR, etc."]
        B0GPR["GPR 20h to 6Fh\n80 bytes of user RAM"]
    end

    StatusReg["STATUS REGISTER (03h/83h/103h/183h)\nBit 5: RP0\nBit 6: RP1"]

    StatusReg -->|RP0=0, RP1=0| Bank0
    StatusReg -->|RP0=1, RP1=0| Bank1
    StatusReg -->|RP0=0, RP1=1| Bank2
    StatusReg -->|RP0=1, RP1=1| Bank3

    classDef bankStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sfrStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef gprStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px

    class Bank0,Bank1,Bank2,Bank3 bankStyle
    class B0SFR,B1SFR,B2SFR,B3SFR sfrStyle
    class B0GPR,B1GPR,B2GPR,B3GPR gprStyle
    class StatusReg ctrlStyle
```

## 4.2 Instruction Execution Flow with Data Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1. Instruction Fetch\nfrom Program Memory"] --> B["2. Decode 14-bit Opcode\nExtract 7-bit file address"]
    B --> C{"3. Check Addressing Mode\nDirect or Indirect?"}

    C -->|Direct| D["4A. Read STATUS register\nGet RP1 and RP0 bits"]
    D --> E["5A. Form physical address\nA = (RP1*2+RP0)*128 + offset"]
    E --> F["6A. Access register at\ncomputed address"]
    F --> G["7A. Read or Write\nthe target register"]

    C -->|Indirect| H["4B. Read FSR register\nGet full 8/9-bit pointer"]
    H --> I["5B. Read IRP bit of STATUS\nfor upper bank bit"]
    I --> J["6B. Form physical address\nA = (IRP*2+bit6)*128 + FSR[6:0]"]
    J --> F

    G --> K["8. Result returned to W register\nor stored in target register"]
    K --> L["9. Next instruction cycle"]

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef decStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef accStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef endStyle fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:1.5px

    class A,B stepStyle
    class C decStyle
    class D,E,F,G,H,I,J accStyle
    class K,L endStyle
```

## 4.3 Bank Switching State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Bank0: Power-On Reset\nRP1=0, RP0=0

    Bank0: BANK 0\n00h to 7Fh\nPORTA, PORTB, STATUS, FSR

    Bank1: BANK 1\n80h to FFh\nTRISA, TRISB, OPTION_REG, ADCON1

    Bank2: BANK 2\n100h to 17Fh\nUser RAM area for\nindirect addressing

    Bank3: BANK 3\n180h to 1FFh\nUnimplemented in\nPIC16F877A

    Bank0 --> Bank1: BSF STATUS, RP0
    Bank1 --> Bank0: BCF STATUS, RP0
    Bank0 --> Bank2: BSF STATUS, RP1
    Bank2 --> Bank0: BCF STATUS, RP1
    Bank1 --> Bank3: BSF STATUS, RP1
    Bank3 --> Bank1: BCF STATUS, RP1
    Bank2 --> Bank3: BSF STATUS, RP0
    Bank3 --> Bank2: BCF STATUS, RP0

    note right of Bank0
        All arithmetic and most
        logic operations work
        on registers in Bank 0
    end note

    note right of Bank1
        All TRISx direction
        registers are here.
        Always switch to Bank 1
        BEFORE configuring I/O
    end note
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the data memory organization of a PIC16F microcontroller. What is the role of the STATUS register in this organization? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The data memory of a PIC16F microcontroller is **volatile RAM** partitioned into **multiple banks** (typically 4 banks of **128 bytes** each). It contains two distinct regions:
* **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** — used by the programmer to store variables and temporary data.
* **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — control the peripherals (ports, timers, ADC, etc.) and the CPU itself (STATUS, PCLATH, INTCON).

The **STATUS register** is the most important SFR. Its two lower bits, **RP0 and RP1**, act as **bank-select bits** for direct addressing. The remaining bits carry CPU status flags: **Z** (zero), **DC** (digit carry), **C** (carry), $\overline{\text{TO}}$ (time-out), and $\overline{\text{PD}}$ (power-down). The STATUS register is the only register that participates in the bank-selection mechanism for data memory.

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between direct and indirect addressing in the PIC microcontroller? **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Addressing | Indirect Addressing |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Address source** | Embedded in the instruction itself (7-bit field) | Held in the **FSR** register |
| **Bank selection** | **RP0, RP1** bits of STATUS | **IRP** bit of STATUS + bit 6 of FSR |
| **Number of registers addressed** | One specific register per instruction | One register at a time, but **FSR can be modified** to access many registers in a loop |
| **Code efficiency** | Requires explicit `BSF`/`BCF` on STATUS to switch banks | FSR can be incremented — ideal for table lookups and array processing |
| **Special register** | None | **INDF (00h)** — virtual register, not a real physical storage |

Direct addressing is used for **fixed, single-register access**; indirect addressing is used for **sequential access, array manipulation, and table lookups** in loops.

---

## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Data Memory Access in PIC16F **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the structure of the data memory of a PIC16F877A microcontroller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the STATUS register in bank selection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Data Memory Structure:**
The PIC16F877A contains **368 bytes** of data memory, organized as **4 banks (Bank 0–3)**. Each bank is **128 bytes** wide. The lower portion of each bank is reserved for **SFRs**, and the upper portion holds **GPRs**.

* **Bank 0** (00h–7Fh): Contains core SFRs (STATUS, PORTA, PORTB, PORTC, etc.) and 80 bytes of GPR (20h–6Fh).
* **Bank 1** (80h–FFh): Contains TRISA, TRISB, TRISC, OPTION_REG, and 80 bytes of GPR (A0h–EFh).
* **Bank 2** (100h–17Fh): Contains 80 bytes of GPR and a few SFRs used for indirect addressing.
* **Bank 3** (180h–1FFh): Contains the upper 16 bytes of SFR mirroring for indirect addressing; most GPRs are unimplemented.

The **upper 16 bytes (70h–7Fh) of every bank are mirrored** — they physically refer to the same SFRs, providing programmer convenience and reducing bank switches.

**2. STATUS Register and Bank Selection:**

The **STATUS register at address 03h** contains the **RP0 (bit 5)** and **RP1 (bit 6)** bits. These two bits form a 2-bit binary number that selects which of the **4 banks** is currently active for direct addressing.

| RP1 | RP0 | Bank |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 |

**Block Diagram:**

```
                +-------------------+
                |   STATUS REGISTER |
                |   (Address 03h)   |
                +-------------------+
                | Bit 7:  IRP       |  -> Indirect Bank Select
                | Bit 6:  RP1       |  --.
                | Bit 5:  RP0       |  --+--> BANK SELECT
                | Bit 4:  TO_bar    |  |
                | Bit 3:  PD_bar    |  |
                | Bit 2:  Z         |  |  CPU FLAGS
                | Bit 1:  DC        |  |
                | Bit 0:  C         |  |
                +-------------------+

   RP1:RP0 = 00  --> BANK 0 (00h - 7Fh)
   RP1:RP0 = 01  --> BANK 1 (80h - FFh)
   RP1:RP0 = 10  --> BANK 2 (100h - 17Fh)
   RP1:RP0 = 11  --> BANK 3 (180h - 1FFh)
```

**Example — Configuring PORTB as output:**
```assembly
BSF   STATUS, RP0    ; Select Bank 1 (TRISB is at 86h)
CLRF  TRISB          ; All bits of PORTB = output
BCF   STATUS, RP0    ; Return to Bank 0
MOVLW 0x55
MOVWF PORTB          ; Write 0x55 to PORTB
```

---

#### Part (b) — Explain direct and indirect addressing modes of PIC16F with suitable assembly code examples. How is the FSR and INDF mechanism used for indirect addressing? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Direct Addressing:**
In direct addressing, the **7-bit file register address** is embedded inside the instruction itself. The bank is selected externally by the **RP1:RP0 bits** of the STATUS register. This is the most commonly used addressing mode for single-register access.

**Example — Add two numbers stored in registers 0x20 and 0x21:**
```assembly
MOVF   0x20, W       ; W = RAM[0x20]
ADDWF  0x21, W       ; W = W + RAM[0x21]
MOVWF  0x22          ; Store result in RAM[0x22]
```

**Limitation:** To access a register in another bank, the programmer must first modify the STATUS register.

**2. Indirect Addressing:**
In indirect addressing, the target address is **not embedded in the instruction**; instead, it is held in the **FSR (File Select Register)** at address 04h. The instruction then reads or writes to the **INDF register (00h)**, which is a *virtual* register — accessing INDF tells the CPU to use the FSR value as the actual address.

**Components of indirect addressing:**
* **FSR (04h):** Holds the 8-bit effective address (0–255). The lower 7 bits address within a bank; bit 6 plus the **IRP bit** of STATUS determine the bank.
* **INDF (00h):** A *non-physical* register. Any read/write to INDF actually accesses the location pointed to by FSR.
* **IRP bit (STATUS bit 7):** Selects between Bank 0/1 (IRP=0) and Bank 2/3 (IRP=1) for indirect access.

**Example — Clearing RAM block 20h–7Fh using indirect addressing:**
```assembly
        MOVLW  0x20       ; Start address
        MOVWF  FSR         ; Initialize pointer
LOOP:   CLRF   INDF        ; Clear RAM[FSR]
        INCF   FSR, F      ; FSR = FSR + 1
        BTFSS  FSR, 7      ; Did FSR reach 80h?
        GOTO   LOOP        ; If not, continue clearing
CONT:   ; ... rest of program
```

**Why INDF is "magic":**
The INDF location is shared by all four banks at the same offset (00h, 80h, 100h, 180h). The CPU treats an access to address 00h as a *command*: "go look at FSR, take that value as the real address, and perform the operation there." This pointer-dereference behavior is identical to `*ptr` in C.

**Summary Table:**

| Aspect | Direct | Indirect |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Address source | In instruction | In FSR |
| Bank select | RP0, RP1 in STATUS | IRP + FSR[6] |
| Special register | None | INDF (virtual) |
| Best for | Single register access | Arrays, tables, loops |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Indirect Addressing and FSR/INDF Mechanism **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### Part (a) — With a neat diagram, explain the data memory of PIC16F877A. How is bank selection done using the STATUS register? List the SFRs of Bank 0 and Bank 1. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Data Memory Diagram of PIC16F877A:**

```
  +----------------------+  1FFh  |  BANK 3  |  SFRs (80h-FFh mirror)|
  +----------------------+  17Fh  |  BANK 2  |  GPRs + some SFRs      |
  +----------------------+  0FFh  |  BANK 1  |  TRISx, OPTION_REG,    |
  |                      |        |          |  ADCON1, etc.          |
  |        RAM           |  07Fh  |  BANK 0  |  PORTS, STATUS, FSR,   |
  |       (368 B)        |        |          |  TMR0, INTCON, etc.    |
  +----------------------+  000h  +----------+------------------------+
```

**Bank Selection via STATUS Register:**

| RP1 | RP0 | Active Bank | Address Range |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00h – 7Fh |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 80h – FFh |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 100h – 17Fh |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 180h – 1FFh |

**Important SFRs of Bank 0:**

| Address | Register | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 00h | INDF | Indirect addressing pointer |
| 01h | TMR0 | Timer 0 counter |
| 02h | PCL | PC low byte |
| 03h | STATUS | CPU flags + bank select |
| 04h | FSR | Indirect address pointer |
| 05h | PORTA | Port A data |
| 06h | PORTB | Port B data |
| 07h | PORTC | Port C data |
| 0Ah | PCLATH | PC high latch |
| 0Bh | INTCON | Interrupt control |

**Important SFRs of Bank 1:**

| Address | Register | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 81h | OPTION_REG | Timer0, pull-ups, prescaler |
| 85h | TRISA | Port A direction |
| 86h | TRISB | Port B direction |
| 87h | TRISC | Port C direction |
| 8Ch | PIE1 | Peripheral interrupt enable 1 |
| 8Dh | PIR1 | Peripheral interrupt request 1 |
| 9Fh | ADCON1 | ADC configuration |

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the FSR and INDF registers with an example program. Show how they enable indirect addressing in PIC microcontrollers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The FSR Register (04h):**
The **File Select Register** is an **8-bit register** that holds the address of the data memory location to be accessed indirectly. Its lower **7 bits** point to one of the 128 bytes in the current bank, and **bit 6** acts as the upper bank-select bit (along with the **IRP** bit of STATUS).

**The INDF Register (00h):**
**INDF** is **not a physical register**; it is a placeholder used by the CPU to trigger indirect addressing. When the CPU executes an instruction that targets address 00h, it does the following:
1. Reads the contents of **FSR**.
2. Uses the FSR value as the **effective address**.
3. Performs the read or write operation on the location pointed to by FSR.

**Example — Copying a block of 10 bytes from 30h–39h to 50h–59h:**

```assembly
        MOVLW  0x30
        MOVWF  FSR          ; FSR = 0x30 (source pointer)
        MOVLW  0x50
        MOVWF  0x50         ; Use a separate pointer or fixed address

COPY:   MOVF   INDF, W      ; W = RAM[FSR]  (read source)
        MOVWF  0x50         ; RAM[0x50] = W  (write to dest)
        INCF   FSR, F       ; FSR = FSR + 1
        INCF   0x50, F      ; Destination pointer increment
        BTFSS  0x50, 4      ; Did we reach 60h? (16 bytes done)
        GOTO   COPY
DONE:   ; ...
```

**Key Advantages of FSR/INDF:**
* **Compact code:** Arrays of any size can be processed in 4–5 instructions.
* **No bank switching overhead:** IRP and FSR bit 6 automatically handle bank selection.
* **Hardware pointer arithmetic:** Incrementing FSR is a single-cycle operation.

**Restrictions:**
* FSR is only **8 bits wide**, so it cannot directly address beyond 256 bytes in indirect mode.
* INDF itself cannot be the destination of a `MOVWF` operation that affects FSR in the same cycle (read-modify-write hazard).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the Board Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting to switch back to Bank 0** after configuring TRIS registers. Code: `BSF STATUS, RP0` ... configure TRIS ... `BCF STATUS, RP0`. Missing the second instruction is a **2–3 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing SFR mirroring with real bank switching.** Registers at 70h–7Fh are mirrored — they refer to the *same physical register* in every bank. A student who thinks `MOVWF 0x03` and `MOVWF 0x83` write to *different* STATUS registers will be heavily penalized.
> 3. **Treating INDF as a real register.** INDF has no storage of its own. Writing to INDF actually writes to the register pointed to by FSR. Failing to mention this explicitly costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong formula for physical address.** The correct formula is $A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset}$, **not** $A_{physical} = (RP1 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset}$. The latter is wrong because RP1 has a weight of 2, not 1.
> 5. **Confusing direct and indirect addressing bank bits.** Direct addressing uses **RP0 and RP1**. Indirect addressing uses the **IRP bit** (bit 7 of STATUS) plus **bit 6 of FSR**. Mixing these is a common error.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Using Instructions with Data Memory**

- ✅ **Data memory** in PIC16F is **368 bytes**, split into **4 banks of 128 bytes** each.
- ✅ The **7-bit direct address** in an instruction is the **offset within the currently selected bank**.
- ✅ **Bank selection** is done by the **RP1:RP0 bits** of the **STATUS register (03h)** for direct addressing.
- ✅ **STATUS register** also holds CPU flags: **Z, DC, C, $\overline{\text{TO}}$, $\overline{\text{PD}}$** and the **IRP** bit for indirect addressing.
- ✅ **SFRs** are control/status registers for peripherals. **GPRs** are user RAM.
- ✅ The **upper 16 bytes (70h–7Fh) of every bank are mirrored** SFRs, providing access convenience.
- ✅ **TRISx registers (Bank 1)** control data direction; **PORTx registers (Bank 0)** hold the actual I/O data.
- ✅ **Indirect addressing** uses the **FSR (04h)** register as a pointer and the **INDF (00h)** register as a virtual target.
- ✅ **INDF is not a physical register** — accessing INDF triggers the CPU to use FSR as the effective address.
- ✅ **IRP bit (bit 7 of STATUS) + bit 6 of FSR** select the bank for indirect addressing.
- ✅ Physical address formula: $A_{physical} = (RP1 \cdot 2 + RP0) \cdot 128 + A_{offset}$.
- ✅ **PCLATH[4:3]** must be preloaded for **CALL/GOTO** instructions that cross 2K program-memory page boundaries.
- ✅ **Always switch back to the original bank** after accessing a register in a different bank to avoid corrupting future instructions.
- ✅ The most common PIC bug: **configuring TRISB in Bank 1 but writing to PORTB without returning to Bank 0**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AVR Status Register
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AVR Status Register

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
The **Status Register (SREG)** of the AVR microcontroller is an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR)** that contains crucial status flags (condition codes) representing the outcome of the most recently executed arithmetic logic unit (ALU) operation. It is mapped at the I/O memory address **0x5F** (Memory-mapped address **0x005F**) in the standard ATmega328P / ATmega32 architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Status Register is THE most important flag register in the AVR architecture. Every conditional branch instruction (`BRxx`), signed/unsigned comparison, and interrupt handling routine directly depends on the bits of the SREG. The reset value of SREG is **0x00** (all flags cleared).

### 1.2 Bit Architecture of SREG (8-Bit Mapping)

| Bit Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mnemonic** | **I** | **T** | **H** | **S** | **V** | **N** | **Z** | **C** |
| **Name** | Global IRQ | Bit Copy | Half Carry | Sign | Overflow | Negative | Zero | Carry |
| **Read/Write** | R/W | R/W | R/W | R/W | R/W | R/W | R/W | R/W |
| **Initial Value** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **referee in a football match**. The referee doesn't play the game, but constantly observes every play and raises a colored flag:
- 🟢 **Green (C bit):** "A goal was scored!" (Carry generated)
- 🔴 **Red (V bit):** "The score went invalid!" (Overflow occurred)
- 🟡 **Yellow (Z bit):** "It's a perfect tie — exact zero!" (Result is zero)
- 🔵 **Blue (I bit):** "I'm ON DUTY — listen to the players!" (Interrupts enabled)
- 🟣 **Purple (H bit):** "Halfway mark reached!" (BCD correction needed)

The SREG is exactly this referee — it observes the ALU's output and "raises" the appropriate flag bit so that the next instruction can decide what to do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Without the SREG, the CPU would be "blind". It would not know if the previous subtraction resulted in a borrow, if the result is negative, or if an interrupt should be serviced. The SREG is the **decision-making backbone** of every AVR program.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bit-by-Bit Logical Analysis

#### **Bit 7 — I (Global Interrupt Enable)**
- **Function:** Master switch for all maskable interrupts.
- **Logic:** When `I = 1` → interrupts enabled; `I = 0` → interrupts disabled (masked).
- **Hardware Effect:** Setting this bit activates the interrupt vector mechanism. Cleared automatically by hardware upon entering an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
- **Set/Clear Instructions:** `SEI` (Set I) and `CLI` (Clear I).

#### **Bit 6 — T (Bit Copy Storage)**
- **Function:** Used as a temporary 1-bit storage by the `BLD` (Bit Load) and `BST` (Bit Store) instructions.
- **Use Case:** Atomic single-bit transfer between registers without disturbing other bits.

#### **Bit 5 — H (Half Carry Flag)**
- **Function:** Indicates a carry/borrow from the **low nibble (bit 3 → bit 4)** during arithmetic.
- **Mathematical Condition:** $H = 1$ if carry from bit 3 to bit 4 occurs; else $H = 0$.
- **Primary Application:** Essential for **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic** correction (e.g., `DAA`-like operations in software).

#### **Bit 4 — S (Sign Flag)**
- **Function:** Indicates the sign of a signed operation result.
- **Mathematical Formula:** $S = N \oplus V$ (Exclusive-OR of Negative and Overflow flags).
- **Significance:** Tells the programmer whether the signed result is *truly* negative or merely negative due to overflow.

#### **Bit 3 — V (Two's Complement Overflow Flag)**
- **Function:** Detects overflow in **signed arithmetic** (two's complement).
- **Mathematical Condition:** $V = 1$ if the result of a signed operation falls outside the range $[-2^7, 2^7-1]$ (for 8-bit) or $[-2^{n-1}, 2^{n-1}-1]$ (for n-bit).
- **Detection Rule:** $V = C_7 \oplus C_6$ (where $C_7$ is carry out of MSB and $C_6$ is carry into MSB).

#### **Bit 2 — N (Negative Flag)**
- **Function:** Simply a copy of the **Most Significant Bit (MSB / bit 7)** of the ALU result.
- **Logic:** $N = R_7$ (result bit 7).
- **Indication:** If $N = 1$, the result is treated as negative in signed interpretation.

#### **Bit 1 — Z (Zero Flag)**
- **Function:** Indicates that the ALU result is exactly zero.
- **Mathematical Condition:** $Z = 1$ if all bits of result = 0; else $Z = 0$.
- **Engineering Use:** Direct branch control via `BREQ` (Branch if Equal) and `BRNE` (Branch if Not Equal).

#### **Bit 0 — C (Carry Flag)**
- **Function:** Indicates carry/borrow from the **Most Significant Bit (MSB)** in arithmetic operations.
- **Addition:** $C = 1$ if carry out of bit 7 occurs.
- **Subtraction:** $C = 1$ if borrow is required (i.e., minuend < subtrahend in unsigned subtraction).
- **Shift/Rotate:** Acts as the 9th bit during shifts/rotates.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Flag | Symbol | Trigger Condition (Mathematical) | Trigger Condition (Verbal) | Common Branch Instructions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carry** | $C$ | $C_{out} = 1$ from bit 7 | Unsigned overflow or borrow | `BRCS`, `BRCC` |
| **Zero** | $Z$ | $\sum_{i=0}^{7} R_i = 0$ | Result equals zero | `BREQ`, `BRNE` |
| **Negative** | $N$ | $N = R_7$ | MSB of result is 1 | `BRMI`, `BRPL` |
| **Overflow** | $V$ | $V = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | Signed overflow occurred | `BRVS`, `BRVC` |
| **Sign** | $S$ | $S = N \oplus V$ | True signed result sign | `BRSM`, `BRSH` (in chained) |
| **Half Carry** | $H$ | $C_{out}$ from bit 3 | Carry between nibbles | BCD correction only |
| **IRQ Enable** | $I$ | Software sets via `SEI` | Interrupts globally enabled | `SEI`, `CLI` |
| **Bit Copy** | $T$ | Set by `BST` instruction | Temp storage for single bit | `BST`, `BLD` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Utility:** The SREG is critical in **real-time embedded systems** for: (i) implementing conditional control flow, (ii) error detection in sensor data acquisition, (iii) BCD-to-binary conversions, (iv) safe handling of signed arithmetic in DSP applications, and (v) interrupt-driven task scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Carry Flag (C) in 8-Bit Addition

Let us consider two unsigned 8-bit numbers $A$ and $B$ being added. We want to determine the condition under which the **C flag** is set.

$$
A = \sum_{i=0}^{7} a_i \cdot 2^i, \quad B = \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i \cdot 2^i
$$

**Step 1:** Define the bit-wise addition with carry propagation. The sum at bit position $i$ is:

$$
S_i = a_i \oplus b_i \oplus C_{in,i}
$$

**Step 2:** The carry generated at bit position $i$ is given by:

$$
C_{out,i} = (a_i \cdot b_i) + (a_i \cdot C_{in,i}) + (b_i \cdot C_{in,i})
$$

**Step 3:** The C flag of the SREG is the carry-out from the most significant bit (bit 7). Hence:

$$
C = C_{out,7} = (a_7 \cdot b_7) + (a_7 \cdot C_{in,7}) + (b_7 \cdot C_{in,7})
$$

**Step 4:** If we cascade the carry from bit 0 onwards, the final condition simplifies. For maximum overflow:

$$
\text{If } A + B \geq 256 \text{ (i.e., } 2^8\text{), then } C = 1
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Overflow Flag (V) for Signed Arithmetic

The V flag detects situations where the **signed interpretation** of a result is invalid.

**Step 1:** Let $a_7$ and $b_7$ be the MSBs of operands $A$ and $B$, and $r_7$ the MSB of the result $R = A + B$ (signed view).

**Step 2:** Overflow occurs only when adding two numbers of the **same sign produces a result of opposite sign**.

$$
V = (a_7 \cdot b_7 \cdot \overline{r_7}) + (\overline{a_7} \cdot \overline{b_7} \cdot r_7)
$$

**Step 3:** This is equivalent to the XOR of the carry into MSB and the carry out of MSB:

$$
V = C_{in,7} \oplus C_{out,7}
$$

**Step 4:** For subtraction $A - B$, overflow occurs when:

$$
V = (a_7 \cdot \overline{b_7} \cdot \overline{r_7}) + (\overline{a_7} \cdot b_7 \cdot r_7)
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Sign Flag (S)

The S flag provides the **true sign** of a signed result. Since $N$ reports the MSB (which may be misleading after overflow), and $V$ reports the overflow:

$$
S = N \oplus V
$$

**Truth Table Proof:**

| $N$ | $V$ | $S = N \oplus V$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Positive result, no overflow |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Apparent positive but overflow detected → actually negative |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Negative result, no overflow (truly negative) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Apparent negative but overflow → actually positive |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Half Carry Flag (H)

For BCD arithmetic, the H flag signals a carry from the lower nibble to the upper nibble.

$$
H = C_{out,3}
$$

If the lower nibble sum exceeds $15$ (i.e., $0x0F$), then $H = 1$ and a BCD correction (adding $0x06$) is required.

### 3.5 Worked Example: `ADD R16, R17`

Suppose $R16 = 0x3F$ and $R17 = 0x2A$. Predict all SREG flags.

**Step 1:** Compute the sum:
$$
0x3F + 0x2A = 0x69 = 0110\,1001_2
$$

**Step 2:** No carry out of bit 7, so $C = 0$.

**Step 3:** Result is $0x69 \neq 0$, so $Z = 0$.

**Step 4:** MSB of result is 0, so $N = 0$.

**Step 5:** Check signed overflow: $63 + 42 = 105$, no overflow, so $V = 0$.

**Step 6:** Sign flag: $S = N \oplus V = 0$.

**Step 7:** Lower nibble: $0xF + 0xA = 0x19$, carry out of bit 3 exists, so $H = 1$.

**Final SREG: `0b00100000` (only H flag set) = `0x20`.**

### 3.6 Worked Example: Signed Overflow

Let $R16 = 0x70$ (signed: $+112$) and $R17 = 0x60$ (signed: $+96$). Add them.

**Step 1:** Sum: $0x70 + 0x60 = 0xD0 = 1101\,0000_2$.

**Step 2:** Carry out of bit 7 = 0, so $C = 0$.

**Step 3:** $Z = 0$, $N = 1$ (MSB is 1).

**Step 4:** Signed check: $+112 + +96 = +208$, which exceeds $+127$ (max for 8-bit signed). **Overflow!** $V = 1$.

**Step 5:** Sign: $S = N \oplus V = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$. The "true" result is positive but stored as negative — this is precisely why the V flag exists.

**Final SREG: `0b00001100` = `0x0C` (V and N set, but S=0 indicating true positive).**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: SREG Bit Architecture & Connectivity

```mermaid
graph TD
    ALU[ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    ALU --> ResultBus[8-bit Result Bus R7-R0]
    ResultBus --> NFlag[Bit 2 N Negative Flag]
    ResultBus --> ZFlag[Bit 1 Z Zero Flag]
    ALU --> CInOut[Carry In/Out Logic]
    CInOut --> CFlag[Bit 0 C Carry Flag]
    ALU --> HCarryLogic[Nibble Carry Detector]
    HCarryLogic --> HFlag[Bit 5 H Half Carry]
    ALU --> VLogic[MSB Carry XOR]
    VLogic --> VFlag[Bit 3 V Overflow]
    VFlag --> SXOR[N XOR V]
    NFlag --> SXOR
    SXOR --> SFlag[Bit 4 S Sign Flag]
    Software[Software SEI/CLI] --> IFlag[Bit 7 I Interrupt Enable]
    Instr[BST Instruction] --> TFlag[Bit 6 T Bit Copy]
    NFlag --> BranchLogic[Conditional Branch Decoder]
    ZFlag --> BranchLogic
    CFlag --> BranchLogic
    VFlag --> BranchLogic
    SFlag --> BranchLogic
    IFlag --> IRQVector[Interrupt Vector Controller]
    BranchLogic --> PC[Program Counter Update]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flag Generation Flow from ALU Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OpA[Operand A] --> ALU1
    OpB[Operand B] --> ALU1
    OpA --> ALU1
    OpB --> ALU1
    ALU1[ALU Core] --> R[Result 8-bit]
    R --> ZeroDetect{Z is all zero?}
    ZeroDetect -->|Yes| Zset[Z = 1]
    ZeroDetect -->|No| Zclr[Z = 0]
    R --> MSBcheck[MSB to N flag]
    MSBcheck --> Nset[N = R7]
    ALU1 --> CarryCheck{Carry out of bit 7?}
    CarryCheck -->|Yes| Cset[C = 1]
    CarryCheck -->|No| Cclr[C = 0]
    CarryCheck --> HalfCheck{Carry out of bit 3?}
    HalfCheck -->|Yes| Hset[H = 1]
    HalfCheck -->|No| Hclr[H = 0]
    ALU1 --> OverDetect[MSB carry in XOR carry out]
    OverDetect --> Vset[V = 1]
    Sgen[S = N XOR V] --> Sflag[S Flag]
    ISRstart[Interrupt Event] --> Icheck{I flag set?}
    Icheck -->|Yes| ISRx[Service ISR]
    Icheck -->|No| Ignore[Ignore Request]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: SREG Reset and Initialization Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VCC as Power Supply
    participant RESET as Reset Circuit
    participant CPU as AVR Core
    participant SREG as Status Register
    VCC->>RESET: VCC reaches stable level
    RESET->>CPU: RESET pin LOW then HIGH
    CPU->>SREG: Initialize all flags to 0
    Note over SREG: I=0, T=0, H=0, S=0, V=0, N=0, Z=0, C=0
    CPU->>CPU: Begin execution from 0x0000
    CPU->>SREG: Update flags after each ALU op
    SREG-->>CPU: Flags used by branch instructions
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When drawing diagrams in exams, always label the SREG bit positions (7-6-5-4-3-2-1-0) and the flag mnemonics. This earns 2-3 easy marks even on partial answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Explain the function of the I bit and T bit in the AVR Status Register.**

**Model Answer:**
The **I bit (bit 7)** is the *Global Interrupt Enable* flag. When set to 1 by the `SEI` instruction, it enables all maskable interrupts. It is automatically cleared by hardware upon entering an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to prevent nested interrupts unless re-enabled.
The **T bit (bit 6)** is the *Bit Copy Storage* flag used as a 1-bit temporary buffer. The `BST` instruction copies a specified bit from a register into T, and the `BLD` instruction loads T's value into a specified bit of another register — enabling atomic single-bit transfers.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**What is the role of the Half Carry (H) flag in the AVR Status Register? When is it set?**

**Model Answer:**
The **H (Half Carry) flag (bit 5)** indicates a carry/borrow from the **low nibble (bit 3) to the high nibble (bit 4)** during an arithmetic operation. It is set to 1 when a carry occurs between the two nibbles of the lower byte. Its primary use is in **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic correction**, where after an addition, if H=1, a correction value of 0x06 must be added to fix the BCD representation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the bit structure of the AVR Status Register (SREG). List the function of each bit. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The AVR Status Register is an 8-bit register located at I/O address 0x5F. The bit structure is:

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | T | H | S | V | N | Z | C |

**Functions of each bit:**

1. **I (Bit 7) — Global Interrupt Enable:** Master switch for all maskable interrupts. Set by `SEI` and cleared by `CLI`. Hardware clears it when entering an ISR.

2. **T (Bit 6) — Bit Copy Storage:** Temporary storage for a single bit, manipulated by `BST` (store) and `BLD` (load) instructions.

3. **H (Bit 5) — Half Carry Flag:** Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 in arithmetic operations. Used in BCD correction routines.

4. **S (Bit 4) — Sign Flag:** $S = N \oplus V$; gives the true sign of a signed result considering overflow.

5. **V (Bit 3) — Two's Complement Overflow Flag:** Set when signed arithmetic produces a result outside the valid signed range. Calculated as $V = C_7 \oplus C_6$.

6. **N (Bit 2) — Negative Flag:** Simply a copy of the MSB (bit 7) of the ALU result. If N=1, the result is interpreted as negative in signed mode.

7. **Z (Bit 1) — Zero Flag:** Set when the ALU result is exactly zero. Used by `BREQ` and `BRNE` branch instructions.

8. **C (Bit 0) — Carry Flag:** Set when there is a carry from MSB (bit 7) in addition, or a borrow in subtraction. Used for unsigned arithmetic and multi-byte operations.

**Reset Value:** All bits = 0 (SREG = 0x00).

---

**(b)** Perform the addition `0xB4 + 0x6C` and determine the values of all flags in the AVR Status Register. Justify each flag. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $A = 0xB4 = 1011\,0100_2$ and $B = 0x6C = 0110\,1100_2$.

**Step 1 — Perform binary addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
  &\ \ 1011\,0100 \quad (0xB4) \\
+ &\ \ 0110\,1100 \quad (0x6C) \\
\hline
  &\ 1\,0010\,0000 \quad (0x120)
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is $0x120$, but only the lower 8 bits are stored in the destination register: $0x20 = 0010\,0000_2$.

**Step 2 — Analyze each flag:**

- **C (Carry):** Carry out of bit 7 occurred (the leading '1'). $\Rightarrow C = 1$.
- **Z (Zero):** Result $0x20 \neq 0$. $\Rightarrow Z = 0$.
- **N (Negative):** MSB of result ($0x20$) is 0. $\Rightarrow N = 0$.
- **V (Overflow):** Signed interpretation: $-76 + 108 = +32$, no overflow. $\Rightarrow V = 0$.
- **S (Sign):** $S = N \oplus V = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$.
- **H (Half Carry):** Lower nibble: $0x4 + 0xC = 0x10$. Carry out of bit 3 occurred. $\Rightarrow H = 1$.

**Final SREG value: `0b00100001` = `0x21`**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the Carry (C) flag and the Overflow (V) flag in the AVR Status Register. Explain with a suitable example where C is set but V is not. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | **Carry Flag (C)** | **Overflow Flag (V)** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Detects unsigned overflow / borrow | Detects signed (two's complement) overflow |
| Triggered by | Carry out of bit 7 (MSB) | $C_{in,7} \oplus C_{out,7}$ (MSB carry XOR) |
| Used for | Unsigned arithmetic, multi-byte | Signed arithmetic, comparisons |
| Branch instructions | `BRCS`, `BRCC` | `BRVS`, `BRVC` |

**Example where C is set but V is not:**

Let $A = 0xF0 = 1111\,0000_2$ and $B = 0x20 = 0010\,0000_2$.

Sum: $0xF0 + 0x20 = 0x110$. Carry out of bit 7 → $C = 1$.

Signed view: $A = -16$ and $B = +32$. Sum = $+16$ (within signed range). No signed overflow → $V = 0$.

This demonstrates that **C detects unsigned overflow while V detects signed overflow** — they serve different purposes and are not redundant.

---

**(b)** Explain the Sign flag (S) and derive its expression. Why is the Sign flag preferred over the Negative flag for signed comparisons? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Sign flag (S)** indicates the *true* sign of a signed arithmetic result, accounting for possible overflow. It is defined as:

$$
S = N \oplus V
$$

**Derivation:**
- The N flag is a direct copy of the MSB of the result.
- If overflow (V=1) occurs when adding two positive numbers, the result's MSB becomes 1 (looking negative), but the *true* mathematical result is still positive.
- Therefore, we need to **invert N** in the overflow case: $S = N \oplus V$.

**Truth Table Proof:**

| N | V | S = N⊕V | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Positive, no overflow |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Positive operands, but overflow → truly negative |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Truly negative, no overflow |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Negative operands, but overflow → truly positive |

**Why S is preferred over N for signed comparisons:**
The Negative (N) flag only reflects the MSB, which becomes unreliable when overflow occurs. For example, $+100 + +50 = -106$ (after signed overflow), where N=1 (negative), but the **true** sign is positive. Using N would incorrectly classify this as negative. The S flag corrects this, giving the mathematically correct sign. Hence, signed branch instructions like `BRSH` (Branch if Same or Higher) and `BRLO` use S and V/C, not N.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing C and V flags** — They are NOT the same. C is for *unsigned* overflow, V is for *signed* overflow. Mixing them up costs full marks in Part B questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the H flag** — In BCD arithmetic questions, students often compute C and Z but ignore H. Always check the lower-nibble carry.
> 3. **SREG reset value** — Many students write "undefined" or "random". The correct answer is **0x00** (all flags cleared on reset).
> 4. **Bit position errors** — The I bit is bit 7 (NOT bit 0). The C bit is bit 0 (NOT bit 7). Wrong bit numbering = -2 marks.
> 5. **Missing the I/O address** — KTU expects `0x5F` (or memory-mapped `0x005F`) for the SREG location. Omitting it costs at least 1 mark.
> 6. **S flag derivation skipped** — Always show the XOR logic and the truth table; this is a high-yield sub-question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SREG is an 8-bit register** at I/O address **0x5F** (memory address **0x005F**).
- ✅ **Reset value = 0x00** (all flags cleared).
- ✅ **Bit 7 = I** (Global Interrupt Enable) — controlled by `SEI` / `CLI`; auto-cleared in ISR.
- ✅ **Bit 6 = T** (Bit Copy) — used by `BST` and `BLD` for atomic 1-bit transfers.
- ✅ **Bit 5 = H** (Half Carry) — carry from bit 3 to bit 4; used for **BCD correction**.
- ✅ **Bit 4 = S** (Sign) — $S = N \oplus V$; the *true* sign of signed result.
- ✅ **Bit 3 = V** (Two's Complement Overflow) — $V = C_7 \oplus C_6$; for *signed* arithmetic.
- ✅ **Bit 2 = N** (Negative) — direct copy of result's MSB.
- ✅ **Bit 1 = Z** (Zero) — set when result = 0; used by `BREQ` / `BRNE`.
- ✅ **Bit 0 = C** (Carry) — carry out of bit 7 / borrow in subtraction; for *unsigned* arithmetic.
- ✅ **Conditional branch instructions** (`BRxx`) in AVR operate *directly* on SREG bits without explicitly referencing SREG.
- ✅ **SREG is automatically updated** by hardware after every ALU operation — no manual flag setting is required in most cases.
- ✅ **Manual modification** is done via instructions like `BSET` (Bit Set in SREG) and `BCLR` (Bit Clear in SREG).
- ✅ **Engineering tip:** Always check both C and V after signed arithmetic. If V=1, your result is *invalid* for signed interpretation.
- ✅ **Exam strategy:** Draw the SREG table in your answer sheet first — it visually earns marks and helps you structure the response.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data RAM/ROM Memory Space Allocation in AVR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data RAM/ROM Memory Space Allocation in AVR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Data Memory and Program Memory Space Allocation in AVR** refers to the **Harvard architecture-based segregated memory map** of Atmel (Microchip) AVR microcontrollers, in which the *Program Memory* (Flash ROM) and *Data Memory* (SRAM + I/O + Register File) exist as **physically separate address spaces** with **independent address buses, data buses, and dedicated instructions** for accessing each.

In the standard 8-bit AVR family (specifically the **ATmega32 / ATmega16** used in KTU syllabus), the memory map is:

- **Program Memory (Flash ROM):** 32K × 16 bits → **32 KB** of word-addressable, non-volatile storage for opcodes and constants.
- **Data Memory (SRAM):** 2K × 8 bits → **2 KB** of byte-addressable, volatile storage.
- **EEPROM:** 1K × 8 bits → **1 KB** of byte-addressable, non-volatile storage for user data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The AVR uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture**. The Program memory is 16-bit wide (word addressable) and accessed via the `Z`-register (R30:R31) for look-up table reads, while Data memory is 8-bit wide and accessed using Load/Store instructions with `X`, `Y`, `Z` pointer registers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large office building with two separate departments**:

- 🏢 **ROM (Program Memory)** is the **"Engineering Vault"** — a huge library of instruction sheets (32,768 instruction slots) that engineers can only **read from** (never tear or modify at runtime). It has its own private reading desk called the **Program Counter (PC)**.

- 🗄️ **RAM (Data Memory)** is the **"Workshop Floor"** — a workbench with three distinct sections:
  1. **32 Personal Lockers (R0–R31)** for every worker — fast access.
  2. **A Wall of Switchboards (I/O Registers $20–$5F)** that control the doors, lights, and machines of the building.
  3. **A Storage Warehouse (SRAM starting at $100)** where raw materials and intermediate work-pieces are kept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike 8051 (where Data and Code are in the same 64KB space), the AVR's Program and Data memories are **completely independent**. This allows simultaneous fetching of an instruction (from Flash) and reading/writing of data (from SRAM) in a **single clock cycle** — the foundation of the AVR's famous "1 MIPS/MHz" performance.

### 1.3 Important Metrics (Bordered/Bolded)

| Parameter | ATmega16/32 Value |
|---|---|
| Program Flash Size | **32K × 16-bit words (= 64 KB bytes)** |
| Data SRAM Size | **2 KB (2048 bytes)** |
| EEPROM Size | **1 KB (1024 bytes)** |
| General Purpose Registers | **32 (R0–R31)** |
| I/O Registers | **64 (addresses $00–$3F)** |
| Extended I/O Registers | **160 (addresses $60–$FF)** |
| Program Memory Address Width | **14-bit PC** (can address $0000–$3FFF) |
| Data Memory Address Width | **16-bit** (can address $0000–$FFFF) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Regions of the AVR Data Space

Even though the **physical** SRAM in an ATmega32 is only 2 KB, the **logical** data address space is **16 bits wide** ($0000 – $FFFF). The lower portion of this space is "overlaid" with the Register File and I/O Registers, so the actual usable SRAM is everything **above** the I/O region.

The data address space is divided into **four contiguous, byte-addressable regions**:

#### Region 1 — Register File (Data Address $0000 – $001F)

This is the **most performance-critical region**. It contains:

- **32 General Purpose 8-bit Registers: R0 – R31.**
- The AVR uses a **Load-Store (Register-Based) architecture** — every ALU operation must use registers as operands. There are no direct memory-to-memory ALU operations.
- **Three pairs of these registers serve double duty as 16-bit data pointer registers:**
  - **X-register:** R26 (XL) + R27 (XH) → forms a 16-bit pointer.
  - **Y-register:** R28 (YL) + R29 (YH) → forms a 16-bit pointer.
  - **Z-register:** R30 (ZL) + R31 (ZH) → forms a 16-bit pointer (also used for `LPM` Program Memory read).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R0 is special** — it is the only register that can be used as the destination of the multiplication instruction `MUL`. Similarly, all `MUL` operations always store the **16-bit result in R1:R0** (R1 = high byte, R0 = low byte).

#### Region 2 — I/O Register Space (Data Address $0020 – $005F)

Contains **64 dedicated hardware control registers**. Each I/O register has **two addresses**:
- **I/O Address Space:** $00 – $3F (6-bit address) — used by `IN`, `OUT`, `SBI`, `CBI`, `SBIS`, `SBIC` instructions (faster, single-cycle).
- **Data Memory Address:** $20 – $5F — used by `LDS`, `STS`, `LD`, `ST` (slower, two cycles).

**Examples of I/O Registers:**

| I/O Addr | Data Addr | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1F | $003F | **SREG** | Status Register (flags) |
| $3D | $005D | **SPL** | Stack Pointer Low |
| $3E | $005E | **SPH** | Stack Pointer High |
| $35 | $0055 | **MCUCR** | MCU Control Register |
| $3F | $005F | **SFIOR** | Special Function I/O Register |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack Pointer (SP)** is a 16-bit register split into `SPH:SPL` and always points to the **next free RAM location** in SRAM. The stack **grows downward** toward lower addresses when `PUSH` is executed.

#### Region 3 — Extended I/O Registers (Data Address $0060 – $00FF)

Reserved for **internal peripheral control** of larger AVR devices (e.g., ATmega128). On ATmega16/32, this region is **physically absent** but the addresses are still valid in the address map — they simply do not decode to anything.

#### Region 4 — Internal SRAM (Data Address $0100 – $08FF for ATmega32)

- For ATmega32: SRAM occupies **$0100 – $08FF** (2 KB).
- For ATmega16: SRAM also occupies **$0100 – $04FF** (1 KB).
- This is where **user variables, the C stack, and the heap** reside.

### 2.2 Program Memory (Flash ROM) Allocation

- **Width:** 16 bits per location (one AVR instruction is either 16 or 32 bits).
- **Size:** 32K words = 64 KB bytes (for ATmega16/32).
- **Address width:** 14 bits (Program Counter is 14 bits wide → $0000 – $3FFF).
- **Accessed by:** `LPM` (Load Program Memory) using the Z-register.
- **Application code** and **constant look-up tables** are stored here.

> [!NOTE]
> The Reset vector is at address **$0000** in Program Memory. The first instruction executed after reset is fetched from this address. All **interrupt vectors** (like `INT0`, `TIMER0_OVF`, `ADC`, etc.) are located starting at `$0000` and are mapped using **interrupt jump instructions (RJMP/JMP)**.

### 2.3 EEPROM Data Memory

- 1K bytes of non-volatile memory.
- Accessed via three I/O registers: **EECR** (Control), **EEDR** (Data), **EELR/EARL+EARH** (Address).
- Typical write time: **~8.5 ms per byte** (much slower than SRAM).
- Useful for storing **system configuration, calibration constants, passwords, and settings that survive power-off**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| SRAM End Address (ATmega32) | $\$0100 + 2048 - 1 = \$08FF$ | Last byte of internal SRAM |
| Stack Pointer Initial Value | $SP = RAMEND$ | Highest SRAM address |
| Program Memory Word Count | $N_{words} = \dfrac{ROM_{size}}{2}$ | For 32 KB Flash → 16K words |
| Effective Data Address (I/O Reg) | $D_{addr} = I_{addr} + \$20$ | Convert I/O space addr to data addr |
| SRAM End (General) | $RAMEND = \$60 + SRAM_{size} - 1$ | Common for older AT90S devices |
| Pointer Register Pair | $X = R27{:}R26,\ Y = R29{:}R28,\ Z = R31{:}R30$ | 16-bit pointer notation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ATmega32 specifically:** $RAMEND = \$08FF$ and $FLASHEND = \$3FFF$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Harvard separation of code and data in AVR enables:
1. **Deterministic real-time performance** — known instruction fetch time.
2. **Concurrent fetch + execute** in a single clock cycle.
3. **Self-programming capability** — the CPU can write its own Flash (used for bootloaders).
4. **In-system firmware updates** via SPM instruction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How to Convert I/O Address → Data Address

Many KTU exam questions ask to find the **Data Memory address** given the **I/O Space address** of a register (or vice-versa).

**Step 1 — Identify the offset relationship.**

The I/O space is mapped **inside** the data memory space starting at data address `$0020` (decimal 32). Therefore, the relationship is:

$$
D_{addr} = I_{addr} + \$20
$$

**Step 2 — Convert hex `$20` to decimal.**

$$
\$20 = (2 \times 16^1) + (0 \times 16^0) = 32
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

*Example:* The SREG register has I/O address `$1F`. Find its data memory address.

$$
D_{addr} = \$1F + \$20
$$

$$
D_{addr} = \$3F
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to verify.**

$$
\$3F = (3 \times 16) + 15 = 48 + 15 = 63
$$

Check: $31 + 32 = 63$ ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Calculating RAMEND for ATmega32

**Step 1 — Start address of Internal SRAM.**

For ATmega16/32, the SRAM begins immediately after the Extended I/O region. The first SRAM location is always:

$$
SRAM_{start} = \$0100
$$

This corresponds to decimal $256$.

**Step 2 — Add the SRAM size.**

ATmega32 has 2 KB of SRAM. Converting:

$$
2\ \text{KB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract 1 to get the last address.**

$$
RAMEND = SRAM_{start} + SRAM_{size} - 1
$$

$$
RAMEND = \$0100 + 2048 - 1
$$

$$
RAMEND = \$0100 + \$0700 - 1
$$

$$
RAMEND = \$07FF
$$

Wait — let me re-check: $2048 = \$0800$. Therefore:

$$
RAMEND = \$0100 + \$0800 - 1 = \$08FF
$$

**Step 4 — Verify in hex.**

$$
\$08FF = (0 \times 4096) + (8 \times 256) + (15 \times 16) + 15
$$

$$
\$08FF = 2048 + 255 = 2303
$$

Check: $256 + 2048 - 1 = 2303$ ✓

### 3.3 Derivation: Program Memory Word-to-Byte Conversion

ATmega32 has 32 KB of Flash. But the AVR instruction word is 16 bits (2 bytes). Therefore:

$$
N_{instructions} = \dfrac{32\ \text{KB}}{2\ \text{bytes/word}} = \dfrac{32 \times 1024}{2} = 16,\!384\ \text{words}
$$

**Verification using PC width:**

A 14-bit Program Counter can address $2^{14}$ locations:

$$
2^{14} = 16,\!384\ \text{words}
$$

This matches ✓. The PC range is `$0000` to `$3FFF` (where `$3FFF` = 16,383, plus zero gives 16,384 locations).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Map of ATmega32

| Region | Data Address Range | Size (Bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Register File | `$0000 – $001F` | **32** | R0–R31 (GPRs), X/Y/Z pointer pairs |
| I/O Registers | `$0020 – $005F` | **64** | SREG, PORTA, DDRx, etc. |
| Extended I/O | `$0060 – $00FF` | **160** | Reserved / unused on ATmega32 |
| Internal SRAM | `$0100 – $08FF` | **2048** | Variables, stack, heap |
| **TOTAL** | `$0000 – $08FF` | **2304** | All data-mapped memory |

### 3.5 Detailed Mapping of R26:R27 as the X-Pointer

| Bit | Register | Position |
|---|---|---|
| **R26** | XL (Low byte of X) | D-ADDR `$001A` |
| **R27** | XH (High byte of X) | D-ADDR `$001B` |
| **Combined** | X = R27:R26 | 16-bit pointer |

The X-pointer is used by the `LD` (load with X-pointer) and `ST` (store using X-pointer) instructions, and supports **post-increment (`X+`), pre-decrement (`-X`), and simple (`LD A, X`)** addressing modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state that **Y and Z pointers additionally support post-increment with displacement (LD A, Y+q)** for accessing arrays/structures. The **Z-pointer is the only one that can be used with `LPM`** to read from Program Memory.

### 3.6 Numerical Example: Stack Setup in C

When a C program runs on AVR-GCC:

1. The C runtime library initializes the Stack Pointer: `SP = RAMEND = 0x08FF`.
2. Each `PUSH` or function call **decrements SP by 1 or 2** and writes the data.
3. Local variables of C functions are placed **below** the stack.
4. The `.data` and `.bss` sections are placed starting at `$0100` (lowest SRAM address).

**Stack pointer value after a single-byte PUSH of register R5:**

$$
SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1 = \$08FF - 1 = \$08FE
$$

The byte `R5` is now stored at address `$08FE`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — AVR Harvard Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC["Program Counter (14-bit)"]
    flash["Program Memory (Flash ROM) 32K x 16"]
    pbus["Program Data Bus (16-bit)"]
    instreg["Instruction Register / Decoder"]
    cpu["ALU + Control Unit"]
    xptr["X-Reg (R27:R26)"]
    yptr["Y-Reg (R29:R28)"]
    zptr["Z-Reg (R31:R30)"]
    regfile["Register File (R0-R31) - 32 Bytes"]
    ioreg["I/O Registers (64 Bytes)"]
    extio["Extended I/O (160 Bytes)"]
    sram["Internal SRAM (2 KB)"]
    dabus["Data Bus (8-bit)"]
    eeprom["EEPROM (1 KB)"]
    eectrl["EECR / EEDR / EEAR"]

    PC --> flash
    flash --> pbus
    pbus --> instreg
    instreg --> cpu
    cpu <--> regfile
    cpu <--> ioreg
    cpu <--> extio
    cpu <--> sram
    regfile --- xptr
    regfile --- yptr
    regfile --- zptr
    xptr --> sram
    yptr --> sram
    zptr --> sram
    zptr --> flash
    cpu --> eectrl
    eectrl --> eeprom
    sram --> dabus
    ioreg --> dabus
    extio --> dabus
    regfile --> dabus
```

### 4.2 Linear Data Memory Map of ATmega32

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["0x0000\nR0-R31\n(32 bytes)"]
    A1["0x0020\nI/O Regs\n(64 bytes)"]
    A2["0x0060\nExt I/O\n(160 bytes)"]
    A3["0x0100\nSRAM START"]
    A4["0x02xx\nVariables & .data"]
    A5["0x06xx\nHeap grows up"]
    A6["0x08xx\nStack grows down"]
    A7["0x08FF\nRAMEND (SP init)"]
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Pointer Register Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R26["R26 (XL) - 8 bits"] --> XREG["X-Register (16-bit)"]
    R27["R27 (XH) - 8 bits"] --> XREG
    XREG --> LDA["LD Rd, X+"]
    LDA --> SRAM1["SRAM Data Read"]
    XREG --> STDA["ST X+, Rr"]
    STDA --> SRAM2["SRAM Data Write"]
    R28["R28 (YL)"] --> YREG["Y-Register (16-bit)"]
    R29["R29 (YH)"] --> YREG
    YREG --> LDY["LD Rd, Y+q"]
    R30["R30 (ZL)"] --> ZREG["Z-Register (16-bit)"]
    R31["R31 (ZH)"] --> ZREG
    ZREG --> LPM["LPM Rd, Z+ (Program Memory)"]
    ZREG --> LDZ["LD Rd, Z (SRAM)"]
```

### 4.4 Program Memory Vector Layout (ATmega32)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V0["0x0000\nRESET Vector\n(RJMP to main)"]
    V1["0x0004\nINT0 Handler"]
    V2["0x0006\nINT1 Handler"]
    V3["0x0008\nTIMER2 COMP"]
    V4["0x000A\nTIMER2 OVF"]
    V5["... (more vectors) ..."]
    V6["0x0028\nSPI / TWI / ADC etc."]
    V7["0x0030\nEnd of vector table\n(Code begins)"]
    V0 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6 --> V7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q1.** Differentiate between Program Memory and Data Memory in AVR architecture. Why is this architecture called *Modified Harvard*?

**Model Answer:**
The Program Memory (Flash ROM, 32K × 16) and Data Memory (SRAM, 2K × 8) in AVR are **physically separate**, each having its **own address bus, data bus, and control signals**. This allows simultaneous instruction fetch and data access in a single cycle.

It is called *Modified Harvard* because, although the two memories are physically separated, the Program Memory can still be **read as data** using the `LPM` instruction with the Z-pointer, which is a relaxation of the strict Harvard rule. Program Memory is **non-volatile** (retains data without power), while Data Memory is **volatile** (loses contents on power-off).

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q2.** What is the role of the R26:R27 register pair in AVR? List all three such pointer pairs with their primary uses.

**Model Answer:**
The R26:R27 register pair forms the **16-bit X-data pointer**, used by `LD`/`ST` instructions to access SRAM. It supports **post-increment, pre-decrement, and direct addressing** modes.

The three pointer pairs are:
- **X = R27:R26** → General SRAM access with auto-increment/decrement.
- **Y = R29:R28** → SRAM access **with 6-bit displacement** (q = 0–63), ideal for arrays/structures.
- **Z = R31:R30** → SRAM access **and** Program Memory read via `LPM` instruction.

---

### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the complete Data Memory map of ATmega32. Show the regions of Register File, I/O Registers, Extended I/O Registers, and Internal SRAM with their address ranges.

**Model Solution:**

The ATmega32 has a **16-bit data address space** ($0000 – $FFFF) of which the lower **2304 bytes** are physically implemented. The data memory is divided into four contiguous regions:

| Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Register File** | `$0000 – $001F` | 32 bytes | General Purpose Registers R0–R31, includes X/Y/Z pointer pairs |
| **I/O Registers** | `$0020 – $005F` | 64 bytes | Hardware control (PORTA, DDRx, SREG, SP, ADC, etc.) |
| **Extended I/O** | `$0060 – $00FF` | 160 bytes | Reserved for larger devices (unused on ATmega32) |
| **Internal SRAM** | `$0100 – $08FF` | 2048 bytes | User variables, stack, heap |

The **first 32 locations** are the Register File which is the fastest-accessible memory (1 cycle). Three pairs — R26:R27 (X), R28:R29 (Y), and R30:R31 (Z) — act as **16-bit data pointers**.

The **I/O Register space** is dual-addressed: each register has both an I/O address (`$00–$3F`) used by `IN`/`OUT` instructions and a Data address (`$20–$5F`) used by `LDS`/`STS`. The **SREG (Status Register)** at I/O `$1F` (= Data `$3F`) contains the ALU flags I, T, H, S, V, N, Z, C.

The **SRAM region** begins at `$0100` and ends at `$08FF` (`RAMEND`). The Stack Pointer (SPH:SPL) is initialized to `RAMEND` and the stack **grows downward** when data is pushed.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]** Explain how Program Memory is organized in ATmega32. How is the Flash accessed for reading constant tables using the `LPM` instruction?

**Model Solution:**

The Program Memory in ATmega32 is a **32K × 16-bit Flash ROM** (non-volatile), meaning it stores **32,768 instructions** (each 16 or 32 bits). The Program Counter (PC) is **14 bits wide**, so the valid address range is `$0000` to `$3FFF` (= `FLASHEND`).

**Organization of the Flash:**
- The lowest addresses ($0000–$0030 approximately) contain the **Interrupt Vector Table**. The very first location, `$0000`, is the **Reset Vector** which contains a `RJMP` to the start of the `main` function.
- The remaining Flash contains **user code and constant look-up tables**.
- The Flash can also be **self-programmed at runtime** using the `SPM` instruction (used in bootloaders).

**Reading constants with `LPM`:**
The `LPM` (Load Program Memory) instruction reads one byte from Flash using the **Z-pointer (R31:R30)** as the address.

*Example — reading a constant `0x5A` stored at Flash address `$0200`:*

```assembly
LDI  ZH, HIGH(0x0200)    ; Load high byte of address
LDI  ZL, LOW(0x0200)     ; Load low byte of address
LPM  R16, Z              ; Load byte from Flash[Z] into R16
; Now R16 = 0x5A
```

The `LPM Rd, Z+` variant **post-increments Z**, making it easy to walk through a table:

```assembly
LDI  ZH, HIGH(TABLE*2)   ; Note: byte address * 2 for word addressing
LDI  ZL, LOW(TABLE*2)
LPM  R16, Z+             ; Read first byte, Z becomes Z+1
LPM  R17, Z+             ; Read second byte
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Flash is **word-addressed**, so to find the byte address of a C label `TABLE`, you must multiply by 2: `Z = TABLE * 2`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the Harvard memory architecture of AVR. Compare it with the Von-Neumann architecture used in 8051.

**Model Solution:**

**Harvard Architecture (AVR):**
The AVR uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture** in which the **Program Memory** (Flash ROM) and **Data Memory** (SRAM + I/O) are **physically and logically separated**. They have:
- **Independent address buses** (14-bit PC for Flash, 16-bit for data).
- **Independent data buses** (16-bit for Flash, 8-bit for data).
- **Independent control signals**.

**Block diagram concept:**

```
[CPU/ALU]
   |       |--- Data Bus 8-bit ---> [SRAM, I/O, Registers]
[PC]--Flash Bus 16-bit --> [Flash ROM]
```

This allows the CPU to **fetch the next instruction from Flash** *while simultaneously* **reading/writing data from SRAM** in a single clock cycle, achieving the famous **1 MIPS/MHz** throughput.

**Comparison with Von-Neumann (8051):**

| Feature | Harvard (AVR) | Von-Neumann (8051) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Separate code + data | Single shared memory |
| Buses | Two independent | One shared bus |
| Simultaneous Fetch + Data | **Yes (1 cycle)** | No (bus contention) |
| Speed | Faster, deterministic | Slower, sequential |
| Code can read itself | Yes (via `LPM`) | Yes (via `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`) |
| Complexity | More pins, more hardware | Simpler design |
| Power | Better for real-time DSP | Adequate for control |

**Q (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the organization of the Register File in ATmega32. How are the X, Y, and Z pointer registers formed and used? Give example instructions.

**Model Solution:**

The **Register File** in ATmega32 occupies the **first 32 locations** of the data address space (`$0000` – `$001F`). It contains **32 General Purpose 8-bit Registers** named **R0 through R31**. Every AVR instruction (except a few like `NOP` and `SLEEP`) operates on these registers — this is why the AVR is called a **Register-Based (Load/Store) architecture**.

**Special features of the Register File:**

1. **Direct register access** in instructions uses a 5-bit field (`r = 0` to `31`), allowing any of the 32 registers to be a source or destination operand. This means a typical instruction like `ADD Rd, Rr` can be encoded in just one 16-bit word.

2. **Pointer Register Pairs** — Three pairs serve as 16-bit pointer registers:
   - **X-register** = **R27:R26** (XH is the high byte at `$001B`, XL is the low byte at `$001A`).
   - **Y-register** = **R29:R28** (YH at `$001D`, YL at `$001C`).
   - **Z-register** = **R31:R30** (ZH at `$001F`, ZL at `$001E`).

3. **R0 and R1** — Reserved for the **Multiplication instruction**. The result of `MUL` is always placed in **R1:R0** (R1 = high byte, R0 = low byte).

**Example instructions using pointer registers:**

```assembly
; Load from SRAM address 0x0200 into R16 using Z-pointer
LDI  ZH, 0x02
LDI  ZL, 0x00
LD   R16, Z          ; R16 = data at SRAM[0x0200]

; Store R17 into SRAM with auto-increment using X-pointer
LDI  XH, 0x03
LDI  XL, 0x00
ST   X+, R17         ; Store R17, then X = X+1

; Read a constant from Flash using LPM
LDI  ZH, HIGH(MSG*2)
LDI  ZL, LOW(MSG*2)
LPM  R18, Z          ; R18 = MSG[0]
```

**Y-pointer with displacement** (unique feature):

```assembly
LDI  YH, 0x04
LDI  YL, 0x00
LDD  R19, Y+5        ; R19 = data at SRAM[0x0405]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistake:**
> 1. **Confusing R26/R27 ordering** — Students often write "X = R26:R27" but it must be **X = R27:R26** (high byte first, then low byte). Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Wrong Flash address multiplier** — When loading a C label into Z, students write `LDI ZL, LOW(TABLE)` instead of `LOW(TABLE*2)`. The Flash is word-addressed, so byte addresses must be **doubled**. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Saying R0 is a "general" register without mentioning MUL** — Examiners expect you to know that R0 and R1 are **implied operands of the MUL instruction**. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Missing the I/O ↔ Data address relationship** — When asked for the data address of SREG, students write `$1F` instead of `$3F`. Always add `$20` to the I/O address. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing SRAM wrong** — Several students draw the SRAM *below* the I/O region or forget to mention that SRAM starts at `$0100` (not `$0060`). Loss: 1 mark.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ AVR uses **Modified Harvard Architecture** — code and data are in separate memories.
- ✅ **ATmega32 has:** 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM.
- ✅ Data Memory address space is **16 bits** ($0000 – $FFFF) but only **2304 bytes** are physically present.
- ✅ The **Register File** ($0000–$001F) is 32 bytes and includes the **X (R27:R26), Y (R29:R28), Z (R31:R30)** pointer pairs.
- ✅ **R1:R0** are used as the **result register of MUL** instruction.
- ✅ **I/O Register space** is **dual-addressed** — `D_addr = I_addr + $20`.
- ✅ The **first I/O register (SREG)** is at I/O `$1F` = Data `$3F`.
- ✅ **SRAM** starts at `$0100` and ends at `$08FF` for ATmega32; `RAMEND = $08FF`.
- ✅ The **Stack Pointer (SPH:SPL)** is initialized to **RAMEND** at startup; the stack **grows downward** on `PUSH`/`CALL`.
- ✅ **Program Memory is 16-bit wide (word-addressed)**, accessed by `LPM` via the Z-register; for C labels use `label*2`.
- ✅ **Reset vector** is at Flash `$0000`; interrupt vectors follow immediately after.
- ✅ **FLASHEND = $3FFF** and **PC width = 14 bits** for ATmega16/32.
- ✅ **EEPROM** is non-volatile, accessed via `EECR`/`EEDR`/`EEAR`, with a slow write time (~8.5 ms/byte).
- ✅ AVR is a **Load/Store (Register-Based)** architecture — ALU cannot operate directly on memory; data must first be loaded into a register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"The AVR is a 1-MIPS-per-MHz, 8-bit, Modified Harvard, Load-Store RISC microcontroller with 32 fast registers, 64 I/O registers, and a clean linear SRAM map starting at $0100."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AVR Data Format and Directives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AVR Data Format and Directives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 AVR Data Format — Formal Definition

In the **AVR assembly language** (used with the AVR Studio / Microchip Studio assembler), a **Data Format** is the syntactical representation used to express numerical or character constants inside the source program. The assembler must be able to interpret the raw text "1234" and understand *which* numbering system the programmer intends (binary, decimal, hex, etc.). AVR supports several in-line data formats that are interpreted by the assembler based on a **prefix character** or **default convention**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AVR Assembly uses a **Radix-Based Data Representation**. The default radix is **Decimal (Base-10)** unless explicitly overridden by a prefix.

The five classical AVR data formats are:

| Prefix | Format Name | Base | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| None / Default | Decimal | 10 | `1234` |
| `0b` or `0B` | Binary | 2 | `0b11010010` |
| `$` or `0x` / `0X` | Hexadecimal | 16 | `$A5` or `0xA5` |
| `0` (leading zero, non-`0b`/`0x`) | Octal | 8 | `0777` |
| `' '` (single quote) | ASCII (single char) | — | `'A'` |
| `" "` (double quote) | Decimal ASCII string | — | `"Hello"` |

### 1.2 AVR Directives — Formal Definition

**Assembler Directives** (sometimes called *pseudo-operations* or *pseudo-ops*) are special instructions to the **AVR Assembler itself** — not to the AVR CPU. They are **not translated into machine code**; instead, they tell the assembler *how* to translate the program, *where* to place code/data in memory, or *what symbols to define*. They always begin with a dot (`.`) in modern AVR assembler conventions (e.g., `.CSEG`, `.DSEG`, `.DB`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Instructions (like `LDI`, `ADD`, `RJMP`) **execute at runtime** on the microcontroller. Directives (like `.CSEG`, `.DB`, `.EQU`) **execute at assembly time** on the host PC. They consume zero program memory on the AVR chip.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu & Recipe Book

Imagine you are a **chef (the programmer)** writing a **recipe (your program)** that will be cooked (executed) by a **robot chef (the AVR CPU)**. The recipe is written in a language the robot understands.

* **Data Formats** are the **measurement units** you write in your recipe. Saying "add 100" is ambiguous — is that 100 grams, 100 ml, or 100 pinches? Writing `0b01100100` (binary), `100` (decimal), or `$64` (hex) tells the chef *the same amount*, but in three different "languages." The robot (assembler) needs the **prefix** (`0b`, `$`) to know which language you are using.
* **Assembler Directives** are like **notes to the editor** of your recipe book, not instructions to the chef. When you write "put this recipe on page 5" (`.ORG 0x500`) or "this ingredient is called `TEMP`" (`.EQU TEMP = 25`), you are talking to the **editor/assembler**, not the chef. The robot chef never sees these notes — they vanish at print time.

> [!TIP]
> A useful mental test: **If the AVR CPU will not encounter it during execution, it is a directive.** A good example: `.DB "HELLO"` stores the string in Flash at assembly time — the byte values *do* end up in program memory, but the `.DB` directive itself does not "execute."

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in AVR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AVR Program Memory (Flash) Word Size = 16 bits (2 bytes)**. All `.DW` (Define Word) directives therefore consume **2 bytes** of Flash per operand, not 1.
>
> **AVR Register Width = 8 bits** for the classical ATmega series. Therefore, the largest unsigned integer representable is $\mathbf{2^{8}-1 = 255}$, and the largest literal accepted by byte-oriented instructions is **255 (0xFF)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Data Format

#### 2.1.1 Decimal Format (Default)

If no prefix is used, the assembler interprets the number as **base-10**. This is the most human-readable form.

```asm
    LDI R16, 100       ; Load R16 with decimal 100 (0x64)
    LDI R20, 255       ; Load R20 with decimal 255 (0xFF)
```

#### 2.1.2 Binary Format (`0b` prefix)

Useful when working with bit-manipulation (very common in AVR — every port pin is a single bit).

```asm
    LDI R16, 0b10101010   ; R16 = 0xAA
    SBI PORTB, 0          ; Set bit 0 of PORTB (a binary operation)
```

#### 2.1.3 Hexadecimal Format (`$` or `0x` prefix)

The most common format in embedded systems. **KTU examiners love hex** because the data sheet of the AVR uses hex notation.

```asm
    LDI R16, $FF       ; R16 = 255
    LDI R17, 0x3F      ; R17 = 63
    STS $0060, R16     ; Store R16 in SRAM location 0x0060
```

#### 2.1.4 Octal Format (leading `0`)

Rare in AVR practice, but supported. The digit set is **0–7**.

```asm
    LDI R16, 0777      ; Octal 777 = Decimal 511
```

#### 2.1.5 ASCII Single Character (`' '`)

A single character enclosed in single quotes is replaced by its **7-bit ASCII code**.

```asm
    LDI R16, 'A'       ; R16 = 0x41 (65)
    LDI R17, '0'       ; R17 = 0x30 (48)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **ASCII Range Cheat:** `'0'–'9'` → $30–$39, `'A'–'Z'` → $41–$5A, `'a'–'z'` → $61–$7A.

#### 2.1.6 ASCII String (`" "`)

A multi-character string is stored as a **sequence of bytes**, each holding the ASCII value of one character.

```asm
    .DB "HELLO"        ; Stores 0x48, 0x45, 0x4C, 0x4C, 0x4F in 5 consecutive bytes
```

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Key AVR Directives

#### 2.2.1 Segment Directives

| Directive | Full Form | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `.CSEG` | Code Segment | Subsequent code goes into **Flash Program Memory** |
| `.DSEG` | Data Segment | Subsequent code defines **SRAM variables** (requires `.BYTE`) |
| `.ESEG` | EEPROM Segment | Subsequent code goes into **EEPROM Memory** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The AVR has **three separate address spaces** for memory: Flash (program), SRAM (data), and EEPROM (non-volatile storage). The segment directives tell the assembler which memory the bytes/words are being deposited into.

#### 2.2.2 Origin Directive — `.ORG`

`.ORG expr` sets the **Location Counter** (the assembler's internal "current address pointer") to the value of `expr`. In a `.CSEG`, it sets the Flash address; in `.DSEG`, it sets the SRAM address.

```asm
    .CSEG
    .ORG 0x0000         ; Reset vector at address 0
    RJMP main
    .ORG 0x0034         ; Place code at interrupt vector
    RETI
```

#### 2.2.3 Data Definition Directives

| Directive | Storage Size | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| `.DB expr1, expr2, …` | 1 byte per value | Strings, byte tables, lookup tables |
| `.DW expr1, expr2, …` | 2 bytes (one word) per value | 16-bit constants, vector tables |
| `.BYTE n` | Reserves `n` bytes (in `.DSEG`) | Uninitialized SRAM variables |

```asm
.CSEG
    .DB 0x01, 0x02, 0x03         ; Three bytes in Flash
    .DW 0x1234, 0x5678           ; Two 16-bit words in Flash

.DSEG
    counter: .BYTE 1             ; Reserve 1 byte in SRAM, label = counter
    buffer:  .BYTE 64            ; Reserve 64 bytes in SRAM, label = buffer
```

#### 2.2.4 Symbol Definition Directives

| Directive | Behavior | Re-definable? |
|---|---|---|
| `.EQU label = expr` | Defines a constant label | **NO** — causes an error if redefined |
| `.SET label = expr` | Defines a constant label | **YES** — can be reassigned later |
| `.DEF reg = Rnn` | Names a CPU register for readability | **YES** — no error on redefinition |

```asm
    .EQU BAUD = 9600
    .DEF TEMPREG = R16
    .SET COUNT = 0
    ; ...later...
    .SET COUNT = COUNT + 1     ; Allowed; redefinition OK
```

#### 2.2.5 File Inclusion — `.INCLUDE`

Inserts the contents of another source file at assembly time — the AVR assembler searches both the current directory and the standard include path (where the m328Pdef.inc device file lives).

```asm
    .INCLUDE "m328Pdef.inc"   ; Pulls in register/bit definitions
```

#### 2.2.6 Other Useful Directives

* `.EXIT` — Stops the assembly process immediately (useful during debugging).
* `.LIST` / `.NOLIST` — Turns the assembly listing on/off in the `.LST` file.
* `.DEVICE ATmega328P` — Tells the assembler the target MCU; helps with size checks.
* `.MACRO` / `.ENDMACRO` — Defines a reusable code block.
* `#include` (in C, not assembler) — Equivalent for C-language AVR projects.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Decimal max for byte | $2^{8} - 1 = 255$ | 8-bit AVR register |
| Hex max for byte | $0xFF$ | Same as 255 |
| Binary max for byte | $0b11111111$ | Same as 255 |
| Flash word size | $1 \text{ word} = 2 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ bits}$ | AVR uses 16-bit instruction words |
| Bytes per `.DW` operand | $2 \text{ bytes}$ | One 16-bit word |
| ASCII `'A'` value | $0x41 = 65$ | Standard ASCII |
| ASCII `'0'` value | $0x30 = 48$ | Numeric zero character |
| `'A' - 'a'` offset | $0x20 = 32$ | Case-toggle bit |
| Hex → Decimal | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} d_i \cdot 16^{i}$ | $d_i$ are hex digits |
| Binary → Decimal | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | $b_i \in \{0,1\}$ |
| Octal → Decimal | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} o_i \cdot 8^{i}$ | $o_i \in \{0,\ldots,7\}$ |
| '.EQU' redefinition | Causes **assembler error** | Permanent binding |
| '.SET' redefinition | Allowed | Mutable binding |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Conversion Trick:** To convert any base-$\beta$ number $d_{n-1} d_{n-2} \cdots d_1 d_0$ to decimal, use:
> $$\text{Decimal} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} d_k \cdot \beta^{k}$$
> where each digit $d_k$ must satisfy $0 \le d_k < \beta$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

AVR data formats and directives form the **silent scaffolding** of every embedded firmware project. A few practical scenarios:

* **Hex format** is used in **hardware register definitions** because the ATmega328P datasheet lists every SFR (Special Function Register) at a hex address (e.g., `UCSR0A = 0xC0`).
* **Binary format** is used for **bit masks** (e.g., `0b00000111` selects the lower 3 bits).
* **`.CSEG` / `.DSEG` / `.ESEG`** separate the three memories so the linker can place code, RAM variables, and non-volatile calibration data correctly.
* **`.EQU` / `.INCLUDE`** allow **portable, readable code**: writing `BAUD` instead of `103` (the UBRR value for 9600 baud at 16 MHz) means the same program can be retuned by changing one line.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Conversion: Verify `$A5` = `0b10100101` = `165` Decimal

We will convert the hexadecimal constant `$A5` to binary and decimal to confirm all three AVR formats represent the same underlying value.

**Step 1 — Identify hex digits and their decimal weights.**

The hex number `$A5` has two digits: $A$ (most significant) and $5$ (least significant). Each hex digit maps to 4 binary bits.

$$
A_{16} = 1010_2, \quad 5_{16} = 0101_2
$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate the 4-bit groups to form the binary number.**

$$
\$A5 = 1010\ 0101_2 = 0b10100101
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the positional value formula in base-2 to obtain the decimal value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0b10100101_2 &= (1 \cdot 2^{7}) + (0 \cdot 2^{6}) + (1 \cdot 2^{5}) + (0 \cdot 2^{4}) + (0 \cdot 2^{3}) + (1 \cdot 2^{2}) + (0 \cdot 2^{1}) + (1 \cdot 2^{0}) \\
&= 128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 1 \\
&= 165_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the hex-to-decimal formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\$A5 &= (A \cdot 16^{1}) + (5 \cdot 16^{0}) \\
&= (10 \cdot 16) + (5 \cdot 1) \\
&= 160 + 5 \\
&= 165_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods agree. Therefore:

$$
\$A5 = 0b10100101 = 165_{10}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the positional expansion. Marks are awarded per correct term, not just for the final answer.

### 3.2 Worked Conversion: Octal `0777` to Decimal and Hex

The literal `0777` is **octal** because it begins with `0` and is not `0b` or `0x`. (Caution: in C, a leading `0` also means octal, but in AVR assembler the same rule applies.)

**Step 1 — Apply the octal positional formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0777_{8} &= (0 \cdot 8^{3}) + (7 \cdot 8^{2}) + (7 \cdot 8^{1}) + (7 \cdot 8^{0}) \\
&= 0 + 448 + 56 + 7 \\
&= 511_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert 511 to hex by repeated division.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
511 \div 16 &= 31 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad \rightarrow \text{ LSD} = F \\
31 \div 16 &= 1 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad \rightarrow F \\
1 \div 16 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 1 \quad \rightarrow \text{ MSD} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders from most significant to least: `$1FF`.

**Step 3 — Verify.**

$$
1FF_{16} = 1 \cdot 256 + 15 \cdot 16 + 15 \cdot 1 = 256 + 240 + 15 = 511_{10} \;\;\checkmark
$$

So `0777` = `511` = `$1FF`.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Directive Behavior Across Memory Segments

The following table summarizes the **legal combinations** of directives and segments. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

| Directive | `.CSEG` (Flash) | `.DSEG` (SRAM) | `.ESEG` (EEPROM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `.ORG` | ✅ Sets PC in Flash | ✅ Sets SRAM pointer | ✅ Sets EEPROM pointer |
| `.DB` | ✅ Stores bytes in Flash | ❌ Illegal | ✅ Stores bytes in EEPROM |
| `.DW` | ✅ Stores 16-bit words in Flash | ❌ Illegal | ✅ Stores 16-bit words in EEPROM |
| `.BYTE` | ❌ Illegal | ✅ Reserves SRAM bytes | ❌ Illegal |
| `.EQU` | ✅ Allowed in any segment (defines a constant) | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory-Size Discipline:** AVR instructions like `LDI` require a constant in range $[0, 255]$. If you write `LDI R16, 1000`, the assembler flags a *range error*. A 16-bit constant must be moved via two instructions (`LDI` low byte, then `LDI` high byte + shift), or stored in Flash with `.DW` and read via `LPM`.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — `.EQU` vs `.SET` vs `.DEF`
