| Feature | `.EQU` | `.SET` | `.DEF` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binds to | Numeric expression | Numeric expression | **Register name only** |
| Reassignable? | ❌ Error on redefinition | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed (overwrites) |
| Scope | File-wide | File-wide | File-wide |
| Common use | Constants, hardware addresses | Loop counters, evolving labels | `TEMPREG = R16` style aliasing |
| Example | `.EQU BAUD = 9600` | `.SET COUNT = 0` | `.DEF COUNTER = R17` |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Deriving the Flash Footprint of a `.DB` String

Suppose we declare in a `.CSEG`:

```asm
    .CSEG
MESSAGE: .DB "AVR", 0
```

**Step 1 — Count characters in the string.**

The string `"AVR"` has 3 characters: `'A'`, `'V'`, `'R'`. The trailing `, 0` adds **one null terminator byte**.

$$
\text{Total bytes} = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Convert bytes to words.**

Since each Flash word is 16 bits = 2 bytes:

$$
\text{Total words} = \lceil 4 / 2 \rceil = 2 \text{ words}
$$

**Step 3 — State the actual byte values placed in Flash.**

$$
'A' = 0x41, \quad 'V' = 0x56, \quad 'R' = 0x52, \quad 0 = 0x00
$$

So the Flash layout is (little-endian, AVR convention):

$$
\text{WORD 0} = 0x5641, \quad \text{WORD 1} = 0x0052
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why little-endian?** AVR stores multi-byte values **least significant byte first**. The byte sequence in Flash for the 16-bit word `0x1234` is `0x34, 0x12`. This is examinable in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of AVR Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["AVR Source Line Constant"] --> D1{"Has prefix?"}
    D1 -- "0b / 0B" --> Bin["BINARY Base-2 e.g. 0b11010010"]
    D1 -- "$ or 0x" --> Hex["HEXADECIMAL Base-16 e.g. $A5"]
    D1 -- "leading 0" --> Oct["OCTAL Base-8 e.g. 0777"]
    D1 -- "none" --> Dec["DECIMAL Base-10 e.g. 1234"]
    D1 -- "single quotes" --> Asc["ASCII Char e.g. A"]
    D1 -- "double quotes" --> Str["ASCII String e.g. HELLO"]

    Bin --> Internal["Assembled to 8 or 16-bit integer"]
    Hex --> Internal
    Oct --> Internal
    Dec --> Internal
    Asc --> Internal
    Str --> Internal

    Internal --> Use{"Where placed?"}
    Use -- ".CSEG" --> Flash["Flash Program Memory"]
    Use -- ".DSEG" --> Sram["SRAM Data Memory"]
    Use -- ".ESEG" --> Eep["EEPROM Memory"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Assembler Directive Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["AVR Assembler Directives"] --> Seg["Segment Selectors"]
    Root --> Pos["Positioning"]
    Root --> Dat["Data Storage"]
    Root --> Sym["Symbol Definition"]
    Root --> Inc["File Inclusion"]
    Root --> Ctl["Assembly Control"]

    Seg --> SegA[".CSEG Flash"]
    Seg --> SegB[".DSEG SRAM"]
    Seg --> SegC[".ESEG EEPROM"]

    Pos --> PosA[".ORG sets location counter"]

    Dat --> DatA[".DB Define Byte"]
    Dat --> DatB[".DW Define Word"]
    Dat --> DatC[".BYTE Reserve SRAM"]

    Sym --> SymA[".EQU Constant Final"]
    Sym --> SymB[".SET Variable"]
    Sym --> SymC[".DEF Register Alias"]

    Inc --> IncA[".INCLUDE file"]

    Ctl --> CtlA[".EXIT Stop Assembly"]
    Ctl --> CtlB[".LIST .NOLIST Listing Control"]
    Ctl --> CtlC[".DEVICE Target MCU"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — How `.DB` Moves Data from Source to Flash

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source File
    participant Asm as AVR Assembler
    participant LocCtr as Location Counter
    participant Flash as Flash Memory Image

    Src->>Asm: Reads line "MSG: .DB HELLO"
    Asm->>LocCtr: Consults current segment (.CSEG)
    LocCtr-->>Asm: Current address = 0x100
    Asm->>Asm: Encodes H E L L O as 0x48 0x45 0x4C 0x4C 0x4F
    Asm->>Flash: Writes bytes at 0x100, 0x101, 0x102, 0x103, 0x104
    Asm->>LocCtr: Increments counter by 5
    Asm-->>Src: Symbol MSG = 0x100 recorded in symbol table
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Typical AVR Assembly File

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Header["Header Block"]
        H1[".DEVICE ATmega328P"]
        H2[".INCLUDE m328Pdef.inc"]
        H3[".EQU declarations"]
    end

    subgraph DataSeg["Data Segment .DSEG"]
        D1["Buffer .BYTE 32"]
        D2["Counter .BYTE 1"]
    end

    subgraph EepSeg["EEPROM Segment .ESEG"]
        E1["Calibration .DB 0xAA 0x55"]
    end

    subgraph CodeSeg["Code Segment .CSEG"]
        C1[".ORG 0x0000 RJMP main"]
        C2["interrupt vectors .ORG 0x0034 RETI"]
        C3["main: LDI R16 hi8 ... ... RJMP main"]
    end

    Header --> DataSeg
    Header --> EepSeg
    Header --> CodeSeg
```

This mirrors the structure expected in KTU lab examinations and the typical layout students must produce in the 14-mark assembly-programming questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** List the different **AVR data formats** with one example each. Convert the hex value `$3F` to binary and decimal.

**Model Answer:**

The AVR assembler supports the following data formats:

1. **Decimal** — default, e.g., `100`
2. **Binary** — prefix `0b`, e.g., `0b1100100`
3. **Hexadecimal** — prefix `$` or `0x`, e.g., `$3F` or `0x3F`
4. **Octal** — leading `0`, e.g., `077`
5. **ASCII character** — single quotes, e.g., `'A'`
6. **ASCII string** — double quotes, e.g., `"Kerala"`

**Conversion of `$3F`:**

* Hex → Binary: $3 = 0011$, $F = 1111$ → `0b00111111`
* Hex → Decimal: $(3 \times 16) + (15 \times 1) = 48 + 15 = 63$

**Final answer:** `$3F` = `0b00111111` = **63 decimal**. **(3 marks: 1 for list, 1 for binary, 1 for decimal)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between `.EQU` and `.SET` directives. State one use-case for `.DEF`.

**Model Answer:**

| `.EQU` | `.SET` |
|---|---|
| Defines a **permanent** symbolic constant | Defines a **mutable** symbolic constant |
| **Re-definition causes an assembler error** | **Re-definition is allowed** |
| Best for hardware addresses, baud rates, etc. | Best for evolving values during assembly |
| Example: `.EQU F_CPU = 16000000` | Example: `.SET INDEX = 0` … `.SET INDEX = INDEX+1` |

**Use-case of `.DEF`:** Aliasing a CPU register for readability, e.g., `.DEF TEMP = R16` allows the programmer to write `TEMP` instead of `R16` throughout the code. **(3 marks: 1 for differences, 1 for distinguishing property, 1 for `.DEF` use)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Question]**
>
> **Question A:**
> (a) Explain any **five AVR assembler directives** with example code snippets. **(7 marks)**
> (b) With a neat diagram/code, demonstrate the use of **`.CSEG`, `.DSEG`, `.ESEG`, `.ORG`, and `.DB`** to define a string `"KTU"` in Flash, a 4-byte buffer in SRAM, and a constant `$AA` in EEPROM. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Directive 1 — `.CSEG` (Code Segment)**

Switches the assembler's output to the Flash program memory. Every subsequent `.DB` or `.DW` writes bytes to Flash.

```asm
    .CSEG
    LDI R16, 0xFF       ; This instruction lives in Flash
```

**Directive 2 — `.ORG` (Origin)**

Sets the location counter. Used heavily to place code at specific addresses such as the reset vector (`0x0000`) or interrupt vectors.

```asm
    .CSEG
    .ORG 0x0000
    RJMP main            ; Reset vector
    .ORG 0x001C
    RETI                 ; Example interrupt service
```

**Directive 3 — `.DB` (Define Byte)**

Stores one or more bytes in the active segment. Each value occupies 1 byte of Flash or EEPROM.

```asm
    .CSEG
LUT: .DB 0x00, 0x01, 0x02, 0x03
```

**Directive 4 — `.DSEG` + `.BYTE`**

`.DSEG` switches to SRAM. `.BYTE n` reserves `n` uninitialized bytes and assigns a label.

```asm
    .DSEG
buf: .BYTE 16            ; Label 'buf' = SRAM address
```

**Directive 5 — `.EQU`**

Defines a permanent constant. The assembler substitutes the value wherever the label appears.

```asm
    .EQU DELAY = 250
    LDI R20, DELAY       ; R20 = 250
```

**(Marking Guide Q.A(a)):** 5 × 1.4 ≈ 7 marks — 1.4 each for directive + example.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

```asm
; --------------------------------------------------
; AVR Assembly — Three-Segment Demonstration
; Target: ATmega328P
; --------------------------------------------------
    .DEVICE ATmega328P
    .INCLUDE "m328Pdef.inc"

    .EQU MAGIC = 0xAA            ; Symbolic constant

; ===== EEPROM SEGMENT =====
    .ESEG
    .ORG 0x0000                  ; First EEPROM byte
EEP_BYTE: .DB MAGIC              ; Store $AA at EEPROM address 0

; ===== SRAM DATA SEGMENT =====
    .DSEG
    .ORG 0x0100                  ; Place buffer at SRAM 0x100
BUFFER: .BYTE 4                  ; Reserve 4 bytes, label = BUFFER

; ===== FLASH CODE SEGMENT =====
    .CSEG
    .ORG 0x0000
    RJMP main

    .ORG 0x0068                  ; Arbitrary Flash address
MSG: .DB "KTU", 0                ; String 'K','T','U','\0' = 4 bytes

main:
    ; Initialize BUFFER with ASCII of "KTU"
    LDI ZL, low(BUFFER)
    LDI ZH, high(BUFFER)
    LDI R16, 'K'
    ST  Z+, R16
    LDI R16, 'T'
    ST  Z+, R16
    LDI R16, 'U'
    ST  Z+, R16
    LDI R16, 0
    ST  Z, R16
LOOP:
    RJMP LOOP
```

**Explanation:**

* The **`.ESEG` / `.ORG 0x0000` / `.DB MAGIC`** triplet deposits a single byte `0xAA` at EEPROM address `0x0000`. `.MAGIC` is a constant from `.EQU`, demonstrating the link between symbols and segments.
* The **`.DSEG` / `.ORG 0x0100` / `.BYTE 4`** triplet reserves 4 bytes of SRAM labelled `BUFFER` starting at SRAM `0x0100`. `.BYTE` is the only directive legal in `.DSEG`.
* The **`.CSEG` / `.DB "KTU",0`** triplet deposits the string `K T U \0` (bytes `0x4B 0x54 0x55 0x00`) into Flash starting at `0x0068`, with `MSG` as the address label usable with `LPM`.
* The runtime code demonstrates **label-to-address binding**: `LDI ZL, low(BUFFER)` loads the SRAM address of the buffer into the Z register, which is then used with `ST Z+, R16` to fill the buffer.

**(Marking Guide Q.A(b)):** 7 marks distributed as — segment demonstration × 3 (1 each), directive legality × 2, complete execution example × 2.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice):**
> (a) Explain the **AVR data formats** in detail. Convert `$7E` to binary, octal, and decimal with full step-by-step working. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Write an AVR assembly program to: store the constant `101` in a register using `LDI`, swap the nibbles of the register, and store the result in SRAM labelled `RESULT`. Use appropriate **assembler directives** (`.DEVICE`, `.INCLUDE`, `.DSEG`, `.CSEG`, `.ORG`, `.EQU`). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**AVR Data Formats — Detailed Explanation:**

*(Same six formats as in Section 2.1; 4 marks for the listing and 3 marks for the conversion, so a compact treatment here.)*

**Conversion of `$7E`:**

* Hex → Binary: $7 = 0111$, $E = 14 = 1110$ → `0b01111110`
* Hex → Octal: first convert to decimal.

$$
\$7E = (7 \times 16) + 14 = 112 + 14 = 126_{10}
$$

Now convert 126 to octal by repeated division:

$$
\begin{aligned}
126 \div 8 &= 15 \text{ rem } 6 \\
15 \div 8 &= 1 \text{ rem } 7 \\
1 \div 8 &= 0 \text{ rem } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders MSB to LSB → `0176`.

**Final answer:** `$7E` = `0b01111110` = `0176` (octal) = **126 decimal**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

```asm
; --------------------------------------------------
; Nibble-swap demo
; --------------------------------------------------
    .DEVICE ATmega328P
    .INCLUDE "m328Pdef.inc"
    .EQU VALUE = 101

    .DSEG
    .ORG 0x0200
RESULT: .BYTE 1                 ; Reserve 1 byte in SRAM

    .CSEG
    .ORG 0x0000
    RJMP main

main:
    LDI  R16, VALUE             ; R16 = 101 = 0x65 = 0110 0101
    SWAP R16                    ; R16 = 0101 0110 = 0x56
    STS  RESULT, R16            ; Store to SRAM
LOOP:
    RJMP LOOP
```

**Explanation:**

* `.EQU VALUE = 101` symbolically defines the constant.
* `LDI R16, VALUE` loads the constant into `R16` (low half).
* `SWAP R16` exchanges the upper and lower 4-bit nibbles. If R16 = `0x65`, after SWAP it becomes `0x56`.
* `STS RESULT, R16` stores the byte at the SRAM address `RESULT` (= 0x0200).
* `.DSEG` / `.BYTE` are mandatory because SRAM allocation requires them — `.DB` is illegal in `.DSEG`.

**(Marking Guide Q.B(b)):** 7 marks — directives correctness × 2.5, instruction logic × 2.5, result correctness × 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write `.DB` inside `.DSEG` — it is **illegal** and costs full marks for the sub-question.
> 2. **Do not** use a literal larger than 255 in a single `LDI` — it must be split into two `LDI` instructions and combined via shift.
> 3. **Do not** confuse `.EQU` with `.SET`. Re-defining an `.EQU` symbol is an error and often penalised.
> 4. **Always** specify the segment before placing data — forgetting `.CSEG` after `.DSEG` is a common loss-of-mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the `0b` prefix** on binary literals is the #1 reason students get "format mismatch" errors. When KTU asks for "binary format," use `0b...`, not plain digits.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — AVR Data Format & Directives**

* **Data formats**: Decimal (default), Binary (`0b`), Hex (`$` or `0x`), Octal (leading `0`), ASCII single (`' '`), ASCII string (`" "`). All are merely *syntactic sugar* for the same underlying integer constant.
* **Three memory segments** — `.CSEG` (Flash), `.DSEG` (SRAM), `.ESEG` (EEPROM) — must be **selected before** writing data into them.
* **`.ORG expr`** sets the location counter of the current segment to `expr`.
* **`.DB`** stores **1 byte** per value; **`.DW`** stores **2 bytes** (one 16-bit word). Both are illegal in `.DSEG`.
* **`.BYTE n`** reserves `n` bytes in SRAM and is the **only** way to allocate SRAM variables. Always paired with `.DSEG`.
* **`.EQU`** is a **final** constant — redefinition errors out. **`.SET`** is a **mutable** constant — redefinition allowed. **`.DEF`** aliases a **register** (R0–R31).
* **`.INCLUDE "file"`** pulls in header definitions at assembly time — it never appears in the Flash image.
* **AVR is little-endian** — multi-byte values are stored **least significant byte first**.
* **`LDI` accepts only constants in $[0, 255]$**; for 16-bit constants, use `.DW` in Flash + `LPM`, or two `LDI` + shift.
* **Directives do not consume Flash at the directive location** — only the data/instructions they describe do.
* **ASCII arithmetic** — to convert `'0'–'9'` to numeric, subtract `$30` (48); to toggle case of an alphabet character, XOR with `$20` (32).
* **KTU's favourite 3-mark questions**: list data formats, differentiate `.EQU` vs `.SET`, convert hex to binary/decimal.
* **KTU's favourite 7-mark sub-questions**: write a complete program using `.CSEG` + `.DSEG` + `.BYTE` + `.DB` showing label-to-address binding.
* **Always** show the **positional expansion** in any base-conversion — partial credit is given per term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** I/O Ports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: I/O PORTS — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PBICT504):** This module covers the **Input/Output (I/O) port architecture** of the 8051 microcontroller family, including the four bidirectional parallel ports (P0, P1, P2, P3), their internal hardware construction, the role of Special Function Registers (SFRs) like `P0`, `P1`, `P2`, `P3`, latch/buffer arrangement, pull-up resistors, and the bit-addressable nature of the ports. This forms the foundation for **embedded interfacing**.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **I/O Port** in a microcontroller is a *physical hardware interface* (group of 8 pins) that enables the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to communicate with the outside world. In the **8051 microcontroller**, four such 8-bit bidirectional ports are provided, designated as **Port 0 (P0)**, **Port 1 (P1)**, **Port 2 (P2)**, and **Port 3 (P3)**. Each port occupies an address in the **Special Function Register (SFR)** memory space (addresses `80H`, `90H`, `A0H`, `B0H` respectively) and can be independently configured as either an **input** or an **output** port under software control.

Mathematically, an 8051 port can be modeled as an **8-bit bidirectional latch-register pair**:

$$ P_x = \{D_7, D_6, D_5, D_4, D_3, D_2, D_1, D_0\}, \quad x \in \{0,1,2,3\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** All four 8051 ports are **bit-addressable** (each of the 32 bits individually accessible via bit-addresses `80H–87H`, etc.). This is a guaranteed exam question!

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Microcontroller as a Building

Imagine a microcontroller as a **secure office building (the CPU)**:

| Microcontroller Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|------------------------|-------------------|
| CPU (Internal Core) | The CEO sitting inside the office |
| I/O Pin | A **door or window** in the building wall |
| I/O Port (8 pins) | A **row of 8 doors** in a wall |
| `Px` SFR register | The **switchboard** controlling door status (open/closed) |
| `1` written to bit | Door **opened outward** (output HIGH = `+5V`) |
| `0` written to bit | Door **closed** (output LOW = `0V`) |
| Reading the pin | **Looking through a peephole** to sense outside |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Trick:** "**P**ort **P**ins **P**ush **P**ower **O**ut or **P**ull **I**n" — direction is controlled by software; the physical pins remain the same.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Reference)

| Parameter | Standard Value (8051, 5V operation) |
|-----------|------------------------------------|
| Logic HIGH Output (V_OH) | **2.4 V (minimum)**, typically 4.9 V |
| Logic LOW Output (V_OL) | **0.45 V (maximum)** |
| Logic HIGH Input (V_IH) | **2.0 V (minimum)** |
| Logic LOW Input (V_IL) | **0.8 V (maximum)** |
| Supply Voltage (V_CC) | **+5 V ± 10%** |
| I/O pin sink current (per pin) | **10 mA (typical), 20 mA absolute max** |
| I/O pin source current (per pin) | **60 µA (with internal pull-up)** |
| Total current per port | **26 mA (max safe)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Port Architecture (Bit-by-Bit)

Each I/O pin internally consists of **three key hardware blocks**:

1. **D-Latch (SFR storage element)** — Holds the data written by the CPU.
2. **Output Driver Circuit** — A pair of MOSFETs (pull-up + pull-down) that physically drive the pin HIGH or LOW.
3. **Input Buffer (Read Path)** — A tri-state buffer that connects the pin back to the internal bus for the CPU to "read" the external logic level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Writing to a port and reading from a port are **two completely different operations**, even though they use the same SFR address. The `RD` and `WR` control signals internally select different paths.

## 2.2 Port-by-Port Structural Analysis (The KTU Favourite!)

### 🔹 Port 1 (P1) — The "True" Bidirectional I/O Port (Pins 1–8)

- **Pins:** 8 pins (`P1.0` to `P1.7`).
- **Internal pull-up resistor:** ✅ Has an **internal pull-up** (~30–50 kΩ) — does NOT need external resistors.
- **Function:** Pure general-purpose I/O. **No alternate function**.
- **Configuration:**
  - To **output**: Write `1` to the corresponding bit of SFR `P1` (this disables the pull-down MOSFET, enabling the pull-up).
  - To **input**: Write `1` to the bit first (this turns OFF the output driver, making the pin "high-impedance" / floating). Then read the external value from the **pin buffer**.

$$\text{Pin Direction} = \begin{cases} \text{OUTPUT} & \text{if } P1.x = 0 \text{ (active-low output drive)} \\ \text{INPUT} & \text{if } P1.x = 1 \text{ (output driver disabled)} \end{cases}$$

### 🔹 Port 0 (P0) — The "Open-Drain" Multiplexed Port (Pins 32–39)

- **Special Role:** Used for **external memory interfacing** (carries the **multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)**) and for **expanding external ROM/RAM**.
- **Internal pull-up:** ❌ **NO internal pull-up resistor!** → Must use **external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ)** when used as a general-purpose output.
- **Configuration Switch:** Controlled by an internal **MUX**:
  - If `EA̅ = 0` or external memory is accessed → P0 acts as **Address/Data bus (no pull-up available)**.
  - Otherwise → P0 acts as a **general-purpose open-drain I/O port**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no pull-up on P0?** Because P0 must drive a **strong 0** on the bus (to switch memory chips) AND must be able to operate as a tri-state input during data reads. Adding a pull-up would conflict with bus contention.

### 🔹 Port 2 (P2) — The "High-Address" Multiplexed Port (Pins 24–31)

- **Internal pull-up:** ✅ Has internal pull-up like P1.
- **Special Role:** Provides the **high-byte address (A8–A15)** when accessing **external memory**.
- **Configuration:** Pure general-purpose I/O when external memory is NOT used; otherwise, automatically outputs high address byte.

### 🔹 Port 3 (P3) — The "Alternate-Function" Port (Pins 10–17)

- **Internal pull-up:** ✅ Has internal pull-up.
- **Special Role:** Each pin has a **dedicated alternate function** (must be enabled by the corresponding SFR latch = 1):

| Bit | Pin | Alternate Function | KTU Tag |
|-----|-----|-------------------|---------|
| P3.0 | RxD | Serial Receive Data | UART |
| P3.1 | TxD | Serial Transmit Data | UART |
| P3.2 | INT0̅ | External Interrupt 0 | Interrupts |
| P3.3 | INT1̅ | External Interrupt 1 | Interrupts |
| P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 External Input | Timers |
| P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 External Input | Timers |
| P3.6 | WR̅ | External Data Write Strobe | Bus Control |
| P3.7 | RD̅ | External Data Read Strobe | Bus Control |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**R**xD, **T**xD, **I**NT0, **I**NT1, **T**0, **T**1, **W**R̅, **R**D̅" → "**R**ead **T**he **I**nstruction **T**o **W**in **R**ecognition"

## 2.3 The Read-Modify-Write (RMW) Anomaly (HIGH-YIELD KTU TOPIC!)

The 8051 SFRs use the **Read-Modify-Write bus structure**. When you execute an instruction like `INC P1` or `CPL P1.3`, the CPU does the following sequence:

1. **Read** all 8 bits of the port pin buffer (NOT the latch).
2. **Modify** the targeted bit.
3. **Write** the entire byte back to the latch.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Trap:** If a port pin is loaded externally (e.g., a heavy capacitive load causes a slow rising edge), the **pin voltage** and the **latch value** may temporarily differ. The RMW cycle will read the **stale pin voltage** and corrupt the latch. The standard fix is to **read the SFR latch directly** by using a shadow register, or to use bit-addressable instructions carefully.

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Port bit position | Bit `n` corresponds to pin $P_x.n$ | n ∈ {0,...,7} |
| 2 | Output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH} = V_{CC} - I_{OH} \cdot R_{pull-up}$ | V (volts) |
| 3 | Output LOW current (sink) | $I_{sink} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{OL}}{R_{load}}$ | A (amperes) |
| 4 | Power dissipation per pin | $P_{pin} = V \cdot I$ | W (watts) |
| 5 | Total port sink current | $\sum I_{sink} \leq 26 \text{ mA}$ | Limiting constraint |
| 6 | Latch write (output) | `MOV P1, #data` | SFR address = 90H |
| 7 | Pin read (input) | `MOV A, P1` | Reads pin, not latch |
| 8 | Bit set | `SETB P1.0` | Bit address = 90H |
| 9 | Bit clear | `CLR P1.7` | Bit address = 97H |
| 10 | Port direction logic | Writing 1 → disables output driver → Input | Boolean Logic |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

I/O port theory is the **backbone of all embedded systems**:

- **Automotive ECUs:** 8051 ports drive relays, read sensors (temperature, pressure).
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machine controllers, microwave oven keypads, TV remote receivers.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC I/O modules use port-expander techniques (e.g., 8255 PPI with 8051) to multiply the 32 I/O lines into 192+ lines.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Even modern ARM Cortex-M0 ports (STM32, ATmega328P) inherit the same conceptual architecture (DDRx, PORTx, PINx registers — directly mapped to 8051's Px concept).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Note:** In the lab, students will be asked to **toggle an LED on P1.0** and **read a switch on P3.2**. Master the *direction logic* — it's the single most tested practical skill in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output LOW Sink Current Through a Load

**Problem (KTU Typical 14-Mark Style):** A 7-segment LED display with forward voltage $V_F = 2.1$ V and forward current $I_F = 15$ mA is connected between **+5 V (V_CC)** and an 8051 port pin configured as output LOW. Calculate the required series current-limiting resistor $R_S$ and verify whether the port is operating within the 8051's safe sink limit.

### Step 1: Identify the Circuit Topology
The circuit is a series loop: $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_S \rightarrow \text{LED Anode} \rightarrow \text{LED Cathode} \rightarrow \text{Pin (0V)} \rightarrow \text{GND}$

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:

$$ V_{CC} = V_{R_S} + V_F + V_{pin} $$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values
The port pin is pulled to **$V_{OL} \approx 0.45$ V (maximum LOW output voltage per 8051 datasheet)**. Thus:

$$ 5\text{ V} = V_{R_S} + 2.1\text{ V} + 0.45\text{ V} $$

### Step 3: Solve for the Resistor Voltage Drop
Rearranging for the unknown $V_{R_S}$:

$$ V_{R_S} = 5\text{ V} - 2.1\text{ V} - 0.45\text{ V} $$

$$ V_{R_S} = 2.45\text{ V} $$

### Step 4: Apply Ohm's Law to Find R_S
By Ohm's Law, $R_S = \dfrac{V_{R_S}}{I_F}$:

$$ R_S = \dfrac{2.45\text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}} $$

$$ R_S = 163.3\ \Omega $$

### Step 5: Select the Closest Standard E-12 Resistor Value
The standard E-12 series gives us **$R_S = 150\ \Omega$** (or 180 Ω). Choosing **150 Ω**:

$$ I_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{R_S}}{R_S} = \dfrac{2.45\text{ V}}{150\ \Omega} = 16.33\text{ mA} $$

### Step 6: Safety Verification
The 8051 datasheet specifies **maximum sink current per pin = 20 mA** and **maximum sink current per port (8 pins) = 26 mA**. Since $16.33$ mA $< 20$ mA per pin **and** we are driving only **one LED** (other 7 pins OFF), the design is **SAFE**.

$$\boxed{R_S = 150\ \Omega \quad (\text{Standard E-12 Value}), \quad I_{sink} = 16.33\text{ mA} \leq I_{max,pin} = 20\text{ mA}\ \checkmark}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: 8051 Port Pin Hardware Comparison

| Hardware Property | Port 0 | Port 1 | Port 2 | Port 3 |
|-------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Internal Pull-up** | ❌ **No** | ✅ Yes (~30 kΩ) | ✅ Yes (~30 kΩ) | ✅ Yes (~30 kΩ) |
| **Output Driver** | Open-Drain | Quasi-Bidirectional | Quasi-Bidirectional | Quasi-Bidirectional |
| **Output Type** | Can sink AND source | Strong LOW, weak HIGH | Strong LOW, weak HIGH | Strong LOW, weak HIGH |
| **Alternate Function** | AD0–AD7 (Bus) | None | A8–A15 (Address) | RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/T1, RD̅, WR̅ |
| **External Resistor Needed?** | **YES** (10 kΩ pull-up) | NO | NO | NO |
| **Address (SFR)** | 80H | 90H | A0H | B0H |
| **Bit-Addressable?** | ✅ Yes (80H–87H) | ✅ Yes (90H–97H) | ✅ Yes (A0H–A7H) | ✅ Yes (B0H–B7H) |
| **Reset State** | FFH (floating) | FFH (weak HIGH) | FFH (weak HIGH) | FFH (weak HIGH) |
| **External Memory Use** | Multiplexed Addr/Data | ❌ Never | High Address Byte | RD̅/WR̅ strobes |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Dissipation Per Port Pin

The power consumed by a single output pin driving a load is given by:

$$ P_{pin} = V_{OL} \cdot I_{sink} $$

For the LED example above:

$$ P_{pin} = 0.45\text{ V} \times 16.33\text{ mA} = 7.35\text{ mW} $$

The **total port power** (with 8 LEDs, each drawing 16.33 mA when ON simultaneously):

$$ P_{port} = 8 \times 7.35\text{ mW} = 58.8\text{ mW} $$

This is well within the **maximum power dissipation rating of 1 W** for the 40-pin PDIP 8051 package.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Trick:** Examiners often ask: *"Why is P0 called 'true' bidirectional but P1, P2, P3 are 'quasi-bidirectional'?"* The answer lies in the **internal pull-up structure**: P1/P2/P3 use a weak pull-up FET in parallel with a strong pull-down FET, meaning the pin can NEVER be truly tri-stated without external hardware. P0, lacking a pull-up, CAN be made truly tri-state by switching off both the pull-up and pull-down FETs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Structure of a Single 8051 Port Pin (Port 1.x as Reference Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[Internal CPU Bus 8-bit] -->|Write Data| Latch[D-Latch SFR Storage]
    CPU -->|Read Enable| InBuf[Tri-State Input Buffer]
    Latch -->|Q Output| DriverCtrl[Output Driver Control Logic]
    DriverCtrl --> PDMOS[Pull-Down N-MOSFET Strong]
    DriverCtrl --> PUMOS[Pull-Up P-MOSFET Weak Internal ~30k to 50k Ohm]
    PDMOS --> PinNode
    PUMOS --> PinNode
    PinNode[Physical Pin P1.x] --> ExternalLoad[External Load LED Relay Sensor]
    PinNode --> InBuf
    InBuf -->|Pin State Readback| CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Mapping:** Notice the **two parallel MOSFET paths** from VCC and GND to the physical pin. The internal pull-up is intentionally weak (high resistance) so it can be overpowered easily by an external driver, allowing the pin to act as an input.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 8051 Port System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SFR_Space[Special Function Register Memory Space 80H to FFH]
        P0Reg[Port 0 SFR 80H]
        P1Reg[Port 1 SFR 90H]
        P2Reg[Port 2 SFR A0H]
        P3Reg[Port 3 SFR B0H]
    end
    CPU[8051 CPU Core] -->|Address Bus| SFR_Space
    CPU -->|Data Bus| SFR_Space
    P0Reg --> P0Hw[Port 0 Hardware Pins 32 to 39 Open Drain AD0 to AD7 Bus]
    P1Reg --> P1Hw[Port 1 Hardware Pins 1 to 8 General Purpose IO]
    P2Reg --> P2Hw[Port 2 Hardware Pins 24 to 31 High Address A8 to A15]
    P3Reg --> P3Hw[Port 3 Hardware Pins 10 to 17 Alternate Functions]
    P0Hw --> ExtMem[External Memory ROM RAM]
    P1Hw --> LEDs[LEDs Switches Keypad]
    P2Hw --> ExtMem
    P3Hw --> UART[UART INT Timers]
```

---

## 4.3 Flowchart: Configuring a Port Pin as Input vs Output

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Student writes 8051 assembly code]) --> Decision1{Pin Direction?}
    Decision1 -->|Output LOW| OpA[CLR Px.y Bit Instruction]
    Decision1 -->|Output HIGH| OpB[SETB Px.y Bit Instruction]
    Decision1 -->|Input| OpC[SETB Px.y first to disable output driver]
    OpC --> OpD[Then MOV A Px to read external value]
    OpA --> Result[Pin sinks current to GND]
    OpB --> Result2[Pin sources current weakly via pull-up]
    OpD --> Result3[CPU receives external logic level in Accumulator]
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Memory-Mapped I/O (KTU High-Yield Concept)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AddrBus[16-bit Address Bus 0000H to FFFFH] --> Decoder[Address Decoder Logic]
    Decoder -->|80H| SFR_P0
    Decoder -->|90H| SFR_P1
    Decoder -->|A0H| SFR_P2
    Decoder -->|B0H| SFR_P3
    Decoder -->|0000H to 1FFFH| ExtROM[External ROM 8051 EA pin low]
    Decoder -->|2000H to 3FFFH| ExtRAM[External RAM]
    SFR_P0[Port 0 SFR] --> IO1[Physical I/O Lines]
    SFR_P1[Port 1 SFR] --> IO1
    SFR_P2[Port 2 SFR] --> IO1
    SFR_P3[Port 3 SFR] --> IO1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The above diagram illustrates **Memory-Mapped I/O** — a key architectural principle where I/O ports are accessed **using the same instructions as memory** (e.g., `MOV P1, A`). This is in contrast to the older **Isolated I/O** (used in 8086 with separate IN/OUT instructions).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Explain why Port 0 requires external pull-up resistors but Ports 1, 2, and 3 do not.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Port 0 is designed as an **8-bit open-drain bidirectional I/O port** that is multiplexed with the external **Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)**. To avoid bus contention during external memory accesses, P0 has **no internal pull-up resistor**. In contrast, Ports 1, 2, and 3 each contain an **internal weak pull-up FET (~30–50 kΩ)**, eliminating the need for external resistors. When P0 is used as a general-purpose output, the designer **must connect external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ)** to V_CC for proper logic-HIGH operation. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** List the alternate functions of Port 3 pins. Why is it necessary to set the corresponding SFR bit to `1` before using an alternate function?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The alternate functions of Port 3 are:

| Pin | Function |
|-----|----------|
| P3.0 | RxD (Serial Input) |
| P3.1 | TxD (Serial Output) |
| P3.2 | INT0̅ (External Interrupt 0) |
| P3.3 | INT1̅ (External Interrupt 1) |
| P3.4 | T0 (Timer 0 Input) |
| P3.5 | T1 (Timer 1 Input) |
| P3.6 | WR̅ (External Write) |
| P3.7 | RD̅ (External Read) |

Setting the SFR bit to `1` **disables the output driver's pull-down MOSFET**, making the pin a "quasi-bidirectional" line that can be driven by the alternate-function peripheral. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔸 **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal hardware structure of **Port 1** of the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss how it acts as a bidirectional port.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly language program to toggle **all 8 bits of Port 1** continuously with a 100 ms delay, using Timer 0 in **Mode 1**. Assume an oscillator frequency of **11.0592 MHz**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The 8051 **Port 1** consists of 8 identical I/O lines (P1.0 to P1.7). Each bit has the following internal hardware structure:

1. **D-Latch (bit-addressable):** Stores the value written by the CPU via SFR `P1` (address 90H).
2. **Output Driver Stage:** Consists of:
   - A **strong N-MOSFET pull-down** transistor (Q1) connected to GND.
   - A **weak P-MOSFET pull-up** transistor (Q2) connected to V_CC through an internal resistor (~30 kΩ).
3. **Read Buffer (Tri-state):** Connected from the physical pin back to the internal CPU bus for the "Read Pin" operation.

**Bidirectional Operation:**

- **As Output (Logic 0):** Writing `0` to the latch turns ON the strong N-MOSFET, which pulls the pin to **GND** (V_OL ≤ 0.45 V). The pin can sink up to **20 mA**.
- **As Output (Logic 1):** Writing `1` to the latch turns OFF the N-MOSFET and turns ON the weak P-MOSFET, raising the pin to **V_CC - V_T** (V_OH ≈ 4.9 V). The pin sources only a **weak current (≈ 60 µA)**.
- **As Input:** Writing `1` to the latch **first** turns OFF the N-MOSFET (output driver disabled), placing the pin in **high-impedance (floating) state**. The external device then drives the pin, and the CPU reads the state via the input buffer using `MOV A, P1`.

```
V_CC
   |
  [P-MOS Weak Pull-up ~30kΩ]
   |
  ---- Pin P1.x  -----> External World
   |
  [N-MOS Strong Pull-down]
   |
  GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Keyword to use in exam:** "Quasi-bidirectional" — P1 cannot be truly tri-stated; when configured as input, the weak pull-up is still active (this is why an open switch reads as `1`).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Objective:** Continuously toggle P1 with 100 ms delay using Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), at 11.0592 MHz.

**Step 1: Calculate Timer Reload Value**
- Machine cycle period = **12 / 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs**
- Required counts for 100 ms:

$$ N = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s}} = 92{,}160 \text{ counts} $$

- Timer 0 is **16-bit (Mode 1)**, max count = 65,536.
- Since 92,160 > 65,536, we must use **multiple overflows**.

$$ 92{,}160 = k \times 65{,}536 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 1.406 $$

So we need **2 overflows + remainder**:

$$ \text{Residual counts} = 92{,}160 - 65{,}536 = 26{,}624 $$

- Reload value for 26,624 counts:

$$ TH0 = \frac{65{,}536 - 26{,}624}{256} = \frac{38{,}912}{256} = 152 = 98H $$

$$ TL0 = (65{,}536 - 26{,}624) \mod 256 = 38{,}912 \mod 256 = 0 = 00H $$

**Step 2: 8051 Assembly Code**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        CPL  02H               ; Toggle overflow flag bit

MAIN:   MOV  TMOD, #01H        ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV  TH0, #98H
        MOV  TL0, #00H
        SETB TR0               ; Start Timer 0
        MOV  IE,  #82H         ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB 02H               ; User flag in bit-addressable RAM

LOOP:   JNB  02H, LOOP         ; Wait until 100 ms elapsed
        CPL  02H
        CPL  P1                ; Toggle ALL 8 bits of Port 1
        SJMP LOOP

ISR_T0: MOV  TH0, #98H         ; Reload timer
        MOV  TL0, #00H
        RETI
        END
```

---

### 🔸 **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the structures of **Port 0** and **Port 1** in the 8051. Why is Port 0 used for the external Address/Data bus?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an 8051-based system to **read 8 switches** connected to Port 2 and display their status on **8 LEDs** connected to Port 1. Show the circuit and write the assembly program.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

| Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Internal pull-up | ❌ None (open-drain) | ✅ Yes (~30 kΩ) |
| External pull-up needed | Yes (10 kΩ) | No |
| Output current sink | 20 mA | 20 mA |
| Output current source | 0 (open-drain) | ~60 µA |
| Alternate function | AD0–AD7 (Address/Data bus) | None |
| Used for external memory | YES (lower address + data) | NO |
| Configuration | True bidirectional (tri-state capable) | Quasi-bidirectional |

**Why P0 is used for the external Address/Data bus:**

1. **Open-drain nature:** P0's output driver can be turned completely OFF (tri-state), which is **essential** for the bidirectional Address/Data bus. The CPU must be able to **release the bus** when external memory is driving data back during a READ cycle.
2. **No internal pull-up:** This allows external memory chips to **actively drive the bus** without fighting against an internal pull-up current.
3. **High drive strength:** P0 has a strong N-MOSFET pull-down, ensuring clean logic LOW on a heavily loaded bus.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner for the exam:** *"P0 is used for the Address/Data bus because it is the only 8051 port that can achieve a true tri-state (high-impedance) condition, which is mandatory for bidirectional bus sharing."*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Circuit Description:**

- **8 Switches (SW1–SW8)** connected between P2.0–P2.7 and GND. **External 10 kΩ pull-up resistors** to +5V on each P2 pin (to ensure defined logic levels when switch is open).
- **8 LEDs (LED1–LED8)** connected with **anodes to +5V (via 150 Ω current-limiting resistor)** and **cathodes to P1.0–P1.7**. (Active-LOW configuration.)
- **Common GND** for the entire circuit.

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
MAIN:   MOV  P2, #0FFH         ; Configure P2 as input (write 1s)
        MOV  P1, #0FFH         ; All LEDs OFF initially (active-low)

LOOP:   MOV  A, P2             ; Read switch status from P2
        CPL  A                 ; Invert (since switches are active-LOW)
        MOV  P1, A             ; Display on LEDs
        SJMP LOOP              ; Continuous polling
        END
```

**Working:** When a switch is **OPEN**, the pull-up holds the P2 pin at `1`. The corresponding LED will be **OFF** (because the bit sent to P1 is `1` in the inverted form... wait, let me recheck).

**Corrected logic with active-LOW LEDs:**

- Switch open → P2.x = 1 (pull-up)
- After `CPL A` → A.x = 0
- Sent to P1 → P1.x = 0 → LED **ON** ❌ (wrong)

**Correction:** Use **active-HIGH LEDs** (anode to P1, cathode to GND via resistor). Then:

- Switch open → P2.x = 1 → after `CPL A` → 0 → LED OFF ✅
- Switch closed → P2.x = 0 → after `CPL A` → 1 → LED ON ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to write `1` to P2 before reading** → The output driver fights with the external switch, causing erratic behavior. **This loses 2–3 marks easily.**
> 2. **Confusing "read latch" vs "read pin"** — `MOV A, P1` reads the **pin**, not the **latch**. If you want to read the latch (to avoid RMW anomalies), you must first copy `P1` into a register.
> 3. **Forgetting pull-up resistors on P0** when used as general-purpose output — the bus will not produce a proper logic HIGH.
> 4. **Confusing `SETB P3.0` (latch operation) with `MOV P3, #01H` (byte operation)** — they look similar but have different bus cycle effects.
> 5. **SFR bit-address calculation errors** — Bit `P3.5` is at bit address `B5H`, NOT `B0H + 5` directly (use the standard KTU bit-address table).

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ 8051 has **4 ports** of **8 bits each** = **32 I/O lines**.
- ✅ All ports are **bit-addressable** (SFR addresses: P0=`80H`, P1=`90H`, P2=`A0H`, P3=`B0H`).
- ✅ **Port 0** is the **only port with NO internal pull-up**; requires **10 kΩ external pull-up** for general-purpose output.
- ✅ **Port 0** carries the **multiplexed AD0–AD7 bus** for external memory.
- ✅ **Port 2** carries the **high address byte A8–A15** for external memory.
- ✅ **Port 3** has **8 alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅).
- ✅ **Port 1** is the "purest" general-purpose port — no alternate function, has internal pull-up.
- ✅ To configure a pin as **input**, you must first write `1` to it (to disable the output driver).
- ✅ **Reading the pin** (`MOV A, P1`) ≠ **Reading the latch** — be aware of the **Read-Modify-Write problem**.
- ✅ **Output LOW** = strong drive (sinks 20 mA). **Output HIGH** = weak drive (sources ~60 µA).
- ✅ Maximum current per **port** (8 pins) = **26 mA**. Maximum per **package** = **150 mA** or **1 W power dissipation**.
- ✅ Reset value of all ports = **`FFH`** (floating for P0, weak HIGH for P1/P2/P3).
- ✅ I/O in 8051 uses **Memory-Mapped I/O** — same `MOV` instructions as RAM access.
- ✅ **True bidirectional** = P0 (can fully tri-state). **Quasi-bidirectional** = P1, P2, P3 (weak pull-up always present).
- ✅ **V_OL ≤ 0.45 V**, **V_OH ≥ 2.4 V** — the standard TTL-compatible logic thresholds.
- ✅ For LED driving, **always use active-LOW configuration** (current flows INTO the 8051 pin when LOW) — safer for the chip's thermal budget.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counters and Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Counters and Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Timer** in a microcontroller is a hardware peripheral that generates precise time delays by counting internal oscillator pulses at a fixed, known frequency. A **Counter** is the same hardware register used to count **external events** applied to a dedicated pin, rather than internal clock pulses. In the 8051 architecture, **Timers 0 and 1** are dual-purpose 16-bit up-counters that can be configured to function either as timers (counting machine cycles) or as counters (counting transitions on the `T0` / `T1` pins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Counters and Timers in the 8051 microcontroller — including the **TMOD** and **TCON** Special Function Registers (SFRs), the four operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3), and applications such as delay generation, event counting, and Pulse Width Modulation (PWM).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stopwatch & The Tally Counter

Imagine you are standing at a school sports day with two devices on your table:

- **A digital stopwatch** (the **Timer**) — it ticks forward on its own, driven by an internal crystal vibration. You pre-set a starting time, and when the time elapses, an alarm rings. The microcontroller's Timer works exactly like this: it counts **internal machine cycles** derived from the crystal oscillator and overflows (or matches a value) to signal that a known time interval has elapsed.

- **A hand-held tally counter** (the **Counter**) — it does not tick on its own; instead, every time you press the button (an **external event**), the count increments by one. The Counter mode of the 8051 works the same way: each **1-to-0 transition** on the `T0` or `T1` pin increments the register.

The genius of the 8051 is that **both functions share the same physical hardware** — a 16-bit register pair (TH + TL). You simply choose its "personality" via a configuration bit.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Machine Cycle Frequency** = **Oscillator Frequency ÷ 12**
- **One machine cycle period** = **12 / Oscillator Frequency (Hz)**
- **Maximum count (16-bit)** = $2^{16} = 65536$ states ($0000\text{H}$ to $\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$)
- **Standard KTU evaluation frequency** = **11.0592 MHz** (chosen because it is an exact multiple of standard baud rates)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** It divides evenly by 12, 32, and 64, producing exact 9600, 4800, and 2400 baud rates with zero error. This is why KTU board questions overwhelmingly use this frequency instead of a round 12 MHz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8051 Timer/Counter Hardware Block — Operational Logic

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counter registers** built from two pairs of 8-bit SFRs:

| Register Pair | High Byte | Low Byte | External Pin (Counter Mode) |
|---------------|-----------|----------|------------------------------|
| Timer 0       | **TH0**   | **TL0**  | **P3.4 (T0)**                |
| Timer 1       | **TH1**   | **TL1**  | **P3.5 (T1)**                |

Each 16-bit register is **incremented by one** for every input pulse, and the pulse source is selected via the **C/T̄** bit in the **TMOD** register.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Pulse Source Selection (C/T̄ bit in TMOD)**
- If **C/T̄ = 0** → The register receives pulses from the **internal oscillator divider** (divided by 12). The peripheral is in **Timer Mode**.
- If **C/T̄ = 1** → The register receives pulses from the **external pin T0/T1**. The peripheral is in **Counter Mode**.

**Step 2 — Prescaler / Frequency Divider**
The 8051 does not have a programmable prescaler like modern microcontrollers. Instead, the fixed divide-by-12 network is always active in Timer mode. This is the **"Why"** behind the 12 machine cycles per instruction cycle design of the original 8051.

**Step 3 — Mode Selection (M1, M0 bits in TMOD)**
The four modes dictate the counter width and behaviour on overflow:
- **Mode 0** → 13-bit timer (8048 legacy mode — rarely used)
- **Mode 1** → 16-bit timer (most flexible delay generator)
- **Mode 2** → 8-bit auto-reload timer (perfect for baud-rate generation)
- **Mode 3** → Split timer (Timer 0 becomes two 8-bit timers)

**Step 4 — Start/Stop Control (TR0/TR1 in TCON)**
Setting `TR0 = 1` starts Timer 0; clearing it stops the count. The TR bit is **software-controlled** (no external pin gating in the original 8051).

**Step 5 — Overflow Detection (TF0/TF1 in TCON)**
When the count rolls over from $\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ to $0000_{\text{H}}$, the hardware **automatically sets the TFx flag** in TCON. This flag can be polled, or it can be configured to trigger an **interrupt** (the Timer Interrupt).

**The "Why" Behind Overflow:** A 16-bit register has only $2^{16} = 65536$ discrete states. It cannot hold a count of 65537 — so it wraps to zero, simultaneously raising a "ceiling-hit" flag (TFx). This overflow IS the timing event.

### 2.3 The TMOD Register (89H) — Bit-by-Bit Anatomy

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| GATE  | C/T̄  | M1    | M0    | GATE  | C/T̄  | M1    | M0    |
| \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} |

- **GATE = 1** → Timer runs only when software sets `TRx` **AND** the corresponding `INTx` pin is HIGH (hardware gating).
- **GATE = 0** → Timer runs whenever `TRx = 1` (software gating only).

### 2.4 The TCON Register (88H) — The Control & Flag Byte

| TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |

- **TFx** = Timer Overflow Flag (set by hardware, cleared by software)
- **TRx** = Timer Run control bit (set/cleared by software)
- **IEx / ITx** = External Interrupt Edge/Flag bits

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Frequency | $f_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Timer Increment Period | $T_{\text{inc}} = T_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds per count |
| 4 | Total Count Required (Mode 1) | $N = \text{FFFF}_{\text{H}} - \text{Initial Value} + 1$ | counts |
| 5 | Total Count Required (Mode 2) | $N = 256 - \text{Reload Value}$ | counts |
| 6 | Delay Generated | $T_{\text{delay}} = N \times T_{\text{inc}}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Maximum Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{\text{max}} = 65536 \times \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Reload Value (Mode 2) | $\text{Reload} = 256 - N$ | unsigned 8-bit |
| 9 | Initial Value (Mode 1) | $\text{IV} = 65536 - N$ | unsigned 16-bit |
| 10 | Counter Mode Frequency | $f_{\text{count}} = \dfrac{\text{External Events}}{\text{second}}$ | Hz (user-supplied) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) is universally used to generate **Serial Port Baud Rate Clocks** for UART communication. Mode 1 (16-bit) is used for **long millisecond delays** (e.g., blinking an LED at 1 Hz). Counter mode is used in applications like **visitor counters, object counters, RPM measurement** (with an encoder), and **frequency measurement**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Delay Calculation from a Given Initial Value (Mode 1)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Form):** The 8051 uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Timer 0 is configured in **Mode 1** with initial value **FC18H**. Compute the delay generated. Assume the timer is started from 0 and the polling method is used (single overflow, then stop).

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle period.**
The oscillator is divided by 12 to form the machine cycle, which is also the timer increment period.

$$
T_{\text{inc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerically evaluate the period.**

$$
T_{\text{inc}} = \frac{12}{11059200} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s} \quad (\text{exactly } 12/11.0592\,\mu\text{s})
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the initial value to decimal.**
The initial value $\text{FC18}_{\text{H}}$ must be subtracted from the maximum 16-bit count $\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ to find the number of increment events until overflow.

$$
\text{FC18}_{\text{H}} = 15 \times 16^{3} + 12 \times 16^{2} + 1 \times 16^{1} + 8 \times 16^{0}
$$

Expanding each positional coefficient explicitly:

$$
\text{FC18}_{\text{H}} = 61440 + 3072 + 16 + 8 = 64536_{10}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total count $N$.**
The number of timer increments required to roll over from $64536_{10}$ to $65536_{10}$ is:

$$
N = 65536 - 64536 = 1000 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total delay.**

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = N \times T_{\text{inc}} = 1000 \times 1.085 \, \mu\text{s} = 1085 \, \mu\text{s} = 1.085 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification via hex subtraction (KTU Board Method).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}} - \text{FC18}_{\text{H}} &= \text{03E7}_{\text{H}} \\
\text{03E7}_{\text{H}} &= 3 \times 16^{2} + 14 \times 16^{1} + 7 \times 16^{0} \\
&= 768 + 224 + 7 = 999 \text{ (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Off-by-one correction:** The exact overflow count is $N + 1 = 1000$ because the transition from $64536 \rightarrow 64537 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow 65535 \rightarrow 0$ includes **1000 distinct increments**, not 999. KTU evaluators accept the $N+1$ convention; this is the most common mark-deduction point.

**Final Result:** $T_{\text{delay}} = 1.085 \text{ ms}$ exactly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Initial Value Calculation for a Desired Delay (Reverse Problem)

**Problem Statement:** Using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal, configure Timer 1 in **Mode 1** to generate a delay of **50 ms**.

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{\text{inc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required count $N$.**

$$
N = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{inc}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}082.95
$$

**Step 3 — Round to the nearest integer count.**
Since the timer can only count discrete events, we round down to $N = 46{,}082$.

**Step 4 — Compute the 16-bit initial value.**

$$
\text{IV} = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46082 = 19454
$$

**Step 5 — Convert $19454_{10}$ to hexadecimal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
19454 \div 16 &= 1216 \text{ remainder } 14 \quad (E) \\
1216 \div 16 &= 76 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (0) \\
76 \div 16 &= 4 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad (C) \\
4 \div 16 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \quad (4)
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders from bottom to top: $\text{IV} = \mathbf{4C0E_{H}}$.

**Step 6 — Split into TH1 and TL1 for SFR loading.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH1} &= \text{4C}_{\text{H}} \quad (\text{high byte}) \\
\text{TL1} &= \text{0E}_{\text{H}} \quad (\text{low byte})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Actual delay achieved (verification).**

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = 46082 \times 1.085 \, \mu\text{s} = 49.998 \, \text{ms} \approx 50 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) Baud Rate Generator

**Problem Statement:** Generate a **9600 baud** UART clock using Timer 1 in **Mode 2 auto-reload** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard UART baud-rate formula.**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

Assuming the standard `SMOD = 0`:

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the denominator.**

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 9600} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the reload value.**

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FD}_{\text{H}}
$$

**Result:** Loading TH1 = TL1 = $\text{FD}_{\text{H}}$ produces **exactly 9600 baud with 0% error** — this is why 11.0592 MHz is the gold standard for serial communication in 8051 designs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — All Four Operating Modes Compared

| Mode | Width | Reload Behaviour | Typical Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|------|-------|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Mode 0 | 13-bit (TH:5-bits + TL:8-bits) | Manual reload by software | Legacy 8048 compatibility | Rare |
| Mode 1 | 16-bit (TH:8-bits + TL:8-bits) | Manual reload by software | **Long delays (ms range)** | **Most Common** |
| Mode 2 | 8-bit (TL only) | **Auto-reload from TH** | **Baud-rate generation** | **Most Common** |
| Mode 3 | Two 8-bit (Timer 0 only) | Manual reload | Split independent timers | Advanced |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Timer/Counter Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc[Oscillator f_osc]:::src
    div12[Divide by 12 Network]:::logic
    ctsel{C/T_bar bit in TMOD}:::mux
    tint[Internal Machine Cycle Pulse]:::pulse
    text[External Pin T0 or T1]:::pulse
    gate{GATE bit logic}:::mux
    tr[TRx software start bit]:::ctrl
    intpin[INTx pin hardware gate]:::pulse
    orx[OR gate combiner]:::logic
    counter[16-bit Up Counter THx TLx]:::reg
    ovf[Overflow Detector]:::logic
    tcon[TCON Flag TFx]:::flag
    int[Interrupt Vector 0Bh 1Bh]:::flag
    app[Application Delay or Event Count]:::app

    osc --> div12 --> tint
    text --> ctsel
    tint --> ctsel
    ctsel -- C/T_bar = 0 Timer --> gate
    ctsel -- C/T_bar = 1 Counter --> gate
    tr --> orx
    intpin --> orx
    orx --> gate
    gate --> counter
    counter --> ovf
    ovf -- sets --> tcon
    ovf -- triggers --> int
    counter --> app

    classDef src fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1d33,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#3a5f8f,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    classDef mux fill:#d97706,stroke:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    classDef pulse fill:#047857,stroke:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef flag fill:#be185d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff
    classDef app fill:#475569,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mode Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Configure Timer]:::start
    q1{Need baud rate clock for UART?}:::mux
    m2[Mode 2 8-bit Auto-Reload]:::mode
    q2{Delay greater than 256 us at 12 MHz?}:::mux
    m1[Mode 1 16-bit Manual Reload]:::mode
    q3{Using Timer 0 and need two independent 8-bit timers?}:::mux
    m3[Mode 3 Split Timer]:::mode
    m0[Mode 0 13-bit Legacy]:::mode

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> m2
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> m1
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> m3
    q3 -- No --> m0

    classDef start fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef mux fill:#d97706,stroke:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    classDef mode fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mode 2 Auto-Reload Mechanism (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    init[Software loads THx and TLx with same value]:::start
    run[TRx = 1 Counter starts]:::start
    tl[TLx increments on each pulse]:::reg
    check{TLx rolled over from FF to 00?}:::mux
    flag[Set TFx flag]:::flag
    copy[Hardware automatically copies THx into TLx]:::logic
    cont[Counter continues without CPU intervention]:::start
    stop[TRx = 0 to halt]:::ctrl

    init --> run --> tl --> check
    check -- No --> tl
    check -- Yes --> flag
    flag --> copy
    copy --> cont
    cont --> tl
    cont -. User decides .-> stop

    classDef start fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef mux fill:#d97706,stroke:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    classDef flag fill:#be185d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#3a5f8f,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between the Timer mode and Counter mode of operation in the 8051 microcontroller. Mention the relevant register bit used for selection.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Pulse Source** | Internal machine cycle (oscillator ÷ 12) | External pin T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) |
| **Selection Bit** | C/T̄ = **0** in TMOD | C/T̄ = **1** in TMOD |
| **Trigger Event** | Every machine cycle (≈ 1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz) | 1-to-0 transition on the external pin |
| **Application** | Delay generation, baud-rate clocks, periodic interrupts | Event counting, frequency measurement, RPM sensing |

The **C/T̄ bit** located in the **TMOD (89H) SFR** is the single configuration bit that toggles the hardware between these two personalities. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of the GATE bit in the TMOD register? How does it affect timer operation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **GATE bit** in TMOD enables **hardware gating** of the timer run control.

- **GATE = 0** → Timer is controlled purely by software via the `TRx` bit in TCON. As soon as `TRx = 1`, the timer starts counting; clearing `TRx` stops it. This is the **normal mode** used in 99% of applications. **(1 mark)**

- **GATE = 1** → Timer runs only when BOTH conditions are simultaneously satisfied: (i) `TRx = 1` in software, AND (ii) the corresponding `INTx` pin (P3.2 for Timer 0, P3.3 for Timer 1) is HIGH. **(2 marks)**

This hardware-gating feature is used to **measure the pulse width of an external signal** — the timer counts only while the INTx pin is HIGH, giving a direct digital measurement of the external pulse duration.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error in count N:** Always remember the overflow requires $N+1$ increments. If you write $N = \text{FFFF} - \text{IV}$ directly, the delay will be short by one machine cycle per overflow. In 50 ms questions, this is negligible, but in short-delay questions, expect 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong oscillator frequency:** KTU questions **always default to 11.0592 MHz** unless stated otherwise. Using 12 MHz by default costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to split THx/TLx:** Many students write the 16-bit initial value in one go. The board expects explicit `MOV THx, #high_byte` and `MOV TLx, #low_byte` instructions.
> 4. **Confusing SMOD bit:** The UART baud-rate formula contains a $2^{\text{SMOD}}$ factor. If the question does not state SMOD, assume **SMOD = 0**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Delay Generation in Mode 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With an **11.0592 MHz** crystal, calculate the initial value to be loaded into **Timer 0 (Mode 1)** to generate a delay of **exactly 30 ms**. Split the value into TH0 and TL0. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the 8051 assembly program to generate a continuous square wave of **50 Hz** on **P1.0** using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with polling. Assume a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Initial Value Calculation for 30 ms Delay — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{\text{inc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Required count.**

$$
N = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 27{,}649.77 \approx 27{,}650 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial value (decimal).**

$$
\text{IV} = 65536 - 27650 = 37{,}886_{10}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hexadecimal (successive division by 16).**

$$
37886 \div 16 = 2367 \text{ R } 14 \;(E) \\
2367 \div 16 = 147 \text{ R } 15 \;(F) \\
147 \div 16 = 9 \text{ R } 3 \\
9 \div 16 = 0 \text{ R } 9
$$

Reading bottom-up: $\text{IV} = \mathbf{93FE_{H}}$.

**Step 5 — Split into SFR bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TH0} &= \text{93}_{\text{H}} \\
\text{TL0} &= \text{FE}_{\text{H}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $65536 - 37886 = 27650$; delay $= 27650 \times 1.085 \, \mu\text{s} = 30.000 \, \text{ms}$ ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) 50 Hz Square Wave on P1.0 — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Determine half-period.**
A 50 Hz square wave has period $T = 1/50 = 20 \text{ ms}$. Each HIGH or LOW half is $10 \text{ ms}$.

**Step 2 — Required count for 10 ms.**

$$
N = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 9216 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial value.**

$$
\text{IV} = 65536 - 9216 = 56320_{10} = \text{DC00}_{\text{H}}
$$

So $\text{TH0} = \text{DC}_{\text{H}}$, $\text{TL0} = \text{00}_{\text{H}}$.

**Step 4 — Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV TMOD, #01H       ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software start
BACK:   MOV TH0, #0DCH       ; Load high byte (1 mark)
        MOV TL0, #00H        ; Load low byte  (1 mark)
        SETB TR0             ; Start Timer 0  (0.5 mark)
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE        ; Wait for overflow (0.5 mark)
        CLR TR0              ; Stop timer (0.5 mark)
        CLR TF0              ; Clear overflow flag (0.5 mark)
        CPL P1.0             ; Toggle P1.0 (1 mark)
        SJMP BACK            ; Repeat forever (1 mark)
        END
```

The `CPL P1.0` instruction toggles the pin, producing a perfect 50 % duty-cycle square wave. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Counter Mode Application

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how an **8051 microcontroller** can be used to count the number of **visitors entering a hall** through a sensor that produces one LOW pulse per entry. Use **Timer 1 in Counter Mode (Mode 1)**. State the role of the **C/T̄** and **GATE** bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sensor outputs **1500 pulses per minute** on the **T0 pin**. The 8051 uses a **12 MHz** crystal. Configure Timer 0 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** to count these pulses for exactly **1 second** and trigger an action when 1500 pulses have arrived. Compute the TH0 reload value. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Visitor Counter Using Timer 1 — 7 Marks**

**Hardware Connection:** The IR/optical sensor output is wired to the **T1 pin (P3.5)**. An open-collector sensor (like an LM393 comparator) is pulled up via a 10 kΩ resistor to +5 V. Each time a visitor crosses the beam, the sensor output produces a single LOW-going pulse.

**TMOD Configuration:** Timer 1 is configured with:
- **M1 M0 = 0 1** → Mode 1 (16-bit counter)
- **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter mode (external pin pulses, NOT internal machine cycle) — **(1 mark)**
- **GATE = 0** → Software-only run control (TR1 alone starts the timer) — **(1 mark)**

So the TMOD value is `#11010000B = #D0H`.

**Operating Logic:** The program initializes `TH1 = TL1 = 00H`, sets `TR1 = 1`, and continuously monitors the **TF1 flag** in TCON. Each LOW-to-HIGH-to-LOW transition on T1 increments the (TH1, TL1) pair. After 65,536 pulses, the register overflows to zero and TF1 is set. A single 16-bit register can therefore count up to **65,535 visitors** between overflows.

**Sample Program Sketch:**
```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #50H      ; Timer1, Mode1, Counter, no gate
        MOV TH1, #00H
        MOV TL1, #00H
        SETB TR1
WAIT:   JNB TF1, WAIT       ; Wait for overflow (65536 visitors)
        CLR TR1
        CLR TF1
        ; Increment display memory location here
        SJMP RELOAD
```

**Role of C/T̄:** Selects internal (timer) vs. external (counter) pulse source. **(1 mark)**
**Role of GATE:** When = 0, allows pure software control; when = 1, requires INT1 pin HIGH. **(1 mark)**
Marks remaining for diagram, program, and explanation. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b) TH0 Reload Value for 1500-Pulse Counter Window — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{\text{inc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Important conceptual note.**
In **Counter Mode** (C/T̄ = 1), the value of $T_{\text{inc}}$ is **irrelevant** because the counter advances on **external pin edges**, not on internal machine cycles. The reload value is determined purely by **how many external pulses we want to count before overflow**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Determine the required count.**
We want the timer to overflow **after exactly 1500 pulses** have arrived at the T0 pin.

$$
N = 1500 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the initial (and reload) value in Mode 2.**
Mode 2 is an **8-bit auto-reload timer** — only TL0 counts, and TH0 is the reload register. So the effective count width is 256.

Since 1500 > 256, **a single Mode-2 cycle is insufficient** (it would overflow ~5.86 times). The proper approach is to **either** use Mode 1 (16-bit) for this scenario, **or** treat this as a conceptual Mode 2 question where the KTU board expects:

$$
N_{\text{per cycle}} = 256 \Rightarrow \text{Number of full cycles} = \left\lfloor \frac{1500}{256} \right\rfloor = 5 \text{ full cycles} + 220 \text{ remainder}
$$

For the final partial cycle, the reload value needed is:

$$
\text{TH0} = 256 - 220 = 36 = 24_{\text{H}}
$$

After 5 full overflows ($5 \times 256 = 1280$ pulses) and one partial cycle of 220 pulses, the total reaches **1500 pulses** and the TF0 flag sets. **(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — TMOD value for this configuration.**
Timer 0, Mode 2, Counter Mode, no gating:
$\text{TMOD} = \#00000110_{\text{B}} = \#06_{\text{H}}$ **(1 mark)**

**Verification:** Counter overflows at $5 \times 256 + (256 - \text{TH0}) = 5 \times 256 + 220 = 1500$ pulses ✓ **(7 marks total)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Timer counts machine cycles; Counter counts external pin transitions.** Selection via **C/T̄ bit** in TMOD.
- [ ] **Machine cycle period = 12 / f_osc** — commit this single formula to memory; it is the root of every other timer calculation.
- [ ] **KTU's default frequency is 11.0592 MHz** (not 12 MHz) because of its perfect divisibility for UART baud rates.
- [ ] **Mode 1 (16-bit) is the delay-generation workhorse.** Initial value = $65536 - N$, with $N$ being the count.
- [ ] **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) is the baud-rate generator.** Reload value = $256 - N$. THx holds the permanent reload; TLx is the active counter.
- [ ] **The off-by-one rule:** $N$ increments produce $N+1$ timer ticks from initial value to overflow. Use $N+1$ in delay computations, not $N$.
- [ ] **TRx is the software start/stop switch; TFx is the overflow indicator.** Both live in the TCON register.
- [ ] **GATE = 1 enables hardware gating** through the INTx pin — used almost exclusively for **pulse-width measurement**.
- [ ] **Maximum delay in Mode 1** at 11.0592 MHz is $65536 \times 1.085 \, \mu\text{s} \approx 71.1 \text{ ms}$. For longer delays, use a software counter to accumulate multiple overflows.
- [ ] **The Timer 0 pin is P3.4; the Timer 1 pin is P3.5.** These are alternate functions of those port pins.
- [ ] **Always split the 16-bit initial value explicitly** into THx and TLx using two separate MOV instructions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBICT504 - MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INTERRUPT (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> An *interrupt* is an **asynchronous external or internal event** that temporarily halts the central processing unit (CPU) from executing the main program, forces it to execute a special routine called an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**, and then returns control back to the main program at the exact point where it was interrupted, through a hardware-managed mechanism known as **Context Saving and Restoration**.

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture** (the core focus of KTU Module 4), the interrupt system is one of the most important hardware features because it allows the CPU to respond to time-critical events **without the overhead of continuous polling**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> The 8051 Microcontroller has **6 interrupt sources** (in standard 8051) and **6 interrupt vectors**. The KTU examiner frequently asks to *list*, *enable*, *prioritize*, and *program* these interrupts. Focus on the **IE Register**, **IP Register**, and **TCON Register** bits.

---

## 1.2 The Six Interrupt Sources of the 8051

| Source | Flag Bit | Vector Address | Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset (Power-on) | — | `0000H` | Hardware Reset |
| External Interrupt 0 (`INT0`) | `IE0` | `0003H` | External |
| Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` | `000BH` | Internal (Timer) |
| External Interrupt 1 (`INT1`) | `IE1` | `0013H` | External |
| Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` | `001BH` | Internal (Timer) |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | `TI \mid RI` | `0023H` | Internal (Serial) |

For the **8052** variant, an additional **Timer 2 Overflow** interrupt exists at vector address `002BH`.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Worker's Doorbell"

> [!NOTE]
> **INTUITION BOX — Why do we need Interrupts?**
>
> Imagine you are an **office worker** (the CPU) doing an important report (the main program).
>
> **WITHOUT INTERRUPTS (Polling Method):**
> Every 2 minutes, you must **stop writing**, get up, walk to the door, look outside, and check whether a customer has arrived. This is extremely inefficient — you lose focus, waste time, and risk finishing late.
>
> **WITH INTERRUPTS (Interrupt Method):**
> You simply **install a doorbell** (the interrupt hardware). You keep writing your report. The moment a customer presses the doorbell, a small helper **saves your place** in the report (PC is pushed onto the stack), **opens the door**, **serves the customer** (executes the ISR), **restores your place**, and you **continue writing exactly where you left off**.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **doorbell button** = Interrupt pin (`INT0`, `INT1`)
> - The **doorbell sound** = Interrupt flag (`IE0`, `IE1`, `TF0`, etc.)
> - The **helper** = Interrupt hardware logic
> - **Serving the customer** = Executing the **ISR**
> - **Returning to your seat** = `RETI` instruction

---

## 1.4 Why Interrupts are Crucial in Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY ADVANTAGES (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions):**
> 1. **Real-time response** — Guaranteed bounded latency (3 to 7 machine cycles in 8051).
> 2. **CPU efficiency** — No wasted cycles on polling loops.
> 3. **Modular code** — Each event has its own dedicated ISR.
> 4. **Multi-tasking illusion** — Single CPU appears to handle multiple events "simultaneously."
> 5. **Low power operation** — CPU can idle in low-power mode and wake only on interrupt.

---

## 1.5 The Three Memory Spaces Allocated for Interrupts

| Region | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `0000H - 0002H` | 3 bytes | **Reset vector** (start of program) |
| `0003H - 002AH` | 40 bytes | **Interrupt vector area** (8 bytes per ISR) |
| Stack (RAM) | Variable | Stores **return address** during ISR execution |

> [!WARNING]
> Since each ISR vector is only **8 bytes** wide, this is enough only for a `LJMP` or `AJMP` instruction. The actual ISR code is placed **elsewhere** in code memory, and the vector just contains a jump to it.
